questions.q: Among $\mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CHO}, \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CHO}$ and $\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CHO}$, which will undergo Cannizaro's reaction?

Cannizaro reaction is possible in which aldehyde where the $\alpha$-hydrogen atoms are absent.
e.g. $\mathrm{HCHO}, \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CHO}$
$2 \mathrm{HCHO}-\xrightarrow[\Delta]{\mathrm{NaOH}} \mathrm{HCOONa}+\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{OH}$
$\begin{aligned}
2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{CHO}-\stackrel{\mathrm{NaOH}}{\Delta} & \mathrm{C}_6 \\
\mathrm{H}_5 & \mathrm{CH}_2 \mathrm{OH} \\
& +\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_5 \mathrm{COONa}
\end{aligned}$

The transfer of hydride ion to the carbonyl aroun
The abstraction of proton from the carboxylic acid
questions.solution: $\mathrm{H}^{-}$ is obtained by fission of $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{H}$ bond of $(\mathrm{B})$. It is transferred to (II). This is the slowest step.

questions.q: The main product of the reaction of $\mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CONH}_2$ with $\mathrm{Br}_2$ in aqueous potassium hydroxide medium is
questions.solution:
This is Hoffmann's bromide reaction. In this reaction, product compound has one less ' $C$ ' atom than reactant.
So it causes shortening of ' $C$ ' chain.
$\begin{aligned} &-\mathrm{CONH}_2 \longrightarrow-\mathrm{NH}_2 \\ & \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{CONH}_2+\mathrm{Br}_2+ 4 \mathrm{KOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{NH}_2 \\ &+2 \mathrm{KBr}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is most basic ?
questions.solution: Due to $-R$ effect of the $\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{O}$ group the electron density on the N -atom decreases, hence amides are weaker base than $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and aliphatic amines. Further due to $-I$ effect of $\mathrm{NH}_2$ group, the electron density on the other N -atom decreases. As a result, $\mathrm{NH}_2 \mathrm{NH}_2$ is a much weaker base than $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and aliphatic amines. Therefore, ethylamine is most basic among these.

questions.q: Carbylamine test is performed in alcholic KOH by heating a mixture of
options: trihalogenated methane and a primary amine
an alkyl halide and a primary amine
an alkyl cyanide and a primary amine

Carbylamine test is performed in alcoholic KOH by heating a mixture of trihalogenated methane and a primary amine.
$\mathrm{RNH}_2 (primary \quad anime)+\mathrm{CHCl}_2 (chloroform) +3 \mathrm{KOH}$ (alc.) $\xrightarrow{\Delta}$ $R \mathrm{NC} (Alkyl \quad isocyanide) +3 \mathrm{KCl}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following reactions is given by only primary amines?
options: Reaction with chloroform and alcoholic KOH
questions.solution:
When a primary amine reacts with chloroform in presence of alco. KOH , it gives iso-cyanide which has abnoxious odour. This reaction is known as carbylamine reaction and it is given only by primary amines.
$\begin{aligned} \underset{\text { primary amine }}{\mathrm{RHN}_2}+\mathrm{CHCl}_3+\underset{\text { alco. }}{3 \mathrm{KOH} \xrightarrow{\Delta}} \mathrm{RNC}+3 \mathrm{KCl}+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}$

questions.solution:
Starch is the main storage polysaccharide of plants. It is a polymer of $\alpha$-D-glucose.
$\underset{\text { Starch }}{\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{10} \mathrm{O}_5\right)_n} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \underset{\text { Maltose }}{\mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}} \xrightarrow{\mathrm{H}^{+}} \underset{\text { D-glucose }}{\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_{12} \mathrm{O}_6}$

questions.solution:
Cane sugar reacts with conc. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ to give charrs. It is because $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ acts as a dehydrating agent.
$\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{C}_{12} \mathrm{H}_{22} \mathrm{O}_{11}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \text { (conc.) } \longrightarrow 12 \mathrm{C} \\ &+11 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the acidity of the carboxylic acids
I. PhCOOH
II. o- $\mathrm{NO}_2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{COOH}$
III. $p-\mathrm{NO}_2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{COOH}$
IV. $m-\mathrm{NO}_2 \mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{COOH}$
Which of the following order is correct?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{-NO}_2$ group at any position shows electron withdrawing effect, thus acid strength is increased. But o-nitro benzoic acid believed to have ortho effect. As a result, resonance gets prevented. Hence, its acid strength is maximum, thus, the order of acid strength (II) $\lt$ (III) $\gt$ (IV) $\gt$ (I)
(The effect is more at para position than meta.)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The spin only magnetic moment of $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$ is _____ (Nearest integer)
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Ni}: 3 d^8 4 s^2$ for the compound $\left[\mathrm{NiCl}_4\right]^{2-}$, Ni is +2 oxidation state. Hence, the electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ is given by $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}: 3 d^8$ Since, $\mathrm{Cl}^{-1}$ is a weak field ligand, so it will not cause pairing of electrons. So, for $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}$ the configuration is
Hence, $n=2$ Spin only magnetic moment is given by $\begin{aligned} & \mu_s=\sqrt{n(n+2)}=\sqrt{2(2+2)} \mathrm{BM}=\sqrt{8}=2.82 \mathrm{BM} \\ & \mu_s \simeq 3 \mathrm{~B} \mathrm{M} \end{aligned}$

questions.q:

Choose the correct match from the options given below
questions.solution:
The correct match is A-IV, B-III, C-I, D-II. 
Hybridisation involved in the given complexes are
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{CuCl}_5^{3-}, \mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{II})=s p^3 d(4 s, 4 p, 4 d) \\
& \mathrm{MnCl}_5^{3-}, \mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{II})=d s p^3(3 d, 4 s, 4 p) \\
& \mathrm{XeOF}, \mathrm{Xe}(\mathrm{VI})=s p^3 d^2(5 s, 5 p, 5 d) \\
& \mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CO})_5, \mathrm{Fe}(0)=d^2 s p^3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Correct order for solubility of alkaline earth metals in water is
questions.solution:
Order of solubility of fluorides of alkaline earth metals in water is
Halides of alkaline earth metals (except Be) are ionic solids and are therefore are water soluble and their solubility in water decreases from Mg to Ba due to the decrease in the hydration energy.
However, fluorides of alkanline earth metals excepts $\mathrm{BeF}_2$ are almost insoluble in water.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The order of relative bond strength of oxygen molecule, superoxide ion and peroxide ion is
questions.solution:
Bond strength depends on bond order. More is the bond order, more is the strength. \begin{array}{cc} Species & Bond order \\\mathrm{O}_2 & 2 \\\mathrm{O}_2^{-} & 1.5 \\\mathrm{O}_2{ }^{2-} & 1.0\end{array}
Hence, the order of bond strength is as : $\mathrm{O}_2\gt\mathrm{O}_2^{-}\gt\mathrm{O}_2^{2-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In the process, $\mathrm{O}_2^{+} \longrightarrow \mathrm{O}_2^{2+}+e^{-}$the electron lost is from
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered} \text { (a) } \mathrm{O}_2^{+}-\mathrm{O}_2^{2+}+e^{-} \\ \mathrm{O}_2^{+} \text {has }\left(15 e e^p=\sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^2, \sigma 2 s^2, \sigma^* 2 s^2\right. \\ \sigma 2 p_x^2, \pi 2 p_y^2, \pi 2 p_z^2, \pi^* 2 p_y^1 \end{gathered}$
So $\mathrm{O}_2^{+}$lost a electron to form $\mathrm{O}_2^{2+}$, the $e^{-}$is So $\mathrm{O}_2^{+}$lost a electron to form $\mathrm{O}_2^{2+}$, the $e^{-}$is from antibonding $\pi$ orbital.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following compounds has lowest boiling point?
questions.solution: According to Fajan's rule, as the size of cation decreases, it polarising power increases. Hence, $\mathrm{K}^{+}$and $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$having bigger cations contain less polarising power. $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}$ polarise $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$ions more than $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$due to its smaller size. Therefore, $\mathrm{CuCl}_2$ has more covalent character and hence, its boiling point is less.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The
bond angle in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $104.5^{\circ}$. This fact can be explained with the help of
options: valence shell electron pair repulsion theory (VSEPR)
electronegativity difference between hydrogen and oxygen atoms
questions.solution:
The
bond angle in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $104.5^{\circ}$ due to presence of two lone pair of electrons. This fact can be explained with the help of valence shell electron pair repulsion theory (VSEPR).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Order the species $\mathrm{CF}^{+}, \mathrm{CF}$ and $\mathrm{CF}^{-}$according to increasing $\mathrm{C}-\mathrm{F}$ bond length
questions.solution:
The molecular orbital electronic configuration of $\mathrm{CF}(15 \bar{e})$ is $\sigma 1 s^2, \quad{ }^* 1 s^2 \sigma 2 s^2 \quad{ }^* 2 s^2 \sigma 2 p_z^2$ $\pi 2 p_x^2 \approx \pi 2 p_y^2, \pi 2 p_x^1$
Bond order $(\mathrm{BO})=\frac{10-5}{2}=2.5$
In $\mathrm{CF}^{+}$, there is one electron less in antibonding MO therefore, $\mathrm{BO}=\frac{10-4}{2}=3$
In $\mathrm{CF}^{-}$, there is one electron more in antibonding MO therefore, $\mathrm{BO}=\frac{10-6}{2}=2$
Bond length $\propto \frac{1}{\text { Bond order }}$
Hence, the order of bond length in given series is $\mathrm{CF}^{+} \lt \mathrm{CF} \lt \mathrm{CF}^{-}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Calculate second electron affinity of oxygen for the process,
$\mathrm{O}^{-}(\mathrm{g})+e^{-}(\mathrm{g}) \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{2-}(\mathrm{g})$
by using the following data
(i) Heat of sublimation of
$\mathrm{Mg}(s)=+147.7 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(ii) Ionisation energy of $\mathrm{Mg}(g)$ to form
$\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(g)=+2189.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(iii) Bond dissociation energy for
$\mathrm{O}_2=+498.4 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(iv) First electron affinity of
$\mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g})=-141.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
(v) Heat formation of
$\mathrm{MgO}=-601.7 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}-1$
(vi) Lattice energy of
$\mathrm{MgO}=-3791.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
questions.solution:
Stepwise formation of MgO involves
\begin{array}{l|l|l}\hline Step & {|c|}{ Reaction } & \Delta H in \mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1} \\\hline (i) & \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{g}) & 147.7 \\(ii) & \mathrm{Mg}(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}+2 \mathrm{e}^{-} & 2189.0 \\(iii) & \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{O}(\mathrm{g}) & 498.4 / 2 \\(iv) & \mathrm{O}(g)+1 e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{-}(g) & -141.0 \\(v) & \mathrm{O}^{-}(g) t e^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{O}^{2-}(g) & q \\(vi) & \mathrm{Mg}^{2+}(g)+\mathrm{O}^{2-}(g) \rightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(s) & -3791.0 \\\hline\end{array}
$\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{s})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{s}) \Delta \mathrm{H}=-1346.1+q$
$\mathrm{Mg}(\mathrm{s})+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{MgO}(\mathrm{s}) \Delta H_2=-601.7$
By Born Herber cycle (based on Hes s law)
$\Delta H_1=\Delta H_2$
$-1346.1+q=-601.7$
$q=744.4 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1}$

questions.solution: $\pi 2 p_x$ and $\pi 2 p_y$ or $\pi^* 2 p_x$ and $\pi^* 2 p_y$ orbitals have nearly equal energy and thus, are called degenerate orbitals.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The molecule (ion) having one unpaired electron is
questions.solution:
Nitric oxide (NO) has one unpaired electron and it is paramagnetic in nature. It has 15 electrons.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { NO : } K K \sigma(2 s)^2 \stackrel{*}{\sigma}(2 s)^2 \sigma\left(2 p_z\right)^2  \pi\left(2 p_x\right)^2 \pi\left(2 p_y\right)^2+\left(2 p_x\right)^1

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following sets of quantum numbers could represent the last electron added to complete the electron configuration for a ground state atom of $\operatorname{Br}(Z=35)$ according to the Aufbau principle, $n ; l ; m_l ; m_s$
questions.solution:
The valence shell configuration is $4 s^2 4 p^5$ and for the last electron $n=4, l=1$ $m=-1,0 \text { or }+1, s=+\frac{1}{2} \text { or }-\frac{1}{2}$
Hence, $4,1,1,-\frac{1}{2}$

The chemical reaction of a gas phase is given below.

Which one of the following changes will affect the value of $K_c$ ?
questions.solution: $K_C$ is a function of temperature

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
If $K_\rho$ for a reaction,
$A(g)+2 B(g) \rightleftharpoons 3 C(g)+D(g)$ is 0.05 atm at 1000 K . Its $K_{\mathrm{c}}$ in term of $R$ is $\frac{x \times 10^{-5}}{R}$. The value of $x$ is ______
questions.solution:
Given reaction,
$\begin{aligned}
& A(g)+2 B(g) \rightleftharpoons 3 C(g)+D(g) \\
& \Delta n_g=4-3=1
\end{aligned}$
Formula for $K_p$,
$\begin{aligned}
K_p & =K_c(R T)^{\Delta n} \\
0.05 & =K_c(R \times 1000)^1 \\
K_c & =\frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{R}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Equivalent amounts of $\mathrm{H}_2$ and $\mathrm{I}_2$ are heated in a closed vessel till equilibrium is obtained. If $80 \%$ of the hydrogen can be converted to HI, the $K_C$ at this temperature is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2+\mathrm{I}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{HI}$
$\begin{array}{lccc}\text { At initial } & 1 & 1 & 0 \\ \text { At equili } & 1-0.8 & 1-0.8 & 2 \times 0.8 \\ & =0.2 & =0.2 & =1.6\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\because & K_C=\frac{[\mathrm{HI}]^2}{\left[\mathrm{H}_2\right]\left[\mathrm{I}_2\right]} \\ \therefore & K_C=\frac{1.6 \times 1.6}{0.2 \times 0.2} \\ \Rightarrow & K_C=64\end{array}$

Which of the structures represents possible resonance forms of this ion?
questions.solution: In structure II, position of N is changed which is not allowed in resonance.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Equal moles of $\mathrm{NO}_2(g)$ and $\mathrm{NO}(g)$ are to be placed in a container to produce $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}$ according, to the reaction, $\mathrm{NO}_2+\mathrm{NO} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2 ; K_C=0.914$
How many moles of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and NO be placed in the 5.0 L container to have an equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}$ to be 0.05 M ?
questions.solution:
Equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}=\mathrm{O}_2=0.05 \mathrm{M}$ Let the equilibrium concentration of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and NO bexM $K_c=0.917=\frac{(0.05)^2}{x^2} \Rightarrow x=0.0522 \mathrm{M}$
Initial concentrations of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and NO were $0.05+x=0.1022 \mathrm{M}$ each. Hence, moles of each gas $\mathrm{NO}_2$ and NO taken initially $=5 \times 0.1022=0.511$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In hydrolysis of a salt of weak acid and strong base, $A^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{HA}+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$, the hydrolysis constant $\left(K_h\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
In hydrolysis of a salt of weak acid and strong base. $A^{-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H} A+\mathrm{OH}^{-}$ the hydrolysis constant $K_h$ $K_h=\frac{[\mathrm{HA}]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right]}{\left[A^{-}\right]}$ (According to law of mass action) ( $\because$ conc. of water remains constant) For the ionisation of weak acid HA, we has $\mathrm{HA} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+A^{-}$ $K_a=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]}{[\mathrm{HA}]}$
Also we know that $\begin{aligned} & \frac{K_w}{K_a}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right][\mathrm{HA}]}{\left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\left[A^{-}\right]} \\ & \frac{K_w}{K_a}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{OH}^{-}\right][\mathrm{HA}]}{\left[A^{-}\right]} \end{aligned}$ $\text { or } \quad \frac{K_w}{K_a}=K_h$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the chemical equilibrium,
$2 \mathrm{NO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(\mathrm{~g}) ;+14.6 \mathrm{kcal}$
increase in temperature
questions.solution: The given reaction is exothermic and according to Le-Chatelier principle, high temperature shifts the equilibrium in backward direction, i.e. high temperature favours the decomposition of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The vapour density of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ at a certain temperature is 30 . What is the percentage dissociation of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ at this temperature?
questions.solution:
The reaction is
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2$
Molecular weight of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4=2 \times 14+4 \times 16$
$=92$
$\therefore \quad$ Vapour density (D) of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$
From, $\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\text { molecular weight }}{2} \\
& =\frac{92}{2}=46 \\
\alpha & =\frac{D-d}{(n-1) d} \\
\alpha & =\frac{46-30}{(2-1) 30}=\frac{16}{30} \\
& =0.533=53.3 \%
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\mathrm{N}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_3 ;+22 \mathrm{kcal}$
The activation energy for the forward reaction is 50 kcal . What is the activation energy for the backword reaction?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_3(g) ;+22 \mathrm{kcal}$
$\because$ The activation energy for the forward reaction
$=50 \mathrm{kcal}$
$\therefore$ The activation energy for the backward reaction
$=50+22=72 \mathrm{kcal}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the order of the reaction which has a rate expression
$\text { rate }=k[A]^{\frac{3}{2}}[B]^{-1} ?$
questions.solution:
$\because$ Rate $=k[A]^{3 / 2}[B]^{-1}$
$\therefore$ Order of reaction $=\frac{3}{2}-1=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a reaction doubles when the initial concentration of the reactant is made four fold. If the initial concentration is made 400 fold, then the rate will become
questions.solution:
$R=k[A]^x$     ....(i)
$2 R=k[4 A]^x$    ....(ii)
Divide Eq. (ii) by (i)
$\begin{aligned} 2 & =[4]^x \\ R & =k[A]^{1 / 2} \\ x & =\frac{1}{2}=0.5 \\ R & =k(400 A)^{1 / 2} \\ \text { Rate } & =20 k[A]^{1 / 2}\end{aligned}$
Rate becomes 20 times.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $99 \%$ of a first order reaction was completed in 32 min . When will $99.9 \%$ of the reaction complete?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {} k=\frac{2.303}{32} \log \frac{a}{a-0.99 a}=0.1439 \mathrm{~min}^{-1} \\ & t=\frac{2.303}{0.1439} \log \frac{a}{a-0.999 a}=48 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a gaseous reaction is given by the expression $k[A][B]$. If the volume of the reaction vessel is suddenly reduced to $1 / 4$ th of the initial volume, the reaction rate relating to original rate will be :
questions.solution:
On reducing the volume $\frac{1}{4}$ th of the initial volume, each concentration will be increased by 4 times.
$\begin{aligned}(\text { Rate })_1 & =k[A][B] \\ (\text { Rate })_2 & =k[4[\mathrm{~A}][4[\mathrm{~B}]]\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\frac{[\text { Rate }]_2}{[\text { Rate }]}=4 \times 4=16$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following does not influence the rate of reaction?
questions.solution: Nature and concentration of the reactants its and temperature of the reaction influence the rate of reaction. But molecularity does not affect the rate of reaction as it includes the number of atoms, ions or molecules that must collide with one another to result into a chemical reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activation energy of a reaction is zero. The rate constant of the reaction:
options: increases with an increase of temperature
decreases with an increase of temperature
questions.solution:
From Arrhenius equation,
$\log k=\log A-\frac{E_a}{2.303 R T}$
As the activation energy of a reaction is zero, the rate constant of this reaction becomes zero. It shows that the reaction is independent of temperature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order reaction, time required for $99 \%$ completion of a reaction is $x$ times the time required for completion of $90 \%$. The value of $x$ is .......
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
t_{99 \%} & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{(a)}{a-0.99 a} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10^2 \Rightarrow 2 \times \frac{2.303}{k} ...(i)\\
t_{90 \%} & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{a}{a-0.90 a} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{k} \log 10=\frac{2.303}{k}...(ii)
\end{aligned}$
Take the ratio of (i) and (ii)
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{t_{99 \%}}{t_{90 \%}}=2 \\
& t_{99 \%}=2 \times t_{90 \%}
\end{aligned}$
Hence, 2 times is the final answer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For the reaction, $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5(g) \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g)$ the value of the rate of disappearance of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5$ is given as $5.15 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. The rate of formation of $\mathrm{NO}_2$ is..
questions.solution:
$\left(1.03 \times 10^{-2}\right)$ For the given reaction,
$\begin{aligned}
\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5(g) \longrightarrow & 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_2(g) \\
-\frac{d\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{d t} & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{d t}=\frac{2 d\left[\mathrm{O}_2\right]}{d t} \\
\therefore \quad \frac{d\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]}{d t} & =-2 \frac{d\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_5\right]}{d t}=2 \times 5.15 \times 10^{-3} \\
& =1.03 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~mol} \mathrm{~L}^{-1} \mathrm{~s}^{-1} .
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A certain reaction rate increases 1000 folds in the presence of a catalyst at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The activation energy of the original pathway is $98 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{mol}$. What is the activation energy of the new pathway?
questions.solution:
$\log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\log 1000$
$\begin{aligned}= & \frac{E_1-E_2}{R T} \\ & =\frac{98000-E_2}{8.314 \times 300} \\ E_2 & \text { (catalyst) }=80.77 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$9.2 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ is heated in 1 L vessel till equilibrium state is established.
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$
In equilibrium state, $50 \% \mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4$ was dissociated, equilibrium constant will be (molecular wt. of $\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4=92$ )
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NO}_2(g)$
Molar concentration of
$\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]=\frac{9.2}{92}=0.1 \mathrm{~mol} / \mathrm{L}$
In equilibrium state,
when it 50\% dissociates
$\begin{aligned} {\left[\mathrm{N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right] } & =0.05 \mathrm{M} \\ {\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right] } & =0.1 \mathrm{M}\end{aligned}$
Now, from
$K_C=\frac{\left[\mathrm{NO}_2\right]^2}{\left[\mathrm{~N}_2 \mathrm{O}_4\right]}$
$K_C=\frac{0.1 \times 0.1}{0.05}=0.2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate of a chemical reaction involving liquids only will not be influenced by the
options: number of reacting particles per mL
questions.solution: Pressure of the system will not influenced the rate of a chemical reaction involving liquids.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction in an aqueous solution
$\mathrm{I}^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{IO}^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q)$
and the following initial concentration and initial rate data for this reaction.
\begin{array}{c|c|c|c}\hline Exp. No & {\left[1^{-}\right] \mathbf{M}} & {\left[\mathrm{OCl}^{-}\right] \mathrm{M}} & Initial rate \mathrm{Ms}^{-1} \\\hline 1. & 0.1000 & 0.0500 & 3.05 \times 10^{-4} \\2. & 0.2000 & 0.0500 & 6.10 \times 10^{-4} \\3. & 0.3000 & 0.0100 & 1.83 \times 10^{-4} \\4. & 0.3000 & 0.0200 & 3.66 \times 10^{-4} \\\hline\end{array}
Which of the following is the correct rate law for this reaction?
questions.solution:
From experiment no -3 and 4, doubling $\left[\mathrm{OCl}^{-}\right]$ doubles the rate; i.e., order with respect to $\mathrm{OCl}^{-}$is one. Also, from experiment no 1 and 2, doubes the concentration of $I^{-}$double the rate, i.e. order with respect tol- is also one.
rate $=k\left[I^{-}\right]\left[\mathrm{OCl}^{-}\right]$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
$\mathrm{N}_2(g)+3 \mathrm{H}_2(g) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_3(g)+2 \mathrm{kcal}$
The activation energy for the forward reaction is 50 kcal . What is the activation energy for the backward reaction?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{N}_2(\mathrm{~g})+3 \mathrm{H}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{NH}_3(\mathrm{~g})+22 \mathrm{kcal}$
$\because$ Activation energy for the forward reaction
$=50 \mathrm{kcal}$
$\therefore$ Activation energy for the backward reaction
$=50+22=72 \mathrm{kcal}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The half-life period of a first order process is 1.6 min . It will be $90 \%$ complete in
questions.solution:
For a first order reaction
$k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-x}$
At half-time,
$\begin{aligned}
x & =\frac{a}{2} \\
\therefore \quad k & =\frac{2.303}{1.6} \times \log \frac{a}{a-\frac{a}{2}} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{1.6} \times \log 2 \\
& =\frac{2.303}{1.6} \times 0.3010 \\
& =0.4332
\end{aligned}$
For $90 \%$ completion,
$\begin{aligned}
x & =0.9 a \\
k & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{a-0.9 a} \\
0.4332 & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log \frac{a}{0.1 a} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{t} \log 10 \\
& =\frac{2.303}{t} \times 1 \\
t & =\frac{2.303}{0.4332} \\
& =5.3 \mathrm{~min}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Radioactive decay is a
questions.solution: Radioactive decay is a first order reaction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The activation energy of exothermic reaction $A \rightarrow B$ is $80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The heat of reaction is $200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$. The activation energy for the reaction $B \rightarrow A$ (in $\mathrm{kJ} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) will be
questions.solution:
$E_a(A \rightarrow B)=80 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
Heat of reaction $(A \rightarrow B)=200 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$
For $(B \rightarrow A)$ backward reaction,
$\begin{aligned}
E_a(B \rightarrow A) & =E_a(A \rightarrow B)+\text { heat of reaction } \\
& =80+200=280 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a reaction of the type $a A+b B \longrightarrow$ Products, the $-\frac{d[A]}{d t}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$a A+b B \longrightarrow$ Products
Rate of reactant for $A=-\frac{1}{a} \frac{d[A]}{d t}$ Rate of reactant for $B=-\frac{1}{b} \frac{d[B]}{d t}$ the $-\frac{d[A]}{d t}$ is equal to $=-\frac{a}{b} \frac{d[B]}{d t}$

the temperature at which $k_1$ becomes equal to $k_2$ is

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The two-third life $\left(t_{2 / 3}\right)$ of a first order reaction in which $k=5.48 \times 10^{-14}$ per sec, is
questions.solution:
For two third of a reaction
$\begin{aligned}
{[A]_0=a_1[A] } & =a-\frac{2}{3} a=\frac{a}{3} \\
t_{2 / 3} & =\frac{2.303}{k} \log \frac{[A]_0}{[A]} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{5.48 \times 10^{-14}} \log \frac{a}{a / 3} \\
& =\frac{2.303}{5.48 \times 10^{-14}} \log 3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following reaction in aqueous solution. $\begin{aligned} & 5 \mathrm{Br}^{-}(a q)+\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}(a q)+6 \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{Br}_2(a q) \\ &+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \end{aligned}$
If the rate of appearance of $\mathrm{Br}_2$ at a particular moment during the reaction is $0.025 \mathrm{Ms}^{-1}$, what is the rate of disappearance (in $\mathrm{Ms}^{-1}$ ) of $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$at that moment?
questions.solution: The two rates are related as- $\left.\frac{1}{5} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{d t}=\frac{1}{3} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]}{d t}\right]$ $-\frac{d\left[\mathrm{Br}^{-}\right]}{d t}=\frac{5}{3} \frac{d\left[\mathrm{Br}_2\right]}{d t}=\frac{5}{3} \times 0.025=0.042 \mathrm{ms}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Raw milk sours in about 4 h at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, but in about 48 h in a refrigerator at $17^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What is the activation energy for souring of milk?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & K \propto \frac{1}{t} \\ & \log \frac{k_2}{k_1}=\log \frac{t_1}{t_2}=\frac{E_a}{R}\left(\frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1 \cdot T_2}\right) \\ & \log \frac{48}{4}=\frac{E_a}{8.314}\left(\frac{10}{300 \times 290}\right) \\ & E_a=78.0 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a first order reaction, the concentration changes from 0.8 to 0.4 in 15 min . The time taken for the concentration to change from 0.1 M to 0.025 M is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & T_{50}=15 \mathrm{~min} \\ & \qquad k=\frac{2.303 \log 2}{T_{50}}=\frac{2.303 \log 2}{15}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
a & =0.1 \mathrm{M} \\
(a-x) & =0.025 \mathrm{M}
\end{aligned}$
For first order reaction,
$k=\frac{2.303}{t} \log \left(\frac{a}{a-x}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{2.303 \log 2}{15} & =\frac{2 \times 2.303}{t} \log \frac{0.1}{0.025} \\ & =\frac{2.303}{t} \log 4\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{2.303 \log 2}{15}=\frac{2 \times 2.303 \log 2}{t}$
$\therefore \quad t=30 \mathrm{~min}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The rate constant for the first order reaction is $60 \mathrm{~s}^{-1}$. How much time will it take to reduce the concentration of the reactant to $\frac{1}{16}$ th value?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} t=\frac{2.303}{k} & \log \frac{[A]_0}{[A]} \\ & =\frac{2.303}{60}=\log \frac{a}{\frac{a}{16}}=\frac{2.303}{60} \log 16 \\ & =\frac{2.303}{60} \times 1.204 \\ & =0.0462 \mathrm{~s}=4.6 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.solution: Indigo is a vat dye. Vat dyes are insoluble in water and cann't be applied directly.

questions.solution: Sodium alkyl benzene sulphonate is used as detergent.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Whose name is not associated with the development of periodic table?
questions.solution:
Prout's gave unitary theory for the development of periodic table. He suggested that the atoms of all the element are made up of hydrogen atom.
Newlands gave law of octaves: According to him, "If elef̂ents are arranged in the increasing order of their atomic weights, the eighth element starting from the given one is the repetition of the first like the eighth node in an octave of music. Lother Meyer's plotted the atomic volumes (i.e., at. wt/density) of various elements against their atomic weight and pointed out that the similar element occupy similar position on the curve.
Rutherford is associated with structure of atom. He bombarded high speed $\alpha$-particles on nitrogen and proved that nuclei of all atoms have protons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Electronegativity of the following elements increases in the order
questions.solution:
As electronegativity increases across the period, therefore, electronegativity of nitrogen ( N ) is more than carbon (C) and electronegativity of $P$ is more than Si .
Also electronegativity decreases down the group, therefore electronegativity of carbon is more than Si and electronegativity of $N$ is more than $P$. Hence, the correct increasing order will be Si, P, C, N.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: All the s-block elements of the periodic table are placed in the groups .....
questions.solution: According to the modern periodic table. If the last electron of element is placed in $s$-orbital, then elements are known as $s$-block element. Group IA has elements with $n s^1$ configuration and IIA has elements with $n s^2$ configuration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q:
Bond between $A$ and $B$ can be represented by
$A-B, A^{+} B^{-}, A^{-} B^{+}$
(I) (II) (III)
If $A$ is more electronegative than $B$, then least contribution to the actual structure comes from
options: All the structures have equal contribution
questions.solution: Structure II shows $A$ to be electropositive. Hence, It will have least contribution.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The correct order of ionic radius of nitrogen family is
questions.solution:
Atomic raddii as well as ionic radii increases down the group. All the element of the nitrogen family contain same charge, i.e. - 3.
Thus, the correct order of ionic radius of nitrogen family is $\mathrm{N}^{3-} \lt \mathrm{P}^{3-} \lt \mathrm{As}^{3-} \lt \mathrm{Sb}^{3-} \lt \mathrm{Bi}^{3-}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which element has maximum first ionisation potential?
questions.solution: Atoms and ions having inert gas configuration (such as helium), i.e. stable (half-filled) configuration have high ionisation potential, i.e. it is relatively easy to remove an electron from a partially filled valence shell, where $Z_{\text {eff }}$ is lower but it is relatively difficult to remove an electron from an atom or ion that has a filled valence shell, where $Z_{\text {eff }}$ is higher.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The ionisation energy of nitrogen is larger than that of oxygen because
options: the size of nitrogen atom is smaller
there is greater attraction of electrons towards the nucleus
half-filled p-orbitals of nitrogen have extra stability
questions.solution: The ionisation energy of nitrogen is larger than that of oxygen because half-filled $p$-orbitals of nitrogen have extra stability while oxygen has $2 p^4$ electronic configuration of the outermost shell, hence its electrons can be easily removed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following has the highest electron affinity?
questions.solution:
In general, electron affinity increases in a period from left to right. This is due to decrease in size and increase in nuclear charge as the atomic number increases in a period. Potassium is a metallic element which is electron donor. $\mathrm{O}^{-}$ and $\mathrm{F}^{-}$ ions gain stability by taking one electron.
Only O (oxygen atoms) has tendency to gain electron to attain stability.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: In which of the following arrangement the order is not according to the property indicated against it?
options: $1 \lt \mathrm{Br} \lt \mathrm{F} \lt \mathrm{Cl}$ (increasing electron gain enthalpy, with negative sign)
$B \lt C \lt N \lt O$ (increasing . first ionisation enthalpy)
questions.solution:
(a) Metallic radii increase in a group from top to bottom. Thus, $\mathrm{Li} \lt \mathrm{Na} \lt \mathrm{K} \lt \mathrm{Rb}$ is true.
(b) Electron gain enthalpy of $\mathrm{Cl}\gt\mathrm{F}$ and afterwards it decreases along a group.
(c) Ionisation enthalpy increase along a period left to right but due to presence of half-filled orbital in N , ionisation enthalpy of $\mathrm{N}\gt\mathrm{O}$. Thus $\mathrm{B} \lt \mathrm{C} \lt \mathrm{N} \lt \mathrm{O}$ is incorrect.
(d) For isoelectronic species, ionic size $\propto \frac{1}{\text { atomic number }}$
Thus , $\mathrm{Al}^{3+} \lt \mathrm{Mg}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Na}^{+} \lt \mathrm{F}^{-}$is true.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The dominant factor in determining the IE of the elements on moving down the groups is its
questions.solution: Along a group $Z$ (effective nuclear charge) is almost constant. Thus, IE depends mainly on $r_n$ (atomic radius).

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which has maximum ionisation potential?
questions.solution: $0=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p_x^2, 2 p_y^1, 2 p_z^1$ Ionisation potential is dependent on the value of $Z$. $Z$ of $\mathrm{O}^{+}(8) \gt Z$ of $\mathrm{N}(7)$ Thus, ionisation potential of $\mathrm{O}^{+} \gt \mathrm{N}$ Na with one valence electron has least ionisation potential.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The set representing the correct order of first ionisation potential is
questions.solution:
The first ionisation potential $\left(\mathrm{IE}_1\right)$ decreases from top to bottom in a group. Hence, the order of ionisation potential is as :
$\mathrm{Be} \gt \mathrm{Mg} \gt \mathrm{Ca}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: The electron affinity of Be is almost similar to that of
questions.solution: The electron affinity of Be is almost similar to that of Ne because they have stable (fully filled subshells) configuration.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following has largest ionic radius?
questions.solution:
Atomic and ionic radii increase from top to bottom in a group due to the inclusion of another shell at every step. Hence, $\mathrm{Cs}^{+}$ion will be the largest among given IA group ions, i.e.
$\mathrm{Na}^{+}, \mathrm{Li}^{+}$and $\mathrm{K}^{+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Screening effect is not observed in
questions.solution: $\mathrm{He}^{+}, \mathrm{Li}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Be}^{3+}$ each has one electron, hence no screening.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Which of the following is a property of non-metal?
questions.solution: Non-metals are electronegative in nature.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: Classification of Elements and Periodicity in Properties
questions.q: Calculate the electronegativity of chlorine from bond energy of $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{F}$ bond ( $61 \mathrm{kcal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) $\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F}\left(38 \mathrm{kcal} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$ and $\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{Cl}$ bond ( 58 kcal $\mathrm{mol}^{-1}$ ) and electronegativity of fluorine 4.0 eV
questions.solution:
Based on Pauling's scale
$(\mathrm{EN})_{\mathrm{F}}-(\mathrm{EN})_{\mathrm{Cl}}=k \sqrt{\Delta}=0.208 \sqrt{\Delta}$
where, $\Delta$ = resonance energy and $k=$ conversion factor which is 0.208 for converting k cal into eV .
$\begin{aligned} \Delta & =(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{F}}-\sqrt{(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{Cl}-\mathrm{Cl}}(\mathrm{BE})_{\mathrm{F}-\mathrm{F}}} \\ & =61-\sqrt{58 \times 38} \\ & =61-46.95=14.05 \mathrm{kcal} \\ (\mathrm{EN})_{\mathrm{Cl}} & =(\mathrm{EN})_{\mathrm{F}}-0.208 \sqrt{\Delta} \\ & =4.0-0.208 \sqrt{14.05} \\ & =4.0-0.78=3.22 \mathrm{eV} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The coordination number of central metal atom in a complex is determined by
options: the number of ligands around a metal ion bonded by sigma bonds
the number of ligands around a metal ion bonded by $\pi$ bonds
the number of ligands around a metal ion bonded by sigma and $\pi$ bonds both
questions.solution:
The number of atoms of the ligands that are directly bound to the central metal atom or ion by coordinate bond is known as the coordination number of the metal atom or ion.
Coordination number of metal $=$ number of $\sigma$ bonds formed by metal with ligands.

questions.subject: Chemistry

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The oxidation state of Cr in $\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3,\left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6\right)_2\right]$, $\mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_2(\mathrm{O})_2\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\right]$ respectively are
questions.solution:
Oxidation state of Cr ,
$\begin{aligned}
& {In} \left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{H}_2O\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3 \\
& x+0 \times 6-1 \times 3=0 \\
& x=3...(i)
\end{aligned}$
$\ln \left[\mathrm{Cr}\left(\mathrm{C}_6 \mathrm{H}_6\right)_2\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & x+0 \times 2=0 \\ & x=0..(ii)\end{aligned}$
$\text { In } \mathrm{K}_2\left[\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{CN})_2(\mathrm{O})_2\left(\mathrm{O}_2\right)\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)\right]$
$(1 \times 2)+x-(1 \times 2)-(2 \times 2)-(1 \times 2)-(0 \times 1)=0$
$x-6=0$
$x=+6...(iii)$
Therefore, oxidation states are $+3,0,+6$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
In the reaction,
$\begin{aligned} 4 M+8 \mathrm{CN}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & +\mathrm{O}_2 \\ & \longrightarrow 4\left[M(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The oxidation number of sulphur in $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~S}_2 \mathrm{O}_8$ is
questions.solution: $\because$ 20-atoms are in peroxide linkage $\therefore$ Their oxidation state $=-1$ and, oxidation state of remaining 60 -atoms, $=-2$ Oxidation state of H -atoms $=+1$ Let oxidation state of S atom $=x$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{S}_2 \quad \mathrm{H}_2 \quad \mathrm{O}_6 \quad \mathrm{O}_2 \\ & \therefore 2 x+2(+1)+6(-2)+2(-1)=0 \\ & 2 x+2-12-2=0 \\ & x=+6 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The effective atomic number of central Cu (at. no. 29) metal in $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right] \mathrm{SO}_4$ is
questions.solution:
EAN of Cu in $\left[\mathrm{Cu}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_4\right] \mathrm{SO}_4$ is as:
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{EAN}=Z-(\mathrm{O} . \mathrm{N} .)+2(\mathrm{C} . \mathrm{N} .) \\
& \mathrm{EAN}=29-2+4 \times 2=35
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following complex ion, the central matal ion is in a state of $s p^3 d^2$ hybridisation?
questions.solution:
$\left[\mathrm{Co} \mathrm{F}_6\right]^{3-}$ ion
$\mathrm{F}^{-}$is a weak ligand. It cannot pair up $d$-electrons and forms outer orbital octahedral complex. $\mathrm{NH}_3$ and $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$are strong ligands. So, they pair up $d$-electrons and form their inner orbital complex.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is diamagnetic?
questions.solution:
Diamagnetic species have all electrons paired, whereas paramagnetic species have unpaired electrons.
$\mathrm{H}_2=\sigma 1 \mathrm{~s}^2$
No. of unpaired electron $=0 \Rightarrow$ diamagnetic
$\mathrm{H}_2^{-}=\sigma 1 s, \sigma^* 1 s^1$
No. of unpaired electron $=1 \Rightarrow$ paramagnetic
$\mathrm{H}_2^{-}=\sigma 1 s^1$
No. of unpaired electron $=1 \Rightarrow$ paramagnetic
$\mathrm{He}_2^{+}=\sigma 1 s^2, \sigma^* 1 s^1$
No. of unpaired electron $=1 \Rightarrow$ paramagnetic

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ions form most stable complex compound?
questions.solution:
The magnitude of stability constants for some divalent metal ions of the first transition series with oxygen or nitrogen donor ligands increases in the order
$\mathrm{Mn}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Co}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Ni}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Cu}^{2+} \lt \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Coordination number of Fe in the complexes $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-},\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{3-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Fe} \mathrm{Cl}_4\right]^{-}$would be respectively
questions.solution: Coordination number is equal to total number of ligands in a complex.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: A group of atoms can function as a ligand only when
options: it has an unshared electron pair
it is a negatively charged ion
it is a positively charged ion
questions.solution: An species or group of atoms can act as ligand only when it carries an unshared pair, ie, lone pair of electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Select the correct statement.
options: $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is $s p^3$ hybridised and square planar in shape whereas $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ is $d s p^2$ hybridised and tetrahedral in shape.
$\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ is paramagnetic and $d s p^2$ hybridised whereas $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ is diamagnetic and $s p^3$ hybridised.
questions.solution:
Both $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$ and $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ are diamagnetic. $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CN})_4\right]^{2-}$
Electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Ni}^{2+}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8$. $\mathrm{CN}^{-}$being strong field ligand cause pairing of electrons.
There are no unpaired electrons. Therefore, it is diamagnetic. Shape : square planar $\left[\mathrm{Ni}(\mathrm{CO})_4\right]$ Electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Ni}=[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8 4 s^2$ CO being strong field ligand cause pairing of electrons.
No unpaired electrons are there. Therefore, it is diamagnetic in nature. Shape : Tetrahedral.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Ethylene diamine is an example of
questions.solution: Ethylene diamine is an example of bidentate ligand. Its structure is as :

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The type of isomerism present in nitropentamine chromium (III) chloride is
questions.solution: The nitro group can attach to metal through nitrogen as $\left(-\mathrm{NO}_2\right)$ or through oxygen as nitro (-ONO), hence nitropentamine chromium (III) chloride shows linkage isomerism.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Low spin complexes are generally formed by elements of
questions.solution: As the crystal field splitting in case of complexes of metal ions in $4 d$ and $5 d$ transition series are much larger,the electrons of the metal ion are paired up first in lower energy set of orbitals before going to the higher energy set within the same $d$-subshell causing maximum pairing and thus, low spin (or spin paired) complexes are formed.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Metal carbonyls can have formula $M(C O)_x$, where $x=$ number of CO units coordinated to metal $M$. What is the formula of the carbonyl of Fo (26) if EAN of Fe in metal carbonyl is 36 ?
questions.solution: EAN $=Z-(\mathrm{O} . \mathrm{N})+.2 x$ or $36=26-0+2 x$ or $x=5$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which one of the following complexes is an outer orbital complex?
(Atomic numbers $\mathrm{Mn}=25, \mathrm{Fe}=26, \mathrm{Co}=27$, $\mathrm{Ni}=28)$
questions.solution:
The complex in which nd orbitals are used in hybridisation, are called outer orbital complex. (a) $\left[\mathrm{Fe}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}=$
(b) $\left[\mathrm{Mn}(\mathrm{CN})_6\right]^{4-}=$
(c) $\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}=$
(d) $\left[\mathrm{Ni}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{2+}=$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: 3.92 g of ferrous ammonium sulphate crystals are dissolved in 100 mL of water, 20 mL of this solution requires 18 mL of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ during titrating for complete oxidation. the weight of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ present in one liter of solution is :
questions.solution:
Normality of salt solution $=\frac{3.92}{392} \times \frac{1}{100} \times 1000$
$=0.1 \mathrm{~N}$
20 mL of 0.1 N salt solution $\equiv 18 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ solution
20 mL of 0.1 N salt solution $\equiv 18 \mathrm{~mL}$ of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ solution
$\therefore$ Normality of $\mathrm{KMnO}_4$ solution $=\frac{20 \times 0.1}{18}$
$\begin{array}{r}\therefore \text { Normality of } \mathrm{KMnO}_4 \text { solution }=\frac{20 \times 0.1}{18} \\ \quad=\frac{1}{9} \mathrm{~N}\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Strength of } \mathrm{KMnO}_4 \text { solution } & =\frac{1}{9} \times 31.6 \\ & =3.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~L}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Pure conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ make iron passive, as the surface is covered with protective layer of
questions.solution: $\mathrm{HNO}_3$ is a strong oxidising agent. So it oxidise iron and form a layer of $\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4$ that works as protective layer.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following ion is colourless inspite of the presence of unpaired electrons?
questions.solution: Gadolinium does not absorb light in the visible region of spectrum, hence it is colourless inspite of the presence of seven unpaired electrons.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which one of the following is not coloured?
questions.solution:
Transition metal ion having electronic configuration $(n-1) d^{1-9}$ forms coloured ion.
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(Z=29):[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^9 \\
& \mathrm{Cu}^{+}(Z=29):[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^{10} \\
& \mathrm{Ni}^{2+}(Z=28):[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^8 \\
& \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}(Z=26):[\mathrm{Ar}] 3 d^5
\end{aligned}$
Hence, $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$ion is not coloured.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The number of oxygen atoms that are directly attached to one chromium in dichromate ion are ........ .
questions.solution: 4 oxygen atoms are directly attached to one chromium ion.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Among $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}, \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ ions.
The number of ions that impart colour is____.
questions.solution: $\mathrm{Cu}^{2+}, \mathrm{Fe}^{3+}, \mathrm{Ti}^{3+}$ have unpaired electron which impart colour to the ions whereas $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ has no unpaired, thus it does not impart any colour.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Acidic dichromate ion reacts with hydrogen peroxide to give deep blue colour. This is due to the formation of
questions.solution: When cold $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ is added in cold mixture of $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ and conc. $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4$ (i.e. $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ in acidic medium), a deep blue solution of blue perchromate is obtained as, $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{SO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When $\mathrm{MnO}_2$ is heated with $\mathrm{PbO}_2$ and conc. $\mathrm{HNO}_3$, pink colour is obtained due to formation of
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{MnO}_2+4 \mathrm{HNO}_3 \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{O}_2 \\ & 2 \mathrm{Mn}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2+5 \mathrm{PbO}_2+6 \mathrm{HNO}_3 \rightarrow \underset{\text { Permanganic acid(violet pink) }}{2 \mathrm{HMnO}_4}+5 \mathrm{~Pb}\left(\mathrm{NO}_3\right)_2+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7$ on heating with aqueous caustic soda gives
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}$ on heating with aqueous alkalies gives chromates
$\mathrm{K}_2 \mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7+2 \mathrm{NaOH} \longrightarrow \mathrm{~K}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4+\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The electronic configurations of chromium (at. no. 24) and copper (at. no. 29) are
questions.solution: Chromium and copper have five and ten electrons in $3 d$-orbitals rather than four and nine because fully-filled and half-filled orbitals have lower energy and thus have extra stability. Therefore, chromium and copper have $d^5$ and $d^{10}$-configurations.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Metals are usually used as catalysts belong to
questions.solution: $d$-block metals, e.g., Ni, Pt, Fe, etc., are used as catalysts.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Due to lanthanide contraction
options: Zr and Hf have equal size
all isoelectronic ions have equal size
questions.solution: The lanthanide contraction cancels almost exactly the normal size increase on descending a group of transition elements. Thus, Zr and Hf have equal size.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The element which forms ions in dimeric state is
questions.solution: Mercurous ion is diamagnetic, this indicates that there is no unpaired electron, hence, mercurous ion is not $\mathrm{Hg}^{+}$but $\mathrm{Hg}_2{ }^{2+}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Gd}(64)$ is written as
questions.solution:
Gd. $(64)=[\mathrm{Xe}] 4 f^7 5 d^1$
All electrons are unpaired, hence greater stability

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: If $\mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g})$ under pressure is passed into $\mathrm{Na}_2 \mathrm{CrO}_4(a q)$, yellow colour solution changes to
questions.solution:
$\begin{gathered}\mathrm{CO}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{CO}_3 \rightleftharpoons \mathrm{H}^{+}+\mathrm{HCO}_3^{-} \\ \underset{\substack{2 \mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-} \\ \text { yellow }}}{2 \mathrm{Cr}^{+}} \longrightarrow \underset{\text { orange red }}{\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}}\end{gathered}$
Aqueous $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is acidic and changes yellow $\mathrm{CrO}_4^{2-}$ into orange $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Spiegeleisn is an alloy of
questions.solution: Spiegeleisn is an alloy of $\mathrm{Fe}, \mathrm{C}$ and Mn .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Chloride of which of the following element is coloured?
questions.solution:
In $\mathrm{CoCl}_3, \mathrm{Co}$ is in +3 state
$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}=1 s^2, 2 s^2 2 p^6, 3 s^2 3 p^6 3 d^6, 4 s^{\circ}$
4 unpaired electrons, so it will be coloured.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When hydrogen peroxide is added to acidified potassium dichromate, a blue colour is produced due to formation of
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2$ oxidises the acidified potassium dichromate solution into blue peroxide of chromium, $\mathrm{CrO}_5$.
$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_7^{2-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+}+4 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{CrO}_5+5 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is Rinmann's green?
questions.solution: \begin{array}{l|l|l}\hline & {|c|}{ Formula } & {c}{ Name } \\\hline (a) & \mathrm{ZnO} & Philosopher's wool \\(b) & \mathrm{ZnS}+\mathrm{BaSO}_4 & Lithopone \\(c) & \mathrm{ZnSO}{ }_4 \cdot 7 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} & White vitriol \\(d) & \mathrm{ZnO} \cdot \mathrm{CoO} & Rinmann's green \\\hline\end{array}

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following has highest second ionisation energy
questions.solution: The electronic configuration of ${ }_{24} C_r$ is $[\mathrm{Ar}]_{18} 3 d_1^5 4 s^1$. Chromium after loss of one electron gain stable configuration due to presence of half filled d-orbitals, therefore its second ionisation enthalphy is highest.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the characteristic valence shell configuration of coinage metals ?
questions.solution: Coinage metals are $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}$ and Au . They are transition metal and present in group II or IB. The valence shell configuration is $(n-1) d^{10} n s^1$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Red hot iron absorbs $\mathrm{SO}_2$ giving the product
questions.solution:
It is the oxidation process. $3 \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{SO}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{FeS}$
Fe is oxidised to form FeO and FeS .

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: What is the general electronic configuration for 2nd row transition series?
questions.solution:
Transition elements have three series $3 d$, $4 d$ and 5d.
$3 d$ series has elements $21(\mathrm{Sc})$ to $30(\mathrm{Zn})$ atomic number. 4 d series has elements 39 (Y) to 48 (Cd) atomic number.
$[\mathrm{Kr}] 4 d^{1-10}, 5 s^{1-2}$ has IInd row series.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Select incorrect statements.
options: Ionisation energies of $5 d$ - elements are greater than those of $3 d$ and $4 d$ elements.
Cu (I) is diamagnetic while Cu (II) is paramagnetic.
questions.solution: Transition elements form comprexers . due to their straller'size, higher nuclear charge and presence of low energy vacant orbitals to accept lone pair of electrons donated by ligands.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Solder is an alloy of lead with
questions.solution: Solder is an alloy of tin and lead. Its melting point is quite low, hence, it is very useful in stitching ICs in various electrical instruments.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: On igniting $\mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ at $1400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, the product obtained is
questions.solution:
On igniting at $1400^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ get reduced to metallic Fe.
$3 \mathrm{Fe}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4+\mathrm{CO}_2$
$\mathrm{Fe}_3 \mathrm{O}_4+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow 3 \mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{CO}_2$
$\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{CO} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Fe}+\mathrm{CO}_2$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The reaction is spontaneous if the cell potential is
questions.solution:
For a reaction to be spontaneous $\Delta G$ should be negative.
$\Delta G=-n F E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$
From this reaction, $\Delta G$ will be negative when $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ is positive.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The standard emf of a cell $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \|$
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}$, if electrode potentials for
$\left(\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}\right)$ and $\left(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}\right)$ and 0.763 V and
-0.44 V respectively is
questions.solution:
Cell $\mathrm{Zn}\left|\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} \| \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}\right| \mathrm{Fe}$
Electrode potential for $\mathrm{Zn} / \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}=0.763 \mathrm{~V}$
Electrode potential for $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}=-0.44 \mathrm{~V} \\ \text { EMF of cell } & =E_{\text {oxidation }(\mathrm{Fe})}^{\circ}+E_{\text {reduction }(\mathrm{Zn})}^{\circ} \\ & =-0.44+0.763 \\ & =0.323 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
From the following molar conductivities at infinite dilution, $\Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ}$ (in $\Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}$ ) for $\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{OH}$ is
$\qquad$
$\begin{aligned} & \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { for } \mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2=446.8 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { for } \mathrm{BaCl}_2=241.6 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\ & \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}}^{\circ} \text { for } \mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}=130 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\end{aligned}$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH} \mathrm{H}_4 \mathrm{OH}\right)=}^{\circ}= \Lambda_{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}}^{\circ}+ \\ &= \Lambda_{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ} \\ &=\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{NH}_4^{+}}^{\circ}+\Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}\right)+\frac{1}{2}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}}^{\circ}+2 \Lambda_{\mathrm{OH}^{-}}^{\circ}\right) \\ &-\frac{1}{2}\left(\Lambda_{\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}}^{\circ}+2 \Lambda_{\mathrm{Cl}^{-}}^{\circ}\right) \\ &= \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{NH}_4 \mathrm{Cl}\right)}^{\circ}+\frac{1}{2} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left[\mathrm{Ba}(\mathrm{OH})_2\right]}^{\circ}-\frac{1}{2} \Lambda_{\mathrm{m}\left(\mathrm{BaCl}_2\right)}^{\circ} \\ &= 130+\frac{1}{2}+446.8-\frac{1}{2} \times 241.6 \\ &= 232.6 \Omega^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2 \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Certain electric current for half an hour can collect 11.2 L of hydrogen at NTP. Same current when passed through an electrolytic solution for one hour, can deposite how much silver?
questions.solution:
$11.2 \mathrm{~L}_2$ gas at NTP $=0.5$ mole of $\mathrm{H}_2$ gas $=1 \mathrm{~g}$
of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{~g}$
$\frac{w_1}{w_2}=\frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{1}{w_2}=\frac{1}{108}$ or $w_2=108 \mathrm{~g}$ silver is deposited in half an hour.
Therefore, $108 \times 2=216 \mathrm{~g}$ silver will be deposited in one hour.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Value of $E_2^0$ is $\qquad$ in the given diagram (ignore -ve sign)
questions.solution:
$\Delta G_3=\Delta G_1+\Delta G_1$
$n_3 F E_3^{\circ}=n_1 F E_1^{\circ}+n_2 F E_2^{\circ}$
$3 \times 0.036=2 \times 0.439+1 \times E_2^{\circ}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E_2^{\circ}=0.108-0.878$
$=-0.77 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
The standard reduction potential $E^{\circ}$ for half reactions are,
$\begin{array}{ll}\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+2 e^{-} ; E^{\circ}=+0.76 \mathrm{~V} \\ \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+2 e^{-} \longrightarrow 10 & ; E^{\circ}=+0.41\end{array}$
The emf of the cell reaction;
$\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Zn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{Zn}^{2+}+\mathrm{Fe}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} E_{\text {cell }} & =E_{(\text {(cathode) })}-E_{(\text {(anode) }}^{\circ} \\ & =0.41-0.76=-0.35 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: How long (in hours) must a current of 5.0 A be maintained to electroplate 60 g of calcium from molten $\mathrm{CaCl}_2$ ?
questions.solution:
$w=60 \mathrm{~g}, i=5 \mathrm{~A}$
Equivalent of $\mathrm{Ca}=\frac{\text { atomic weight }}{\text { Valency }}=\frac{40}{2}=20$
According to first law of faraday electrolysis
$\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{Zit}=\frac{\text { Equivalent weight }}{96500} \times i \times T$
$\begin{aligned} 60 & =\frac{20}{96500} \times 5 \times T \\ T & =\frac{96500 \times 60}{20 \times 5} s \\ T & =\frac{96500 \times 60}{20 \times 5 \times 60 \times 60} \mathrm{~h} \\ & =16.08 \mathrm{~h} . \approx 16 \mathrm{~h} .\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Suppose that gold is being plated on to another metal in an electrolytic cell. The half-cell reaction producing the $\mathrm{Au}(\mathrm{s})$ is $\mathrm{AuCl}_4{ }^{-} \rightarrow \mathrm{Au}(s)+4 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}-3 e^{-}$
If a 0.30 A current runs for 15.00 min , what mass of $\mathrm{Au}(s)$ will be plated, assuming all the electrons are used in the reduction of $\mathrm{AuCl}_4^{-}$? The Faraday constant is $96485 \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{mol}$ and molar mass of Au is 197 .
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {Mass of } \mathrm{Au} \text { deposited }=\text { Number of Faraday passed } \times \text { Eq. mass } \\ & =\frac{0.30 \times 15 \times 60}{96485} \times \frac{197}{3}=0.184 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Consider the following redox reaction occurring in acidic medium
The unknown standard reduction potential is
questions.solution:
The given half reactions are
$\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}+4 e^{-}+4 \mathrm{H}^{+} \rightarrow E^{\circ}(\mathrm{inV}) \Delta G^{\circ}=-n F E^{\circ}$
$\mathrm{BrO}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \quad 1.50 \quad-6 \mathrm{~F}$
$\mathrm{BrO}^{-}+\mathrm{e}^{-}+2 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow 1.60 \quad-1.6 \mathrm{~F}$
$\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_2+\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
Adding $\mathrm{BrO}_3^{-}+5 \mathrm{e}^{-}+6 \mathrm{H}^{+} \longrightarrow \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{Br}_2+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}$
$\begin{gathered}\Delta G^{\circ}=-7.6 \mathrm{~F}=-5 E^{\circ} \mathrm{F} \\ E^{\circ}=\frac{7.6}{5}=1.52 \mathrm{~V}\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
What is the cell potential (standard emf, $E^0$ ) for the reaction below?
$\begin{aligned}
& {\left[E^0\left(\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q) \mathrm{Fe}\right)=0.44 \mathrm{~V}\right. \text { and }} \\
& \left.E^0\left(\mathrm{O}_2(g) / \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} / \mathrm{OH}^{-}\right)=+0.4 \mathrm{~V}\right] \\
& 2 \mathrm{Fe}(s)+\mathrm{O}_2(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \leftrightharpoons 2 \mathrm{Fe}^{2+}(a q)
\end{aligned}$
$+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}(a q)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} E_{\text {cell }}^0 & =E_{\text {cathode }}^0-E_{\text {anode }}^0 \\ & =0.40-(-0.44)=0.84 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: In which of the following case, increase in concentration of ion cause increase in $E_{\text {cell }}$ ?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { (a) } \quad \frac{1}{2} \mathrm{H}_2(g) \longrightarrow \mathrm{H}^{+}(a q)+e^{-} \\ & E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-0.0591 \log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]\end{aligned}$
$E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-0.0591 \log \left[\mathrm{H}^{+}\right]^2$ (c) $\begin{gathered} \mathrm{Ag}(s) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q) \\ E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-0.0591 \log \left[\mathrm{Ag}^{+}\right] \end{gathered}$ (d) $\begin{gathered} \mathrm{Ag}(s)+\mathrm{Cl}^{-}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{AgCl}(s)+e^{-} \\ E_{\text {cell }}=E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}-0.0591 \log \frac{1}{\left[\mathrm{Cl}^{-}\right]} \end{gathered}$ Thus, in case (d), the $E_{\text {cell }}$ increases with increase in the concentration of ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is not used as propellant?
questions.solution: Rocket propellants are a combination of an oxidiser and fuel. Phenyl hydrazine can't act as propellant.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Out of $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}, \mathrm{Fe}$ and Zn , the metal which can displace all others from their salt solution, is
questions.solution:
The metal placed above in electrochemical series can displace the metals placed below from their salt solution.
The order of given metals in the series is $\mathrm{Zn}, \mathrm{Fe}$, $\mathrm{Cu}, \mathrm{Ag}$. Thus, Zn metal can displace all other three metals from their salt solutions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Rusting of iron is catalysed by which of the following?
questions.solution: Rusting of iron is catalysed by $\mathrm{H}^{+}$ions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: For a cell reaction involving a two electron change, the standard emf of the cell is found to be 0.295 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The equilibrium constant of the reaction at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta G^{\circ}= & n F E^{\circ} \\ \Delta G^{\circ} & =-2.303 R T \log K_c \\ n F E^{\circ} & =2.303 R T \log K_c \\ \log K_c & =\frac{n F E^{\circ}}{2.303 R T}=\frac{2 \times 96500 \times 0.295}{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 298} \\ \log K_c^{\prime} & =9.97 \\ K_c & =1 \times 10^{10}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ionic conductance of $\mathrm{Ba}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$are respectively 127 and $76 \mathrm{ohm}^{-1}-\mathrm{cm}^2$ at infinite dilution. The equivalent conductance (inohm ${ }^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ ) of $\mathrm{BaCl}_2$ at infinite dilution will be
questions.solution:
Equivalence conductance = conducting power of all the ion produced by one gram equivalent of an electrolyte.
Since, Eq. wt. of $\mathrm{BaCl}_2=\frac{\mathrm{mol} \text {. wt. }}{2}$ then
$\begin{aligned} \lambda_{\mathrm{BaCl}_2}^{\infty} & =\frac{1}{2} \lambda^{\infty} \mathrm{Ba}^{2+}+\lambda^{\infty} \mathrm{Cl}^{-} \\ & =\frac{127}{2}+76 \\ & =139.5 \mathrm{ohm}^{-1} \mathrm{~cm}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Both $\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Pt}^{4+}$ have a coordination number of six. Which of the following pairs of complexes will show approximately the sáme 'electrical conductance for their - 0.001 M aqueous solutions?
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Co}^{3+}$ and $\mathrm{Pt}^{4+}$ have 6 coordination number.
$\mathrm{CoCl}_3 \cdot 6 \mathrm{NH}_3 \text { and } \mathrm{PtCl}_4 \cdot 5 \mathrm{NH}_3$
$\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3 \xrightarrow{\text { In solution }}\left[\mathrm{Co}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_6\right]^{3+}+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
$\left[\mathrm{PtCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right] \mathrm{Cl}_3 \xrightarrow{\text { In solution }}\left[\mathrm{PtCl}\left(\mathrm{NH}_3\right)_5\right]^{3+}+3 \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
Number of ionic species are same in the solution of both complexes, therefore their equimolar solutions will show same conductance.

questions.q: The standard emf of a cell involving one electron change is found to be 0.591 V at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The equilibrium constant of the reaction is $\left(F=96,500 \mathrm{C} \mathrm{mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution:
$E^{\circ}{ }_{\text {cell }}=\frac{2.303 R T}{n F} \log K_{\mathrm{eq}}$
$E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_{\mathrm{eq}} \quad$ (At 298 K$)$
$0.591=\frac{0.0591}{1} \log K_{\mathrm{eq}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \log K_{\mathrm{eq}}=10 \\ & \therefore  K_{\mathrm{eq}}=1 \times 10^{10}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $E^{\circ}$ values of $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+} / \mathrm{Mg}$ is -2.37 V , of $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Zn}$ is -0.76 V and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}$ is -0.44 V . Which of the statements is correct?
questions.solution:
Higher the negative value of $E^{\circ}$, more is the reducing power. The order of $E^{\circ}$ values (negative value) is
$-\frac{2.37}{(\mathrm{Mg})}\gt-\frac{0.76}{(\mathrm{Zn})}\gt-\frac{0.44}{(\mathrm{Fe})}$
$\therefore \mathrm{Mg}$ can reduce both $\mathrm{Zn}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} . \mathrm{Zn}$ can reduce $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}$, but not $\mathrm{Mg}^{2+}$. Fe cannot reduce Mg and Zn but can oxidise them.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
For the cell reaction,
$\mathrm{Cu}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{2Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq}) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})$ $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$ is 0.46 V . The equilibrium constant of the reaction is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{Cu}(s)+2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(a q) \longrightarrow \mathrm{Cu}^{2+}(a q)+2 \mathrm{Ag}(s)$
$n=2, E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}=0.46 \mathrm{~V}$
Now using the equation for $E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ}$
$\begin{aligned}
E_{\text {coll }}^{\circ} & =\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c \\
0.46 & =\frac{0.0591}{n} \log K_c \\
K_c & =\text { Antilog } 15.5668 \\
K_c & =3.92 \times 10^{15}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 e^{-} \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s}) ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=0.80 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow \mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq})+2 e^{-} ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=0.40 \mathrm{~V} \\ & 2 \mathrm{Ag}^{+}(\mathrm{aq})+\mathrm{Cd}(\mathrm{s}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{Ag}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{Cd}^{2+}(\mathrm{aq}) ; \mathrm{E}^{\circ}=1.20 \mathrm{~V} \\ & \Delta \mathrm{G}=-\mathrm{nFE}^{\circ}=-2 \times 96500 \times 1.20=-231.6 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}\)

Specific conductivity
$\begin{aligned}
(K) & =\frac{1}{R} \times \text { Cell constant } \\
\text { Cell constant } & =K \times R \\
& =0.0129 \times 100=1.29
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The standard reduction potential for $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+} / \mathrm{Fe}$ and $\mathrm{Sn}^{2+} / \mathrm{Sn}$ electrodes are -0.44 and -0.14 volt respectively. For the cell reaction $\mathrm{Fe}^{2+}+\mathrm{Sn} \longrightarrow \mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}+\mathrm{Sn}^{2+}$, the standard emf is
questions.solution:
For the cell reaction, Fe acts as cathode and Sn as anode
Hence,
$\begin{aligned} E_{\text {cell }}^{\circ} & =E_{\text {cathode }}^{\circ}-E_{\text {anode }}^{\circ} \\ & =-0.44-(-0.14)=-0.30 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
The negative emf suggests that the reaction goes spontaneously in reversed direction.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following is a measurement of water pollution?
questions.solution:
(a) PSC (polar stratospheric clouds) are special type of clouds formed over Antarctica in winter that play important role in ozone layer depletion.
(b) PCB (poly chlorinated biphenyls) are toxic chemicals with high stability and resistance to oxidation. These are used for fluids in transformers and capacitors.
(c) BOD (biochemical oxygen demand) is a measure of dissolved oxygen that would be needed by the microorganisms to oxidise organic and inorganic compounds present in polluted water.
(d) COD (chemical oxygen demand) is the amount of oxygen, in ppm, that would be required to oxidise the contaminants.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The process of 'eutrophication' is due to
options: increase in concentration of radioactive substances in water
the reduction in concentration of the dissolved oxygen in water due to phosphate pollution in water
increase in concentration of fluoride ion in water
increase in concentration of insecticide in water
questions.solution: The enrichment of water by plant nutrients is known as eutrophication. It is due to the reduction in concentration of dissolved oxygen in water due to phosphate pollution.

questions.solution: Kerosene is a thin, clear liquid formed from hydro carbons obtained from the fractional distillation of petroleum between $150-275^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ resulting in a mixture with a density of $0.78-0.81 \mathrm{~g} / \mathrm{cm}^3$ composed of carbon chains that typically contain between 6 and 16 carbon atoms per molecule.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q:
Which is the decreasing order of stability?
(i) $\mathrm{CH}_3-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{CH}_3$
(ii) $\mathrm{CH}_3-\stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H}-\mathrm{O}-\mathrm{CH}_3$
(iii) $\mathrm{CH}_3 \stackrel{+}{\mathrm{C}} \mathrm{H} \cdot \mathrm{CO} \cdot \mathrm{CH}_3$
questions.solution: Presence of -/ group in a carbocation decreases delocalisation of the positive charge and hence decreases stability of the carbocation and vice versa. Thus the carbocation (iii) having $-\mathrm{COCH}_3$ grouping is the least stable, followed by (ii) having $-\mathrm{OCH}_3$ grouping, followed by (i) having only alkyl group.

questions.solution: Inductive effect is a permanent effect operates on $\sigma$-electrons, while resonance involves delocalisation of $\pi$-electrons.

questions.q:
For the following,
(i) $\mathrm{I}^{-}$
(ii) $\mathrm{Cl}^{-}$
(iii) $\mathrm{Br}^{-}$
the increasing order of nucleophilicity would be
questions.solution:
Halogens are the most reactive elements. High reactivity is due to their low dissociation energy. Further, among halogen, reactivity decreases as we move down the group. Hence, the correct order of nucleophilicity would be
$\mathrm{I}^{-} \lt \mathrm{Br}^{-} \lt \mathrm{Cl}^{-}$

questions.solution:
Alkanes have a general formula $\mathrm{C}_n \mathrm{H}_{2 n+2}$ So, when $\begin{aligned} & n=1, \mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{H}_{2 \times 1+2}=\mathrm{CH}_4 \\ & n=2, \mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_{2 \times 2}+2=\mathrm{C}_2 \mathrm{H}_6 \\ & n=3, \mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_{2 \times 3+2}=\mathrm{C}_3 \mathrm{H}_8\end{aligned}$
So, the all have a difference of $\mathrm{CH}_2$.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following would react most readily with nucleophiles?
questions.solution: Nucleophile always attacks on electron deficient site. Presence of electron withdrawing group such as $\mathrm{NO}_2, \mathrm{CHO}$ etc., decreases the electron density on benzene nucleous, hence such group activates the ring towards nucleophilic attack. While presence of electron releasing groups such as $R$ or $O R$ increase the electron density, thus deactivate the nucleus toward nucleophilie attack. $\mathrm{NO}_2$ group activate the ring more than Cl toward nucleophilic attack hence react readily with nucleophile.

questions.solution: Substances which resist high temperatures and do not become soft are called refractories material. The neutral refractories are graphite, chromite and carboren, dum ( SiC ). Acidic refractories are $\mathrm{SiO}_2$, basic refractories are CaO and MgO.

questions.solution: When more electropositive metal displace less electropositive metals from their salt solution, this is reduction by precipitation and the process is called hydrometallurgy.

the minerals are obtained from mines

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: In blast furnace, the highest temperature is in
questions.solution:
In blast furnace the highest temperature is in combustion zone. In the first stage, coke burns to produce $\mathrm{CO}_2$ which rises temperature.
$\mathrm{C}+\mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_2+$ Heat
This reaction is exothermic and hence temperature is 2170 K . It is known as combustion zone.

questions.q: Which of the following statements is correct regarding the slag obtained during the extraction of iron?
options: The slag is lighter and has lower melting point than the metal.
The slag is lighter and has higher melting point than the metal.
The slag is heavier and has lower melting point than metal.
The slag is heavier and has higher melting point than the metal.
questions.solution: Slag formed during the extraction of iron is always lighter than the molten metal. Its melting point should also be less than the molten metal.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q:
Mond's process is used for refining _____ Whereas van Arkel method is used for refining
_____ .
questions.solution: Mond's process is used for refining nickel. Whereas, van Arkel method is used for refining zirconium and titanium.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: The final step for the extraction of copper form copper pyrites in Bessemer convertor involves the reaction.
questions.solution:
Ore- copper pyrites , $\mathrm{CuFeS}_2$
Concentrated pyrites ore (froth- floatation process) is roasted below $800^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. FeS is converted to FeO while copper remains as sulphide.
Roasting $2 \mathrm{FeS}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \xrightarrow{\Delta} 2 \mathrm{FeO}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2^{-} \uparrow$
Smelting $\mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{SiO}_2 \longrightarrow \underset{\text { slag }}{\mathrm{FeSIO}_3}$
$\left(\mathrm{SiO}_2\right.$ either is present ore or is added)
Bessemerisation
$2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S}+3 \mathrm{O}_2 \longrightarrow 2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+2 \mathrm{SO}_2$
$2 \mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}+\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{~S} \longrightarrow 6 \mathrm{Cu}+\mathrm{SO}_2$
(final step)

questions.solution: Cyanide process is used in the extraction of silver and gold.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: Calcination is used in metallurgy for removal of
questions.solution:
Calcination is used in metallurgy proceeds only with the expulsion of some small molecules like water, $\mathrm{CO}_2, \mathrm{SO}_2$ etc. without any chemical changes. e.g.
$\begin{gathered}2 \mathrm{Al}(\mathrm{OH})_3 \xrightarrow{1500{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}} \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+3 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \uparrow \\ \mathrm{FeCO}_3 \xrightarrow{\Delta} \mathrm{FeO}+\mathrm{CO}_2 \uparrow\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.chapter: General Principles and Processes of Isolation of Metals
questions.q: Electrolytic reduction of alumina to aluminium by Hall-Heroult process is carried out
options: in the presence of cryolite which forms a melt with lower melting temperature
in the presence of cryolite which forms a melt with higher melting temperature
questions.solution:
In the metallurgy of alumina, purified $\mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ is mixed with $\mathrm{Na}_3 \mathrm{AlF}_6$ or $\mathrm{CaF}_2$ which lowers the melting point of the mix and brings conductivity.
$2 \mathrm{Al}_2 \mathrm{O}_3+3 \mathrm{C} \longrightarrow 4 \mathrm{Al}+3 \mathrm{CO}_2$

options: to form complexes which are water soluble
to form salts which are water soluble
questions.solution: $4 \mathrm{Au}(\mathrm{s})+8 \mathrm{CN}^{-}+\mathrm{O}_2(g)+2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O} \longrightarrow \underset{\text {soluble complex}}{4\left[\mathrm{Au}(\mathrm{CN})_2\right]}+4 \mathrm{OH}^{-}$
