questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which is true for a cyclic process?
questions.solution: For a cyclic process, $\Delta E=0$. In cyclic process, system returns to its original state after a number of reactions.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.solution:
For the reaction $\begin{array}{r} \mathrm{CH}_3 \mathrm{COOH}(l)+2 \mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightleftharpoons 2 \mathrm{CO}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \\ +2 \mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(l) \\ \Delta H=-874 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{array}$
We know that $\Delta H=\Delta E+P \Delta V$ From ideal gas equation $P \Delta V=\Delta n R T$ Then $\Delta H=\Delta E+\Delta n R T$ Number of moles of reactant $\begin{aligned} & =\text { Number of moles of products } \\ & \therefore \quad \Delta n=0 \\ & \text { Then } \\ & \Delta H=\Delta E \\ & \Delta E=-874 \mathrm{~kJ} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: $(\Delta H-\Delta U)$ for the formation of carbon monoxide (CO) from its elements at 298 K is $\left(R=8.314 \mathrm{JK}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathrm{C}_{(s)}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{O}_{2(g)} & \longrightarrow \mathrm{CO}_{(s)}(\Delta H-\Delta U)=-\Delta n R T \\ = & -\frac{1}{2} \times 8.314 \times 298=-1238.78\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: When ice melts into water, the entropy
questions.solution:
Entropy is the measure of randomness. In liquids randomness is more than solids.
$\because$ When ice melts, randomness increases, (solid $\longrightarrow$ liquid)
$\therefore$ Entropy increases.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: The ionisation potential order for which set is correct?
questions.solution:
The ionisation potential decreases down the group (due to increase in size of atom) and increases in a period from left to right.
$\therefore$ Out of the given choices $\mathrm{Li}\gt\mathrm{K}\gt\mathrm{Cs}$ is correct.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: An ideal gas at constant temperature and pressure expands, then its
options: entropy first increases and then decreases
questions.solution: At constant temperature and pressure, internal energy of ideal gas remains unaffected.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: Which of the following conditions regarding the chemical process ensures its spontaneity at all temperatures?
questions.solution: $\Delta H \lt 0$ and $\Delta S\gt0$ are always favourable conditions for spontaneity at all temperatures.

questions.subject: Chemistry
questions.q: One mole of ice is converted into water at 273 K . The entropies of $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(s)$ and $\mathrm{H}_2 \mathrm{O}(t)$ are 38.20 and $\quad 60.01 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$ respectively. The enthalpy change for the conversion is
questions.solution:
$\because \quad \Delta G=\Delta H-T \Delta S$
At equilibrium, $\Delta G=0$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore \quad \Delta H & =T \Delta S \\
\Delta H & =273 \times(60.01-38.20) \\
& =5954.13 \mathrm{~J} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}=5.954 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1} \\
& \approx 6 \mathrm{~kJ} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A function $f(x)=\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+2 x-3}$ is
options: function is increasing in its domain
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\frac{x^2-3 x+2}{x^2+2 x-3}$
On differentiating with respect to $x$, we get $f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{\left(x^2+2 x-3\right)(2 x-3)-\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)}{(2 x+2)}$ $\left(2 x^3+4 x^2-6 x-3 x^2-6 x+9\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{-\left(2 x^3-6 x^2+4 x+2 x^2-6 x+4\right)}{\left(x^2+2 x-3\right)^2} \\ & =\frac{5 x^2-10 x+5}{\left(x^2+2 x-3\right)^2}\gt0\end{aligned}$ $=\frac{5(x-1)^2}{(x+3)^2(x-1)^2}\gt0=\frac{5}{(x+3)^2}\gt0$ $\therefore f(x)$ is increasing in its domain.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum value of $x^2+\frac{1}{1+x^2}$ is, at
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=x^2+\frac{1}{1+x^2}$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $f^{\prime}(x)=2 x-\frac{1}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} 2 x$ For a minimum, put $f^{\prime}(x)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=0$
So, the function has minimum value at $x=0$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $\sin x(1+\cos x), 0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$ has maximum value, when $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { ( Let } z=\sin x(1+\cos x) \\ & \text { or } z=\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x\end{aligned}$ On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{d z}{d x}=\cos x+\cos 2 x$ For maximum or minimum, put $\frac{d z}{d x}=0$ $\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & \cos x+\cos 2 x & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \cos ^2 x-1+\cos x & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & (\cos x+1)(2 \cos x-1) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & \cos x & =-1, \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & & x & =\frac{\pi}{3}, \pi\end{array}$ But $0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, therefore we take only $x=\frac{\pi}{3}$. $\therefore \quad \frac{d^2 z}{d x^2}=-\sin x-2 \sin 2 x=-$ ve, for $x=\pi / 3$
$\therefore$ It is maximum at $x=\pi / 3$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} \sin \left(\frac{\pi x}{2}\right), & \text { if } x \lt 1 \\ 3-2 x, & \text { if } x \geq 1\end{aligned}\right.$, then $f(x)$ has
options: Both local maximum and local minimum at $x=1$
questions.solution:
Given function is $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}\sin \left(\frac{\pi x}{2}\right), & \text { if } x \lt 1 \\ 3-2 x, & \text { if } x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$ $\because$ LHL and RHL of $f(x)$ is 1 , when $x \rightarrow 1$ and $f(1)=3-2=1$ $\therefore f(x)$ is continuous at $x=1$. Now, graph of function $f(x)$ is
From the above graph of function, we see that $f(x)$ has local maxima at $x=1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $2 a+3 b+6 c=0$, then the equation $a x^2+b x+c=0$ has atleast one real root in
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=\frac{1}{3} a x^3+\frac{1}{2} b x^2+c x$
Then, $f(x)$ is a polynomial.
So, it is continuous in $R$.
Now, $\quad f(0)=0$
and $\quad f(1)=\frac{a}{3}+\frac{b}{2}+c=\frac{2 a+3 b+6 c}{6}$
$\Rightarrow \quad f(1)=0 \quad[\because 2 a+3 b+6 c=0$, given $]$
$\because f(x)$ is a polynomial, so it is differentiable in $R$; so in $(0,1)$.
Hence, by Rolle's theorem, there exists atleast one point $x \in(0,1)$, there exists such that
$f^{\prime}(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a x^2+b x+c=0$
Hence, required interval is $(0,1)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The curve, for which the area of the triangle formed by $X$-axis, the tangent line at any poin $P$ and line $O P$ is equal to $a^2$, is given by
questions.solution:
Tangent drawn at any point $(x, y)$ is $Y-y=\frac{d y}{d x}(X-x)$ When $Y=0, X=x-y \frac{d x}{d y}$
$\because \quad$ Area of $\triangle O P Q=a^2$ (given) $\therefore \quad\left|\frac{1}{2} x \cdot y\right|=a^2$ $\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \left|\left(x-y \frac{d x}{d y}\right) y\right|=2 a^2 \\ \Rightarrow & x y-y^2 \frac{d x}{d y}= \pm 2 a^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d x}{d y}-\frac{x}{y}= \pm \frac{2 a^2}{y^2}\end{array}$ Here, $P=-\frac{1}{y}$ and $Q= \pm \frac{2 a^2}{y^2}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mathrm{IF}=e^{\int P d y} & =e^{\int-\frac{1}{y} d y} \\ & =e^{-\log y}=e^{\log \frac{1}{y}}=\frac{1}{y}\end{aligned}$ Hence, required solution is $\begin{aligned} & & x \times \frac{1}{y} & =\int \pm \frac{2 a^2}{y^2} \times \frac{1}{y} d y \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{x}{y} & = \pm \frac{2 a^2 y^{-2}}{-2}+C \\ \Rightarrow & & x & =C y \pm \frac{a^2}{y}\end{aligned}$ which is the required curve.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: By Newton-Raphson method, the positive root of the equation $x^4-x-10=0$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=x^4-x-10$
We assume $x_0=2$ is the approximate root of $f(x)$.
Then, $h=-\frac{f\left(x_0\right)}{f\left(x_0\right)}=-\frac{f(2)}{f(2)}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & h=-\left[\frac{(2)^4-2-10}{4(2)^3-1}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & h=-\left[\frac{16-12}{31}\right]=\frac{-4}{31} \\ \Rightarrow & h=-0.129\end{array}$
$\therefore$ Positive square root of $f(x)$ by Newton-Raphson method,
$\begin{aligned} x_1=x_0+h & =2+(-0.129) \\ & =2-0.129=1.871\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the rate of change in the circumference of a circle of $0.3 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, then the rate of change in the area of the circle when the radius is 5 cm , is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\int_0^1 e^{|t-x|} d t \quad(0 \leq x \leq 1)$, then the maximum value of $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\int_0^x e^{|t-x|} d t+\int_x e^{|t t-x|} d t$
$=e^x+e^{1-x}-2$
$\therefore \quad f^{\prime}(x)=e^x-e^{1-x}$
Clearly, $x=\frac{1}{2}$ is the point of minima of $f(x)$.
Also, $\quad f^{\prime}(0)=f(1)=e-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the tangent to the curve $\left(1+x^2\right) y=2-x$, where it crosses the $x$-axis, is
questions.solution:
The given equation of curve is $\left(1+x^2\right) y=2-x$, it meets $x$-axis at $(2,0)$ or it can be rewritten as $y=\frac{2-x}{1+x^2}$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{d y}{d x} & =\frac{\left(1+x^2\right)(-1)-(2-x)(2 x)}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} \\
& =\frac{-1-x^2-4 x+2 x^2}{\left(1+x^2\right)^2} \\
\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(2,0)} & =\frac{-1-4-8+8}{25}=-\frac{1}{5}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Required equation of tangent is
$(y-0)=-\frac{1}{5}(x-2) \Rightarrow x+5 y=2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $h(x)=\min \left(x, x^2\right)$, for every real numbers $x$. Then,
options: $h$ is not differentiable at two values of $x$
questions.solution:
$h(x)=\min \left(x, x^2\right)=\left\{\begin{array}{cl}x, & x \lt 0 \\ x^2, & 0 \leq x \lt 1 \\ x, & x \geq 1\end{array}\right.$
It is evident from the above graph that the given function is continuous for all $x$. Since, there are sharp edges at $x=0$ and $x=1$, the function is not differentiable at these points. Also, at $x \geq 1$, the function represents a straight line having slope 1 , therefore $h^{\prime}(x)=1, \forall x \geq 1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The points on the curve $x y^2=1$ which are nearest to the origin, are
questions.solution:
Let $P(x, y)$ be such a point, then
$O P^2=x^2+y^2$
Let
$s=O P^2=x^2+\frac{1}{x}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d s}{d x}=2 x-\frac{1}{x^2}$
For maximum or minimum, put $\frac{d s}{d x}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x-\frac{1}{x^2}=0 \Rightarrow x^3=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \quad x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{3}}$
Also, $\quad \frac{d^2 s}{d x^2}=2+\frac{2}{x^3}$
Now, $\left(\frac{d^2 s}{d x^2}\right)_{x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}}=2+\frac{2}{1 / 2}\gt0$
$\therefore s$ is minimum at $x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}$.
Thus, for nearest point $x=\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{1 / 3}$,
$y= \pm\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{-1 / 6}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\theta$ is the semi-vertical angle of a cone of maximum volume and given slant height, then $\tan \theta$ is given by
questions.solution:
Volume of cone, $V=\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 h$ $\Rightarrow \quad V=\frac{\pi}{3} r^2 \sqrt{1^2-r^2}$
On differentiating w.r.t. $I$, we get $\frac{d V}{d r}=\frac{\pi}{3}\left[2 r \sqrt{1^2-r^2}+\frac{r^2}{2 \sqrt{1^2-r^2}}(-2 r)\right]$ Put $\quad \frac{d V}{d r}=0$, we get$r= \pm 1 \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}$ At$r=1 \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}, \frac{d^2 V}{d r^2} \lt 0,$ (maximum) $\therefore \quad h=\sqrt{I^2-\frac{2}{3} l^2}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}$ In $\triangle A B C, \tan \theta=\frac{r}{h}=\frac{1 \sqrt{\frac{2}{3}}}{1 / \sqrt{3}}=\sqrt{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of values of $x$, where $f(x)=\cos x+\cos \sqrt{2} x$ attains its maximum is
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\cos x+\cos \sqrt{2} x$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(x)=-\sin x-\sqrt{2} \sin \sqrt{2} x \\ \Rightarrow & f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\cos x-2 \cos \sqrt{2} x\end{array}$
For extremum value, put $f^{\prime}(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad-\sin x-\sqrt{2} \sin \sqrt{2} x=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=0$
At $x=0, f^{\prime \prime}(x) \lt 0$ (maximum)
Hence, $f(x)$ is maximum only once.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The approximate value of $(1.0002)^{3000}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $y=x^{3000}, x=1$ and $x+\Delta x=1.0002$ Then, $\Delta x=1.0002-1=0.0002$
Also, $\quad y=1$, when $x=1$ Now, $\quad y=x^{3000}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=3000 x^{2999}$ $\Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_{x=1}=3000$ $\because \quad \Delta y=\frac{d y}{d x} \Delta x$ $\therefore \quad \Delta y=3000 \times 0.0002=\frac{6}{10}=0.6$ Hence, $(1.0002)^{3000}=y+\Delta y=1+0.6=1.6$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $c$ prescribed by Lagrange's mean value theorem, when $f(x)=\sqrt{x^2-4}, a=2$ and $b=3$, is
questions.solution:
Clearly, $f(x)=\sqrt{x^2-4}$ is continuous on $[2,3]$ and differentiable on $(2,3)$.
So, by Lagrange's mean value theorem, there exists $c \in(2,3)$ such that
$f^{\prime}(c)=\frac{f(3)-f(2)}{3-2}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{c}{\sqrt{c^2-4}}=\sqrt{5}-0 \\ & {\left[\because f(x)=\sqrt{x^2-4} \Rightarrow f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{x}{\sqrt{x^2-4}}\right] }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & & c^2 & =5\left(c^2-4\right) \\ \Rightarrow & 4 c^2 & =20 \\ \Rightarrow & & c & =\sqrt{5}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=x e^{x(1-x)}$, then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=x e^{x(1-x)}$ On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get $\begin{aligned} & f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x(1-x)}+x(1-2 x) e^{x(1-x)} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=\left(1+x-2 x^2\right) e^{x(1-x)} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=-(x-1)(2 x+1) e^{x(1-x)} \end{aligned}$ Since, $e^{x(1-x)}\gt0$ for all $x$. Therefore, signs of $f^{\prime}(x)$ for different values of $x$ are as shown below:
Clearly, $f(x)$ is increasing on $\left[-\frac{1}{2}, 1\right]$ and decreasing on $\left(-\infty,-\frac{1}{2}\right] \cup[1, \infty)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the tangent to the curve $y=(2 x-1) e^{2(1-x)}$ at the point of its maximum, is
questions.solution:
We have,
$y=(2 x-1) e^{2(1-x)}$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\begin{aligned} \frac{d y}{d x} & =2 e^{2(1-x)}-2(2 x-1) e^{2(1-x)} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x} & =2 e^{2(1-x)}(2-2 x)=4 e^{2(1-x)}(1-x)\end{aligned}$
At points of maximum, we must have
$\frac{d y}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=1$
Now,
$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=-8 e^{2(1-x)}(1-x)-4 e^{2(1-x)}$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left[\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right]_{x=1}=-4 \lt 0$
So, $y$ is maximum at $x=1$. Clearly, $y=1$ for $x=1$.
Thus, the point of maximum is $(1,1)$.
The equation is the tangent at $(1,1)$ is
$y-1=0(x-1)$
$\Rightarrow \quad y=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two tangents to the curve $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=1, \quad a\gt0$ at the points, where it crosses $X$-axis, are
questions.solution:
At the point where the given curve crosses $X$-axis, we have
$y=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad a x^2=1$
[putting $y=0$ in $a x^2+2 h y x+b y^2=1$ ]
$\Rightarrow \quad x= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}$
Thus, the given curve cuts $X$-axis at $P\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}, 0\right)$ and
$Q\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{a}}, 0\right)$.
Now, $a x^2+2 h x y+b y^2=1$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$2 a x+2 h\left(x \frac{d y}{d x}+y\right)+2 b y \frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{a x+h y}{h x+b y} \\ \Rightarrow & {\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_P=-\frac{a}{h} \text { and }\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_Q=-\frac{a}{h}} \\ \Rightarrow & {\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_p=\left[\frac{d y}{d x}\right]_Q}\end{array}$
Hence, the tangents are parallel.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f^{\prime}(x)\gt0 \forall x \in R, f^{\prime}(3)=0$ and $g(x)=f\left(\tan ^2 x-2 \tan x+4\right), 0 \lt x \lt \frac{\pi}{2}$, then $g(x)$ is increasing in
questions.solution:
Given, $g(x)=f\left(\tan ^2 x-2 \tan x+4\right)$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\begin{aligned} g^{\prime}(x)=f^{\prime}\left(\tan ^2 x-2 \tan x\right. & +4) \\ & \times\left(2 \tan x \sec ^2 x-2 \sec ^2 x\right)\end{aligned}$
$=f^{\prime}\left(\tan ^2 x-2 \tan x+4\right) 2 \sec ^2 x(\tan x-1)$
$\because \quad f^{\prime}(x)\gt0 \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Also, $2 \sec ^2 x\gt0 \forall x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\left[\because x \in\left(0, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)\right.$, given $]$
But $\tan x-1\gt0 \forall x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
$\therefore \quad g^{\prime}(x)\gt0 \forall x \in\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$
Hence, $g(x)$ is increasing in $\left(\frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The values of $a$, if $f(x)=2 e^x-a e^{-x}+(2 a+1) x-3$ increases $x$, are in
questions.solution:
Given function is
$f(x)=2 e^x-a e^{-x}+(2 a+1) x-3$
For increasing function, $\quad f(x)\gt0, \forall x$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(x)=2 e^x+a e^{-x}+(2 a+1)\gt0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(2 e^x+1\right)+a\left(e^{-x}+2\right)\gt0 \\ \Rightarrow & a\left(e^{-x}+2\right)\gt-\left(1+2 e^x\right) \\ \Rightarrow & a\gt\frac{-\left(1+2 e^x\right)}{\left(e^{-x}+2\right)}\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{lrl}\Rightarrow & a & \gt\frac{-\left(1+2 e^x\right)}{\left(1+2 e^x\right)} \cdot e^x \\ \Rightarrow & a & \gt-e^x \\ \because & e^x \in(0, \infty) & \Rightarrow-e^x \in(-\infty, 0)\end{array}$
and $a\gt-e^x$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & a \in[0, \infty)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $\sin x(1+\cos x), 0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$ has maximum value, when $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $z=\sin x(1+\cos x)$ or $z=\sin x+\frac{1}{2} \sin 2 x$ On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{d z}{d x}=\cos x+\cos 2 x$
For maximum or minimum, put $\frac{d z}{d x}=0$ $\begin{array}{rr} \Rightarrow & \cos x+\cos 2 x=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 \cos ^2 x-1+\cos x=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (\cos x+1)(2 \cos x-1)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \cos x=-1, \frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & x \end{array}$
But $0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$, therefore we take only $x=\frac{\pi}{3}$. $\therefore \frac{d^2 z}{d x^2}=-\sin x-2 \sin 2 x=-\mathrm{ve}, \text { for } x=\pi / 3$ $\therefore$ It is maximum at $x=\pi / 3$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A cone whose height is always equal to its diameter, is increasing in volume at the rate of $40 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{s}$. At what rate is the radius increasing when its circular base area is $1 \mathrm{~m}^2 ?$
questions.solution:
Let $h$ be the height, $r$ be the radius of the base and $V$ be the volume of the cone at time $t$. Then,
$V=\frac{1}{3} \pi r^2 h$
$\Rightarrow \quad V=\frac{2}{3} \pi r^3 \quad[\because h=2 \pi$, given $]$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $t$, we get
$\frac{d V}{d t}=2 \pi r^2 \frac{d r}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 40=2(10)^4 \frac{d r}{d t}$
$\left[\because \pi r^2=1 \mathrm{~m}^2=10^4 \mathrm{~cm}^2\right.$ and $\frac{d V}{d t}=40 \mathrm{~cm}^3 / \mathrm{s}$, given $]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d r}{d t}=\frac{2}{1000} \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}=0.002 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of ' $a$ ' for which the function $f(x)=a \sin x+\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \sin 3 x$ has an extremum at $x=\frac{\pi}{3}$, is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=a \sin x+\frac{1}{3} \sin 3 x$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\begin{gathered} f^{\prime}(x)=a \cos x+\frac{3}{3} \cos 3 x=0, \text { at } x=\frac{\pi}{3}, \\ a \cos \frac{\pi}{3}+\cos \frac{\pi}{3} \cdot 3=0 \Rightarrow a \cdot \frac{1}{2}+(-1)=0 \\ \therefore \quad a=2 \end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The radius of a cylinder is increasing at the rate of $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and its height is decreasing at the rate of $3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. When the radius is 3 m and height is 5 m , then the volume of the cylinder would change at the rate of
questions.solution:
Given,
$\frac{d r}{d t}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$   ....(i)
$\frac{d h}{d t}=-3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\begin{aligned} \because \text { Volume of cylinder, } V & =\pi r^2 h \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{d V}{d t} \\ & =\pi\left[2 r h \frac{d r}{d t}+r^2 \frac{d h}{d t}\right]\end{aligned}$
At $r=3 \mathrm{~m}$ and $h=5 \mathrm{~m}$,
$\frac{d V}{d t}=\pi[2 \times 3 \times 5 \times 2-9 \times 3] \quad$ [using Eq. (i) $]$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d t}=\pi[60-27]=33 \pi$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d V}{d t}=33 \pi \mathrm{~m}^3 / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $x^x$ is increasing, when
questions.solution:
Let $y=x^x$ $\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=x^x(1+\log x)$
For increaṣing function, $\frac{d y}{d x}\gt0$ $\begin{array}{cc} \Rightarrow & x^x(1+\log x)\gt0 \\ \Rightarrow & 1+\log x\gt0 \\ \Rightarrow & \log _e x\gt\log _e \frac{1}{e} \\ \Rightarrow & x\gt\frac{1}{e} \end{array}$ $\therefore$ The function is increasing when $x\gt\frac{1}{e}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The triangle formed by the tangent to the curve $f(x)=x^2+b x-b$ at the point $(1,1)$ and the coordinate axes lies in the first quadrant. If its area is 2 , then the value of $b$ is
questions.solution:
Given curve is $y=f(x)=x^2+b x-b$ On differentiating w.r.t.x, we get $\frac{d y}{d x}=2 x+b$
The equation of the tagent at $(1,1)$ is $\begin{array}{rlrl} & & y-1=\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(1,1)}(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow & y-1=(b+2)(x-1) \\ \Rightarrow & (2+b) x-y=1+b \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x}{(1+b) /(2+b)}-\frac{y}{(1+b)}=1 \end{array}$
So, $O A=\frac{1+b}{2+b}$ and $O B=-(1+b)$
Now, area of $\triangle A O B$ $\begin{array}{rrrl} & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{(1+b)}{(2+b)}[-(1+b)]=2 (given)\\ \Rightarrow & 4(2+b)+(1+b)^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 8+4 b+1+b^2+2 b=0 \\ \Rightarrow & b^2+6 b+9=0 \\ \Rightarrow &(b+3)^2=0 \Rightarrow b=-3 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The largest term in the sequence $a_n=\frac{n^2}{n^3+200}$ is given by
questions.solution:
Consider the function $f(x)=\frac{x^2}{x^3+200}$ $\begin{aligned} & & f^{\prime}(x) & =x \frac{\left(400-x^3\right)}{\left(x^3+200\right)^2}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & & x^3 & =400 \\ \Rightarrow & & x & =(400)^{1 / 3} \end{aligned}$
When $x \lt 0, f^{\prime}(x)\gt0$ $x\gt0, f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0$ $\therefore f(x)$ has maximum at $x=(400)^{1 / 3}$ Since, $7 \lt (400)^{1 / 3} \lt 8$, either $a_7$ or $a_8$ is the greatest term of the sequence. $\because \quad a_7=\frac{49}{543}, a_8=\frac{8}{89}$ and $\quad \frac{49}{543}\gt\frac{8}{89}$ $a_7=\frac{49}{543}$ is the greatest.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: What are the values of $c$ for which Rolle's theorem for the function $f(x)=x^3-3 x^2+2 x$ in the interval $[0,2]$ is verified?
questions.solution:
Here, we observe that (a) $f(x)$ is a polynomial, so it is continuous in the interval $[0,2]$. (b) $f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+2$ exists for all $x \in(0,2)$.
So, $f(x)$ is differentiable for all $x \in(0,2)$ and (c) $f(0)=0, f(2)=2^3-3(2)^2+2(2)=0$ $\Rightarrow f(0)=f(2)$
Thus, all the three conditions of Rolle's theorem are satisfied.
So, there must exist $c \in[0,2]$ such that $f^{\prime}(c)=0$ $\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & f^{\prime}(c)=3 c^2-6 c+2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & c=1 \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \in[0,2] \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \left|x^3+x^2+3 x+\sin x\right| \cdot\left(3+\sin \frac{1}{x}\right), & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0 \end{array}\right.$
The number of points, where $f(x)$ attains its minimum value, is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned}\left|x^3+x^2+3 x+\sin x\right| \\ 0\end{aligned}\left(3+\sin \frac{1}{x}\right) \quad \begin{array}{l}x \neq 0 \\ x=0\end{array}\right.$
Let $g(x)=x^3+x^2+3 x+\sin x$ $\begin{aligned} g^{\prime}(x) & =3 x^2+2 x+3+\cos x \\ & =3\left(x^2+\frac{2 x}{3}+1\right)+\cos x \\ \Rightarrow g^{\prime}(x) & =3\left\{\left(x+\frac{1}{3}\right)^2+\frac{8}{9}\right\}+\cos x\gt0 \end{aligned}$ and $2 \lt 3+\sin \left(\frac{1}{x}\right) \lt 4$ Hence, minimum value of $f(x)$ is 0 at $x=0$ Hence, the number of points $=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum value of $\frac{x}{\log x}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\begin{aligned} f(x) & =\frac{x}{\log x} \\ f^{\prime}(x) & =\frac{\log x-1}{(\log x)^2} \end{aligned}$
Pur $f^{\prime}(x)=0$, for maxima or minima, we get $\log x-1=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=e$ Now, $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=\frac{(\log x)^2 \cdot \frac{1}{x}-(\log x-1) \cdot \frac{2 \log x}{x}}{(\log x)^4}$ $\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime \prime}(e)=\frac{\frac{1}{e}-0}{1}=\frac{1}{e}\gt0$ $\therefore$ Function is minimum at $x=e$. Hence, minimum value of $f(x)$ at $x=e$ is $f(e)=e .$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A man 1.6 m high walks at the rate of $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$ away from a lamp which is 4 m above ground. How fast is the man's shadow lengthening?
questions.solution:
Let $P Q=4 m$ be the height of pole and, $A B=1.6 \mathrm{~m} \quad$ be height of man. Let the end of shadow is $R$ and it is at a distance of $l$ from $A$ when the man is at a distance $x$ from $P Q$ at some instant. Since, $\triangle P Q R$ and $\triangle A B R$ are similar,
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { we have, } \frac{P Q}{A B}=\frac{P R}{A R} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{4}{1.6}=\frac{x+l}{l} \\ \Rightarrow & 2 x=3 l\end{array}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 d x}{d t}=3 \cdot \frac{d l}{d t} \quad\left\{\right.$ given $\left.\frac{d x}{d t}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}\right\}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d l}{d t}=\frac{2}{3} \times 30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value $P$ such that the length of subtangent and subnormal is equal for the curve $y=e^{P x}+P x$ at the point $(0,1)$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{d y}{d x}=P e^{p x}+P$
$\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{(0,1)}=2 P$
Subtangent $=\left|y \frac{d x}{d y}\right|$, subnormal $=\left|y \frac{d y}{d x}\right|$
Given; Subtangent = Subnormal
$\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}= \pm 1 \Rightarrow 2 p= \pm 1 \Rightarrow p= \pm \frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $y=a(1-\cos x)$ is maximum when $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $y=a(1-\cos x)$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\frac{d y}{d x}=a \sin x$
Again differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=a \cos x$
Put $\frac{d y}{d x}=0$ for maxima or minima
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow  a \sin x=0 \Rightarrow x=\pi \\
& \therefore \left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{x=\pi}=a \cos \pi=-a
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Function is maximum at $x=\pi$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $A B$ is a diameter of a circle and $C$ is any point on the circumference of the circle. then
options: The area of $\triangle A B C$ is maximum when it is isosceles
The area of $\triangle A B C$ is minimum when it is isosceles
The perimeter of $\triangle A B C$ is minimum when it is isosceles
questions.solution:
Area of $\triangle A B C, A=\frac{1}{2} \times \sqrt{d^2-x^2}$ for $\max / \min \frac{d A}{d x}=0$ $\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2} \sqrt{d^2-x^2}+\frac{1}{2} \times\left\{\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{d^2-x^2}}\right\}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d^2-x^2-x^2}{2 \sqrt{d^2-x^2}}=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$
Also for $\quad x \lt \frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d A}{d x}\gt0$ and for $\quad x\gt\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}, \frac{d A}{d x} \lt 0$. Hence; $x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$ is the point of maxima. $\therefore A$ is max at $x=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$, the area is maximum $y=\frac{d}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\therefore$ when $\Delta$ is isosceles,

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $y^{1 / m}+y^{-1 / m}=2 x$, then $\left(x^2-1\right) y_2+x y_1$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $y^{1 / m}+y^{-1 / m}=2 x$ 
or $y^{1 / m}+\frac{1}{y^{1 / m}}=2 x$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { or }\left(y^{1 / m}\right)^2-2 x y^{1 / m}+1=0 \\ & \therefore y^{1 / m}=x \pm \sqrt{\left(x^2-1\right)}\end{aligned}$
or $y=\left[x \pm \sqrt{x^2-1}\right]^m$
$\therefore \quad y_1=m\left[x \pm \sqrt{\left(x^2-1\right)}\right]^{m-1}\left[1 \pm \frac{2 x}{2 \sqrt{x^2-1}}\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad y_1=\frac{ \pm m\left[x \pm \sqrt{\left(x^2-1\right)}\right]^m}{\sqrt{\left(x^2-1\right)}}=\frac{ \pm m y}{\sqrt{\left(x^2-1\right)}}$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & \left(x^2-1\right) y_1^2=m^2 y^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(x^2-1\right) 2 y_1 y_2+2 x y_1^2=2 m^2 y y_1 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(x^2-1\right) y_2+x y_1=m^2 y\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $P$ be any point on the curve $x^{2 / 3}+y^{2 / 3}=a^{2 / 3}$. Then, the length of the segment of the tangent between the coordinate axes of length
questions.solution:
Let the coordinates of the point $P$ be $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$. This point lies on the curve $\begin{aligned} & x^{2 / 3}+y^{2 / 3} & =a^{2 / 3} \quad \text{....(i)} \\ \therefore & x_1^{2 / 3}+y_1^{2 / 3} & =a^{2 / 3} \quad \text{....(ii)} \end{aligned}$
On differentiating Eq. (i) w.r.t. $x$, we get $\begin{array}{rlrl} \frac{2}{3} x^{-1 / 3}+\frac{2}{3} y^{-1 / 3} \frac{d y}{d x} & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x} =-\frac{y^{1 / 3}}{x^{1 / 3}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{\left(x_1, y_1\right)} =-\left(\frac{y_1}{x_1}\right)^{1 / 3}\end{array}$ The equation of the tangent at $\left(x_1, y_1\right)$ to the given curve is $\begin{aligned} y-y_1 & =-\frac{y_1^{1 / 3}}{x_1^{1 / 3}}\left(x-x_1\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad x x_1^{-1 / 3}+y y_1^{-1 / 3} & =x_1^{2 / 3}+y_1^{2 / 3} \\ \Rightarrow \quad x x_1^{-1 / 3}+y y_1^{-1 / 3} & =a^{2 / 3} \quad \text { [using Eq. (ii)] } \end{aligned}$ This tangent meets the coordinate axes at $A\left(a^{2 / 3} x_1^{1 / 3}, 0\right)$ and $B\left(0, a^{2 / 3} y_1^{1 / 3}\right)$. $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad A B & =\sqrt{\left(0-a^{2 / 3} x_1^{1 / 3}\right)^2+\left(a^{2 / 3} y_1^{1 / 3}-0\right)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{a^{4 / 3}\left(x_1^{2 / 3}+y_1^{2 / 3}\right) \quad \quad \text { [from Eq. (ii)] }} \\ & =\sqrt{a^{4 / 3} \cdot a^{2 / 3}} \\ & =\sqrt{a^2}=a \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Local maximum value of the function $\frac{\log _e x}{x}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $f(x)=\frac{\log _e x}{x}$
On differentiating, w.r.t. $x$, we get
$f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{1}{x^2}-\frac{\log _e x}{x^2}$
For maximum or minimum value of $f(x)$,
$\begin{array}{rlr}
\text { Put } & f^{\prime}(x) & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{1-\log _e x}{x^2} & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & \log _e x & =1
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=e$, which lies in $(0, \infty)$.
For $x=e, f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-v e$
Hence, $y$ is maximum at $x=e$ and its maximum
$\text { value }=\frac{\log _e e}{e}=\frac{1}{e}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The two curves $x^3-3 x y^2+2=0$ and $3 x^2 y-y^3=2$
questions.solution:
Given curves are $x^3-3 x y^2+2=0$..(i) and $\quad 3 x^2 y-y^3-2=0$...(ii) On differentiating Eqs. (i) and (ii), with respect to $x$, we get $\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) C_1=\frac{x^2-y^2}{2 x y}$
$\begin{aligned}\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right) C_2 & =\frac{2 x y}{x^2-y^2} \\ \left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{C_1} \times\left(\frac{d y}{d x}\right)_{C_2} & =-1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x-2 y=4$, then the minimum value of $x y$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $x-2 y=4$
Let
$A=x y$
$\begin{array}{lrl}\Rightarrow & A & =2 y^2+4 y \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d A}{d y} & =4+4 y\end{array}$
For extremum value, $\frac{d A}{d y}=0 \Rightarrow y=-1$
Now, $\quad \frac{d^2 A}{d y^2}=4\gt0$,
$\begin{aligned} & \text { At } \quad y=-1, x=4+2(-1)=2 \\ & \therefore \quad A=x y=2(-1)=-2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Hence, the minimum value of $x y$ is -2

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The point in the interval $[0,2 \pi]$, where $f(x)=e^x \sin x$ has maximum slope, is
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=e^x \sin x$
$\Rightarrow  f^{\prime}(x)=e^x \cos x+\sin x \cdot e^x$
and $f^{\prime \prime}(x)=-\sin x \cdot e^x+\cos x \cdot e^x+\cos x \cdot e^x+\sin x \cdot e^x$
Now, for maximum or minimum slope put $\left(f^{\prime}(x)\right)^{\prime}=0 \Rightarrow f^{\prime \prime}(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow  2 \cos x \cdot e^x=0$
$\Rightarrow \cos x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{\pi}{2}$
Also, $f^{\prime \prime \prime}(x)=-2 \sin x \cdot e^x+2 \cos x \cdot e^x=$ negative
$\therefore$ Slope is maximum at $x=\pi / 2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\frac{a x+b}{(x-1)(x-4)}$ has a local maxima at $(2,-1)$, then
questions.solution:
Clearly, $f(2)=-1$
$\Rightarrow -1=\frac{2 a+b}{(2-1)(2-4)} \Rightarrow 2 a+b=2$
Now, 
$\begin{gathered}
f^{\prime}(x)=\frac{4 a+5 b-2 b x-a x^2}{(x-1)^2(x-4)^2} \\
f^{\prime}(2)=0
\end{gathered}$
$\Rightarrow  b=0 \Rightarrow a=1$
$\Rightarrow  f^{\prime}(x)=-\frac{(x-2)(x+2)}{(x-1)^2(x-4)^2}$
Clearly, for $x\gt2, f^{\prime}(x) \lt 0$ and for $x \lt 2$, $f^{\prime}(x)\gt0$.
Thus, $x=2$ is indeed the point of local maxima for $y=f(x)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The greatest value of the function $f(x)=x e^{-x}$ in $[0, \infty)$, is
questions.solution:
We have,
$f(x)=x e^{-x}$
On differentiating both sides w.r.t. $x$, we get
$f^{\prime}(x)=e^{-x}(1-x)$
Put $\quad f^{\prime}(x)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad x=1$
Now, $\quad f(0)=0$
and $\quad \lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} x e^{-x}$
$=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{x}{e^x}=\lim _{x \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{\theta^x}=0$
Hence, the greatest value of $f(x)$ is $\frac{1}{e}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation of the tangent to the curve $\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^n+\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)^n=2$ at $(a, b)$ is
questions.solution:
The given curve is $\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^n+\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)^n=2$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow n\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{a}+n\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{1}{b} \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \cdot \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} \frac{\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)^{n-1}}{\left(\frac{y}{b}\right)^{n-1}} \end{aligned}$
At $(a, b), \frac{d y}{d x}=-\frac{b}{a} \frac{(1)^{n-1}}{(1)^{n-1}}=-\frac{b}{a}$ $\therefore$ The tangent at $(a, b)$ is $\begin{array}{cc} \Rightarrow & y-b=-\frac{b}{a}(x-a) \\ \Rightarrow & a y-a b=-b x+a b \\ \Rightarrow & b x+a y=2 a b \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{x}{a}+\frac{y}{b}=2 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The approximate value of $f(5.001)$, where $f(x)=x^3-7 x^2+15$, is
questions.solution:
Firstly, break the number 5.001 as $x=5$ and $\Delta x=0.001$ and use the relation $f(x+\Delta x)=f(x)+\Delta x f^{\prime}(x)$.
Consider, $f(x)=x^3-7 x^2+15$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-14 x$
Let $\quad x=5$
and $\quad \Delta x=0.001$
Also, $f(x+\Delta x) \simeq f(x)+\Delta x f^{\prime}(x)$
Therefore,
$\begin{aligned}
& f(x+\Delta x)=\left(x^3-7 x^2+15\right)+\Delta x\left(3 x^2-14 x\right) \\
& \Rightarrow \quad f(5.001) \simeq\left(5^3-7 \times 5^2+15\right) \\
& \quad+\left(3 \times 5^2-14 \times 5\right)(0.001)
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}
(\text { as } x & =5, \Delta x=0.001) \\
& =125-175+15+(75-70)(0.001) \\
& =-35+(5)(0.001) \\
& =-35+0.005=-34.995
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If an edge of a cube measure 2 m with a possible error of 0.5 cm . Find the corresponding error in the calculated volume of the cube
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the length of an edge of a cube and $V$ be the volume of that cube. $V=x^3$ On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get $\frac{d V}{d x}=3 x^2$ Let $\delta V$ be error in $V$ and corresponding error $\delta x$ in $x$. $\therefore \quad \delta V=\frac{d V}{d x} \delta x=3 x^2 \delta x$
Given that, $x=2 \mathrm{~m}$ and $\delta x=0.5 \mathrm{~cm}=\frac{0.5}{100} \mathrm{~m}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \delta V & =3(2)^2\left(\frac{0.5}{100}\right) \\ & =\frac{12 \times 0.5}{100}=\frac{6}{100}=0.06 \mathrm{~m}^3 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The minimum value of $x \log x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let \(y=x \log _e x\)
On differentiating w.r.t. \(x\), we get
\(\frac{d y}{d x}=x \cdot \frac{1}{x}+\log x=(1+\log x)\)
Again, differentiating, we get
\(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=\frac{1}{x}\)
Put, \(\frac{d y}{d x}=0\) for maxima or minima.
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 1+\log x=0 \\
& \Rightarrow x=\frac{1}{e} \\
& \therefore\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{\left(x=\frac{1}{c}\right)}=e
\end{aligned}\)
\(\therefore y\) is minimum at \(x=\frac{1}{e}\)
\(\therefore y_{\min }=\frac{1}{e} \log _e\left(\frac{1}{e}\right)=-\frac{1}{e}\)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area included between the curves $y^2=2 x$ and $x^2=2 y$ is
questions.solution: The point of intersection of the curves $y^2=2 x$ and $x^2=2 y$ are $O(0,0), A(2,2)$. $\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Required area } & =\int_0^2\left(\sqrt{2 x}-\frac{x^2}{2}\right) d x \\ & =\left[\sqrt{2} \frac{x^{3 / 2}}{3 / 2}-\frac{x^3}{6}\right]_0^2 \\ & =\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot 2^2-\frac{2^3}{6}\right]=\left[\frac{8}{3}-\frac{4}{3}\right] \\ & =\frac{4}{3} \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the curves $y=|x-2|, x=1, x=3$ and the $x$-axis is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \because \text { Required area } & =\int_1^3 y d x \\ & =\int_1^3|x-2| d x \\ & =\int_1^2-(x-2) d x+\int_2^3(x-2) d x \\ & =\int_1^2(2-x) d x+\int_2^3(x-2) d x \\ = & {\left[2 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2+\left[\frac{x^2}{2}-2 x\right]_2^3 } \\ = & {\left[4-2-\left(2-\frac{1}{2}\right)\right]+\left[\frac{9}{2}-6-(2-4)\right] } \\ = & 2-\frac{3}{2}-\frac{3}{2}+2 \\ = & 1 \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bf the region bounded by the curves $y=|5-x|, x=1, x=6$ and the $X$-axis
questions.solution:
Required area $=\left|\int_1^6\right| 5-x|d x|$
$=\left|\int_1^5-(5-x) d x+\int_5^6(5-x) d x\right|$
$=\left|\left[-5 x+\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^5+\left[5 x-\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_5^6\right|$
$=\left|\left[-25+\frac{25}{2}+5-\frac{1}{2}\right]+\left[30-18-25+\frac{25}{2}\right]\right|$
$=\left[\frac{-50+25+10-1}{2}\right]+\left[\frac{-26+25}{2}\right]$
$=\left|-8-\frac{1}{2}\right|=\frac{17}{2}$ sq units

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area between $y^2+4 x-8=0$, the $x$-axis and the line $x=1$ is
questions.solution:
Given equation can be rewritten as $y^2=-4(x-2)$ $\therefore$ Required area $=2 \int_1^2 y d x=\int_1^2 \sqrt{8-4 x} d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\frac{(8-4 x)^{3 / 2}}{\frac{3}{2}(-4)}\right]_1^2=-\frac{1}{6}[0-8] \\ & =\frac{4}{3} \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area in square unit enclosed by the curve $x^2 y=36$, the $x$-axis and the lines $x=6$ and $x=9$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Required area } & =\int_6^9 \frac{36}{x^2} d x=\left[-\frac{36}{x}\right]_6^9 \\ & =-36\left[\frac{1}{9}-\frac{1}{6}\right]=3 \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area bounded by the curves $y=x^2$ and $y=2-x^2$ is
questions.solution:
Required area $=2$ Area of curve $O A B O$
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \int_0^1\left[\left(2-x^2\right)-\left(x^2\right)\right] d x=2 \int_0^1\left(2-2 x^2\right) d x \\ & =4\left[x-\frac{x^3}{3}\right]_0^1=\frac{8}{3} \text { sq units }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area included between the curves $y^2=2 x$ and $x^2=2 y$ is
questions.solution:
The point of intersection of the curves $y^2=2 x$ and $x^2=2 y$ are $O(0,0), A(2,2)$.
$\therefore$ Required area $=\int_0^2\left(\sqrt{2 x}-\frac{x^2}{2}\right) d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left[\sqrt{2} \frac{x^{3 / 2}}{3 / 2}-\frac{x^3}{6}\right]_0^2 \\ & =\left[\frac{2}{3} \cdot 2^2-\frac{2^3}{6}\right]=\left[\frac{8}{3}-\frac{4}{3}\right] \\ & =\frac{4}{3} \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region enclosed by the curves $y=x, x=e, y=\frac{1}{x}$ and the positive $X$-axis, is
questions.solution:
Required area $=\int_0^1 x d x+\int_1^e \frac{1}{x} d x$
$=\left[\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_0^1+[\log x]_1^\theta=\frac{1}{2}+1=\frac{3}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by the parabola $(y-2)^2=(x-1)$, the tangent to the parabola at the point $(2,3)$ and the $X$-axis, is
questions.solution:
Given equation of parabola is $y^2-4 y-x+5=0$ Then, the equation of tangent at $(2,3)$ is $3 y-2(y+3)-\frac{(x+2)}{2}+5=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 y-x-4=0$
$\therefore$ Required area is given by $\begin{aligned} A & =\int_0^3\left(x_2-x_1\right) d y \\ & =\int_0^3\left[\left\{(y-2)^2+1\right\}-\{2 y-4\}\right] d y \\ & =\int_0^3\left(y^2-6 y+9\right) d y=\int_0^3(3-y)^2 d y \\ & =-\left[\frac{(3-y)^3}{3}\right]_0^3\end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad A=9$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by $y=x|\sin x|$ and $X$-axis between $x=0$ and $x=2 \pi$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} y & =x|\sin x| \\ \Rightarrow y & =\left\{\begin{array}{c}x \sin x, 0 \leq x \leq \pi \\ -x \sin x, \pi \lt x \leq 2 \pi\end{array}\right\}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \text { Required Area } & =\int_0^\pi x \sin x+\int_\pi^{2 \pi}(-x \sin x) d x \\ & =[x(-\cos x)]_0^\pi-\int_0^\pi(-\cos x) d x \\ - & {[\{x(-\cos x)\}]_\pi^{2 \pi}+\int_\pi^{2 \pi}(-\cos x) d x } \\ & =[\pi+\sin x]_0^\pi+[(2 \pi+\pi)-\sin x]_\pi^{2 \pi} \\ = & 4 \pi \text { sq units }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region $\left[(x, y): x^2+y^2 \leq 1 \leq x+y\right]$ is;
questions.solution:
$x^2+y^2=1, x+y=1$ meet when $\begin{array}{rlrl} & & x^2+(1-x)^2 & =1 \\ \Rightarrow & x^2+1+x^2-2 x & =1 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 x^2-2 x & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & 2 x(x-1) & =0\end{array}$ $\Rightarrow x=0, x=1$
$\Rightarrow$ Meet at points $A(1,0), B(0,1)$ $\therefore$ Required area $=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}(1) \cdot(1)$ $=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2} i . e ., \quad[$ quad. $O A B-\triangle O A B]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area enclosed by the curves $|y+x| \leq 1$, $|y-x| \leq 1$ and $2 x^2+2 y^y=1$ is
questions.solution:
The curves $y+x \leq 1$ and $|y-x| \leq 1$ form a square $A B C D$ and $2 x^2+2 y^2=1$ is a circle of radius $\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ and centre $(0,0)$.
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Required Area } \\ &=\text { Area of square }- \text { Area of circle } \\ &=\left(2-\frac{\pi}{2}\right) \text { sq units }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the parabolas $y^2=4 a(x+a)$ and $y^2=-4 a(x-a)$ is
questions.solution:
Required area $=4 \int_0^a \sqrt{4 a(a-x)} d x$

$\begin{aligned} & =4 \cdot 2 \sqrt{a} \cdot\left[\frac{-2(a-x)^{3 / 2}}{3}\right]_0^a \\ & =\frac{16}{3} a^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the figure bounded by the parabola $(y-2)^2=x-1$, the tangent to it at the point with the ordinate 3 and the $x$-axis is
questions.solution:
Given parabola, is $(y-2)^2=x-1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2(y-2)}$
when, $y=3, x=2$ $\therefore \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{2(3-2)}=\frac{1}{2}$ Tangent at $(x, 3)$ is $y-3=\frac{1}{2}(x-2)$ $\Rightarrow \quad x-2 y+4=0$ $\therefore$ Required area $\int_0^3\left\{(y-2)^2+1\right\} d y-\int_0^3(2 y-4) d y$ $=\left[\frac{(y-2)^3}{3}+y\right]_0^3-\left[y^2-4 y\right]_0^3$ $=\frac{1}{3}+3+\frac{8}{3}-(9-12)=9$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by $y=-1$, $y=2, x=y^3$ and $x=0$ is
questions.solution:
Required area $=\int_{-1}^2\left|x_1\right| d y$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_{-1}^0\left(-x_1\right) d y+\int_0^2 x_1 d y \\ & =-\int_{-1}^0 y^3 d y+\int_0^2 y^3 d y\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & =-\left[\frac{y^4}{4}\right]_{-1}^0+\left[\frac{y^4}{4}\right]_0^2=\frac{1}{4}+4 \\ & =\frac{17}{4} \text { sq units. }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the curves $y=|x|-1$ and $y=-|x|+1$ is
questions.solution: The region is clearly square with vertices at the points $(1,0),(0,1),(-1,0)$ and $(0,-1)$. So, its area $=\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}=2$ sq units.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the curve $y^2=2 x+1$ and the straight line $x-y-1=0$ is given by
questions.solution:
The point of intersection of curve $y^2=2 x+1$ and line $x-y-1=0$ is $(14,3)$ and $(0,-1)$

$\therefore$ Required area $=\int_{-1}^3\left(x_2-x_1\right) d y$ $\begin{aligned} & =\int_{-1}^3\left\{(y+1)-\frac{\left(y^2-1\right)}{2}\right\} d y \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{-1}^3\left(2 y+3-y^2\right) d y \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[y^2+3 y-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^3 \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[(9+9-9)-\left(1-3+\frac{1}{3}\right)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left(9+\frac{5}{3}\right) \\ & =\frac{16}{3} \mathrm{sq} \text { units }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the loop the curve $a y^2=x^2(a-x)$ is
questions.solution:
The area of the loop of the curve $a y^2=x^2(a-x)$ is given by
$2 \int_0^a y d x=2 \int_0^a x \sqrt{\frac{a-x}{a}} d x$ $=4 a^2 \int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin ^3 \theta \cdot \cos ^2 \theta \cdot d \theta$ [Let $x=a \sin ^2 \theta$ $\Rightarrow d x=2 a \sin \theta \cdot \cos \theta \cdot d \theta]$ $=4 a^2 \cdot \frac{2 \cdot 1}{5 \cdot 3 \cdot 1}=\frac{8 a^2}{15}$ sq unit.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region described by $A=\left\{(x, y): x^2+y^2 \leq 1\right.$ and $\left.y^2 \leq 1-x\right\}$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $A=\left\{(x, y): x^2+y^2 \leq 1\right.$ and $\left.y^2 \leq 1-x\right\}$
$\begin{aligned} \because \text { Required area } & =\frac{1}{2} \pi r^2+2 \int_0^1\left(1-y^2\right) d y \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \pi(1)^2+2\left[y-\frac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^1 \\ & =\frac{\pi}{2}+\frac{4}{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the curves $y=\tan x$, $-\frac{\pi}{3} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{3}, y=\cot x, \frac{\pi}{6} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}$ and the $X$-axis is
questions.solution:
The given curves, $y=\tan x$ and $y=\cot x$ meet at $x=\frac{\pi}{4}$.
$\therefore$ Required area $=\int_0^{\pi / 4} \tan x d x+\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} \cot x d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =(\ln \sec x)_0^{\pi / 4}+(\ln \sin x)_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} \\ & =\ln \sqrt{2}-\ln \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=2 \ln \sqrt{2}=\ln 2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region $\left\{(x, y): x^2+y^2 \leq 1 \leq x+y\right\}$ is
questions.solution: Required area $=\frac{1}{4}($ Area of circle $)-($ Area of $\triangle A O B)$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\pi(1)^2}{4}-\frac{1}{2} \times O A \times O B \\ & =\left(\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}\right) \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by the curve $y=x^4-2 x^3+x^2+3$ with $x$-axis and ordinates corresponding to the minima of $y$ is
questions.solution:
The equation of given curve is
$y=x^4-2 x^3+x^2+3$
On differentiating w.r.t. $x$, we get
$\frac{d y}{d x}=4 x^3-6 x^2+2 x$
Again, differentiating, we get
$\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=12 x^2-12 x+2$
Put, $\frac{d y}{d x}=0$ for maxima or minima.
$\Rightarrow 2 x\left(2 x^2-3 x+1\right)=0$
$\Rightarrow  x=0,1, \frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore \left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{(x=0)}=2$
$\therefore$ Function is minimum at $x=0$ 
and $\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{x=1}=12-12+2=2$
$\therefore$ Function is minimum at $x=1$
Also, $\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)_{\left(x=\frac{1}{2}\right)}=-1 \lt 0$
$\therefore$ Function is maximum at $x=\frac{1}{2}$
$\therefore$ Required area $=\int_0^1\left(x^4-2 x^3+x^2+3\right) d x$
$=\left[\frac{x^5}{5}-\frac{2 x^4}{4}+\frac{x^3}{3}+3 x\right]_0^1$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{5}-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{3}+3 \\ & =\frac{91}{30} \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area bounded by $y=1+\frac{8}{x^2}$ and the ordinates $x=2$ and $x=4$ is
questions.solution: Required area $\begin{aligned} & =\int_2^4\left(1+\frac{8}{x^2}\right) d x \\ & =\left[x-\frac{8}{x}\right]_2^4=4-\frac{8}{4}-2+\frac{8}{2} \\ & =4-2-2+4 \\ & =4 \text { sq unit }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of the region bounded by $1-y^2=|x|$ and $|x|+|y|=1$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Since, }|x|+|y|=1 \\ & \Rightarrow\left\{\begin{array}{cll} x+y=1, & x\gt0, & y\gt0 \\ x-y=1, & x\gt0, & y \lt 0 \\ -x+y=1, & x \lt 0, & y\gt0 \\ -x-y=1, & x \lt 0, & y \lt 0 \end{array}\right. \end{aligned}$ and $\quad 1-y^2=|x|$ $\Rightarrow \begin{cases}1-y^2=x, & x \geq 0 \\ 1-y^2=-x, & x \lt 0\end{cases}$
$\therefore$ Required area $\begin{aligned} & \begin{array}{l} =\left|2 \int_0^1 \sqrt{(1-x)} d x\right| \\ \quad+\left|2 \int_{-1}^0 \sqrt{(x+1)} d x\right|-4\left(\frac{1}{2} \cdot 1 \cdot 1\right) \end{array} \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \text { sq unit } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The area of figure bounded by $y=e^x, y=e^{-x}$ and the straight line $x=1$ is
questions.solution:
Required area $=\int_0^1\left(e^x-e^{-x}\right) d x$ $=\left[e^x+e^{-x}\right]_0^1$

$=\left(e+\frac{1}{e}-2\right)$ sq unit

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The solution of the equation $\log \left(\log _4(\sqrt{x+4}+\sqrt{x})\right)=0$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \because \log \left(\log _4(\sqrt{x+4}+\sqrt{x})\right)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & \log _4(\sqrt{x+4}+\sqrt{x})=1 \\
\Rightarrow & \sqrt{x+4}+\sqrt{x}=4^1=4
\end{aligned}$
From the given options, $x=\frac{9}{4}$ satisfy the equation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $y$ is the sum of three numbers, one of which is a constant, the 2nd varies as $x$ and the 3rd varies inversely as $x$. The values of $y$ at $x=1,-1$ and 3 are respectively $6,-4$ and 8 . Then, $y$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$y=a+k x+p / x$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\therefore   6 & =a+k+p \\   -4  &= a-k-p \\\text { and }  8 & =a+3 k+p / 3 \\ \Rightarrow  6-1 & =k+p \Rightarrow 5=k+p\end{array}$
On solving these equations, we get
$a=1, k=2, p=3$
$\therefore$ Required equation is $y=1+2 x+\frac{3}{x}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a^2+b^2+c^2=1$, then $a b+b c+c a$ lies in the interval
questions.solution:
We know, $(a+b+c)^2 \geq 0$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow a^2+b^2+c^2+2 a b+2 b c+2 c a \geq 0 \\ \Rightarrow  1+2(a b+b c+c a) \geq 0\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(a b+b c+c a) \geq \frac{-1}{2}$...(i)
Also, $\quad a^2+b^2+c^2=1$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow a^2+b^2+c^2-(a b+b c+c a) \\ & \quad=\frac{1}{2}\left[(a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2\right] \geq 0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 1-(a b+b c+c a) \geq 0\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad a b+b c+c a \leq 1$...(ii)
Hence, from Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$-\frac{1}{2} \leq a b+b c+c a \leq 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $9^{-x}=\frac{1}{27 \sqrt{27}}=(81)^{-y}$, then $(x, y)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $9^{-x}=\frac{1}{27 \sqrt{27}}=(81)^{-y}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & (3)^{-2 x}=(3)^{-3}(3)^{-\frac{3}{2}}=(3)^{-4 y} \\ \Rightarrow & -2 x=-\frac{9}{2} \text { and }-4 y=-\frac{9}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & x=\frac{9}{4} \text { and } y=\frac{9}{8}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}-\sqrt{7}}{2 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{7}}$, then $x+x^{-1}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $x=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}-\sqrt{7}}{2 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{7}}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad x+\frac{1}{x} & =\frac{2 \sqrt{2}-\sqrt{7}}{2 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{7}}+\frac{2 \sqrt{2}+\sqrt{7}}{2 \sqrt{2}-\sqrt{7}} \\ & =\frac{8+7-4 \sqrt{14}+8+7+4 \sqrt{14}}{8-7}=30\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $n$ be any integer, then $n(n+1)(2 n+1)$ is:
options: does not necessarily have any of the foregoing proof
questions.solution:
Given that $n$ be any integer, then $n(n+1)(2 n+1)$ always an integral multiple of even numbers (i.e. $6,30,84, \ldots$ ) when
$\begin{aligned}
& n=1, n(n+1)(2 n+1)=1(2)(3)=6 \\
& n=2, n(n+1)(2 n+1)=2(3)(5)=30
\end{aligned}$
and so on.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The decimal equivalent of the binary number 10011.1 is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The binary represents of 60 is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{c|c|c}
2 & 60 & \\
\hline 2 & 30 & 0 \\
\hline 2 & 15 & 0 \\
\hline 2 & 7 & 1 \\
\hline 2 & 3 & 1 \\
\hline & 1 & 1
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad(60)_{10}=(111100)_2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $3^x+2^{2 x} \geq 5^x$, then the solution set for $x$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\quad 3^x+2^{2 x} \geq 5^x$
$\begin{aligned}
& 3^x+\left(2^2\right)^x \geq 5^x ; 3^x+4^x \geq 5^x \\
& \Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{3}{5}\right)^x+\left(\frac{4}{5}\right)^x \geq 1
\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad(\sin \theta)^x+(\cos \theta)^x \geq 1$
[by triangle inequality]
$\Rightarrow \quad x \leq 2$
$\therefore$ Solution set is $(-\infty, 2]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which is the correct order for a given number $\alpha$ in increasing order?
questions.solution: Since, $10,3, e, 2$ are in decreasing order. $\therefore \log _{10} \alpha, \log _3 \alpha, \log _e \alpha, \log _2 \alpha$ are in increasing order.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $3^{|3 x+4|}=9^{2 x-2}, x\gt0$ has the solution
questions.solution:
$3^{|3 x+4|}=9^{2 x-2}, x\gt0$
When $x\gt0$, then $\begin{aligned} & & |3 x+4| & =3 x+4 \\ \therefore & & 3^{3 x+4} & =3^{2(2 x-2)} \\ \Rightarrow & & 3 \hat{x}+4 & =4 \dot{x}-4 \\ \Rightarrow & & x & =8 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\left|\frac{x}{2}-1\right| \lt 3$ implies that $x$ lies in the interval
questions.solution:
$\left|\frac{x}{2}-1\right| \lt 3 \Rightarrow-3 \lt \frac{x}{2}-1 \lt 3$
$\begin{array}{lc}\Rightarrow & -3+1 \lt \frac{x}{2} \lt 3+1 \\ \Rightarrow & -2 \lt \frac{x}{2} \lt 4\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & -2 \lt \frac{x}{2} \lt 4 \\ \Rightarrow & -4 \lt x \lt 8 \\ \therefore & x \in(-4,8)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\log _3 2, \log _3\left(2^x-5\right)$ and $\log _3\left(2^x-\frac{7}{2}\right)$ are in AP , the value of $x$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \log _3 2, \log _3\left(2^x-5\right), \log _3\left(2^x-\frac{7}{2}\right) \text { are in AP. } \\ \Rightarrow & \log _3\left(2^x-5\right)=\frac{\log _3\left(2^x-\frac{7}{2}\right)+\log _3 2}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & \left(2^x-5\right)^2=2 \cdot\left(2^x-\frac{7}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \left(2^x\right)^2+25-10 \cdot 2^x=2 \cdot 2^x-7 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(2^x\right)^2-12 \cdot 2^x+25+7=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \left(2^x\right)^2-12 \cdot 2^x+32=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2^x=8 \text { or } 2^x=4 \\ \Rightarrow & x=3 \text { or } 2 \\ \Rightarrow & x=3(\because x=2 \text { does not satisfy the given series })\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $77 x \equiv 88(\bmod 5)$, then $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Give that, $77 x \equiv 88(\bmod 5)$
$\Rightarrow 77 x-88$ is divisible by 5 .
$\begin{array}{rr}
\therefore x=4 \\
\end{array}$
$[\because 77 \times 4-88=308-88=220 \text { is divisible by 5] }$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of integral solution of $\frac{x+1}{x^2+2}\gt\frac{1}{4}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {  We have, } \frac{x+1}{x^2+2}\gt\frac{1}{4} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad x^2+2-4 x-4 \lt 0 \Rightarrow x^2-4 x-2 \lt 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \quad(x-2)^2-(\sqrt{6})^2 \lt 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \\
& \Rightarrow \quad\{(x-2)-\sqrt{6})\}(x-2+\sqrt{6}) \lt 0 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2-\sqrt{6} \lt x \lt 2+\sqrt{6} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad 2-2.449 \lt x \lt 2+2.449 \\
& \Rightarrow \quad-0.449 \lt x \lt 4.449
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ In between -0.445 and 4.449 five values of $x$ lies, i.e. $0,1,2,3,4$.
$\therefore$ There are 5 integral solution of inequality.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$\frac{1}{\log _{25} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _4 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{2}} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{5}} 10} \text { is }$
equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\log _{25} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _4 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{2}} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{5}} 10} \\ = & \frac{\log 25}{\log 10}+\frac{\log \frac{4}{\log 10}}{\log }+\frac{\log \sqrt{2}}{\log 10}+\frac{\log \sqrt{5}}{\log 10} \\ = & \frac{2 \log 5+\frac{1}{2} \log 5}{\log 10}+\frac{2 \log 2+\frac{1}{2} \log 2}{\log 10} \\ = & \frac{5}{2}\left(\frac{\log 5+\log 2}{\log 10}\right)=\frac{5}{2} \frac{\log 10}{\log 10}=\frac{5}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\log _2\left(9-2^x\right)=10^{\log (3-x)}$, solve for $x$
questions.solution:
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Given, } \log _2\left(9-2^x\right)=10^{\log _{10}(3-x)} \\
& \Rightarrow \log _2\left(9-2^x\right)=(3-x) \ldots\left[\because a^{\log _a b}=1\right] \\
& \Rightarrow 2^{3-x}=9-2^x \\
& \Rightarrow \frac{2^3}{2^x}=9-2^x \\
& \Rightarrow 8=2^x \times\left(9-2^x\right) \\
& \Rightarrow 2^{2 x}-2^x \times 9+8=0
\end{aligned}\)
Let \(2^x=y\), then
\(\begin{aligned}
& y^2-9 y+8=0 \\
& \Rightarrow(y-8)(y-1)=0 \\
& \Rightarrow y=8 \text { or } y=1 \\
& \Rightarrow 2^x=2^3 \text { or } 2^x=2^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow x=3 \text { or } x=0
\end{aligned}\)
But \(\mathrm{x}=3\) does not satisfy the given equation, since \(\log 0\) is not defined.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A graph which has no edges or node is known
questions.solution:
A graph that has no edges or nodes is called a null graph. It is also known 
as an empty graph.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} &  x^{4 / 3}+x^{-1 / 3}=1 \Rightarrow x^{4 / 3} \cdot x^{1 / 3}+1=x^{1 / 3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x^{5 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}=-1\end{aligned}$
On cubing both sides, we get
$\begin{array}{rr} & x^5-x-3 x^2\left(x^{5 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}\right)=-1 \\ \Rightarrow & x^5-x+3 x^2=-1\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\left|\frac{x}{2}-1\right| \lt 3$ implies that $x$ lies in the interval
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{r}\left|\frac{x}{2}-1\right| \lt 3 \Rightarrow-3 \lt \frac{x}{2}-1 \lt 3 \\ -3+1 \lt \frac{x}{2} \lt 3+1\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & -2 \lt \frac{x}{2} \lt 4 \\ \Rightarrow & -4 \lt x \lt 8 \\ \therefore & x \in(-4,8)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a=\log _2 3, \quad b=\log _2 5, \quad c=\log _7 2$, then $\log _{140} 63$ in terms of $a, b, c$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\frac{1}{\log _{25} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _4 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{2}} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{5}} 10}$ is equal to 11
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{\log _{25} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _4 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{2}} 10}+\frac{1}{\log _{\sqrt{5}} 10} \\ = & \frac{\log 25}{\log 10}+\frac{\log 4}{\log 10}+\frac{\log \sqrt{2}}{\log 10}+\frac{\log \sqrt{5}}{\log 10} \\ = & \frac{2 \log 5+\frac{1}{2} \log 5}{\log 10}+\frac{2 \log 2+\frac{1}{2} \log 2}{\log 10} \\ = & \frac{5}{2}\left(\frac{\log 5+\log 2}{\log 10}\right)=\frac{5}{2} \frac{\log 10}{\log 10}=\frac{5}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions of $\log _4(x-1)=\log _2(x-3)$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{lc}
\log _4 & (x-1)=\log _2(x-3)=\log _{4^{1 / 2}}(x-3) \\
\Rightarrow & \log _4(x-1)=2 \log _4(x-3) \\
\Rightarrow & \log _4(x-1)=\log _4(x-3)^2 \\
\Rightarrow & (x-1)=(x-3)^2 \\
\Rightarrow & x^2+9-6 x=x-1 \\
\Rightarrow & x^2-7 x+10=0 \\
\Rightarrow & (x-2)(x-5)=0 \\
\Rightarrow & x=2 \text { or } x=5
\end{array}$
Hence, $x=5$
$[\because x=2$ makes $\log (x-3)$ undefined. $]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a \lt b$, then $a \lt \frac{a+b}{2} \lt \ldots$.
questions.solution:
$a \lt b \Rightarrow \frac{a+b}{2} \lt \frac{b+b}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{a+b}{2} \lt b$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{3}$ and $\frac{b}{c}=\frac{3}{4}$, then the value of $\frac{a+2 b}{b+2 c}$ is
questions.solution:
$\because \frac{a}{b}=\frac{1}{3} \Rightarrow b=3 a$ and $\frac{b}{c}=\frac{3}{4} \Rightarrow \frac{3 a}{c}=\frac{3}{4}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{a}{c}=\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow c=4 a$
Then, $\frac{a+2 b}{b+2 c}=\frac{a+2(3 a)}{(3 a)+2(4 a)}$
$=\frac{a+6 a}{3 a+8 a}=\frac{7 a}{11 a}=\frac{7}{11}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\log x+\log x^3+\log x^5+\ldots .+\log x^{2 n-1}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\log x+\log x^3+\log x^5+\ldots+\log x^{2 n-1}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\log \left(x \cdot x^3 \cdot x^5 \ldots x^{2 n-1}\right) \\ & =\log \left(x^{1+3+5+\ldots+2 n-1}\right) \\ & =\log x^{\frac{n}{2}[2+(n-1) 2]}=\log x^{n^2}=n^2 \log x\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The square root of $\sqrt{50}+\sqrt{48}$ is
questions.solution:
Now, $\begin{aligned} & \sqrt{50}+\sqrt{48}=5 \sqrt{2}+4 \sqrt{3} \\ &= \sqrt{2}[5+2 \cdot \sqrt{2} \cdot \sqrt{3}] \\ &= \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})^2\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \sqrt{\sqrt{50}+\sqrt{48}}=2^{1 / 4}(\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2})$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of solutions of $\log _4(x-1)=\log _2(x-3)$ is/are
questions.solution:
Given that, $\log _4(x-1)=\log _2(x-3)$
$\therefore \quad x-1=(x-3)^2$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & x^2-7 x+10=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (x-5)(x-2)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x=2,5\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow x=5$, but $x=2 \log _2(x-3)$ is not satisfied.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Which is the correct order for a given number $\alpha$ in increasing order?
questions.solution: Since, $10,3, e, 2$ are in decreasing order. $\therefore \quad \log _{10} \alpha, \log _3 \alpha, \log _e \alpha, \log _2 \alpha \quad$ are in increasing order.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The inequation $n!\gt2^{n-1}$ is true for
questions.solution:
Given inequation is
$n!\gt2^{n-1}$
For $\quad n=1,2$
$\begin{array}{ll} & 1!\gg 1 \\ \Rightarrow & 1 \gg 1 \\ \text { and } & 2!\ngtr 2^1 \Rightarrow 2 \ngtr 2\end{array}$
For $n=3$
$\begin{aligned} & 3!\gt2^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & 6\gt4\end{aligned}$
Hence, it is true for $n\gt2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If all edges are directed in a graph then it is called as
questions.solution: Full form of Digraph is Directed graph because it's edges are directed.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x=\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}, y=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}$, then $3 x^2+4 x y-3 y^2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given that, $y=\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}$
and $\quad x=\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & y=\frac{1}{x} \\ \Rightarrow & x y=1\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 3 x^2+4 x y-3 y^2=3(x-y) (x+y)+4 x y\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} &=3\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}-\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}\right)  \times\left(\frac{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2}}\right)+4\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =3\left[\frac{(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2})^2-(\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2})^2}{(5-2)(5-2)}\right] \left[(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt{2})^2+(\sqrt{5}-\sqrt{2})^2\right]+4 \\ & =\frac{1}{3} \cdot 4 \sqrt{10} \cdot 2(5+2)+4 \\ & =\frac{56}{3} \sqrt{10}+4 \\ & =\frac{1}{3}(56 \sqrt{10}+12)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{3}}-\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2}}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{3 \sqrt{2}}{\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{3}}-\frac{4 \sqrt{3}}{\sqrt{6}+\sqrt{2}}+\frac{\sqrt{6}}{\sqrt{3}+\sqrt{2}} \\ & =  \frac{3 \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{6}-\sqrt{3})}{6-3}-\frac{4 \sqrt{3}(\sqrt{6}-\sqrt{2})}{6-3} +\frac{\sqrt{6}(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2})}{3-2}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned} &= \sqrt{2}(\sqrt{6}-\sqrt{3})-\sqrt{3}(\sqrt{6}-\sqrt{2}) +\sqrt{6}(\sqrt{3}-\sqrt{2}) \\ &=0\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the coefficients of $x^3$ and $x^4$ in the expansion of $\left(1+a x+b x^2\right)(1-2 x)^{18}$ in powers of $x$ are both zero, then $(a, b)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
In the expansion of $\left(1+a x+b x^2\right)(1-2 x)^{18}$ coefficient of $x^3$ in $\left(1+a x+b x^2\right)(1-2 x)^{18}$
$=$ Coefficient of $x^3$ in $(1-2 x)^{18}+$ coefficient of $x^2$ in $a(1-2 x)^{18}+$ coefficient of $x$ in $b(1-2 x)^{18}$.
$=-{ }^{18} C_3 \cdot 2^3+a^{18} C_2 \cdot 2^2-b^{18} C_4 \cdot 2$
$\because \quad-{ }^{18} C_3 \cdot 2^3+a^{18} C_2 \cdot 2^2-b^{18} C_4 \cdot 2=0$
$\Rightarrow \frac{18 \times 17 \times 16}{3 \times 2} \cdot 8+a \cdot \frac{18 \times 17}{2} 2^2-b \cdot 18 \cdot 2=0$
$\Rightarrow 17 a-b=\frac{34 \times 16}{3}\ldots$ (i)
Similarly, coefficient of $x^4$
${ }^{18} C_4 \cdot 2^4-a \cdot{ }^{18} C_3 2^3+b \cdot{ }^{18} C_2 \cdot 2^2=0$
$\therefore \quad 32 a-32 b=240\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$a=16$ and $b=\frac{272}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of the middle term in the binomial expansion in powers of $x$ of $(1+\alpha x)^4$ and $(1-\alpha x)^6$ is the same, if $\alpha$ is equal to
questions.solution:
The coefficient of the middle term in power of $x(1+\alpha x)^4={ }^4 C_2 \alpha^2$
The coefficient of the middle term in power of $x(1-\alpha x)^6={ }^6 C_3(-\alpha)^3$
According to given condition, $\begin{array}{rlrl} & & { }^4 C_2 \alpha^2 & ={ }^6 C_3(-\alpha)^3 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{4!}{2!2!} \alpha^2 & =-\frac{6!}{3!3!} \alpha^3 \\ \Rightarrow & 6 \alpha^2 & =-20 \alpha^3 \\ \Rightarrow & & \alpha=-\frac{6}{20} \\ \therefore & & \alpha & =-\frac{3}{10} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The middle term of $\left(\sqrt{x}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^6$ is
questions.solution:
Middle term is $\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)$ th term for $n$ even.
$\therefore$ Middle term $=\left(\frac{6}{2}+1\right)=4$ th term
$\therefore \quad T_4={ }^6 C_3(\sqrt{x})^3\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{6-3}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{6!}{3!3!}(\sqrt{x})^3 \frac{(-1)^3}{(\sqrt{x})^3} \\ & =\frac{-6 \times 5 \times 4}{6} \\ & =-20\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Last two digits in $(9)^{50}$ be____
questions.solution:
$\because(9)^{50}=(10-1)^{50}$
$\begin{aligned} &=\left[{ }^{50} C_0(10)^{50}(-1)^0-{ }^{50} C_1(10)^{49}(1)\right.+\ldots .]-{ }^{50} C_{49}(10)^1 \\ &+{ }^{50} C_{50}(10)^0\end{aligned}$
$\because$ All the terms in the bracket are multiples of $10^2$.
So, we can write as $100 \lambda$
$=100 \lambda-50 \times 10+1=100 \lambda-500+1$
so, last 2 digits will be $=01$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the number of terms in the expansion of $(x \sqrt{180}+\sqrt[3]{432})^{200}$ having integral coefficients is $n$, then the vaule of $\left[\frac{n}{6}\right]$ is (where [.] denotes greater integer function)
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text T_{r+1}={ }^{200} C_r(x \cdot \sqrt{180})^{200-r} \cdot(\sqrt[3]{432})^r \\
& ={ }^{200} C_r 2^{200-r} \cdot 3^{200-r} \cdot 5^{\frac{200-r}{2}} \cdot x^{200-r} 2^r 3^r 2^{\frac{r}{3}} \\
& \therefore \text { Coefficient }={ }^{200} C_r 2^{200} \cdot 3^{200} \cdot 5^{100} \cdot \frac{2^{\frac{r}{3}}}{5^{\frac{r}{2}}}
\end{aligned}$
$r$ must be multiple of 6 and takes values between 0 to 200.
$\therefore 34$ terms $=n$
$\left[\frac{n}{6}\right]=\left[\frac{34}{6}\right]=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The middle term in the expansion of $\left(x-\frac{1}{2} y\right)^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
Middle term $=T_6=T_{5+1}$
$\begin{aligned} & ={ }^{10} C_5(x)^5\left(-\frac{1}{2} y\right)^5=\frac{10!}{5!\times 5!} \frac{(x)^5}{(-2)^5} y^5 \\ & =-\frac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7 \times 6}{5 \times 4 \times 2 \times 3 \times 2^5} x^5 y^5 \\ & =-\frac{63}{8} x^5 y^5\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the usual notation, $\frac{{ }^n C_1}{2}+\frac{{ }^n C_2}{3}+\ldots+\frac{{ }^n C_n}{n+1}$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\frac{C(n, 2)}{(n+1)!}$ is
questions.solution:
Now, $\frac{{ }^n C_2}{(n+1)!}=\frac{\frac{n!}{(n-2)!2!}}{(\theta+1)!}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{n!}{2(n+1)!(n-2)!}=\frac{n(n-1)}{2(n+1)!} \\ & =\frac{n^2-1+1-n}{2(n+1)!} \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{(n-7)}{n!}-\frac{1}{n!}+\frac{2}{(n+1)!}\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[\frac{1}{(n-1)!}-\frac{2}{n!}+\frac{2}{(n+1)!}\right]\end{aligned}$
Put $n=0,1,2,3, \ldots$, we get
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}[e-2(e)+2(e-1)] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[e-2]=\frac{e}{2}-1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}$ is
questions.solution:
$\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}=\left[\frac{\left(x^4-1\right)}{x-1}\right]^{-3}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(x^4-1\right)^{-3}(x-1)^3 \\ & =(-1)^{-3}\left(1-x^4\right)^{-3}(x-1)^3 \\ & =(-1)\left(1+3 x^4+\ldots\right)\left(x^3-3 x^2+3 x-1\right)\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Coefficient of $x$ in $\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}$ is -3 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The remainder obtained when $1!+2!+\ldots+200!$ is divided by 14 is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x^{-9}$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{2}{x}\right)^9$ is
questions.solution:
Let the coefficient of $x^{-9}$ is in the $(r+1)$ th term in the expansion of $\left(\frac{x^2}{2}-\frac{2}{x}\right)^9$, then $\begin{aligned} T_{r+1} & ={ }^9 C_r\left(\frac{x^2}{2}\right)^{9-r}\left(-\frac{2}{x}\right)^r \\ & ={ }^9 C_r \frac{x^{18-2 r}}{2^{9-r}} \cdot \frac{(-1)^r \cdot 2^r}{x^r} \\ & ={ }^9 C_r \frac{x^{18-3 r}}{2^{9-2 r}}(-1)^r \end{aligned}$
For coefficient of $x^{-9}$, put $\begin{aligned} & & x^{18-3 r} & =x^{-9} \\ \Rightarrow & & 18-3 r & =-9 \\ \Rightarrow & & & r \end{aligned}$ $\therefore$ Coefficient of $x^{-9}={ }^9 C_9 \frac{1}{\gamma^{-9}}(-1)^9=-2^9=-512$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least positive remainder when $123 \times 125 \times 127$ is divided by 124 is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Since, } \quad 123=-1(\bmod 124) \\
& 125=1(\operatorname{modl} 24) \\
& 127=3(\operatorname{modl} 24) \\
& \therefore \quad 123 \times 125 \times 127=(-1)(1)(3) \ldots \text { nd }(124) \\
& =-3 \bmod (124)=121 \bmod (124)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(3-x) \equiv(2 x-5)(\bmod 4)$, then one of the values of $x$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{lc}\text {  }(3-x) \equiv(2 x-5)(\bmod 4) \\ \Rightarrow & 3-x-2 x+5 \text { is divisible by } 4 \\ \Rightarrow & 8-3 x \text { is divisible by } 4 \\ \therefore & x=4\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x^4$ in $\left(1+x+x^3+x^4\right)^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
Let $E=\left(1+x+x^3+x^4\right)^{10}$
$=\left[1+x+x^3(1+x)\right]^{10}=(1+x)^{10}\left(1+x^3\right)^{10}$
$\begin{aligned}=\left[{ }^{10} C_0+{ }^{10} C_1 x^1\right. & +{ }^{10} C_2 x^2+\ldots+{ }^{10} C_9 x^9 \\ & \left.+{ }^{10} C_{10} x^{10}\right] \times\left[^{10} C_0 1+{ }^{10} C_1 1 \times\left(x^3\right)\right. \\ & \left.+{ }^{10} C_2\left(x^3\right)^2+{ }^{10} C_3\left(x^3\right)^3+\ldots\right]\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Coefficient of $x^4$ in $E={ }^{10} C_4 \times{ }^{10} C_0+{ }^{10} C_1 \times{ }^{10} C_1$
$=\frac{10!}{6!\times 4!} \times 1+10 \times 10$
$=\frac{10 \times 9 \times 8 \times 7}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1}+100$
$=210+100=310$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The remainder obtained when $5^{124}$ is divided by 124 is
questions.solution:
We have, $5^{124}=\left(5^3\right)^{41} \cdot 5$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Now, } & 5^3 & =1(\bmod 124) \\
& \therefore & \left(5^3\right)^{41} & =1(\bmod 124) \\
& & \left(5^3\right)^{41} \cdot 5 & =1(\bmod 124) \\
\Rightarrow & & 5^{124} & =5(\bmod 124)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the coefficients of the $r$ th term and the $(r+1)$ th term in the expansion of $(1+x)^{20}$ are in the ratio $1: 2$, then $r$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$T_r=T_{(r-1)+1}={ }^{20} C_{r-1} \cdot x^{r-1}$
$\therefore$ Coefficient of the $r$ th term $={ }^{20} C_{r-1}$ and coefficient of the $(r+1)$ th term $={ }^{20} C_r$ $\therefore \quad \frac{{ }^{20} C_{r-1}}{{ }^{20} C_r}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & \frac{r}{21-r}=\frac{1}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & r=7\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}=\left[\frac{\left(x^4-1\right)}{x-1}\right]^{-3} \\
= & \left(x^4-1\right)^{-3}(x-1)^3 \\
= & (-1)^{-3}\left(1-x^4\right)^{-3}(x-1)^3 \\
= & (-1)\left(1+3 x^4+\ldots\right)\left(x^3-3 x^2+3 x-1\right)\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Coefficient of $x$ in $\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^{-3}$ is -3 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $C(2 n, 3): C(n, 2)=44: 3$, then $n$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }{ }^{2 n} C_3:{ }^n C_2=\frac{44}{3} \\ \Rightarrow  & \frac{2 n!}{(2 n-3)!3!} \times \frac{2!(n-2)!}{n!}=\frac{44}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2 n(2 n-1)(2 n-2)}{3} \times \frac{1}{n(n-1)}=\frac{44}{3} \\ \Rightarrow  & \frac{2}{3} \times 2(2 n-1)=\frac{44}{3} \\ \Rightarrow  & 2 n-1=11 \Rightarrow 2 n=12 \Rightarrow n=6\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of positive integers satisfying the inequality ${ }^{n+1} C_{n-2}-{ }^{n+1} C_{n-1} \leq 50$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, }{ }^{n+1} C_{n-2}-{ }^{n+1} C_{n-1} \leq 50 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(n-1)!}{3!(n-2)!}-\frac{(n+1)!}{2!(n-2)!} \leq 50\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{(n+1)!}{3!}\left[\frac{1}{(n-2)!}-\frac{3}{(n-1)!}\right] \leq 50$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & (n+1)!\left(\frac{n-1-3}{(n-1)!}\right) \leq 300 \\ \Rightarrow & (n+1) n(n-4) \leq 300\end{array}$
For $n=8$, it satisfy to the above inequality.
But $n=1$ it does not satisfy the above inequality.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of the term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(\frac{x+1}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x-x^{1 / 2}}\right)^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{x+1}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x-x^{1 / 2}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\left(x^{1 / 3}\right)^3+1^3}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x^{1 / 2}\left(x^{1 / 2}-1\right)} \\ & =\frac{\left(x^{1 / 3}+1\right)\left(x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1\right)}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x^{1 / 2}+1}{x^{1 / 2}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =x^{1 / 3}+1-1-x^{-1 / 2} \\ & =x^{1 / 3}-x^{-1 / 2}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore\left(\frac{x+1}{x^{2 / 3}-x^{1 / 3}+1}-\frac{x-1}{x-x^{1 / 2}}\right)^{10}=\left(x^{1 / 3}-x^{-1 / 2}\right)^{10}$
$\begin{aligned} & T_{r+1} \text { for }\left(x^{1 / 3}-x^{-1 / 2}\right) \text { is } \\ & { }^{10} C_r\left(x^{1 / 3}\right)^{10-r}(-1)^r\left(x^{-1 / 2}\right)^r .\end{aligned}$
For term independent of $x$,
$\frac{10-r}{3}-\frac{r}{2}=0$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & 20-2 r-3 r & =0 \\ \Rightarrow & & r & =4\end{array}$
Hence, required coefficient $={ }^{10} C_4(-1)^4=210$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If there is a term containing $x^{2 r}$ in $\left(x+\frac{1}{x^2}\right)^{n-3}$, then
options: $n-2 r$ is a positive integral multiple of 3.
questions.solution:
Suppose $(s+1)$ th term contains $x^{2 r}$.
Then, we have
$\begin{aligned} T_{s+1} & ={ }^{n-3} C_s x^{n-3-s}\left(\frac{1}{x^2}\right)^s \\ & ={ }^{n-3} C_s x^{n-3-3 s}\end{aligned}$
This will contain $x^{2 r}$, if
$n-3-3 s=2 r$
$\begin{array}{lr}\Rightarrow & s=\frac{n-3-2 r}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & s=\frac{n-2 r}{3}-1 \\ \Rightarrow & s+1=\frac{n-2 r}{3} \\ \Rightarrow & n-2 r=3(s+1)\end{array}$
Hence, $(n-2 r)$ is a positive integral multiple of 3.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^4\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^3$, is
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{gathered}\left(x-\frac{1}{x}\right)^4\left(x+\frac{1}{x}\right)^3 \\ =\left({ }^4 C_0 x^4-{ }^4 C_1 x{ }^2+{ }^4 C_2-{ }^4 C_3 \frac{1}{x^2}+{ }^4 C_4 \frac{1}{x^4}\right) \\ \quad \times\left({ }^3 C_0 x^3+{ }^3 C_1 x+{ }^3 C_2 \frac{1}{x}+{ }^3 C_3 \frac{1}{x^3}\right)\end{gathered}$
Clearly, there is no term free from $x$ on RHS. Therefore, the term independent of $x$ on LHS is zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the coefficient of $x^m, m \in N$ in the expansion of $\left(\sqrt{2 x}+\sqrt[3]{\frac{3}{x^2}}\right)^9$ is equal to $k(k \neq 0)$, the $k$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $C(2 n, 3): C(n, 2)=44: 3$, then $n$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, ${ }^{2 n} C_3:{ }^n C_2=\frac{44}{3}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2 n!}{(2 n-3)!3!} \times \frac{2!(n-2)!}{n!}=\frac{44}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{2 n(2 n-1)(2 n-2)}{3} \times \frac{1}{n(n-1)}=\frac{44}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2}{3} \times 2(2 n-1)=\frac{44}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 n-1=11 \Rightarrow 2 n=12 \Rightarrow n=6\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The term independent of $x$ in the expansion of $\left(2 x^4-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)^{12}$ is
questions.solution:
The general term is
$\begin{aligned} T_{r+1} & ={ }^{12} C_r\left(2 x^4\right)^r\left(-\frac{1}{x^2}\right)^{12-r} \\ & ={ }^{12} C_r(2)^r x^{4 r}(-1)^{12-r}(x)^{-2(12-r)}\end{aligned}$
For term independent of $x$, we put
$\begin{aligned} & 4 r-24+2 r=0 \Rightarrow 6 r=24 \Rightarrow r=4 \\ & \begin{aligned} T_5 & ={ }^{12} C_4(2)^4(-1)^{12-4} \\ \quad & =\frac{12 \times 11 \times 10 \times 9}{4 \times 3 \times 2 \times 1} \times(2)^4=7920\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Middle term in the expansion of $\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}+2\right)^n$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A person is permitted to select at least one and at most $n$ coins from a collection of $(2 n+1)$ distinct coins. It the total number of ways in which he can select coins is 255 , then $n$ equals
questions.solution:
We have
${ }^{2 n+1} C_1+{ }^{2 n+1} C_2+\ldots+{ }^{2 n+1} C_n=255$
Also, the sum of binomial coefficients
$\begin{aligned} & { }^{2 n+1} C_0+{ }^{2 n+1} C_1+{ }^{2 n+1} C_2+\ldots+{ }^{2 n+1} C_n \\ & \quad+{ }^{2 n+1} C_{n+1}++{ }^{2 n+1} C_{n+2}+\ldots+{ }^{2 n+1} C_{2 n+1}\end{aligned}$
$=(1+1)^{2 n+1}=2^{2 n+1}$
$\begin{array}{r}\Rightarrow{ }^{2 n+1} C_0+2\left\{{ }^{2 n+1} C_1+{ }^{2 n+1} C_2+\ldots+{ }^{2 n+1} C_n\right\} \\ +{ }^{2 n+1} C_{2 n+1}=2^{2 n+1}\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}\Rightarrow & & 1+2(255)+1 & =2^{2 n+1} \\ \Rightarrow & 1+255 & =2^{2 n} \\ \Rightarrow & & 2^{2 n} & =2^8 \\ \Rightarrow & & n & =4\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $x$ in the expression $\left(x+x^{\log _{10} x}\right)^0$, if the third term in the expansion is $1,000,000$, is
questions.solution:
$\log x$ is defined only when $x\gt0$
Now, the $3^{\text {rd }}$ term in the expansion
$T_{2+1}={ }^5 C_2 \cdot x^{5-2} \cdot\left(x^{00_{10} x}\right)^2=1,000,000 \quad$ (given)
$\Rightarrow x^{3+2 \log _{10} x}=10^5$.
Taking logarithm of both sides, we get
$\begin{aligned} & \left(3+2 \log _{10} x\right) \cdot \log _{10} x\end{aligned}=5$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 y^2+3 y-5=0$
where $\log _{10} x=y$
$\begin{array}{lc}\Rightarrow & (y-1)(2 y+5)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & y=1 \text { or }-5 / 2 \\ \Rightarrow & \log _{10} x=1 \text { or }-5 / 2 \\ \Rightarrow & x=10^1=10 \text { or } 10^{-5 / 2}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\sum_{k=0}^{10}{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\sum_{k=0}^{10}{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_k={ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_1+\ldots+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[2 \cdot{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_0+2 \cdot{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_1+\ldots+2 \cdot{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}\right]$
$\begin{aligned}=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left({ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{20}\right)+\left({ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{19}\right)+\right. \\ \left.\ldots+2{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}\right]\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{1}{2}\left[\left({ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_0+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_1+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_2+\ldots+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{19}\right.\left.+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{20}\right)+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2}\left[2^{29}+{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}\right] \\ & =2^{19}+\frac{1}{2}{ }^{20} \mathrm{C}_{10}\end{aligned}$
