questions.q: The middle term in the expansion of $\left(x^2+\frac{1}{x^2}+2\right)^n$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x+y=1$, then $\sum_{r=0}^n r^{2 \cdot n} C_r x^r y^{n-r}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \sum_{r=0}^n & r^2{ }^n C_r x^r y^{n-r} \\ & =\sum_{r=0}^n[r(r-1)+r]{ }^n C_r x^r y^{n-r} \\ & =\sum_{r=0}^n r(r-1)^n C_r x^r y^{n-r}+\sum_{r=0}^n r^n C_r x^r y^{n-r}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} = & \sum_{r=2}^{n-2} r(r-1) \frac{n}{r} \cdot \frac{n-1}{r-1}{ }^{n-2} C_{r-2} x^2 \cdot x^{r-2} y^{n-r} +\sum_{r=1}^n r \cdot \frac{n}{r}{ }^{n-1} C_{r-1} x \cdot x^{r-1} y^{n-r} \end{aligned}$
$=n(n-1) x^2 \sum_{r=2}^{n-2}{ }^{n-2} C_{r-2}x^{r-2} y^{(n-2)-(r-2)}+n x \sum_{r=1}^n{ }^{n-1} C_{r-1} x^{r-1} y^{(n-1)-(r-1)}$
$=n(n-1) x^2+n x \quad(\because x+y=1)$ $=n x(n x-x+1)$ $=n x(n x+y) \quad(\because x+y=1)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least positive remainder when $123 \times 125 \times 127$ is divided by 124 is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Since, } \quad 123 =-1(\bmod 124) \\ 125=  (\bmod 124) \\ 127  =3(\bmod 124) \\ \therefore \quad 123 \times 125 \times 127  =(-1)(1)(3) \bmod (124) \\  =-3 \bmod (124) \\  =121 \bmod (125)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The coefficient of $x^4$ in the expansion of $\left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^n$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \left(1+x+x^2+x^3\right)^n=(1+x)^n\left(1+x^2\right)^n \\ & \quad=\left(1+{ }^n C_1 x+{ }^n C_2 x^2+\ldots \ldots+{ }^n C_n x^n\right)  \times\left(1+{ }^n C_1 x^2+{ }^n C_2 x^4+\ldots \ldots+{ }^n C_n x^{2 n}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ The coefficient of $x^4$
$\begin{aligned} & ={ }^n C_2+{ }^n C_2 \cdot{ }^n C_1+{ }^n C_4 \\ & ={ }^n C_4+{ }^n C_2+{ }^n C_1 \cdot{ }^n C_2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The least remainder when $17^{30}$ is divided by 5 , is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & (17)^2=289 \equiv(-1)(\bmod 5) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(17^2\right)^{15} \equiv(-1)^{15}(\bmod 5)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & 17^{30} \equiv-(\bmod 5) \\ \text { i.e., } & 17^{30} \equiv 4(\bmod 5)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The term independent of $x$ in
$\left[\sqrt{\left(\frac{x}{3}\right)}+\sqrt{\left(\frac{3}{2 x^2}\right)}\right]^{10}$ is
questions.solution:
The general term is $T_{r+1}={ }^{10} C_r\left(\sqrt{\frac{x}{3}}\right)^{10-r}\left(\sqrt{\frac{3}{2 x^2}}\right)^r$
$={ }^{10} C_r\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{5-(r / 2)}\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^{r / 2} \cdot x^{5-(3 r / 2)}$
for term independent of $x$.
$5-\frac{3 r}{2}=0 \Rightarrow r=\frac{10}{3}$, which is not a positive integer. Hence, there is no term independent of $x$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The statement $P(n)=9^n-8^n$, when divided 8 , always leaves the remainder
questions.solution:
Given, $P(n)=9^n-8^n$
$\therefore \quad P(1)=9-8=1$
$\therefore \quad P(\mathrm{l})-\mathrm{l}=0$, which is divisible by 8 .
Now, $P(2)=9^2-8^2=17$
When divided by 8 , it leaves the remainder 1 .

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The greatest coefficient in the expansion of $(1+x)^{2 n}$ is
questions.solution:
$\frac{\text { The coefficient of } t_{r+1}}{\text { The coefficient of } t_r}=\frac{{ }^{2 n} C_r}{{ }^{2 n} C_{r-1}}$
$=\frac{2 n-r+1}{r}$
The coefficient of $t_{r+1} \geq$ The coefficient of $t_r$, provided
$\frac{2 n-r+1}{r} \geq 1$ or $2 n+1 \geq 2 r$
$\text { or, }  r \leq \frac{2 n+1}{2} \text { or } r \leq n+\frac{1}{2}$
Hence, the greatest coefficient $=$ The coefficient of $(n+1)$ th term $={ }^{2 n} C_n=\frac{(2 n)!}{(n!)^2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The first three terms in the expansion of $(1+a x)^n(n \neq 0)$ are 1, $6 x$ and $16 x^2$. Then, the values of $a$ and $n$ are respectively
questions.solution:
$\because(1+a x)^n=1+a x n+\frac{n(n-1)}{2!}(a x)^2+\ldots$
Given that,
$\begin{aligned}
& a x n & =6 x \\
\Rightarrow & a n & =6...(i)
\end{aligned}$
and $\quad \frac{a^2 n(n-1)}{2}=16$...(ii)
On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $a=\frac{2}{3}$ and $n=9$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The constant term in the expansion of $(1+x)^m\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)^n$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $(1+x)^m\left(1+\frac{1}{x}\right)^n=(1+x)^m\left(\frac{x+1}{x}\right)^n$
$=\frac{(1+x)^{m+n}}{x^n}=x^{-n}(1+x)^{m+n}$
$\therefore$ Required term independent of $x$
$=$ Coefficient of $x^0$ in $x^{-n}(1+x)^{m+n}$
$=$ Coefficient of $x^n$ in $(1+x)^{m+n}$
$={ }^{m+n} C_n$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of terms in the expansion of $(\sqrt{5}+\sqrt[4]{11})^{124}$ which are integers, is equal to
questions.solution:
The general term in the expansion of $(\sqrt{5}+4 \sqrt{11})^{124}$ is
$\begin{gathered}T_{r+1}={ }^{124} C_r(\sqrt{5})^{124-r}(\sqrt[4]{11})^r \\ \quad={ }^{124} C_r \cdot 5^{(124-r) / 2} \cdot 11^{r / 4}\end{gathered}$
For integer terms $r=0,4,8,12, \ldots, 124$
$\therefore$ Number of terms which are integers $=32$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Sum of the last 30 coefficients in the expansion of $(1+x)^{59}$, when expanded in ascending power of $x$ is
questions.solution:
Since, total number of terms $=59+1=60$ $\therefore$ 
Required sum $=\frac{2^{59}}{2}=2^{58}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The middle term of $\left(\sqrt{x}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^6$ is
questions.solution:
Middle term is $\left(\frac{n}{2}+1\right)$ th term for $n$ even.
$\therefore$ Middle term $=\left(\frac{6}{2}+1\right)=4$ th term
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore  T_4 & ={ }^6 C_3(\sqrt{x})^3\left(-\frac{1}{\sqrt{x}}\right)^{6-3} \\
& =\frac{6!}{3!3!}(\sqrt{x})^3 \frac{(-1)^3}{(\sqrt{x})^3} \\
& =\frac{-6 \times 5 \times 4}{6} \\
& =-20
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $t_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{1}{\left({ }^n C_r\right)^k}$ and $S_n=\sum_{r=0}^n \frac{r}{\left({ }^n C_r\right)^k}$, where $k \in Z^{+}$, then $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{S_n}{n t_n}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
$S_n=\frac{0}{\left({ }^n C_0\right)^k}+\frac{1}{\left({ }^n C_1\right)^k}+\frac{2}{\left({ }^n C_2\right)^k}+\ldots+\frac{n}{\left({ }^n C_n\right)^k}$
$\Rightarrow \quad S_n=\frac{n}{\left({ }^n C_0\right)^k}+\frac{n-1}{\left({ }^n C_1\right)^k}+\frac{n-2}{\left({ }^n C_2\right)^k}+\ldots+\frac{0}{\left({ }^n C_0\right)^k}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & 2 S_n=n t_n \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{S_n}{n t_n}=\frac{1}{2}\end{array}$
Now, $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{S_n}{n t_n}\right)=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the term free from $x$ in the expansion of $\left(\sqrt{x}-\frac{k}{x^2}\right)^{10}$ is 405 , then the value of $k$ is
questions.solution:
General term in the expansion of $\left(\sqrt{x}-\frac{k}{x^2}\right)^{10}$ is
$\begin{aligned}
T_{r+1} & ={ }^{10} C_r(\sqrt{x})^{10-r} \cdot\left(\frac{-k}{x^2}\right)^r \\
& ={ }^{10} C_r x^{\frac{10-r}{2}} \cdot(-k)^r \cdot x^{-2 r} \\
& ={ }^{10} C_r(-k)^r x^{\left(\frac{10-5 r}{2}\right)}
\end{aligned}$
$\because$ The term is free from $x$
$\begin{array}{lrl}
\therefore \text { Put } & \frac{10-5 r}{2} & =0 \\
\Rightarrow & r & =2 \\
\text { Now, } & { }^{10} C_2(-k)^2 & =405 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{10 \times 9}{1 \times 2} \cdot k^2 & =405 \\
\Rightarrow & & k^2=\frac{405}{45}=9 \\
\Rightarrow & k & = \pm 3
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Given positive integers $r\gt1, n\gt2$ and the coefficient of (3r) th and $(r+2)$ th terms in the binomial expansion of $(1+x)^{2 n}$ are equal, then
questions.solution:
$3 r$ th term in the expansion of $(1+x)^{2 n}$
$={ }^{2 \pi} C_{3 r-1} x^{3 r-1}$
and $(r+2)$ th term in the expansion of $(1+x)^{2 n}$
$={ }^{2 n} C_{r+1} x^{r+1}$
Given that the binomial coefficients of (3r)th and $(r+2)$ th terms are equal.
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & { }^{2 n} C_{3 r-1}={ }^{2 n} C_{r+1} \\
\Rightarrow & 3 r-1=r+1 \\
\text { or } & 2 n=(3 r-1)+(r+1) \\
\Rightarrow & 2 r=2 \quad \text { or } \quad 2 n=4 r \\
\Rightarrow & r=1 \quad \text{or} \quad  n=2 r \\
\text { But } & r\gt1 \\
\therefore & n=2 r
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The differential equation of all circles which pass through the origin and whose centres lies on $Y$-axis is
questions.solution:
The equation of family of circles which passess through the origin and whose centrer lies on $Y$-axis is $(x-0)^2+(y-k)^2=k^2$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2+k^2-2 y k=k^2 \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+y^2-2 y k=0\end{aligned}$
where, $k$ is parameter.
On differentiating w.r.t . $x$, we get
$2 x+2 y \frac{d y}{d x}-2 \frac{d y}{d x} k=0$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & x+(y-k) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x+\left\{y-\frac{x^2+y^2}{2 y}\right\} \frac{d y}{d x}=0\end{array}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & x+\frac{1}{2 y}\left(y^2-x^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 x y=\left(x^2-y^2\right) \frac{d y}{d x}\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{2 x y}{x^2-y^2}$,
which is required equation.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of centre of circles which cuts orthogonally the circle $x^2+y^2-4 x+8=0$ and touches $x+1=0$, is
questions.solution:
Let equation of circle be
$x^2+y^2+2 g x+2 f y+c=0$
Given equation of circle is
$x^2+y^2-4 x+8=0$
The centres of above circles are $(-g,-f)$ and $(2,0)$.
Condition of orthogonality is
$2\left(g_1 g_2+f_1 f_2\right)=c_1+c_2$
$\therefore \quad 2(g \times(-2)+(f) \times 0)=c+8$
$\Rightarrow \quad-4 g=c+8$   ....(i)
Also, the assume circle touch the line $x+1=0$.
$\therefore$ The perpendicular drawn from centre to the line is equal to radius.
$\therefore \quad \frac{-g+1}{\sqrt{1^2}}=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-c}$
$\Rightarrow \quad-g+1=\sqrt{g^2+f^2-c}$
On squaring both sides, we get
$g^2+1-2 g=g^2+f^2-c$
$\Rightarrow \quad c=f^2+2 g-1$
Putting the value of $c$ in Eq. (i), we get $-4 g=f^2+2 g-1+8$
$\Rightarrow f^2+2 g+4 g+7=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^2+6 g+7=0$
$\therefore$ Locus of centre of circle is $y^2+6 x+7=0$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $Z_1=1+i, Z_2=-2+3 i$ and $Z_3=\frac{a l}{3}$ are collinear, where $i^2=-1$, then the value of $a$ will be
questions.solution:
Given complex númbers are
$Z_1=(1+i) \equiv(1,1)=\left(a_1, b_1\right)$
$Z_2=(-2+3 i) \equiv(-2,3) \equiv\left(a_2, b_2\right)$
and $\quad Z_3=\frac{a i}{3}=\left(0, \frac{a}{3}\right) \equiv\left(a_3, b_3\right)$
These three points will be collinear, if
$\frac{1}{2}\left[a_1\left(b_2-b_3\right)+a_2\left(b_3-b_1\right)+a_3\left(b_1-b_2\right)\right]=0$
$\begin{array}{cc}\Rightarrow & 1\left(3-\frac{a}{3}\right)-2\left(\frac{a}{3}-1\right)+\alpha(1-3)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{9-a}{3}-2\left(\frac{a-3}{3}\right)+0=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 9-a-2 a+6=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 3 a=15 \\ \Rightarrow & a=5\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z=\frac{-2}{1+\sqrt{3} i}$, then the value of $\arg (z)$ is
questions.solution:
Given that, $z=\frac{-2}{1+\sqrt{3} i} \times \frac{1-\sqrt{3} i}{1-\sqrt{3} i}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =-\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=-\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2} \\
\theta & =\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{y}{x}\right)=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 2\right) \\
& =\tan ^{-1}(\sqrt{3})=\frac{\pi}{3}
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \arg (z)=\pi-\theta=\pi-\frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{2 \pi}{3}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The complex number $z$ which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$ lies on
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$ $\therefore \quad i+z=x+i(1+y)$ and $\quad i-z=-x+i(1-y)$ $\because \quad\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$
$\begin{array}{rlrl}&\Rightarrow |i+z| =|i-z| \\ & \Rightarrow x^2+(1+y)^2 =x^2+(1-y)^2 \\ &\Rightarrow x^2+y^2+2 y+1 =x^2+y^2+1-2 y \\ &\Rightarrow 4 y =0 \\ &\Rightarrow y =0\end{array}$ ie, $x$-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z=x+i y, z^{1 / 3}=a-i b$, then $\frac{x}{a}-\frac{y}{b}=k\left(a^2-b^2\right)$, where $k$ is equal to:
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Here, } z^{1 / 3}=a-i b \\ & \Rightarrow z=(a-i b)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow z=a^3-3 a^2(i b)+3 a(i b)^2-(i b)^3 \\ & \Rightarrow x+i y=\left(a^3-3 b^2 a\right)+i\left(-3 a^2 b+b^3\right) \end{aligned}$
Equating real and imaginary parts, we get $x=a\left(a^2-3 b^2\right) \text { and } y=b\left(b^2-3 a^2\right)$
Now, $\frac{a\left(a^2-3 b^2\right)}{a}-\frac{b\left(b^2-3 a^2\right)}{b}=k\left(a^2-b^2\right)$ $\Rightarrow 4 a^2-4 b^2=k\left(a^2-b^2\right) \Rightarrow k=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If complex number $z$ lies in the interior or on the boundary of circle of radius 3 units, then maximum and minimum values of $|z+1|$ are
questions.solution: Here, we have Radius of circle $=3$ units we find the maximum and minimum value of $|z+1|$ for $z$ lying in the interier or boundary of the circle $|z|=3$ For $\operatorname{Max}|z+1|, z$ must lies on the bondary of the circle, then $|z|=3$ and $z=3 e^\theta$, where $\theta$ is the argument of $z$. Now, $|z+1|=\left|3 e^{i \theta}+1\right|$ $\begin{aligned} & =|(3 \cos \theta+1)+(3 i \sin \theta)| \\ & =\sqrt{(3 \cos \theta+1)^2+(3 \sin \theta)^2}\end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\sqrt{9+1+6 \cos \theta} \\ & =\sqrt{10+6 \cos \theta}\end{aligned}$ $\sin \theta, \cos \theta$ ranges from -1 to 1 , the maximum value of $|z+1|$ occur, when $\cos \theta=1$. Therefore, $\operatorname{Max}|z+1|=\sqrt{10+6}=\sqrt{16}=4$ For minimum value The minimum value of $z+1$ occures, when $z$ is at the centre of the circle $|z|=3$. In this case $z=0$. On putting $z=0$ into the expression, we have $\operatorname{Min}(|z+1|)=|0+1|=1$ Hence, maximum value of $|z+1|=4$ minimum value of $|z+1|=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $Z=i \log (2-\sqrt{3})$, then the value of $\cos Z$ will be
questions.solution:
Given, $Z=i \log (2-\sqrt{3})$
$\Rightarrow, \cos Z_4=\cos [i \log (2-\sqrt{3})]$
$=\cosh \{\log (2-\sqrt{3})\}$
$[\because \cos i x=\cosh x$, for $x \in R]$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{e^{\log (2-\sqrt{3})}+e^{-\log (2-\sqrt{3})}}{2} \\ & \quad\left[\because \cosh x=\frac{e^x+e^{-x}}{2}\right]\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{e^{\log (2-\sqrt{3})}+e^{\log \left(\frac{1}{2-\sqrt{3}}\right)}}{2}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{2-\sqrt{3}+\frac{1}{2-\sqrt{3}}}{2} \\ & =\frac{(2-\sqrt{3})^2+1}{2(2-\sqrt{3})}=\frac{4+3-4 \sqrt{3}+1}{2(2-\sqrt{3})} \\ & =\frac{8-4 \sqrt{3}}{2(2-\sqrt{3})}=\frac{4(2-\sqrt{3})}{2(2-\sqrt{3})}=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\omega$ is a cube root of unity, then $\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right)\left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right)$ is
questions.solution:
If $\omega$ is a cube root of unity, then
$\begin{aligned} & 1+\omega+\omega^2=0 \text { and } \omega^3=1 . \\ & \begin{aligned} \therefore\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right) & \left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right) \\ & =\left(-\omega^2-\omega^2\right)(-\omega-\omega) \\ & =2 \omega^2 \cdot 2 \omega=4\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $z_1, z_2, z_3$ be three vertices of an equilateral triangle circumscribing the circle $|z|=\frac{1}{2}$. If $z_1=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $z_1, z_2$, $z_3$ were in anticlockwise sense, then $z_2$ is
questions.solution:
Given that, $|z|=\frac{1}{2}$ and $z_1=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3} i}{2}$
Here, one of the number must be a conjugate of $z_1=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3} i}{2}$ i.e. $z_2=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{\sqrt{3} i}{2}$ and $z_2=z_1 e^{-i 2 \pi / 3}$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad z_2 & =\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{i \sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\left[\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right] \\ & =-2 \times \frac{1}{2}=-1 \\ & {\left[\because(1+i \sqrt{3})\left(\cos \frac{2 \pi}{3}+i \sin \frac{2 \pi}{3}\right)=-2\right] } \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\frac{(3-i)^2}{2+i}=A+i B$, where $A$ and $B$ are real numbers, then $A$ and $B$ are equal to
questions.solution:
Given equation is
$\frac{(3-i)^2}{2+i}=A+i B$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{9-1-6 i}{2+i}=A+i B$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{8-6 i}{2+i}=A+i B$
$\begin{array}{cc}\Rightarrow & \frac{2(4-3 i)}{2+i} \times \frac{2-i}{2-i}=A+i B \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{28-4 i-6 i-3]}{4+1}=A+i B \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2[5-10 i]}{5}=A+i B \\ \Rightarrow & 2-4 i=A+i B\end{array}$
On equating the real and imginary parts from both sides, we get
$A=2$ and $B=-4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The locus of the point $z$ satisfying $\arg \left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=k$, (where $k$ is non-zero) is
options: a straight line making an angle $60^{\circ}$ with the $x$-axis
questions.solution:
We have, arg $\left(\frac{z-1}{z+1}\right)=k,(k \neq 0)$
Put $z=x+i y$, we get $\begin{array}{cc} & \arg \left(\frac{x-1+i y}{x+1+i y}\right)=k \\ \Rightarrow & \arg \left[\frac{(x-1+i y)(x+1-i y)}{(x+1+i y)(x+1-i y)}\right]=k \\ \Rightarrow & \arg \left[\frac{\left(x^2-1\right)+i y(2)+y^2}{(x+1)^2+y^2}\right]=k \\ \Rightarrow & \arg \left[\frac{x^2+y^2-1+2 i y}{(x+1)^2+y^2}\right]=k \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{2 y}{x^2+y^2-1}=k \\ \Rightarrow & {\left[\because \arg (z)=\tan ^{-1} \frac{y}{x}\right]} \\ \Rightarrow & \left(x^2+y^2\right) k-k-2 y=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & k x^2+k y^2-2 y-k=0 \\ \Rightarrow & x^2+y^2-\frac{2 y}{k}-1=0 \end{array}$
Which represent a circle of centre $(0,1 / k)$ i.e. on $y$-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $(\sqrt{3}-i)^{50}=2^{48}(x-i y)$, then $x^2+y^2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\because \quad(\sqrt{3}-i)=2\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{6}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad(\sqrt{3}-i)^{50} & =2^{50}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{6}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{6}\right)^{50} \\ & =2^{50}\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{3}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{3}\right) \\ & =2^{50}\left(\frac{1}{2}-i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)\end{aligned}$
But $(\sqrt{3}-i)^{50}=2^{48}(x-i y)$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & x & =2 \text { and } y=2 \sqrt{3} \\ & \therefore & x^2+y^2 & =4+12=16\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Area of the triangle in the argand diagram formed by the complex numbers $z, i z, z+i z$, where $z=x+i y$ is
questions.solution:
Since, $i z=z e^{i \pi / 2}$
This implies that $i z$ is the vector obtained by rotating vector $z$ in anticlock wise direction through $90^{\circ}$. $\therefore \quad O A \perp A B$ So, area of $\triangle O A B=\frac{1}{2} O A \times O B=\frac{1}{2}|z||i z|=\frac{1}{2}|z|^2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_1, z_2$ and $z_3$ are complex number such that $\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_3\right|=\left|\frac{1}{z_1}+\frac{1}{z_2}+\frac{1}{z_3}\right|=1$ then $\left|z_1+z_2+z_3\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Since, $\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_3\right|=1$
we get $z_1 \bar{z}_1=z_2 \bar{z}_2=z_3 \overline{\bar{z}}_3=1$
Now, $\quad 1=\left|\frac{1}{z_1}+\frac{1}{z_2}+\frac{1}{z_3}\right|$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\left|\bar{z}_1+\bar{z}_2+\bar{z}_3\right|=\left|\overline{z_1+z_2+z_3}\right| \\
& =\left|z_1+z_2+z_3\right|
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\omega$ is a cube root of unity, then $\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right)\left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right)$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\omega$ is an imaginary cube root of 1 , then $\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right)^5+\left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right)^5$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} &\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right)^5+\left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right)^5 \\ &=\left(-\omega^2-\omega^2\right)^5+(-\omega-\omega)^5 \\ &=-32\left(\omega^2\right)^5+(-32) \omega^5 \\ &=-32\left(\omega+\omega^2\right)=32\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $x_n=\cos \frac{\pi}{3^n}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{3^n}$, then $x_1 \cdot x_2 \cdot x_3 \ldots$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have,
$x_n=\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3^n}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3^n}\right)$
$\therefore \mathrm{x}_1 \cdot \mathrm{x}_2 \cdot \mathrm{x}_3 \ldots$
$\begin{aligned} & =\cos \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{3^2}+\ldots\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{\pi}{3}+\frac{\pi}{3^2}+\ldots\right) \\ & =\cos \left(\frac{\pi / 3}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right)+i \sin \left(\frac{\pi / 3}{1-\frac{1}{3}}\right) \\ & =\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{2}=0+i=i\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The complex number $z$ which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|=1$ lies on
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & i+z & =x+i(1+y) \\ & \text { and } & i-z & =-x+i(1-y) \\ & \because & & \left|\frac{i+z}{i-z}\right|\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{cc}
\Rightarrow & x^2+(1+y)^2=x^2+(1-y)^2 \\
\Rightarrow & x^2+y^2+2 y+1=x^2+y^2+1-2 y \\
\Rightarrow & 4 y=0 \\
\Rightarrow & y=0
\end{array}$
i.e. $x$-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $1, \omega, \omega^2, \ldots \omega^{n-1}$ are $n, n^{\text {th }}$ roots of unity, then the value of $(9-\omega) \cdot\left(9-\omega^2\right) \cdot\left(9-\omega^3\right) \ldots\left(9-\omega^{n-1}\right)$ will be
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } x=(1)^{1 / n} \\ & \Rightarrow x^n-1=0 \\ & \text { or } x^n-1=(x-1)(x-\omega)\left(x-\omega^2\right) \ldots\left(x-\omega^{n-1}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{x^n-1}{x-1}=(x-\omega)\left(x-\omega^2\right)\left(x-\omega^3\right) \end{aligned}$
Putting $x=9$ on both sides, we get $(9-\omega)\left(9-\omega^2\right)\left(9-\omega^3\right) \ldots\left(9-\omega^{n-1}\right)=\frac{9^n-1}{8}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a, b, c$ are cube roots of unity, then $\left|\begin{array}{lll}e^a & e^{2 a} & e^{3 a}-1 \\ e^b & e^{2 b} & e^{3 b}-1 \\ e^c & e^{2 c} & e^{3 c}-1\end{array}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \Delta & =\left|\begin{array}{lll}e^a & e^{2 a} & e^{3 a} \\ e^b & e^{2 b} & e^{3 b} \\ e^c & e^{2 c} & e^{3 c}\end{array}\right|-\left|\begin{array}{lll}e^a & e^{2 a} & 1 \\ e^b & e^{2 b} & 1 \\ e^c & e^{2 c} & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & \\ & =e^a \cdot e^b \cdot e^c\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & e^a & e^{2 a} \\ 1 & e^b & e^{2 b} \\ 1 & e^c & e^{2 c}\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}e^a & 1 & e^{2 a} \\ e^b & 1 & e^{2 b} \\ e^c & 1 & e^{2 c}\end{array}\right| \\ & \\ & =e^{(a+b+c)}\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & e^a & e^{2 a} \\ 1 & e^b & e^{2 b} \\ 1 & e^c & e^{2 c}\end{array}\right|-\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & e^a & e^{2 a} \\ 1 & e^b & e^{2 b} \\ 1 & e^c & e^{2 c}\end{array}\right| \\ &\\ &  =\left(e^{a+b+c}-1\right)\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & e^a & e^{2 a} \\ 1 & e^b & e^{2 b} \\ 1 & e^c & e^{2 c}\end{array}\right| \\ & =0\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The complex number $z=x+i y$ which satisfy the equation $\left|\frac{z-5 i}{z+5 i}\right|=1$, lie on
options: a circle passing through the origin
questions.solution:
$\left|\frac{z-5 i}{z+5 i}\right|=1 \Rightarrow|z-5 i|=|z+5 i|$ $\binom{\text { Using definition }\left|z-z_1\right|=\left|z-z_2\right| \text { gives }}{\text { Perpendicular bisector of } z_1 \text { and } z_2 .}$
$\Rightarrow$ Perpendicular bisector of points $(0,5)$ and $(0,-5)$. wheh lies on $y$-axis.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $A+i B \text { form of } \frac{(\cos x+i \sin x)(\cos y+i \sin y)}{(\cot u+i)(1+i \tan v)}$is
questions.solution:
LHS
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{(\cos x+i \sin x)(\cos y+i \sin y)}{(\cos u+i \sin u)(\cos v+i \sin v)} \sin u \cos v \\
& =\sin u \cos v e^{i x} \cdot e^{i y} \cdot e^{-i u} \cdot e^{-i v} \\
& =\sin u \cos v[\cos (x+y-u-v) \\
& \quad+i \sin (x+y-u-v)]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $z_1$ and $z_2$ are two complex numbers such that $\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|+\left|z_1-z_2\right|$, then
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{lc}\because & \left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|+\left|z_1-z_2\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \left|z_1\right|-\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_1-z_2\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \left(\left|z_1\right|-\left|z_2\right|\right)^2=\left|z_1-z_2\right|^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \left|z_1\right|^2+\left|z_2\right|^2-2\left|z_1\right|\left|z_2\right|=\left|z_1\right|^2+\left|z_2\right|^2 \\ -2\left|z_1\right|\left|z_2\right| \cos \left(\theta_1-\theta_2\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \cos \left(\theta_1-\theta_2\right)=1 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad \theta_1-\theta_2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \arg \left(z_1\right)-\arg \left(z_2\right)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{z_1}{z_2} \text { is purely real. } \\ \Rightarrow & \operatorname{lm}\left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right)=0\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
Given that,
$\begin{gathered}
1+\omega+\omega^2=0 \text { and } \omega^3=1 \\
\therefore 2(1+\omega)\left(1+\omega^2\right)+3(2 \omega+1)\left(2 \omega^2+1\right) \\
+\ldots+(n+1)(n \omega+1)\left(n \omega^2+1\right) \\
=2(1)+3(3)+4(7)+\ldots+\left(n^3+1\right)
\end{gathered}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\text {Let } T_n=n^3+1 \\ \Rightarrow \Sigma T_n=\left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2}\right]^2+n\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The equation $|z+i|-|z-i|=k$ represent a hyperbola, if
questions.solution:
Let $z=x+i y$, then $|z+i|-|z-i|=k$ becomes $\therefore  \sqrt{x^2+(y+1)^2}-\sqrt{x^2+(y-1)^2}=k...(i)$
or $x^2+(y+1)^2-x^2-(y-1)^2$
$=k\left\{\sqrt{x^2+(y+1)^2}+\sqrt{x^2+(y-1)^2}\right\}$
$\therefore \sqrt{x^2+(y-1)^2}+\sqrt{x^2+(y+1)^2}=\frac{4 y}{k}...(ii)$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii),
$2 \sqrt{x^2+(y+1)^2}=k+\frac{4 y}{k}$
$\Rightarrow 4 x^2+4 y^2+8 y+4=k^2+\frac{16 y^2}{k^2}+8 y$
$\Rightarrow  4 x^2+\left(4-\frac{16}{k^2}\right) y^2=k^2-4$
For an hyperbola, $\frac{4 k^2-16}{k^2} \lt 0 \Rightarrow k^2-4 \lt 0$
$\Rightarrow |k| \lt 2$ or $-2 \lt k \lt 2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $n_1, n_2$ are positive integers, then $(1+i)^{n_1}+\left(1+i^3\right)^{n_1}+\left(1+i^5\right)^{n_2}+\left(1+i^7\right)^{n_2}$ is a real number if and only if
options: $n_1, n_2$ are any two negative integers
$n_1, n_2$ are both any positive integers
questions.solution:
Expression can be written as $=(1+i)^{n_1}+(1-i)^{n_1}+(1+i)^{n_2}+(1-i)^{n_2}$
$=2^{n_1 / 2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n_1}+2^{n_1 / 2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n_1}+2^{n_2 / 2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n_2}+2^{n_2 / 2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-i \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n_2}$
$=2^{n_1 / 2}\left\{\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{n_1}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{n_1}\right\}+2^{n_2 / 2}\left\{\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{n_2}+\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{4}-i \sin \frac{\pi}{4}\right)^{n_2}\right\}$
$+2^{n_2 / 2}\left\{\cos \frac{n_2 \pi}{4}+i \sin \frac{n_2 \pi}{4}\right.\left.+\cos \frac{n_2 \pi}{4}-\sin \frac{n_2 \pi}{4}\right\}$
$=2^{n_1 / 2} \cdot 2 \cos \frac{n_1 \pi}{4}+2^{n_2 / 2} \cdot 2 \cos \frac{n_2 \pi}{4}=\mathrm{real}$
ie, $n_1, n_2$ are any two positive integers.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $1, \omega$ and $\omega^2$ are the cube roots of unity, then the value of $\left(1-\omega+\omega^2\right)\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}(1-\omega & \left.+\omega^2\right)\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right) \\ & =\left(1+\omega^2-\omega\right)\left(1+\omega-\omega^2\right) \\ & =(-\omega-\omega)\left(-\omega^2-\omega^2\right)\left(\because 1+\omega+\omega^2=0\right) \\ & =(-2 \omega)\left(-2 \omega^2\right)\end{aligned}$
$=4\left(\omega^3\right)=4 \times 1 \quad\left(\because \omega^3=1\right)$
$=4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|z+\frac{2}{z}\right|=2$, then the minimum value of $|z|$ is
questions.solution:
We can write $\begin{aligned} & \quad|z|=\left|z+\frac{2}{|z|}-\frac{2}{|z|}\right| \leq\left|z+\frac{2}{z}\right|+\left|\frac{2}{|z|}\right| \\ & \quad=2+\frac{2}{|z|} \\ & \therefore \quad|z|^2-2|z|-2 \leq 0 \end{aligned}$
Here, $|z|$ lies between $\frac{2 \pm \sqrt{12}}{2}=1 \pm \sqrt{3}$ $\therefore$ Minimum value of $|z|=1-\sqrt{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $w$ is an imaginary cube root of unity, then the value of $(1+\omega)\left(1+\omega^2\right)\left(1+\omega^3\right)\left(1+\omega^4\right) \ldots\left(1+\omega^{3 n}\right)$
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{aligned} & (1+\omega)\left(1+\omega^2\right)\left(1+\omega^3\right)\left(1+\omega^4\right) \ldots\left(1+\omega^{3 n}\right) \\ & =\left[\left\{(1+\omega)\left(1+\omega^2\right)\right\}\left\{\left(1+\omega^4\right)\left(1+\omega^5\right)\right\} \ldots\right. \\ & \left\{\left(1+\omega^{3 n-2}\right)\left(1+\omega^{3 n-1}\right)\right\} \times\left(1+\omega^3\right)\left(1+\omega^6\right) \\ & \left.\left(1+\omega^9\right) \ldots\left(1+\omega^{3 n}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =2^n\left[\left\{\left(-\omega^2\right)(-\omega)\right\}\left\{\left(-\omega^2\right)(-\infty)\right] \ldots\left\{\left(-\omega^2\right)(-\infty)\right\}\right] \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left[(1+w)\left(1+w^2\right)\left\{(1+w)\left(1+w^2\right)\right\}\left\{(1+w)\left(1+w^2\right)\right\}\right. \\ & \left.\quad \quad \ldots\left\{(1+w)\left(1+w^2\right)\right\}\right] \times(1+1)(1+1) \ldots(1+1) \\ & =2^n\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\left|\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right|=1$ and $\arg \left(z_1 z_2\right)=0$, then
questions.solution:
Let $z_1=\gamma_1\left(\cos \theta_1+i \sin \theta_1\right)$. Given that, $\left|\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right|=1$ $\Rightarrow\left|q_1\right|=\left|z_2\right| \Rightarrow\left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right|=\gamma_1$
Now, $\arg \left(z_1 z_2\right)=0 \Rightarrow \arg \left(z_1\right)+\arg \left(z_2\right)=0$ $\Rightarrow \quad \arg \left(z_2\right)=-\theta_1$
Therefore, $z_2=\gamma_1\left(\cos \left(-\theta_1\right)+i \sin \left(-\theta_1\right)\right.$ $\begin{array}{cc} & =\gamma_1\left(\cos \theta_1-i \sin \theta_1\right)=\bar{z}_1 \\ \Rightarrow & \bar{z}_2=\left(\bar{z}_1\right)=z_1 \Rightarrow z_2 \bar{z}_2=z_1 z_2 \\ \Rightarrow & \left|z_2\right|^2=z_1 z_2 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $Q$ is real and $z_1, z_2$ are connected by $z_1^2+z_2^2+2 z_1 z_2 \cos \theta=0$, then triangle with vertices $0, z_1$ and $z_2$ is
questions.solution:
$z_1^2+z_2^2+2 z_1 z_2 \cos \theta=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right)^2+2\left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right) \cos \theta+1=0$
$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{z_1}{z_2}+\cos \theta\right)^2=-\left(1-\cos ^2 \theta\right)=-\sin ^2 \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{z_1}{z_2}=-\cos \theta \pm i \sin \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|\frac{z_1}{z_2}\right|=\sqrt{(-\cos \theta)^2+(\sin \theta)^2}=1$
$\begin{array}{lrl}\Rightarrow & \left|z_1\right|=\left|z_2\right| \\ \Rightarrow & \left|z_1-0\right|=\left|z_2-0\right|\end{array}$
Thus, triangle with vertices $0, z_1, z_2$ is iscosceles.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma=\sin \alpha+\sin \beta+\sin \gamma=0$, then the value of $\cos 3 \alpha+\cos 3 \beta+\cos 3 \gamma$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } a=\cos \alpha+i \sin \alpha \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} b & =\cos \beta+i \sin \beta \\ c & =\cos \gamma+i \sin \gamma\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
then; $a+b+c=(\cos \alpha+\cos \beta+\cos \gamma)+$
$i(\sin \alpha+\sin \beta+\sin \gamma)$
$=0+i 0=0$
$\Rightarrow a^3+b^3+c^3=3 a b c$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow(\cos 3 \alpha+i \sin 3 \alpha)+(\cos 3 \beta \\ &+i \sin 3 \beta) \\ &+(\cos 3 \gamma+i \sin 3 \gamma)\end{aligned}$
$=3[\cos (\alpha+\beta+\gamma)+i \sin (\alpha+\beta+\gamma)]$
$\Rightarrow \cos 3 \alpha+\cos 3 \beta+\cos 3 \gamma=3 \cos (\alpha+\beta+\gamma)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathbf{i} z^4+1=0$, then $z$ can take the value
questions.solution:
$i z^4=-1$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & z^4=-\frac{1}{i} \\ \Rightarrow & z^4=i \\ \Rightarrow & z=(i)^{1 / 4}=(0+i)^{1 / 4} \\ \Rightarrow & z=\left(\cos \frac{\pi}{2}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{2}\right)^{1 / 4} \\ \Rightarrow & z=\cos \frac{\pi}{8}+i \sin \frac{\pi}{8}\end{array}$
(using De Moirrels theorem)

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1-b x)}{x}$ for $x \neq 0$ and $f(0)=k$ and $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$, then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1-b x)}{x}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \quad f(x) \text { is continuous at } x=k \text { and } f(0)=k . \\
& \therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1-b x)}{x} \\
& \quad \quad\left(\frac{0}{0} \text { form }\right) \\
& \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\left(\frac{1}{1+a x} \cdot a+\frac{b}{1-b x}\right)=a+b \\
& \therefore \quad a+b=f(0)=k
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If the function
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}(\cos x)^{1 / x}, & x \neq 0 \\ k, & x=0\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=0$, then the value of $k$ is
questions.solution:
For $f(x)$ to be continuous at $x=0$, we must have
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x)=f(0)$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}(\cos x)^{1 / x}=k$
$\Rightarrow \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0}\{1+(\cos x-1)\}^{1 / x}=k$
$\Rightarrow \quad e^{\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\cos x-1}{x}}=k$
$\Rightarrow \quad e^{-\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2 \sin ^2 x / 2}{x}}=k$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & e^0=k \\ \therefore & k=1\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}3 x^2+12 x-1, & -1 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 37-x, & 2 \leq x \leq 3\end{array}\right.$, then
questions.solution:
Given, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{r}3 x^2+12 x-1,-1 \leq x \leq 2 \\ 37-x, 2 \leq x \leq 3\end{array}\right.$
On differentiating, we get
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& f^{\prime}(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
6 x+12, & -1 \leq x \leq 2 \\
-1, & 2 \leq x \leq 3
\end{array}\right. \\
\Rightarrow  & f^{\prime}(x)\gt0 \text { for }-1 \leq x \leq 2
\end{array}$
$\therefore f(x)$ is increasing in $[-1,2]$
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{+}} f^{\prime}(x)=-1 \text { and } \lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f^{\prime}(x)=24$
$\therefore f^{\prime}(2)$ does not exist.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The number of points, where $f(x)=[\sin x+\cos x]$ (where [.] denotes the greatest integer function) and $x \in(0,2 \pi)$ is not continuous, is
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} f(x) & =[\sin x+\cos x] \\ & =\left[\sqrt{2}\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin x+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos x\right)\right] \\ & =\left[\sqrt{2} \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
We know that, greatest integer function is discontinuous on integer values.
Function $\sqrt{2} \sin \left(x+\frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ will gives integer values at
$x=90^{\circ}, 135^{\circ}, 180^{\circ}, 270^{\circ}, 315^{\circ}$,
Hence, there are five points in the given interval, in which $f(x)$ is not continuous.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|f(x)|$ is continuous at $x=a$, then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$|f(x)|$ is continuous at $x=a, f(x)$ is not be continuous at $x=a$.
$\therefore \quad|f(x)|=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
f(x), & x \gt a \\
-f(x), & x \lt a
\end{array}\right.$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function and its derivative are same for
questions.solution:
Let $y=e^x$
$\frac{d}{d x}(y)=\frac{d}{d x}\left(e^x\right) \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=e^x$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=[x] \cos \left[\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right] \pi$ where [] denotes the greatest integer function, is discontinuous at
questions.solution:
We have $f(x)=[x] \cos \left[\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right] \pi$
Since, $g(x)=[x]$ is always discontinuous at all integral values of points. Hence, $f(x)$ is discontinuous for all integral points.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If [] denotes the greatest integer function, then $f(x)=[x]+\left[x+\frac{1}{2}\right]$
questions.solution:
We have,
$f(x)=[x]+\left[x+\frac{1}{2}\right]= \begin{cases}0, & \text { if } 0 \lt x \lt \frac{1}{2} \\ 1, & \text { if } x=\frac{1}{2} \\ 1, & \text { if } \frac{1}{2} \lt x \lt 1\end{cases}$
Clearly, $f(x)$ is discontinuous at $x=\frac{1}{2}$.
Also, $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1 / 2^{-}} f(x)=0$ and $\lim _{x \rightarrow 1 / 2^{+}} f(x)=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
$f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{\sin ^3(\sqrt{3}) \cdot \log (1+3 x)}{\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right)^2\left(e^{5 \sqrt{x}}-1\right) x}, & x \neq 0 \\ a, & x=0\end{cases}$
is continuous in $[0,1]$, if ' $a$ ' equals to
questions.solution:
We have, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc} \frac{\sin ^3(\sqrt{x}) \cdot \log (1+3 x)}{x \cdot\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right)^2 \cdot\left(e^{5 \sqrt{x}}-1\right)}, & x \neq 0 \\ a, & x=0 \end{array}\right.$
For continuity in $[0,1], f(0)=f\left(0^{+}\right)$, otherwise it is discontinuous. $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\sin ^3(\sqrt{x}) \cdot \log (1+3 x)}{x \cdot\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right)^2 \cdot\left(e^{5 \sqrt{x}}-1\right)} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}}\left[\frac{3}{5} \cdot \frac{\sin ^3 \sqrt{x}}{(\sqrt{x})^3} \cdot \frac{(\sqrt{x})^2}{\left(\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}\right)^2}\right. \\ & \left.x \frac{\log (1+3 x)}{3 x} \cdot \frac{5 \sqrt{x}}{e^{5 \sqrt{x}}-1}\right] \\ & =\frac{3}{5} \lim _{x \rightarrow 0^{+}} \frac{\sin ^3 \sqrt{x}}{(\sqrt{x})^3} \cdot \frac{(\sqrt{x})^2}{\tan ^{-1} \sqrt{x}} \times \frac{\log (1+3 x)}{3 x} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{5 \sqrt{x}}{e^{5 \sqrt{x}}-1}=a \end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow \quad a=\frac{3}{5}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\frac{e^{1 / x}-1}{e^{1 / x}+1}, x \neq 0$ and $f(0)=0$, then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$\because f^{\prime}\left(0^{-}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0-h)$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}\left(0^{-}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{-1 / h}-1}{e^{1 /-h}+1}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & f^{\prime}\left(0^{-}\right)=\frac{0-1}{0+1} \\ \therefore & f^{\prime}\left(0^{-}\right)=-1\end{array}$
and $\quad f^{\prime}\left(0^{+}\right)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(0+h)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{e^{1 / h}-1}{e^{1 / h}+1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad f^{\prime}\left(0^{+}\right)=\frac{1-0}{1+0}=1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)= \begin{cases}\frac{\sin (\cos x)-\cos x}{(\pi-2 x)^3}, & x \neq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ k, & x=\frac{\pi}{2}\end{cases}$ is a continuous at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$, then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
It is given that $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=\frac{\pi}{2}$.
$\therefore \quad k=\lim _{x \rightarrow \pi / 2} f(x)$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad k=\lim _{x \rightarrow \pi / 2} \frac{\sin (\cos x)-\cos x}{(\pi-2 x)^3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad k=\lim _{x \rightarrow \pi / 2} \frac{\sin (\cos x)-\cos x}{\cos ^3 x} \times \frac{\sin ^3\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)}{8\left(\frac{\pi}{2}-x\right)^3} \\ & \therefore \quad k=-\frac{1}{6} \times \frac{1}{8}=-\frac{1}{48}\left[\because \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\sin x-x}{x^3}=-\frac{1}{6}\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Function $f(x)$ is defined as follows
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}a x-b, & x \leq 1 \\ 3 x, & 1 \lt x \lt 2 \\ b x^2-a, & x \geq 2\end{array}\right.$
If $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=1$, but discontinuous at $x=2$ then the locus of the point $(a, b)$ is a straight line excluding the point where it cuts the line
questions.solution:
Given; $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=1$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore f(1)=\text { RHL } \\ & \Rightarrow f(1)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 1}+f(x) \Rightarrow f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow a-b=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 3(1+h) \Rightarrow a-b=3$   ...(i)
Again, given $f(x)$ is discontinuous at $x=2$.
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathrm{LHL} \neq f(x) \\ & \Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 2^{-}} f(x) \neq f(2) \Rightarrow \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(2-h) \neq f(2)\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 3(2-h) \neq 4 b-a \Rightarrow 6 \neq 4 b-a$    ....(ii)
Assume, $6=4 b-a$ 
then from (i) and (ii), we get $b=3$
 $\therefore \quad$ locus $\quad y=3$
Which is impossile $(\because 6 \neq 4 b-a)$
Hence, locus of $(a, b)$ is $x-y=3$ excluding the point when it cuts the line $y=3$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have; $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{x^2-x}{x^2-x}=1, & \text { if } x \lt 0 \text { or } x\gt1 \\ -\frac{\left(x^2-x\right)}{\left(x^2-x\right)}=-1, & \text { if } 0 \lt x \lt 1 \\ 1, & \text { if } x=0 \\ -1, & \text { if } x=1\end{array}\right.$ $=\left\{\begin{array}{ccc}1, & \text { if } & x \leq 0 \text { or } x\gt1 \\ -1, & \text { if } & 0 \lt x \leq 1\end{array}\right.$
So, $f(x)$ is not continuous at $x=0$. It can be easily seen that it is not continuous at $x=1$ also.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\min \left\{1, x^2, x^3\right\}$, then
options: $f(x)$ is not differentiable at two points
$f(x)$ is not differentiable at one point
questions.solution: It is evident from the graph of $f(x)$ that $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}1, & x \geq 1 \\ x^3, & x \lt 1\end{array}\right.$ Clearly, $f(x)$ is everywhere continuous but it is not differentiable at $x=1$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\left(x^2-1\right)\left|x^2-3 x+2\right|+\cos |x|$ is non-differentiable at
questions.solution:
we know that function $|x|$ is not differentiable at $\begin{aligned} & x=0 \\ & \therefore\left|x^2-3 x+2\right|=|(x-1)(x-2)| \end{aligned}$
Hence, it is not differentiable at $x=1$ and 2 Now, $f(x)=\left(x^2-1\right)\left|x^2-3 x+2\right|+\cos |x|$ is not differentiable at $x=2$. For $1 \lt x \lt 2, f(x)=-\left(x^2-1\right)\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)$ $+\cos x$
For $2 \lt x \lt 3, f(x)=\left(x^2-1\right)\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)+\cos x$ $L f^{\prime}(x)=-\left(x^2-1\right)(2 x-3)-2 x\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)$ $-\sin x$ $L^{\prime}(2)=-3 \sin 2$ $\begin{aligned} & R f^{\prime}(x)=\left(x^2-1\right)(2 x-3)+2 x\left(x^2-3 x+2\right)-\sin x \\ & R^{\prime}(2)=(4-1)(4-3)+0-\sin 2=3-\sin 2 \end{aligned}$
Hence, $L^{\prime}(2) \neq R f^{\prime}(2)$. So, $f(x)$ is not differentiable at $x=2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{|x+2|}{\tan ^{-1}(x+2)}, & x \neq-2 \\ 2 & x=-2\end{array}\right.$, then $f(x)$ is
questions.solution:
$f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}\frac{|x+2|}{\tan ^{-1}(x+2)}, & x \neq-2 \\ 2, & x=-2\end{array}\right.$
$\begin{array}{r}\therefore \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(-2-h) \\ \quad=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{|-2-h+2|}{\tan ^{-1}(-2-h+2)}\end{array}$
$=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{-h}{\tan ^{-1} h}=-1$
and $\lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(-2+h)$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}\left[\frac{|-2+h+2|}{\tan ^{-1}(-2+h+2)}\right] \\
& =\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} \frac{h}{\tan ^{-1} h}=1 \\
\because \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{-}} f(x) \neq & \lim _{x \rightarrow-2^{+}} f(x)
\end{aligned}$
So, $f(x)$ is not continuous as well as not differentiable at $x=-2$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=\frac{\ln (1+a x)-\ln (1-b x)}{x}$ is not defined at $x=0$. The value which should be assigned to $f$ at $x=0$ so that it is continuous at $x=0$, is
questions.solution:
For $f(x)$ to be continuous, we must have
$\begin{aligned}
& f(0)=\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x) \\
\therefore \quad & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log (1+a x)-\log (1-b x)}{x} \\
= & \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{a \log (1+a x)}{a x}+\frac{b \log (1-b x)}{-b x} \\
= & a \cdot 1+b \cdot 1 \quad\left[\text { using, } \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\log (1+x)}{x}=1\right] \\
= & a+b \\
\therefore & f(0)=a+b
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $f(0)$, so that the function $f(x)=\frac{2-(256-7 x)^{1 / 8}}{(5 x+3 x)^{1 / 5}-2}, x \neq 0$ is continuous everywhere is given by
questions.solution:
For $f(x)$ to be continuous everywhere, we must have
$\begin{aligned} f(0) & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x) \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{2-(256-7 x)^{1 / 8}}{(5 x+32)^{1 / 5}-2} \\ & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{\frac{7}{8}(256-7 x)^{-7 / 8}}{(5 x+32)^{-4 / 5}} \\ & =\frac{7}{8} \times \frac{2^{-7}}{2^{-4}}=\frac{7}{64}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The set of points where the function $f(x)=x|x|$ is differentiable, is
questions.solution:
Given that, $f(x)=x|x|$ $\therefore \quad f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x^2 & , x \geq 0 \\ -x^2 & , x \lt 0\end{array}\right.$
It is clear from the graph that $f(x)$ is differentiable everywhere.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}a x^2+b, & 0 \leq x \lt 1 \\ x+3, & 1 \lt x \leq 2 \\ 4, & x=1\end{array}\right.$, then the value of $(a, b)$ for which $f(x)$ cannot be continuous at $x=1$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(1-h)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0} a(1-h)^2+b=a+b$
$\Rightarrow \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} f(1+h)=\lim _{h \rightarrow 0}(1+h)+3=4$
and $f(1)=4$
$\therefore f(x)$ will not be continuous at $x=1$, if $a+b \neq 4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x+1, & x \leq 1 \\ 3-a x^2, & x\gt1\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=1$, then the value of $a$ is
questions.solution:
Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=1$.
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & \lim _{h \rightarrow 0^{+}} f(1+h)=f(1) \\
\Rightarrow & \lim _{h \rightarrow 0} 3-a(1+h)^2=2
\end{array}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & 3-a(1+0)^2 & =2 \\ \Rightarrow & a & =1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 2 x-1, & 1 \lt x\end{array}\right.$, then
options: $f$ is continuous but not differentiable at $x=1$
questions.solution:
Given that, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}x, & 0 \leq x \leq 1 \\ 2 x-1, & 1 \lt x\end{array}\right.$
From graph $f(x)$ is continuous, $\forall x \in R$. i.e. $f^{\prime}(x)=1$ when $0 \leq x \leq 1$ and $f^{\prime}(x)=2-0$ when $x\gt1$. $\therefore$ Left hand limit $\neq$ Right hand limit $\therefore$ At $x=1, f(x)$ is continuous but not differentiable.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{ll}\frac{x^2-9}{x-3}, & \text { if } x \neq 3 \\ 2 x+k, & \text { otherwise }\end{array}\right.$ is continuous at $x=3$, then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=3$, then
$\lim _{x \rightarrow 3} f(x)=f(3)$...(i)
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \lim _{x \rightarrow 3} \frac{x^2-9}{x-3}=\lim _{x \rightarrow 3} x+3=3+3=6 \\
& \text { and } \quad f(3)=2 \times 3+k=k+6
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ From Eq. (i),
$6=k+6 \Rightarrow k=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $f(x)=\frac{3 x+\tan ^2 x}{x}$ is continuous at $x=0$, then $f(0)$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Now,
$\begin{aligned}
\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} f(x) & =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{3 x+\tan ^2 x}{x} \\
& =\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{3+2 \tan x \sec ^2 x}{1} \\
& =3
\end{aligned}$
Since, $f(x)$ is continuous at $x=0$
$\therefore f(0)=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The function $f(x)=[x] \cos \left[\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right] \pi$, where $[\cdot]$ denotes the greatest integer function, is discontinuous at
questions.solution:
We have $f(x)=[x] \cos \left[\frac{2 x-1}{2}\right] \pi$
Since, $g(x)=[x]$ is always discontinuous at all integral values of points. Hence, $f(x)$ is discontinuous for all integral points.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $f(x)=x^p \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$, when $x \neq 0$ and $f(x)=0$, when $x=0$. Then $f(x)$ will be differentiable at $x=0$, if
questions.solution:
Given that, $f(x)=\left\{\begin{aligned} x^p \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right), & x \neq 0 \\ 0, & x=0\end{aligned}\right.$
$f(x)$ will be differentiable at $x=0$, if $\lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x-0}$ exists finitely.
$\Rightarrow \quad \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} \frac{x^p \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)}{x}$ exists finitely
$\Rightarrow \lim _{x \rightarrow 0} x^{p-1} \cos \left(\frac{1}{x}\right)$ exists finitely
$\Rightarrow \quad p-1\gt0 \Rightarrow p\gt1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^1 x \sin \pi x d x$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{rl}\int_0^1 & x \sin \pi x d x \\ \quad & =\left[-x \frac{\cos \pi x}{\pi}\right]_0^1+\int_0^1 \frac{\cos \pi x}{\pi} d x \\ \quad=\left[-x \frac{\cos \pi x}{\pi}+\frac{\sin \pi x}{\pi^2}\right]_0^1=\left[\frac{-(-1)}{\pi}\right]=\frac{1}{\pi}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_0^{\sqrt{\ln (\pi / 2)}} \cos \left(e^{x^2}\right) 2 x e^{x^2} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $I=\int_0^{\sqrt{\ln (\pi / 2)}} \cos \left(e^{x^2}\right) \cdot 2 x e^{x^2} d x$
Put $\quad e^{x^2}=t \Rightarrow 2 x e^{x^2} d x=d t$
Now, lower limit, $t=1$
Upper limit, $t=e^{\ln \pi / 2}=\frac{\pi}{2}$
$\therefore \quad I=\int_1^{\pi / 2} \cos (t) d t=[\sin t]_1^{\pi / 2}$
$=\sin \left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)-\sin 1$
$\Rightarrow \quad I=1-\sin 1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^1 \frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)\right] d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_0^1 \frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x}{1+x^2}\right)\right] d x$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore  I & =\int_0^1 \frac{d}{d x}\left[\sin ^{-1}(\sin 2 \theta)\right] d x \quad(\text { put } x=\tan \theta) \\
& =2 \int_0^1 \frac{d}{d x}\left[\tan ^{-1} x\right] d x \\
& =2\left[\tan ^{-1}(1)-\tan ^{-1}(0)\right] \\
& =2 \cdot \frac{\pi}{4}=\frac{\pi}{2}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: By Simpson's $\frac{1}{3}$ rd rule, the approximate value of the integral $\int_1^2 e^{-x / 2} d x$ using four intervals, is
questions.solution:
Given integral is $\int_1^2 e^{-x / 2} d x$
On dividing interval $[1,2]$ in four parts, we have
$h=\frac{2-1}{4}=\frac{1}{4}$
Now, value of $f(x)=e^{-x / 2}$ is given below
$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c}\hline x & 1 & \frac{5}{4} & \frac{3}{2} & \frac{7}{4} & e \\\hline f(x) & 0.6065 & 0.5352 & 0.4724 & 0.4168 & 0.3679 \\\hline\end{array}$
Now, Simpson's $\frac{1}{3}$ rule is
$\begin{aligned} \int_{x_0}^{x_0+n h} f(x) d x= & \frac{h}{3} \\ & {\left[\left(y_0+y_n\right)+4\left(y_1+y_3+\ldots+y_{n-1}\right)\right.} \\ & \left.+2\left(y_2+y_4+\ldots+y_{n-2}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \int_1^2 e^{-x / 2} d x= & \frac{1}{12}[(0.6065+0.3679) \\ & +4(0.5352+0.4168)+2(0.4724)]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{12}[0.9744+3.808+0.9448] \\ & =\frac{1}{12} \times 5.7272=0.477\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^1 x(1-x)^{12} d x$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The approximate value of $\int_1^5 x^2 d x$ using trapezoidal rule with $n=4$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { } \int_1^5 x^2 d x \\ & =\frac{4}{4}\left[\frac{1}{2} f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4)+\frac{1}{2} f(5)\right] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}\left[x^2 F\left(x^2\right)-\int F\left(x^2\right) d\left(x^2\right)\right] \\ & =\left[\frac{1}{2}+4+9+16+\frac{25}{2}\right]=42\left[u \operatorname{sing} f(x)=x^2\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \frac{\sin ^n \theta}{\sin ^n \theta+\cos ^n \theta} d \theta$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^n \theta}{\sin ^n \theta+\cos ^n \theta} d \theta....(i) \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^n(\pi / 2-\theta)}{\sin ^n(\pi / 2-\theta)+\cos ^n(\pi / 2-\theta)} d \theta \\ & =\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\cos ^n \theta}{\cos ^n \theta+\sin ^n \theta} d \theta....(ii) \end{aligned}$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $2 I=\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin ^n \theta+\cos ^n \theta}{\cos ^n \theta+\sin ^n \theta} d \theta=\int_0^{\pi / 2} d \theta=\frac{\pi}{2} \Rightarrow I=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_1^e \frac{\log x}{x} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_1^e \frac{\log x}{x} d x$
Put $\quad \log x=t \Rightarrow \frac{1}{x} d x=d t$
$\therefore \quad I=\int_0^1 t d t=\left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_0^1=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^1 \sin ^{-1} x d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Let } I=\int_0^1 \sin ^{-1} x d x \\ & \qquad=\left[x \sin ^{-1} x\right]_0^1-\int_0^1 \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x\end{aligned}$
Put $1-x^2=t^2 \Rightarrow-2 x d x=2 t d t$
$\therefore \quad I=\left[\frac{\pi}{2}-0\right]+\int_1^0 \frac{t d t}{t}$
$=\frac{\pi}{2}+\int_1^0 d t=\frac{\pi}{2}+[t]_1^0=\frac{\pi}{2}-1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^1 x(1-x)^{12} d x$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \frac{d x}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{1 / 2}}$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_{-1 / 2}^{1 / 2} \frac{d x}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{1 / 2}}$
Again, let $f(x)=\frac{1}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{1 / 2}}$
$\begin{gathered}
f(-x)=\frac{1}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{1 / 2}} = f(x) \\
\therefore  I=2 \int_0^{1 / 2} \frac{d x}{\left(1-x^2\right)^{1 / 2}}=\left[2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{x}{1}\right]_0^{1 / 2} \\
\end{gathered}$
$=2 \sin ^{-1} \frac{1}{2}=2 \times \frac{\pi}{6}=\frac{\pi}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}|\sin x| d x$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2}|\sin x| & d x=2 \int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin x d x \\ & =2[-\cos x]_0^{\pi / 2}=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{\sqrt{\ln 2}}^{\sqrt{\ln 3}} \frac{x \sin x^2}{\sin x^2+\sin \left(\ln 6-x^2\right)} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\quad I=\int_{\sqrt{\ln 2}}^{\sqrt{\ln 3}} \frac{x \sin x^2}{\sin x^2+\sin \left(\ln 6-x^2\right)} d x$
Putting $x^2=t$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 x d x=d t$
$\therefore \quad I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{\ln 2}^{\ln 3} \frac{\sin t}{\sin t+\sin (\ln 6-t)} d t$   ...(i)
Using $\int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x$, we get
$I=\frac{1}{2} \int_{\ln 2}^{\ln 3} \frac{\sin (\ln 6-t)}{\sin (\ln 6-t)+\sin t} d t$   ...(ii)
On adding Eqs.(i) and (ii), we get
$2 I=\int_{\ln 2}^{\ln 3} 1 d t=[t]_{\ln 2}^{\ln 3}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow \quad & 2 I=\frac{1}{2}(\ln 3-\ln 2)=\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(\frac{3}{2}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & I=\frac{1}{4} \ln \left(\frac{3}{2}\right)\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{2 a} \frac{f(x)}{f(x)+f(2 a-x)} d x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_0^{2 a} \frac{f(x)}{f(x)+f(2 a-x)} d x...(i)$
$I=\int_0^{2 a} \frac{f(2 a-x)}{f(2 a-x)+f(x)} d x...(ii)$
On adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\begin{aligned}
2 I & =\int_0^{2 a} \frac{f(2 a-x)+f(x)}{f(2 a-x)+f(x)} d x=\int_0^{2 a} d x=2 a \\
\Rightarrow I & =a
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int \frac{\left(2 x^{12}+5 x^9\right)}{\left(1+x^3+x^5\right)^3} d x$ equals
questions.solution:
Let
$I=\int \frac{2 x^{12}+5 x^9}{\left(1+x^3+x^5\right)^3} d x=\int \frac{5 x^{-6}+2 x^{-3}}{\left(1+x^{-2}+x^{-5}\right)^3} d x$
Put $1+x^{-2}+x^{-5}=t,\left(-2 x^{-3}-5 x^{-6}\right) d x=d t$
$\therefore \quad I=-\int \frac{d t}{t^3}=\frac{1}{2 t^2}=\frac{1}{2\left(1+x^{-2}+x^{-5}\right)^2}$
$=\frac{x^{10}}{2\left(x^5+x^3+1\right)^2}+C$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\frac{3 \pi}{2}} \sin \left[\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right] \cdot d x$, where $[\cdot]$ denotes the greatest integer function, is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \int_0^{\frac{3 \pi}{2}} \sin \left(\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right) d x=\int_0^{\frac{\pi}{2}} \sin \left(\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right) d x+\int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi \sin \left(\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right) d x \\ & \quad+\int_\pi^{\frac{3 \pi}{2}} \sin \left(\frac{2 x}{\pi}\right) d x \\ & =0+\sin 1 \int_{\frac{\pi}{2}}^\pi d x+\sin 2 \int_\pi^{\frac{3 \pi}{2}} d x \\ & \end{aligned}$
$=\frac{\pi}{2}(\sin 1+\sin 2)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_0^\pi\left|\sin ^3 \theta\right| d \theta$ is
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_0^\pi \sin ^3 \theta d \theta$  $[\because \sin \theta\gt0$ for $0 \lt \theta \lt \pi]$
$=\int_0^\pi \sin \theta\left(1-\cos ^2 \theta\right) d \theta$
Put $\quad \cos \theta=t$ 
$\Rightarrow \quad-\sin \theta d \theta=d t$
$\therefore \quad I=\int_{-1}^1\left(1-t^2\right) d t=\left[t-\frac{t^3}{3}\right]_{-1}^1$
$=2-\frac{2}{3}=\frac{4}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_{1 / n}^{(a n-1) / n} \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{a-x}+\sqrt{x}} d x$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
The given limit
$\begin{aligned} & L=\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty}\left\{\frac{1}{n a}+\frac{1}{n a+1}+\frac{1}{n a+2}+\ldots+\right. \\ &\left.\qquad \frac{1}{n a+n(b-a)}\right\}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{r=0}^{n(b-a)} \frac{1}{n a+r} \\ & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1^{(b-a) n}}{\sum_{r=0}^{(b-a} \frac{1}{a+r / n}} \\ & =\int_0^{(b-a)} \frac{d x}{a+x}=[\log (a+x)]_0^{b-a} \quad\left(\frac{r}{n}=x\right) \\ & =\log b-\log a \\ & =\log \left(\frac{b}{a}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_0^{\pi / 2} \frac{\sin x-\cos x}{1+\sin x \cdot \cos x} d x$ is equal to

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_a^b \frac{|x|}{x} d x$ is
questions.solution:
Case I. If $0 \leq a \lt b$, then $\frac{|x|}{x}=1$
$\therefore \quad I=\int_a^b 1 \cdot d x=b-a=|b|-|a|$
Case II: If $a \lt b \leq 0$, then $|x|=-x$
$\therefore \quad I=\int_a^b \frac{-x}{x} d x=\int_a^b(-1) d x=[-x]_a^b$
$=-b-(-a)=|b|-|a|$
Case III If $a \lt 0 \lt b$.
then $|x|=-x \quad$ when $a \lt x \lt 0$
and $|x|=x \quad$ when $0 \lt x \lt b$
$I=\int_a^b \frac{|x|}{x} d x \quad=\int_a^0 \frac{|x|}{x} d x+\int_0^b \frac{|x|}{x} d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_a^0 \frac{-x}{x} d x+\int_0^b \frac{x}{x} d x \\ & =\int_a^0(-1) d x+\int_0^b(1) d x \\ & =[-x]_a^0+[x]_0^b \\ & =a+b=b-(-a) \\ & =|b|-|a|\end{aligned}$
Hence, in all cases,
$I=\int_a^b \frac{|x|}{x} d x=|b|-|a|$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $I(m, n)=\int_0^1 t^m(1+t)^n d t$, then the expression for $I(m, n)$ in terms of $I(m+1, n-1)$ is
questions.solution:
Here, $I(m, n)=\int_0^1 t^m(1+t)^n d t$
[We apply integration by parts, taking $(1+t)^n$ as first and $t^m$ as second function]
$\begin{aligned}
& I(m, n)=\left[(1+t)^n \cdot \frac{t^{m+1}}{m+1}\right]_0^1 \\
& \quad-\int_0^1 n(1+t)^{n-1} \cdot \frac{t^{m+1}}{m+1} d t
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \quad=\frac{2^n}{m+1}-\frac{n}{m+1} \int_0^1(1+t)^{n-1} \cdot t^{m+1} d t \\ & \therefore I(m, n)=\frac{2^n}{m+1}-\frac{n}{m+1} \cdot I(m+1, n-1)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\int_0^{\pi / 2}|\sin x-\cos x| d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \int_0^{\pi / 2}|\sin x-\cos x| d x \\ & \quad=\int_0^{\pi / 4}-(\sin x-\cos x) d x +\int_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2}-(\sin x-\cos x) d x\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \begin{aligned}= & -[-\cos x-\sin x]_0^{\pi / 4} +[-\cos x-\sin x]_{\pi / 4}^{\pi / 2} \\ = & -\left[-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}-\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+1\right]  +\left[-0-1+\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \\ = & \sqrt{2}-1-1+\sqrt{2} \\ = & 2(\sqrt{2}-1)\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: By trapezoidal rule, the approximate value of the integral $\int_0^6 \frac{d x}{1+x^2}$ is
questions.solution:
Given that,
$I=\int_0^6 \frac{d x}{1+x^2}$    ....(i)
From Eq. (i), $f(x)=\frac{1}{1+x^2}$
Now, divide the interval $[0,6]$ into six parts each of width
$h=\frac{6-0}{6}=1$
The value of $f(x)$ are given below
$\begin{array}{c|c|c|c|c|c|c|c}\hline x & 0 & 1 & 2 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 6 \\\hline f(x) & 1 & 0.5 & 0.2 & 0.1 & 0.0588 & 0.0385 & 0.027 \\\hline\end{array}$
The trapezoidal rule is
$\begin{aligned} \int_{x_0}^{x_0+n h} y d x= & \frac{h}{2}\left[\left(y_0+y_n\right)\right. \\ & \left.+2\left(y_1+y_2+y_3+\ldots+y_{n-1}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \int_0^6 \frac{1}{1+x^2} d x & =\frac{1}{2}[(1+0.027)+2\{0.5+0.2 \\ & +0.1+0.0588+0.0385\}] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[1.027+2(0.8973)] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[1.027+1.7946] \\ & =\frac{1}{2}[2.8216]=1.4108\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{2}\left[\sec ^2 \frac{\pi}{4 n}+\sec ^2 \frac{2 \pi}{4 n}+\ldots+\sec ^2 \frac{n \pi}{4 n}\right]$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} & {\left[\sec ^2 \frac{\pi}{4 n}+\sec ^2 \frac{2 \pi}{4 n}+\ldots+\sec ^2 \frac{n \pi}{4 n}\right] } \\ & =\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{1}{n} \sum_{r=1}^n \sec ^2 \frac{r \pi}{4 n} \\ & =\int_0^1 \sec ^2 \frac{\pi x}{4} d x \\ & =\frac{4}{\pi}\left[\tan \frac{\pi x}{4}\right]_0^1=\frac{4}{\pi}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\cdot \int_{-\frac{\pi}{2}}^{\frac{\pi}{2}}|\sin x| d x$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \int_{-\pi / 2}^{\pi / 2}|\sin x| d x & =2 \int_0^{\pi / 2} \sin x d x \\ & =2[-\cos x]_0^{\pi / 2}=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x=4$ and $\int_2^4[3-f(x)] d x=7$, then the value of $\int_{-1}^2 f(x) d x$ is
questions.solution:
$\int_2^4[3-f(x)] d x=7$
$\begin{array}{rr}\Rightarrow & {[3 x]_2^4-\int_2^4 f(x) d x=7} \\ \Rightarrow & 6-\int_2^4 f(x) d x=7 \\ \Rightarrow & \int_2^4 f(x) d x=-1...(i)\end{array}$
and
$\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x=4$
$\int_{-1}^4 f(x) d x=\int_{-1}^2 f(x) d x+\int_2^4 f(x) d x$
$\Rightarrow \quad 4=\int_{-1}^2 f(x) d x-1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \int_{-1}^2 f(x) d x=5$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of the integral $\int_{\pi / 2}^{3 \pi / 2}[\sin x] d x$, where [.] denotes the greatest integer function, is
questions.solution:
$\int_{\pi / 2}^{3 \pi / 2}[\sin x] d x=\int_{\pi / 2}^\pi[\sin x] d x+\int_\pi^{3 \pi / 2}[\sin x] d x$
$=\int_{\pi / 2}^\pi 0 d x+\int_\pi^{3 \pi / 2}(-1) d x=-[x]_x^{3 \pi / 2}$
$=-\left(\frac{3 \pi}{2}-\pi\right)=-\frac{\pi}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The approximate value of $\int_1^5 x^2 d x$ using trapezoidal rule with $n=4$ is
questions.solution:
$\int_1^5 x^2 d x$
$=\frac{4}{4}\left[\frac{1}{2} f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4)+\frac{1}{2} f(5)\right]$
$=\left[\frac{1}{2}+4+9+16+\frac{25}{2}\right]=42\left[\right.$ using $\left.f(x)=x^2\right]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $f(x)$ be a positive function. Let
$\begin{aligned}
I_1 & =\int_{1-k}^k x f\{x(1-x)\} d x, \\
I_2 & =\int_{1-k}^k f\{x(1-x)\} d x,
\end{aligned}$
where $2 k-1\gt0$, then $\frac{I_1}{I_2}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} I_1 & =\int_{1-k}^k x f\{x(1-x)\} d x \\ & =\int_{1-k}^k(1-x) f\{(1-x)(1-(1-x))\} d x \\ & \quad\left(\because \int_a^b f(x) d x=\int_a^b f(a+b-x) d x\right) \\ & =\int_{1-k}^k(1-x) f\{(1-x) x\} d x \\ & =\int_{1-k}^k f\{x(1-x)\} d x -\int_{1-k}^k x f\{x(1-x)\} d x \\ \Rightarrow & \quad I_1=I_2-I_1 \Rightarrow 2 I_1=I_2\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\int_1^4 \log _e[x] d x$ equals
questions.solution:
Let $I=\int_1^4 \log _\theta[x] d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_1^2 \log _\theta 1 d x+\int_2^3 \log _\theta 2 d x+\int_3^4 \log _\theta 3 d x \\ & =\log _e 2+\log _e 3=\log _e 6\end{aligned}$

questions.q: The value of $\int_2^3 \frac{\sqrt{x}}{\sqrt{5-x}+\sqrt{x}} d x$ is

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: $\begin{aligned} & y=\int_{1 / 8}^{\sin ^2 x} \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{t} d t+\int_{1 / 8}^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^{-1} \sqrt{t} d t, \\ & 0 \leq x \leq x / 2 .\end{aligned}$
options: Is the equation of a straight line parallel to the $x$-axis
Is the euqation of a straight line which is the bisector of first quadrant
Is the equation of a straight line which is the bisector of second quadrant
questions.solution:
Here, we have to prove that, 
$y=$ constant or derivative of $y$ 
with respect to $x$ is zero.
$y=\int_{1 / 8}^{\sin ^2 x} \sin ^{-1} \sqrt{t} d t+\int_{1 / 8}^{\cos ^2 x} \cos ^{-1} \sqrt{t} d t \quad \ldots(i)$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}=\sin ^{-1} \sqrt{\sin ^2 x} \cdot 2 \sin x \cdot \cos x+\cos ^{-1} \sqrt{\cos ^2 x} \\ & (-2 \sin x \cdot \cos x)=2 x \sin x \cdot \cos x-2 x \cdot \sin x \cdot \cos x \\ & =0 \text { for all } x\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\quad I_1=\int_\alpha^{\pi-\alpha} x f(\sin x) d x, I_2$ $=\int_{x f(\sin x), d x}^{\pi-\alpha} f(\sin x) d x$, then $I_2$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Since, $I_1=\int_a^{\pi-a} x f(\sin x) d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_a^{\pi-a}(\pi-x) f(\sin (\pi-x)) d x \\ & =\int_a^{\pi-a}(\pi-x) f(\sin x) d x \\ & =\int_a^{\pi-a} \pi f(\sin x) d x-I_1 \\ \Rightarrow 2 I_1 & =\pi I_2 \\ \Rightarrow I_2 & =\frac{2}{\pi} I_1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $c=2 \cos \theta$, then the value of the determinant $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}c & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & c & 1 \\ 6 & 1 & c\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Given that $c=2 \cos \theta$ $\therefore \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll} c & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & c & 1 \\ 6 & 1 & c \end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \cos \theta & 1 & 0 \\ 1 & 2 \cos \theta & 1 \\ 6 & 1 & 2 \cos \theta \end{array}\right|$
On expanding, we get $\begin{aligned} \Delta= & 2 \cos \theta\left[\left(4 \cos ^2 \theta-1\right)\right] \\ & \quad-1[(2 \cos \theta-6)]+0[(1-12 \cos \theta)] \\ = & 8 \cos ^3 \theta-2 \cos \theta-2 \cos \theta+6 \\ = & 8 \cos ^3 \theta-4 \cos \theta+6 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & a & a^2 \\ 1 & b & b^2 \\ 1 & c & c^2\end{array}\right|=k(a-b)(b-c)(c-a)$, then $k$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & a & a^2 \\
1 & b & b^2 \\
1 & c & c^2
\end{array}\right|=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & a & a^2 \\
0 & b-a & b^2-a^2 \\
0 & c-a & c^2-a^2
\end{array}\right| \\
& \left(\text { using } R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_1 \text { and } R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1\right) \\
& =(b-a)(c-a)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & a & a^2 \\
0 & 1 & b+a \\
0 & 1 & c+a
\end{array}\right| \\
& =(b-a)(c-a)(c+a-b-a) \\
& \Rightarrow(b-a)(c-a)(c-b) \\
& =k(a-b)(b-c)(c-a)(given) \\
& \Rightarrow  k=1
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & a & b \\ b & x & a \\ a & b & x\end{array}\right|$ and $\Delta_2=\left|\begin{array}{ll}x & b \\ a & x\end{array}\right|$ are the given determinants, then
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \Delta_1=\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & a & b \\ b & x & a \\ a & b & x\end{array}\right|, \Delta_2=\left|\begin{array}{ll}x & b \\ a & x\end{array}\right| \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \frac{d}{d x}\left(\Delta_1\right) \\ &=\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & 0 & 0 \\ b & x & a \\ a & b & x\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & a & b \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ a & b & x\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{lll}x & a & b \\ b & x & a \\ 0 & 0 & 1\end{array}\right| \\ & \quad=\left|\begin{array}{ll}x & a \\ b & x\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ll}x & b \\ a & x\end{array}\right|+\left|\begin{array}{ll}x & a \\ b & x\end{array}\right|=3 \Delta_2 \\ & \therefore \frac{d}{d x}\left(\Delta_1\right)=3 \Delta_2\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
The value of ' $a$ ' for which the system of equations
$a x+y+z=0, x+a y+z=0, x+y+z=0$
possesses non-zero solutions are given by
questions.solution:
Given, system of equations are
$\begin{aligned} & a x+y+z=0, x+a y+z=0 \\ & \text { and } x+y+z=0 \text { possesses non-zero solution. }\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & a & 1 \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|=0$
$\begin{array}{rr}\Rightarrow & a(a-1)-1(1-1)+1(1-a)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2-a+0+1-a=0 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2-2 a+1=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (a-1)^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & a=1\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $a, b$ and $c$ are negative and different real numbers, then $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & b & c \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & b & c \\ b & c & a \\ c & a & b\end{array}\right|$
Applying, $C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3$ and taking common from $C_1$ $\begin{aligned} \Delta & =(a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & b & c \\ 1 & c & a \\ 1 & a & b \end{array}\right| \\ & =-(a+b+c)\left[a^2+b^2+c^2-a b-b c-c a\right] \\ & =-\frac{(a+b+c)}{2}\left[(a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2\right] \\ & \gt0 \text { as } a, b, c \lt 0 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In the determinant $\left|\begin{array}{rrr}3 & x & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & y & -3\end{array}\right|$, the sum of the cofactors of $x$ and $y$ is
questions.solution:
Given, determinant $\left|\begin{array}{rrr}3 & x & -1 \\ 2 & -1 & 4 \\ 1 & y & -3\end{array}\right|$
Here, cofactor of $x=10$ and cofactor of $y=-14$
$\therefore$ Their sum $=-4$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a $\triangle A B C$, if $\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & a & b \\ 1 & c & a \\ 1 & b & c\end{array}\right|=0$, then $\sin ^2 A+\sin ^2 B+\sin ^2 C$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have,
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & a & b \\ 1 & c & a \\ 1 & b & c\end{array}\right| & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & a & b \\ 0 & c-a & a-b \\ 0 & b-a & c-b\end{array}\right| & =0\end{aligned}$
[applying $R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_1$ and $R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1$ ]
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & (c-a)(c-b)+(a-b)^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & a^2+b^2+c^2-a b-b c-c a=0 \\ \Rightarrow & 2 a^2+2 b^2+2 c^2-2 a b-2 b c-2 c a=0 \\ \Rightarrow & (a-b)^2+(b-c)^2+(c-a)^2=0 \\ \Rightarrow & \quad a=b=c\end{array}$
$\therefore \triangle A B C$ is an equilateral triangle.
$\Rightarrow \quad A=B=C=\frac{\pi}{3}$
$\therefore \sin ^2 A+\sin ^2 B+\sin ^2 C=3 \sin ^2 \frac{\pi}{3}=\frac{9}{4}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\quad D_k=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 2^k & 2^{16}-1 \\ b & 3\left(4^k\right) & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) \\ c & 7\left(8^k\right) & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right)\end{array}\right|$, then the value of $\sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k$ is
questions.solution:
We have,
$\sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & \left(\sum_{k=1}^{16} 2^k\right) & 2^{16}-1 \\ b & 3\left(\sum_{k=1}^{16} 4^4\right) & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) \\ C & 7\left(\sum_{k=1}^{16} 8^k\right) & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right)\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & 2\left(\frac{2^{16}-1}{2-1}\right) & 2^{16}-1 \\ b & 3 \times 4\left(\frac{4^{16}-1}{4-1}\right) & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) \\ c & 7 \times 8\left(\frac{8^{16}-1}{8-1}\right) & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right)\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k=\left|\begin{array}{lll}a & 2\left(2^{16}-1\right) & \left(2^{16}-1\right) \\ b & 4\left(4^{16}-1\right) & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) \\ c & 8\left(8^{16}-1\right) & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right)\end{array}\right|$
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k=2\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & \left(2^{16}-1\right) & \left(2^{16}-1\right) \\ b & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) & 2\left(4^{16}-1\right) \\ c & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right) & 4\left(8^{16}-1\right)\end{array}\right|$
[taking 2 common from $C_2$ ]
$\Rightarrow \quad \sum_{k=1}^{16} D_k=2 \times 0=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\lambda$ and $\mu$ for which the system of equations $x+y+z=6, x+2 y+3 z=10$ and $x+2 y+\lambda z=\mu$ have no solution, are
questions.solution:
Given system of equation is $\begin{aligned} x+y+z & =6 \\ x+2 y+3 z & =10 \\ x+2 y+\lambda z & =\mu \end{aligned}$
If given system of equation has no solution, then $D=0$ and atleast one of the determinants $D_1, D_2, D_3 \neq 0$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { Here } \\ & \begin{aligned} & D=0 \\ &\left|\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 1 & 2 & \lambda \end{array}\right|=0 \end{aligned} \\ & \Rightarrow 1(2 \lambda-6)-1(\lambda-3)+1(2-2)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad 2 \lambda-6-\lambda+3+0=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lambda-3=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \lambda=3 \end{aligned}$
Also, $D_1=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}6 & 1 & 1 \\ 10 & 2 & 3 \\ \mu & 2 & 3\end{array}\right| \neq 0$ $\begin{array}{rlrl} \Rightarrow & 6(6-6)-1(30-3 \mu)+1(20-2 \mu) & \neq 0 \\ \Rightarrow & 0-30+3 \mu+20-2 \mu \neq 0 \\ \Rightarrow & \mu-10 \neq 0 \\ \Rightarrow & \mu \neq 10 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Coefficient of $x$ in $f(x)=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & (1+\sin x)^3 & \cos x \\ 1 & \log (1+x) & 2 \\ x^2 & (1+x)^2 & 0\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Coefficient $x$ in $f(x)$ is equal to coefficient of $x$ in
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & \left(1+x-\frac{x^3}{3!} \ldots\right)^3 & 1-\frac{x^2}{2!} \\ 1 & x-\frac{x^2}{2} & 2 \\ x^2 & 1+x^2 & 0\end{array}\right|$
$=$ coefficient of $x$ in $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}x & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & x & 2 \\ x^2 & 1 & 0\end{array}\right|$
$\begin{aligned} & =\text { Coefficient of } x \text { in }\left[x(0-2)-1\left(0-2 x^2\right)\right. \\ & \left.+1\left(1-x^3\right)\right]\end{aligned}$
$=-2$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\alpha_1, \alpha_2$ and $\beta_1, \beta_2$ be the roots of $a x^2+b x+c=0 \quad$ and $\quad p x^2+q x+r=0$ respectively. If the system of equations $\alpha_1 y+\alpha_2 z=0$ and $\beta_1 y+\beta_2 z=0$ has $a$ non-trivial solution, then
questions.solution:
Given system has non-trivial solution then
$\left|\begin{array}{ll}\alpha_1 & \alpha_2 \\ \beta_1 & \beta_2\end{array}\right|=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \alpha_1 \beta_2=\alpha_2 \beta_1$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{\alpha_1}{\beta_1}=\frac{\alpha_2}{\beta_2}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{\beta_1+\beta_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\alpha_1 \alpha_2}{\beta_1 \beta_2}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{-b / a}{-q / p}=\sqrt{\frac{c / a}{r / p}} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{b p}{a q}=\sqrt{\frac{c p}{a r}} \\ \Rightarrow & b^2 p^2 a r=a^2 q^2 c p \\ \Rightarrow & b^2 p r=q^2 a c\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\omega=-\frac{1}{2}+i \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, then value of the determinant $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 1 & -1-\omega^2 & \omega^2 \\ 1 & \omega^2 & \omega\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Applying $\quad R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_1 ; \quad R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1$, the given determinant becomes
$\begin{aligned}
& \Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}
1 & 1 & 1 \\
0 & -2-\omega^2 & \omega^2-1 \\
0 & \omega^2-1 & \omega-1
\end{array}\right| \\
& =1\left[(\omega-1)\left(-2-\omega^2\right)-\left\{\left(\omega^2-1\right)\left(\omega^2-1\right)\right\}\right] -1(0)+1(0) \\
& =\left(-2-\omega^2\right)(\omega-1)-\left(\omega^2-1\right)^2 \\
& =-2 \omega+2-\omega^3+\omega^2-\left(\omega^4+1-2 \omega^2\right) \\
& =-2 \omega+1+\omega^2-\left(\omega+1-2 \omega^2\right) \\
& =3 \omega^2-3 \omega \\
& =3 \omega(\omega-1)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
Let $\lambda$ and $\alpha$ be real
$\begin{array}{r}
\lambda x+\sin \alpha \cdot y+\cos \alpha \cdot z=0 \\
x+\cos \alpha \cdot y+\sin \alpha \cdot z=0 \\
-x+\sin \alpha \cdot y-\cos \alpha \cdot z=0
\end{array}$
has a non-trivial solution. If $\lambda=1$, then all the values of $\alpha$ is equals
questions.solution:
The given system has a non-trivial solution, if $\left|\begin{array}{rrr} \lambda & \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \\ 1 & \cos \alpha & \sin \alpha \\ -1 & \sin \alpha & -\cos \alpha \end{array}\right|=0$
By expanding the determinant along ( $C_1$ ), we get $\begin{gathered} \lambda\left(-\cos ^2 \alpha-\sin ^2 \alpha\right)-1(-\sin \alpha \cos \alpha \\ -\sin \alpha \cos \alpha) \\ -1\left(\sin ^2 \alpha-\cos ^2 \alpha\right)=0 \\ \lambda=\sin 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \alpha \end{gathered}$ For $\quad \lambda=1, \sin 2 \alpha+\cos 2 \alpha=1$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \sin 2 \alpha+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cos 2 \alpha=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \cos \left(2 \alpha-\frac{\pi}{4}\right)=\cos \left(2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$ $\Rightarrow 2 \alpha=2 n \pi \pm \frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{\pi}{4}, n$ being an integer. $\Rightarrow \quad \alpha=n \pi+\frac{\pi}{4}, n \pi$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\quad a_1 x+b_1 y+c_1 z=0, \quad a_2 x+b_2 y+c_2 z=0$, $a_3 x+b_3 y+c_3 z=0$ and $\left|\begin{array}{lll}a_1 & b_1 & c_1 \\ a_2 & b_2 & c_2 \\ a_3 & b_3 & c_3\end{array}\right|=0$, then the given system has
options: one trivial and one non-trivial solution
questions.solution: Given system has infinite solution.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ (a+1)^2 & (b+1)^2 & (c+1)^2 \\ (a-1)^2 & (b-1)^2 & (c-1)^2\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ (a+1)^2 & (b+1)^2 & (c+1)^2 \\ (a-1)^2 & (b-1)^2 & (c-1)^2\end{array}\right|$
Applying $R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_3$
$=4\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ a & b & c \\ (a-1)^2 & (b-1)^2 & (c-1)^2\end{array}\right|$
Applying $R_3 \rightarrow R_3-\left(R_1-2 R_2\right)$
$=4\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a^2 & b^2 & c^2 \\ a & b & c \\ 1 & 1 & 1\end{array}\right|$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The existence of the unique solution of the system $\quad x+y+z=\lambda, \quad 5 x-y+\mu z=10$, $2 x+3 y-z=6$ depends on
questions.solution:
For unique solution of the given system, $\Delta \neq 0$
i.e., $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 5 & -1 & \mu \\ 2 & 3 & -1\end{array}\right| \neq 0$
Hence, it depends only on $\mu$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the matrix $A=\left[\begin{array}{ccc}y+a & b & c \\ a & y+b & c \\ a & b & y+c\end{array}\right]$ has rank 3, then
questions.solution:
Here, the rank of $A$ is 3 .
Therefore, the minor of order 3 of $A \neq 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad\left|\begin{array}{ccc}y+a & b & c \\ a & y+b & c \\ a & b & y+c\end{array}\right| \neq 0$
[Applying $\quad C_1 \rightarrow C_1+C_2+C_3$ and taking $(y+a+b+c)$ common from $\left.C_1\right]$
$\Rightarrow \quad(y+a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & b & c \\ 1 & y+b & c \\ 1 & b & y+c\end{array}\right| \neq 0$
[Applying $R_2 \rightarrow R_2-R_1, R_3 \rightarrow R_3-R_1$ ]
$\Rightarrow \quad(y+a+b+c)\left|\begin{array}{lll}1 & b & c \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & y\end{array}\right| \neq 0$
Expanding along $C_1$
$\Rightarrow \quad(y+a+b+c)\left(y^2\right) \neq 0$
$\Rightarrow \quad y \neq 0$ and $y \neq-(a+b+c)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+b & a+2 b \\ a+2 b & a & a+b \\ a+b & a+2 b & a\end{array}\right|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$\left|\begin{array}{ccc}a & a+b & a+2 b \\ a+2 b & a & a+b \\ a+b & a+2 b & a\end{array}\right|$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left|\begin{array}{ccc}3(a+b) & 3(a+b) & 3(a+b) \\ a+2 b & a & a+b \\ a+b & a+2 b & a\end{array}\right|\left(R_1 \rightarrow R_1+R_2+R_3\right) \\ & =3(a+b)\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & 0 & 0 \\ a+2 b & -2 b & -b \\ a+2 b & b & -b\end{array}\right| \quad\binom{C_2 \rightarrow C_2-C_1}{C_3 \rightarrow C_3-C_1} \\ & =3(a+b)\left|\begin{array}{cc}-2 b & -b \\ b & -b\end{array}\right| \\ & =3 b^2(a+b)\left|\begin{array}{rr}2 & 1 \\ -1 & 1\end{array}\right|=3 b^2(a+b)(2+1)=9 b^2(a+b)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: For positive numbers $x, y, z$ the numerical value of the determinant $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \log _x y & \log _x z \\ \log _y x & 1 & \log _y z \\ \log _z x & \log _z y & 1\end{array}\right|$ is
questions.solution:
We have, $\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \log _x y & \log _x z \\ \log _y x & 1 & \log _y z \\ \log _z x & \log _z y & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\left|\begin{array}{ccc}1 & \frac{\log y}{\log x} & \frac{\log z}{\log x} \\ \frac{\log x}{\log y} & 1 & \frac{\log z}{\log y} \\ \frac{\log x}{\log z} & \frac{\log y}{\log z} & 1\end{array}\right|$
$=\frac{1}{\log x \cdot \log y \cdot \log z}\left|\begin{array}{lll}\log x & \log y & \log z \\ \log x & \log y & \log z \\ \log x & \log y & \log z\end{array}\right|=0$
$[\because$ all rows are identical]

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The system of linear equations $x+y+z=0$, $2 x+y-z=0,3 x+2 y=0$ has
questions.solution:
Given system of equations are $x+y+z=0,2 x+y-z=0,3 x+2 y=0$
Let $\Delta=\left|\begin{array}{rrr}1 & 1 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & -1 \\ 3 & 2 & 0\end{array}\right|=1(0+2)-1(0+3)+1(4-3)$ $=+2-3+1=0$
Hence, it has infinitely many solutions.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The system $x+4 y-2 z=3,3 x+y+5 z=7$, $2 x+3 y+z=5$ has
questions.solution:
Given system of equations are
and
$\begin{aligned}
