The dot product of the given two vectors is $\begin{aligned} & (q-r) \log a+(r-p) \log b+(p-q) \log c \\ & \Rightarrow(q-r)[\log \alpha+(p-1) \log \beta]+(r-p) \\ & {[\log \alpha+(q-1) \log \beta]+(p-q)[\log \alpha+(r-1) \log \beta]} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \alpha[q-r+r-p+p-q]+ \\ & \log \beta[(p-1)(q-r)+(r-p)(q-1) \\ & \quad+(r-1)(p-q)] \\ & \quad=0+0=0 \end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow$ The two vectors are perpedicular.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the scalar projection of the vector $x \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ on the vector $2 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k}$ is $\frac{1}{\sqrt{30}}$ then value of $x$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Projection of $\mathbf{x i}-\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ on $2 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}+5 \mathbf{k}$
$=\frac{(x i-j+k) \cdot(2 i-j+5 k)}{\sqrt{4+1+25}}=\frac{2 x+1+5}{\sqrt{30}}$
But, given, $\frac{2 x+6}{\sqrt{30}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{30}}$
$\begin{array}{rrr}\Rightarrow & 2 x+6=1 \\ \Rightarrow & x=\frac{-5}{2}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: A unit vectors coplanar with $\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+2 \mathbf{k}$ and $\mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ and perpendicular to $\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Let } \mathbf{a} & =t(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+2 \mathbf{k})+s(\mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k}) \\ & =(t+3 s) \mathbf{i}+(t+2 s) \mathbf{j}+(2 t+s) \mathbf{k}\end{aligned}$
given, $\mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k})=0$
$\therefore t+s+t+2 s+2 t+s=0$
$\begin{array}{rrr}\Rightarrow & 4(t+s)=0 \\ \Rightarrow & t+s=0\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \mathbf{a}=t(\mathbf{1}+\mathbf{j}+2 \mathbf{k})-t(\mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}+\mathbf{k})$
$=t(-1+\mathbf{k})$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { But }|\mathbf{a}|=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 1=2 t^2\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad t= \pm \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\therefore \quad a= \pm\left(\frac{k-j}{\sqrt{2}}\right)$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}]$ is
questions.solution:
Now, $\left[\begin{array}{lll}\mathbf{a} & \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b} & \mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}\end{array}\right]$
$\begin{aligned}
& =[\mathbf{a} \times(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b})] \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c}) \\
& =(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) \cdot(\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}+\mathbf{c})=(\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}) \cdot \mathbf{c} \\
& =[\mathbf{a} \mathbf{b} \mathbf{c}]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\quad \mathbf{a}=-\mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}-\mathbf{k}, \quad \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}-3 \mathbf{k} \quad$ and $\mathbf{c}=-4 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{k}$, then $\mathbf{a} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{c}$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{a}=-\mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}-\mathbf{k}, \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}-3 \mathbf{k}, \mathbf{c}=-4 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{k} \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathbf{a} \times(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})+(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{c}=(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{c}) \mathbf{b} \\ &=(4+1)(\mathbf{i}+\mathbf{j}-3 \mathbf{k}) \\ &=5 \mathbf{i}+5 \mathbf{j}-15 \mathbf{k}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ are non-collinear vectors such that for some scalars $\mathrm{x}, \mathrm{y}, \mathrm{z}, x a+yb+zc=0$, then
questions.solution:
Since, $x \mathbf{a}+y \mathbf{b}+z \mathbf{c}=0$
Also, $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}$ and c are non-collinear vectors, then $x=y=z=0$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$, be three vectors such that $\mathbf{c} \neq 0$ and $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \quad=2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{c},|\mathbf{a}|=|\mathbf{c}|=1,|\mathbf{b}|=4 \quad$ and $|b \times c|=\sqrt{15}$, if $b-2 c=\lambda \alpha$. then, $\lambda$ equals
questions.solution:
Let angle between $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ is $\alpha$, then
$|\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}|=\sqrt{15}$ (given)
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad|b||c| \sin \alpha=\sqrt{15} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin \alpha=\frac{\sqrt{15}}{4}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \cos \alpha=\frac{1}{4}$
Also, $\quad \mathbf{b}-2 \mathrm{c}=\lambda \mathbf{a}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & (b-2 c)^2=\lambda^2(a)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & |b|^2+4|c|^2-4 b \cdot c=\lambda^2|a|^2 \\ \Rightarrow & 16+4-4\{|b||c| \cos \alpha\}=\lambda^2 \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda^2=16 \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda= \pm 4\end{array}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: In a trapezoid of the vector $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}$. We will, then find that $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BD}}$ is collinear with $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}$. If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\mu \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}$, then
questions.solution:
We have,
$\text {} \begin{aligned}
& \overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BD}}=\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{BC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}} \\
= & \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}+\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}=\lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AC}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{CD}}) \\
= & \lambda \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}+\overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}=(\lambda+1) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}
\end{aligned}$
But, $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{P}}=\mu \overrightarrow{\mathrm{AD}}$
$\therefore  \mu=\lambda+1$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If the projection of $\mathbf{P Q}$ on $O X, O Y, O Z$ are respectively 12, 3 and 4, then the magnitude of $\mathbf{P Q}$ is
questions.solution:
Since, projection of $\mathbf{P Q}$ on $O X, O Y, O Z$ are 12 , 3 and 4 respectively, then $\mathbf{P Q}=12 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+4 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore |\mathbf{P Q}|=\sqrt{12^2+3^2+4^2} & =\sqrt{169} \\
& =13
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|$, then $\mathbf{a}$ and $\mathbf{b}$ are
questions.solution:
Given, $|\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|=|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|$
$\begin{array}{l}
\Rightarrow |\mathbf{a}+\mathbf{b}|^2 =|\mathbf{a}-\mathbf{b}|^2 \\
\Rightarrow |\mathbf{a}|^2+|\mathbf{b}|^2+2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} =|\mathbf{a}|^2+|\mathbf{b}|^2-2 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \\
\Rightarrow 4 \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} =0
\end{array}$
Hence, $\mathbf{a}$ is perpendicular to $\mathbf{b}$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}$, if $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}$ is a vector such that $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}=|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}|,|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}-\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}|=2 \sqrt{2}$ and the angle between $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}$ is $30^{\circ}$, then $|(\vec{a} \times \vec{b}) \times \vec{c}|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $\vec{a} \cdot \vec{c}=|\vec{c}|$ and $|\vec{c}-\vec{a}|=2 \sqrt{2}$
$\Rightarrow \overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}=|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}| \text { and }|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}}|^2+|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}}|^2-2(\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{c}})=8$
$\Rightarrow |\vec{c}|^2+9-2|\vec{c}|=8$
$\Rightarrow (|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}|-1)^2=0$
$\Rightarrow |\vec{c}|=1$
$\Rightarrow |(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}) \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}|=|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}||\overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}| \sin 30^{\circ}$
$=\frac{1}{2}|\overrightarrow{\mathrm{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}}|=\frac{3}{2}$
$[\because  \overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}=2 \hat{\mathbf{i}}-2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+\hat{\mathbf{k}}]$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let a,b and c be non-zero vectors such that no two are collinear and $(a \times b) \times c=\frac{1}{3}|b||c| a$. If $\theta$ is the acute angle between the vectors $b$ and $c$, then $\sin \theta$ equals
questions.solution:
We have,
$(a \times b) \times c=\frac{1}{3}|b||c| a$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & (a \cdot c) b-(b \cdot c) a=\frac{1}{3}|b||c| a \\ \Rightarrow \quad & (a \cdot c) b-\left\{(b \cdot c)+\frac{1}{3}|b||c|\right\} a=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & a \cdot c=0 \text { and } b \cdot c+\frac{1}{3}|b||c|=0 \\ & {[\because a, b \text { are non-collinear }] }\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad|\mathbf{b}||\mathbf{c}| \cos \theta+\frac{1}{3}|\mathbf{b}||\mathbf{c}|=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=-\frac{1}{3} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta=\sqrt{1-\cos ^2 \theta}=\sqrt{1-\left(-\frac{1}{3}\right)^2}\end{aligned}$
$=\sqrt{1-\frac{1}{9}}=\sqrt{\frac{8}{9}}=\frac{2 \sqrt{2}}{3}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.solution:
We have, $|[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}]|=V$
Let $V_1$ be the volume of parallelopiped formed by the vectors $\vec{\alpha}, \vec{\beta}$ and $\vec{\gamma}$. Then,
$V_1=|[\vec{\alpha} \vec{\beta} \vec{\gamma}]|$
$\Rightarrow \quad V_1=\left|\begin{array}{lll}\vec{a} \cdot \vec{a} & \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} \\ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{b} \cdot \vec{b} & \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c} \\ \vec{a} \cdot \vec{c} & \vec{b} \cdot \vec{c} & \vec{c} \cdot \vec{c}\end{array}\right|[\vec{a} \vec{b} \vec{c}]$
$\Rightarrow \quad V_1=[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}]^2[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}]$
$\Rightarrow \quad V_1=[\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{b}} \overrightarrow{\mathbf{c}}]^3$
$\Rightarrow \quad V_1=V^3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Consider a tetrahedron with faces $F_1, F_2, F_3, F_4$. Let $\vec{V}_1, \vec{V}_2, \vec{V}_3, \vec{V}_4$ be the vectors whose magnitudes are respectively equal to areas of $F_1, F_2, F_3, F_4$ and whose directions are perpendicular to these faces in outward direction, then $\left|\vec{V}_1+\vec{V}_2+\vec{V}_3+\vec{V}_4\right|$ equals

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let us define the length of a vector $a \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b \hat{\mathbf{j}}+c \hat{\mathbf{k}}$ as $|a|+|b|+|c|$. This definition coincides with the usual definition of length of a vector $a \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b \hat{\mathbf{j}}+c \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, iff
options: any two of $a, b$ and $c$ are zero
any one of $a, b$ and $c$ is zero
questions.solution:
We have, $|a \hat{\mathbf{i}}+b \hat{\mathbf{j}}+c \hat{\mathbf{k}}|=|a|+|b|+|c|$
$\Rightarrow \sqrt{a^2+b^2+c^2}=|a|+|b|+|c|$
$\Rightarrow a^2+b^2+c^2$
$=a^2+b^2+c^2+2(|a||b|+|b||c|+|c||a|)$
$\Rightarrow|a||b|+|b||c|+|c||a|=0$.
$\Rightarrow a b=b c=c a=0$
$\Rightarrow$ Any two of $a, b$ and $c$ are zero.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: If $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}=\mathbf{a}$ and $a, b, c$ be the moduli of the vectors $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}$, c respectively, then
questions.solution:
Since, $\mathbf{a} \times \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \times \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c} \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{c}-(\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}) \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{c} \\ & \Rightarrow(\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{b})=1, \quad \mathbf{c} \cdot \mathbf{b}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad b^2=1, \text { i.e., } b=1\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad$ b is a unit vector
$\therefore|\mathbf{c}|=|\mathbf{a}| \quad \Rightarrow c=a$.

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q:
If $\mathbf{b}$ and $\mathbf{c}$ are any two non-collinear unit vectors and $\mathbf{a}$ is any vector, then
$(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{b}+(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{c})+\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}}{|\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}|} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})$ is equal to
questions.solution:
Let $\mathbf{b}=\hat{\mathbf{j}}$ and $\mathbf{c}=\hat{\mathbf{j}}$
$\therefore \quad|\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}|=|\hat{\mathbf{k}}|=1$
Again, let $\quad \mathbf{a}=a_1 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+a_2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+a_3 \hat{\mathbf{j}}$
Now, $\quad \mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}=\mathbf{a} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{i}}=\mathbf{a}_1, \mathbf{b} \cdot \overline{\mathbf{c}}=\mathbf{a} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}=\mathbf{a}_2$
and $\quad \mathbf{a} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{b} \cdot \mathbf{c}}{|\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}|}=\mathbf{a} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{k}}=\mathbf{a}_3$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{b}) \mathbf{b}+(\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{c}) \mathbf{c}+\frac{\mathbf{a} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})}{|\overline{\mathbf{b}} \times \mathbf{c}|} \cdot(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c}) \\ & =a_1 \mathbf{b}+a_2 \mathbf{c}+a_3(\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{c})=a_1 \hat{\mathbf{i}}+a_2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+a_3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}=\underline{a}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The value of $\left\{(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}})^2+(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}})^2\right\} \div a^2 b^2$ is
questions.solution:
$\left\{(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}})^2+(\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}})^2\right\} \div a^2 b^2$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left\{(|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}}||\overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}| \sin \theta \hat{\mathbf{n}})^2+(|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{a}} \| \overrightarrow{\mathbf{b}}| \cos \theta)^2\right\}+a^2 b^2 \\ & =a^2 b^2\left(\sin ^2 \theta+\cos ^2 \theta\right)+a^2 b^2=1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: Let $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}, \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}}$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{w}}=\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, if $\hat{\mathbf{n}}$ is a unit vector such that $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}=0$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}=0$, then $|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{w}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}|$ is equal to
questions.solution:
We have, $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}=0$ and $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}=0$
$\Rightarrow  \hat{\mathbf{n}} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}}$ and $\hat{\mathbf{n}} \perp \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}$
$\Rightarrow  \hat{\mathbf{n}}= \pm \frac{\overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}}{|\hat{\mathbf{u}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}|}$
Now, $\overrightarrow{\mathbf{u}} \times \overrightarrow{\mathbf{v}}=(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+\hat{\mathbf{j}}) \times(\hat{\mathbf{i}}-\hat{\mathbf{j}})=-2 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
$\hat{\mathbf{n}}= \pm \hat{\mathbf{k}}$
Hence, $|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{w}} \cdot \hat{\mathbf{n}}|=|(\hat{\mathbf{i}}+2 \hat{\mathbf{j}}+3 \hat{\mathbf{k}}) \cdot( \pm \hat{\mathbf{k}})|=3$

questions.subject: Mathematics
questions.q: The vectors $\mathbf{c}, \mathbf{a},=x \mathbf{i}+y \mathbf{j}+z \mathbf{k}$ and $\mathbf{b}=\mathbf{j}$ are such that $\mathrm{a}, \mathrm{b}, \mathrm{c}$ form a right handed system, then $\mathbf{c}$ is
questions.solution:
Since, $\mathbf{a}, \mathbf{b}, \mathbf{c}$ form $\mathbf{a}$ right handed system
$\therefore \quad \mathbf{c}=\mathbf{b} \times \mathbf{a}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\mathbf{j} \times(x \mathbf{i}+y \mathbf{j}+z \mathbf{k}) \\ & =x(\mathbf{j} \times \mathbf{i})+z(\mathbf{j} \times \mathbf{k}) \\ & =-x \mathbf{k}+z \mathbf{i}=z \mathbf{i}-x \mathbf{k}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $L-C-R$ circuit which one of the following statements is correct?
options: The inductive reactance increases with frequency
The capacitive reactance increases with frequency
questions.solution:
In $L-C-R$ circuit, inductive reactance is given by
$\begin{aligned}X_L & =\omega L \\& =2 \pi f L \quad[\because \omega=2 \pi f]\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad X_L \propto f$
Thus, inductive reactance increases with increase frequency.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transformer of $100 \%$ efficiency has 200 turns in the primary and 40000 turns in secondary. It is connected to a 220 V main supply and secondary feeds to a $100 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ resistance. The potential difference per turn is
questions.solution:
From transformer ratio $\begin{aligned} \frac{V_s}{V_p} & =\frac{N_s}{N_p} \\ \Rightarrow \quad V_s & =\frac{V_p \times N_s}{N_p} \\ & =\frac{220 \times 40000}{200} \\ & =44000 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$
Potential difference per turn is $\frac{V_s}{N_s}=\frac{44000}{40000}=1.1 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a step-up transformer the voltage in the primary is 220 V and the current is 5 A . The secondary voltage is found to be 22000 V . The current in the secondary (neglect losses) is
questions.solution:
$\frac{V_s}{V_p}=\frac{I_s}{I_p}$
$\Rightarrow \quad I_s=I_p \times \frac{V_s}{V_p}=5 \times \frac{220}{22000}=0.05 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $L C R$ circuit having $L=8 \mathrm{H}, C=0.5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ and $R=100 \Omega$ in series, the resonance frequency in $\mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ is
questions.solution:
From the formula, $\omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}$
Given, $L=8 \mathrm{H}$,
$\begin{aligned}
& C=0.5 \mu \mathrm{~F}=0.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F} \\
\therefore \quad & \omega=\frac{1}{\sqrt{8 \times 0.5 \times 10^{-6}}}
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \omega=\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-3}}=500 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \omega=\frac{250}{\pi} \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.solution:
For (I): The net capacitance \(\mathrm{C}=\frac{\mathrm{C}_1 \mathrm{C}_2}{\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2}=\frac{2 \times 4}{2+4}=4 / 3 \mu \mathrm{~F}\) and net resistance
\(\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2}=\frac{1 \times 2}{1+2}=2 / 3 \Omega\)
Thus, time constant \(\tau=\mathrm{CR}=(4 / 3)(2 / 3)=(8 / 9) \mu \mathrm{S}\)
For (II): \(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2=2+4=6 \mu \mathrm{~F}\) and \(\mathrm{R}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2=1+2=3 \Omega\)
Thus, time constant \(\tau=\mathrm{CR}=(6)(3)=18 \mu \mathrm{~S}\)
For (III): \(\mathrm{C}=\mathrm{C}_1+\mathrm{C}_2=2+4=6 \mu \mathrm{~F}\) and \(\mathrm{R}=\frac{\mathrm{R}_1 \mathrm{R}_2}{\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2}=2 / 3 \Omega\)
Thus, time constant \(\tau=\mathrm{CR}=(6)(2 / 3)=4 \mu \mathrm{~S}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When 100 V DC is applied across a solenoid, a current of 1 A flows in it. When 100 VAC is applied across the same coil, the current drop is 0.5 A . If the frequency of the $A C$ source is 50 Hz , then inductance of solenoid is found to be $x \mathrm{mH}$, then find the value of $x$.
questions.solution:
When DC is applied across a solenoid, then resistance, i.e. $R=\frac{V}{I}$
$R=\frac{100}{1}=100 \Omega$
When $A C$ is applied, then
$Z=\frac{100}{0.5}=200 \Omega$
Net impedance,
$\begin{aligned}
Z & =\sqrt{R^2+X_L^2} \\
200 & =\sqrt{(100)^2+X_L^2} \\
\Rightarrow (200)^2 & =(100)^2+X_L^2 \\
\Rightarrow  30000 & =X_L^2 \quad \Rightarrow X_L=173.205 \Omega
\end{aligned}$
Now, $X_L=\omega L$
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow  173.205=2 \pi f L \Rightarrow \frac{173.205}{2 \pi \times f}=L \\
& \Rightarrow  \frac{173.205}{2 \times 3.14 \times 50}=L \Rightarrow 0.55 \Omega=L
\end{aligned}$
$L=0.55 \mathrm{H}=550 \mathrm{mH}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The alternating voltage and current in an electric circuit are respectively given by $E=100 \sin 100 \pi t, I=5 \sin 100 \pi t$
The reactance of the circuit will be
questions.solution:
The general equations of alternating voltage and current in an AC circuit are as follows
$E=E_0 \sin \omega t$   ...(i)
$I=I_0 \sin \omega t$   ...(ii)
Given that,
$E=100 \sin 100 \pi t$   ...(iii)
$I=5 \sin 100 \pi t$   ...(iv)
Comparing Eqs. (ii) and and (iv), we get, Peak value of current, $I_0=5 \mathrm{~A}$ Comparing Eqs. (i) and (iii), we get Peak value of voltage, $E_0=100 \mathrm{~V}$ Now, r.m.s. value of voltage is given by
$E_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{E_0}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~V}$
Similarly, r.m.s. value of current is given by
$I_{\mathrm{rms}}=\frac{I_0}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~A}$
Hence, reactance of the circuit is given by
$Z=\frac{E_{\mathrm{rms}}}{I_{\mathrm{rms}}}=\frac{\frac{100}{\sqrt{2}}}{\frac{5}{\sqrt{2}}}=20 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sinusoidal voltage of peak value 300 V and an angular frequency $\omega=400 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ is applied to a series $L-C-R$ circuit, in which $R=3 \Omega$, $L=20 \mathrm{mH}$ and $C=625 \mu \mathrm{~F}$. The peak current in the circuit is
questions.solution:
The impedance of the circuit is
$\begin{aligned}
Z & =\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2} \\
X_L & =\omega L=400 \times 20 \times 10^{-3}=8 \mathrm{H} \\
X_C & =\frac{1}{\omega C}=\frac{1}{400 \times 625 \times 10^{-6}}=4 \mathrm{C} \\
Z & =\sqrt{(3)^2+(8-4)^2}=5 \\
i & =\frac{E}{Z}=\frac{300}{5}=60 \mathrm{~A}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In an AC circuit the potential difference and current are represented respectively by $V=100 \sin (100 t)$ volt,
$I=100 \sin \left(100 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ milliampere. The power in the circuit is
questions.solution:
Given, $V=100 \sin 100 t$ $i=100 \sin \left(100 t+\frac{\pi}{3}\right)$ Comparing with standard forms $\begin{aligned} & V=V_0 \sin \omega t \\ & I=I_0 \sin (\omega t+\phi)\end{aligned}$ $\therefore \quad V_0=100 \mathrm{~V}$ $\begin{aligned} & I_0=100 \mathrm{~mA}=100 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \\ & \phi=\frac{\pi}{3}\end{aligned}$
Power $P=\frac{V_0 I_0}{2} \cos \phi$ $\therefore \quad P=\frac{100 \times 100 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \cos \frac{\pi}{3}=2.5 \mathrm{~W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 220 V main supply is connected to a resistance of $100 \mathrm{k} \Omega$. The effective current is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a step-up transformer the turn ratio is $1: 2$. A Leclanche cell (emf 1.5 V ) is connected across the primary. The voltage across the secondary is
questions.solution: Transformer works only in alternating current. Primary coil is connected to Lechlanche celli.e., connected by DC source, so voltage across secondary will be zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time taken by AC of 50 Hz in reaching from zero to the maximum value is
questions.solution:
The time taken by AC in reaching from zero to maximum value is
$\begin{aligned} t & =\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{4 f} \\ & =\frac{1}{50 \times 4}=5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The peak value of an alternating emf $E$ given by $E=E_0 \cos \omega t$ is 10 V and its frequency is 50 Hz . At a time $t=\frac{1}{600} \mathrm{~s}$, the instantaneous value of the emf is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} E=E_0 \cos \omega t & =10 \cos (2 \pi \times f t) \\ & =10 \cos \left(2 \pi \times 50 \times \frac{1}{600}\right)\end{aligned}$
$=10 \cos \left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=10 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The number of turns in primary coil of a transformer is 20 and the number of turns in the secondary is 10 . If the voltage across the primary is 220 V , what is the voltage across the secondary?
questions.solution:
Given, $N_P=20, N_S=10, e_P=220 \mathrm{~V}$
$\therefore$ Transformation ratio, $k$
$\frac{e_S}{e_P}=\frac{N_S}{N_P}$
or$\quad e_S=\frac{N_S}{N_P} \times e_P$
$=\frac{10}{20} \times 220=110 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Voltage and current in an AC circuit are given by $V=5 \sin \left(100 \pi t-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$ and $I=4 \sin \left(100 \pi t+\frac{\pi}{6}\right)$
questions.solution:
Net phase difference between current and voltage $=\frac{\pi}{6}-\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right)=\frac{\pi}{3}=60^{\circ}$
Hence, current leads the voltage by $60^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A step up transformer has turn ratio $10: 1$. A cell of emf 2 V is fed to the primary, then the secondary voltage developed is
questions.solution: A transformer is essentially an AC device, it does not work on DC. So, voltage developed across secondary is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In non-resonant circuit, what will be the nature of the circuit for frequencies higher than the resonant frequency?
questions.solution:
At resonant frequency
$X_L=X_C \quad\left(\omega L=\frac{1}{\omega C}\right)$
At frequencies higher than resonance frequencies
$X_L \gt X_C$
i.e., behaviour is inductive.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an $L-C-R$ series circuit, the values of $R, X_L$ and $X_C$ are $120 \Omega, 180 \Omega$ and $130 \Omega$, what is the impedance of the circuit?
questions.solution:
Given, $R=120 \Omega$
$X_L=180 \Omega, X_C=130 \Omega$
The impedance of $L-C-R$ circuit
$Z=\sqrt{R^2+\left(X_L-X_C\right)^2}$
$\begin{aligned} Z & =\sqrt{(120)^2+(180-130)^2} \\ & =130 \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How does the energy difference between the consecutive energy level vary as the quantum number $n$ increases?
questions.solution:
We know that, $E_n \propto \frac{1}{n^2}$.
As $n$ increases $\left(E_n-E_{n+1}\right)$ decreases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If levels 1 and 2 are separated by an energy $E_2-E_1$, such that the corresponding transition frequency falls in the middle of the visible range, calculate the ratio of the populations of two levels in the thermal equilibrium at room temperature.
questions.solution:
At thermal equilibrium, the ratio $\frac{N_2}{N_1}$ is given as $\frac{N_2}{N_1}=\exp \left(-\frac{E_2-E_1}{k T}\right)$
The middle of the visible range is taken at $\begin{array}{ll} \lambda=550 \mathrm{~nm} \\ \Rightarrow & E_2-E_1=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=3.16 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{N_2}{N_1}=\exp \left(\frac{-3.16 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}}{\left(1.38 \times 10^{-23} 1 / \mathrm{k}\right) \cdot(300 \mathrm{k})}\right) \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{N_2}{N_1}=1.1577 \times 10^{-38} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature of the black body increases from $T$ to $2 T$. The factor by which the rate of emission will increases is
questions.solution:
$E=\sigma T^4$
$T_1=T_1 \quad T_2=2 T$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{T_1^4}{T_2{ }^4}=\left(\frac{T}{2 T}\right)^4=\frac{1}{16} \\ & E_2=16 E\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the wavelength for $2 \rightarrow 1$ transition in $\mathrm{Li}^{+}, \mathrm{He}^{+}$and H is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Using } \frac{1}{\lambda}=R Z^2\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right) \Rightarrow \lambda \propto \frac{1}{Z^2} \Rightarrow \\ & \lambda_{\mathrm{Li}}: \lambda_{\mathrm{He}}: \lambda_{\mathrm{H}}=\frac{1}{9}: \frac{1}{4}: \frac{1}{1}=4: 9: 36\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Minimum energy required to take out the only one electron from ground state of $\mathrm{He}^{+}$is
questions.solution:
Ionisation energy $E=13.6 Z^2$
For $\mathrm{He}^{+}, Z=2$ $E=13.6(2)^2=13.6 \times 4=54.4 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is 13.6 eV . How much energy need to be supplied to ionise the hydrogen atom in the first excited state?
questions.solution:
Ionisation energy of nth state $=-E_n$
For the first excited state, $n=2$
and energy in that state is, $\frac{13.6}{2^2} \mathrm{eV}=\frac{13.6}{4} \mathrm{eV}$
$=3.4 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If an electron in $n=4$ orbit of hydrogen atom jumps down to $n=3$ orbit, the amount of energy released and the wavelength of radiation emitted are
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} E & =\frac{-13.6}{n^2} \mathrm{eV} \\ E_4-E_3 & =13.6\left[\frac{1}{(3)^2}-\frac{1}{(4)^2}\right]\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =13.6\left[\frac{7}{144}\right] \mathrm{eV}=0.66 \mathrm{eV} \\ \lambda & =\frac{1}{R\left[\frac{1}{(3)^2}-\frac{1}{(4)^2}\right]}=\frac{144}{1.09 \times 10^7 \times 7} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =1.88 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ionisation potential of hydrogen atom is 13.6 V . Hydrogen atoms in the ground state are excited by monochromatic radiation of photon energy 12.1 eV . The spectral lines emitted by hydrogen atoms according to Bohr's theory will be
questions.solution:
Final energy of electron
$=-13.6+12.1=-1.51 \mathrm{eV}$
This energy corresponds to third level i.e., $n=3$
Hence, number of spectral lines emitted
$=\frac{n(n-1)}{2}=\frac{3(3-1)}{2}=3$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelength of the $k_a$ line for an element of atomic number 43 is $\lambda$. Then the wavelength of the $K_\alpha$ line for an element of atomic number 29 is
questions.solution:
As, $\quad \sqrt{V}=a(Z-b)$
$\begin{aligned} \sqrt{V} & =a(Z-1) \\ V & =a^2(Z-1)^2 \\ \frac{C}{\lambda} & =a^2(Z-1)^2 \\ \lambda & =\frac{c}{a^2(Z-1)^2}\end{aligned}$
$z=43$, wavelength $=\lambda$
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{c}{a^2(43-1)^2} \\ & \lambda=\frac{c}{a^2 \times 42}\end{aligned}$
for $z=29$, wavelength $=\lambda^{\prime}$
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{C}{a^2(29-1)^2} \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\frac{c}{28 a^2} \\ & \frac{\lambda^{\prime}}{\lambda}=-\left(\frac{42}{28}\right)^2=\left(\frac{3}{2}\right)^2 \\ & \lambda^{\prime}=\left(\frac{9}{4}\right) \lambda\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The radius of the smallest electron orbit in hydrogen-like ion is $\left(0.51 \times 10^{-10} / 4\right) \mathrm{m}$, then it is
questions.solution:
For hydrogen like atom, the radius of $n$th orbit
Here,
$\begin{aligned}
& r_n^z=\frac{n^2}{Z} a_0 \\
& a_0=0.51 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\
& r_n^Z=\frac{0.51 \times 10^{-10}}{4} \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$
In the ground state, $n=1$
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore & \frac{0.51 \times 10^{-10}}{4} & =\frac{1^2}{Z} \times 0.51 \times 10^{-10} \\
& \therefore & Z & =4
\end{aligned}$
So, the atom is triply ionised beryllium $\left(\mathrm{Be}^{3+}\right)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The velocity of an electron in the second orbit of sodium atom (atomic number $=11$ ) is $v$. The velocity of an electron in its fifth orbit will be
questions.solution:
Velocity of electron in $n$th orbit
$\begin{aligned} v_n & =\frac{Z e^2}{2 \varepsilon_0 n h} \\ \Rightarrow \quad v & \propto \frac{1}{n} \\ v_1 & =v, n_1=2, n_2=5\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v}{v_2}=\frac{n_2}{n_1}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v}{v_2}=\frac{5}{2}$
$\Rightarrow \quad v_2=\frac{2 v}{5}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Band spectrum is characteristic of the
questions.solution: Band spectrum is defined as a molecular spectrum consisting of numerous closely spaced, often unresolved emission lines or absorption lines which occur across a limited range of frequencies. Each line represents an increment of energy due to a change in the rotational state of the molecule. Bands caused by titanium oxide, zirconium, and carbon compounds are characteristic of low-temperature stars.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the wavelength of first line of Balmer series to the first line of Lyman series is
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{\lambda}=R\left(\frac{1}{n_1^2}-\frac{1}{n_2^2}\right)$
For Lyman series $n=1$
For Balmer series $n=2$
$\therefore  \frac{1}{\lambda_L}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}\right), \frac{1}{\lambda_B}=R\left(\frac{1}{1^2}-\frac{1}{2^2}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_B}{\lambda_L}=\frac{3}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The energy of a hydrogen atom in its ground state is -13.6 eV . The energy of the level corresponding to the quantum number $n=5$ is
questions.solution:
The energy of the hydrogen atom in nth state is givne by
$E_n=\frac{-13.6}{\left(n^2\right)} \mathrm{eV}$
Here, $\quad n=5$
$\therefore \quad E=\frac{-13.6}{(5)^2} \mathrm{eV}=-0.54 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Continuous emission spectrum is produced by
questions.solution: In candescent electric lamp produces continuous emission spectrum whereas mercury and sodium vapour given line emission spectrum. Polyatomic substance such as $\mathrm{H}_2, \mathrm{CO}_2$ and $\mathrm{KMNO}_4$ produces band absorption spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the energy of a hydrogen atom in $n^{\text {mn }}$ orbit is $E_n$ then energy in the $n^{\text {th }}$ orbit of a singly ionized helium atom will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & E=\frac{-m z^2 e^4}{8 \varepsilon_0 h^2} \cdot \frac{1}{n^2} \\ & Z_H=1, \\ & Z_{\mathrm{He}}=2 \\ & \frac{E_H}{E_{\mathrm{He}}}=\frac{Z_H^2}{Z_{\mathrm{He}}^2}=\frac{1}{4} \\ & E_H=\frac{Z_H^2}{Z_{\mathrm{He}}^2}=\frac{2}{4} \\ & E_{\mathrm{He}}=4 E_H \\ & E_H=E_n \\ & E_{\mathrm{He}}=4 E_n\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin metal plate $P$ is inserted half way between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor of capacitance $C$ in such a way that it is parallel to the two plates. The capacitance now becomes
questions.solution: When a thin metal plate is placed between the plates of a parallel plate capacitor, then its capacitance increases so, out of the given choices, 4 C will be the right answer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel plate capacitor has an electric field of $10^5 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ between the plates. If the charge on the capacitor plate is $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$, the force on each capacitor plate is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical air core capacitors are connected in series to a voltage source of 15 V . If one of the capacitors is filled with a medium of dielectric constant 4, the new potential across this capacitor is
questions.solution:
Let capacitance of each capacitor is $C$. Then equivalent capacitance in series is
$\begin{aligned} \quad & \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{2}{C} \\ \Rightarrow & C^{\prime}=\frac{C}{2} \end{aligned}$ Charge $Q=C^{\prime} V=\frac{C}{2} \times 15...(i)$ When filled with dielectric $\begin{gathered} C_1=4 C, C_2=C \\ \frac{1}{C^{\prime}}=\frac{1}{4 C}+\frac{1}{C}=\frac{5}{4 C} \\ \end{gathered}$ $\Rightarrow C^{\prime}=\frac{4 C}{5}$ Since, charge is conserved $Q=C^{\prime} V^{\prime}=\frac{4 C}{5} V^{\prime}...(ii)$ From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\begin{gathered} \frac{C}{2} \times 15=\frac{4 C}{5} V^{\prime} \\ \Rightarrow V^{\prime}=\frac{15 \times 5}{4 \times 2}=9.4 \mathrm{~V} \approx 10 \mathrm{~V} \end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a dielectric slab is introduced between the plates of a capacitor connected to a battery, then
options: potential difference across the capacitor increases
questions.solution:
As the battery remains connected. potential difference $(V)$ of the capacitor remains constant. Suppose dielectric of dielectric constant $K$ is introduced between the plates. Now, capcitance of the capacitor is
$C^{\prime}=K C_0$
where, $C_0$ is original capacitance.
Initial energy stored in the capacitor
$U_i=\frac{1}{2} C_0 V^2$
Final energy stored in the capacitor
$U_t=\frac{1}{2} C^{\prime} V^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(K C_0\right) V^2$
Clearly, $\quad \frac{U_t}{U_i}=K \gt 1 \Rightarrow U_f \gt U_i$
As, $\quad Q=C V$
$\therefore V$ remains constant and $C$ increases.
$\therefore Q$ increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacitance $2 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ and $6 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ are connected in series. A potential difference of 800 V is applied to the outer plates of the two capacitors system. The charge on each capacitor will be :
questions.solution:
$\frac{1}{C_S}=\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{6}=\frac{3+1}{6}=\frac{4}{6}=\frac{2}{3}$
$\therefore \quad C_S=\frac{3}{2} \mu \mathrm{~F}$
In combination charge on each capacitor in same.
So,      $Q=C_S V=\frac{3}{2} \times 800=1200 \mu \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors, one 4 pF and the other 6 pF , connected in parallel, are charged by a 100 V battery. The energy stored in the capacitors is
questions.solution:
The energy stored in capacitor is given by
$E=\frac{1}{2} C V^2$
Resultant capacitance
$\begin{aligned}
C^{\prime} & =C_1+C_2=4+6=10 \mathrm{pF} \\
E & =\frac{1}{2} \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times(100)^2 \left(1 \mathrm{pF}=10^{-12} \mathrm{~F}\right) \\
& =5 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the following circuit diagram, potential difference across $4 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ capacitor is
questions.solution:
1. Parallel Combination:
The \(2 \mu F\) and \(6 \mu F\) capacitors are in parallel, so their equivalent capacitance:
\(C_{e q}=2+6=8 \mu F\)
2. Series Combination:
The \(4 \mu F^{\prime}\) capacitor is in series with the \(8 \mu F\) equivalent capacitor. The total capacitance is:
\(\frac{1}{C_{t o t a l}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}=\frac{3}{8} \Longrightarrow C_{t o t a l}=\frac{8}{3} \mu F\)
3. Charge Calculation:
The total charge in the circuit is:
\(Q=C_{t o t a l} \cdot V=\frac{8}{3} \cdot 12=32 \mu C\)
4. Voltage Across \(4 \mu F\) Capacitor:
Since the charge is the same in series, the voltage across the \(4 \mu F\) capacitor is:
\(V=\frac{Q}{C}=\frac{32}{4}=8 \mathrm{~V}\)
Final answer: \(\mathrm{8} \mathrm{V}\).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor of capacitance $10 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is charged to potential 50 V with a battery. The battery is now disconnected and an additional charge $200 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is given to the positive plate of the capacitor. The potential difference across the capacitor will be
questions.solution:
Charge acquired by the plates of the capacitor $q_0=C V=(10 \mu \mathrm{~F}) \times(50 \mathrm{~V})=500 \mu \mathrm{C}$
Now, let the charge distribution is as follows. Total charge on positive plate has now become $700 \mu \mathrm{C}$ while that in negative plate is still $-500 \mu \mathrm{C}$,
Here, charges are in $\mu \mathrm{C}$. Net electric field at point $P$ is zero. $\begin{array}{lc}\therefore & \frac{(700-q)}{2 A \varepsilon_0}+\frac{q}{2 A \varepsilon_0}+\left(\frac{500-q}{2 A \varepsilon_0}\right)=\frac{q}{2 A \varepsilon_0} \\ \Rightarrow & q=600 \mu \mathrm{C}\end{array}$ $\therefore$ Potential difference between the plates is $\Delta V=\frac{q}{C}=\frac{600 \mu \mathrm{C}}{10 \mu \mathrm{~F}}=60 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The amount of work done in increasing the voltage across the plates of a capacitor from 5 V to 10 V is W . The work done in increasing it from 10 V to 5 V will be
questions.solution:
As we know that work done $=U_{\text {final }}-U_{\text {initial }}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(V_2^2-V_1^2\right)$
When potential difference increases from 5 V to 10 V then $W=\frac{1}{2} C\left(10^2-5^2\right)$...(i) When potential difference increases form 10 V to 15 V , then $W^{\prime}=\frac{1}{2} C\left(15^2-10^2\right)$...(ii) On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii) we get $W^{\prime}=1.67 W$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor is connected to a cell of emf $E$ and some internal resistance $r$. The potential difference across the
questions.solution: When a capacitor is fully charged, then no current is drawn by it. When no current flows in "the circuit, potential difference across the cell = emf of cell = potential difference across the capacitor.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A capacitor of capacitance $5 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ is connected as shown in the figure. The internal resistance of the cell is $0.5 \Omega$. The amount of charge on the capacitor plate is
questions.solution:
We know that capacitor offers infinite resistance for DC source.
Current taken from the cell
$\begin{aligned} & i=\frac{E}{R+r} \\ \therefore \quad i & =\frac{2.5}{1+1+0.5}=1 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$
Potential drop across capacitor
$\begin{aligned} V & =E-i r \\ & =2.5-1 \times 0.5=2 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
The current in $2 \Omega$ resistor is zero.
Charge on capacitor
$\begin{aligned} Q & =C V \\ & =5 \times 2=10 \mu \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An alternating voltage $E=200 \sqrt{2} \sin (100 t) \mathrm{V}$ is connected to a $1 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ capacitor through an AC ammeter. The reading of ammeter is
questions.solution:
Given $E=200 \sqrt{2} \sin (100 t)$..(i)
Comparing Eq. (i) with
$E=E_0 \sin \omega t$
Peak voltage $E_0=200 \sqrt{2}, \omega=100$
$C=1 \mu \mathrm{~F}=1 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~F}$
Reáding of ammeter, $i=\frac{E_{\mathrm{rms}}}{X_C}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{E_0 \omega_C}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{E_{\mathrm{rms}}}{1 / \omega C}=\frac{E_0 \omega C}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & =\frac{200 \sqrt{2} \times 100 \times 1 \times 10^{-6}}{\sqrt{2}} \\ & =20 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A} \\ & =20 \mathrm{~mA}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2=2 C_1$ are connected in a circuit with a switch between them as shown in the figure. Initially the switch is open and $C_1$ holds charge $Q$. The switch is closed. At steady state, the charge on each capacitors will be
questions.solution:
Charge redistributes on the capacitors in the ratio of their capacitance.
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} & =\frac{C_1}{C_2}=\frac{C_1}{2 C_1}=\frac{1}{2} \\
Q_1: Q_2 & =1: 2 \\
Q_1 & =\frac{1}{1+2} \times Q=\frac{Q}{3} \\
Q_2 & =\frac{2}{1+2} Q=\frac{2}{3} Q
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Force between two identical charges placed at a distance of $r$ in vacuum is $F$. Now a slab of dielectric of dielectric constant 4 is inserted between these two charges. If the thickness of the slab is $r / 2$, then the force between the charges will become
questions.solution:
From Coulomb's law the force $(F)$ between two charges is
$F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 k} \frac{q^2}{r^2}$
First case : $k=1$
$F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q^2}{r^2}...(i)$
Second case : $k=4$
$F^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \times 4} \cdot \frac{q^2}{r^2}....(ii)$
Dividing Eq. (i) by Eq. (ii), we have
$\frac{F}{F^{\prime}}=4 \Rightarrow F^{\prime}=\frac{F}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged capacitor when filled with a dielectric $K=3$ has charge $Q_0$, voltage $V_0$ and field $E_0$. If the dielectric is replaced with another one having $K=9$, the new values of charge voltage and field will be respectively
questions.solution:
When charging battery is disconnected then charge remains constant, $V$ becomes $1 / K$ times and $E$ becomes $1 / K$ times.
So, new value of charge is $Q_0$.
New value of $V=\frac{V_0 \times 3}{9}=\frac{V_0}{3}$
New value of $E=\frac{E_0 \times 3}{9}=\frac{E_0}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two capacitors of capacitances $3 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ and $6 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ are charged to a potential of 12 V each. They are now connected to each other, with the positive plate of each joined to the negative plate of the other. The potential difference across each will be
questions.solution:
Given, $C_1=3 \mu \mathrm{~F}, C_2=6 \mu \mathrm{~F}, V_1=V_2=12 \mathrm{~V}$
$V=?$
The common potential across capacitors is given by
$V=\frac{C_1 V_1+C_2 V_2}{C_1+C_2}$
$V=\frac{3 \times 10^{-6} \times 12+6 \times 10^{-6} \times 12}{(3+6) \times 10^{-6}}=12 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two parallel plates of area $A$ are separated by two different dielectrics as shown in figure. The net capacitance is
questions.solution:
The net capacitance $C=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d_1}{K_1}+\frac{d_2}{K_2}}$
$=\frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{\frac{d}{2}\left(\frac{1}{1}+\frac{1}{2}\right)}=\frac{4 \varepsilon_0 A}{3 d}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin dielectric rod of length $l$ lies along $X$-axis. One end of the rod is placed at the origin and the other end of the rod is placed at $(l, 0)$. A total charge $Q$ is distributed uniformly along the length of the rod. What is the potential at a point $(x, 0)$ when $x\gt1$ ?
questions.solution: The potential due to a small element of length $d r$ having charge $d Q$ of the dielectric rod is given by $d V=\frac{d Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r}$ or $d V=\frac{\lambda d r}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} \quad\left[\begin{array}{c}\because \lambda=\frac{d Q}{d r}, \\ \lambda d r=d Q\end{array}\right]$ or $V=\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \int_x^{x-l} \frac{d r}{r}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}\left[\log _e r\right]_x^{x-l} \\ & =\frac{\lambda}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \log \left(\frac{x-l}{x}\right)\end{aligned}$ $V=\frac{Q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 1} \cdot \log e\left(\frac{x-l}{x}\right)$ When $\lambda=\frac{Q}{l}$ (for whole length of rod)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The bob $A$ of a simple pendulum is released when the string makes an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with the vertical. It hits an other bob $B$ of the same material and same mass kept at rest on the table. If the collision is elastic
options: both $A$ and $B$ rise to the same height
both $A$ and $B$ come to rest at $B$
both $A$ and $B$ move with the same velocity of $A$
$A$ comes to rest and $B$ moves with the velocity of $A$
questions.solution: Since, colljision is elastic hence, velocities of bobs $A$ and $B$ get interchanged. Thus, $A$ will come to rest and $B$ will move with the velocity of $A$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The linear momentum is increased by $50 \%$, the kinetic energy will increase by
questions.solution:
Let $p_1=p, p_2=p_1+50 \%$ of $p_1$
$=p_1+\frac{p_1}{2}=\frac{3 p_1}{2}$
$E \propto p^2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{E_2}{E_1}=\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{3 p_1 / 2}{p_1}\right)^2=\frac{9}{4}$
$\Rightarrow  E_2=2.25 E_1=E_1+1.25 E_1$
$i e$, kinetic energy will increase by $125 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A body of mass 2 kg makes an elastic collision with another body at rest and continues to move in the original direction with one-fourth its original speed. The mass of the second body which collides with the first body is
questions.solution:
Conservation of linear momentum gives $\begin{aligned} & \\ m_1 u_1+m_2 u_2 & =m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & m_2 v_2 \end{aligned}=\frac{3 u}{2}$...(i)
Conservation of kinetic energy gives $\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m_1 u_1^2+\frac{1}{2} m_2 u_2^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{1}{2} m_1 v_1^2+\frac{1}{2} m_2 v_2^2 \\ m_2 v_2^2 & =\frac{15 u^2}{8}...(ii) \end{aligned}$
Hence, on solving Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $m_2=1.2 \mathrm{~kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bullet is fired by a light rifle and the other with a heavy rifle by the same force. Which rifle will cause more injury to the shoulder?
options: Both will cause the same injury
questions.solution: While firing a bullet from a rifle, there will be equal back thrust on light rifle as well as heavy rifle. Due to this, the light rifle will move backwards with greater speed than heavy rifle. That is why light rifle will cause more injury.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A particle is moving in a circular path with a constant speed $v$. If $\theta$ is the angular displacement. Then starting from $\theta=0$, the maximum and minimum changes in the momentum will occur, when value of $\theta$ is respectively:
questions.solution:
When $\theta=180^{\circ}$, the particle will be at diametrically opposite point. Where its velocity is opposite to the direction of motion.
The change in momentum $=m v-(-m v)+2 m v$ (maximum).
When $\theta=360^{\circ}$, the particle is at the initial position with momentum $m v$ change in momentum $=m v-m v=0$ (minimum).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bullet of mass 10 g is fired from a gun of mass 1 kg . If the recoil velocity is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, the velocity of the muzzle is
questions.solution:
From law of conservation of momentum
$\begin{aligned}
& M V=m v \\
& 1 \times 5=10 \times 10^{-3} \times v \\
& v=500
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two point masses 1 and 2 move with uniform velocities $\mathbf{v}_1$ and $\mathbf{v}_2$ respectively. Their initial position vectors are $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{r}_2$, respectively. Which of the following should be satisfied for the collision of the point masses?
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text { For collision, } & \mathbf{r}_1+\mathbf{v}_1 t=\mathbf{r}_2+\mathbf{v}_2 t \\ \Rightarrow & \mathbf{r}_1-\mathbf{r}_2=\left(\mathbf{v}_2-\mathbf{v}_1\right) t\end{array}$
Equating unit vectors, we get $\frac{r_1-r_2}{\left|r_2-r_1\right|}=\frac{v_1-v_2}{\left|v_2-v_1\right|}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A neutron moving with a speed $v$ makes a head on collision with a hydrogen atom in ground state kept at rest. The minimum kinetic energy of neutron for which inelastic collision will take place is
questions.solution:
Let $v=$ speed of neutron before collision
$v_1=$ speed of neutron after collision
$v_2=$ speed of hydrogen atom after collision and
$\Delta E=$ energy of the excitation
From the conservation of linear momentum,
$m v=m v_1+m v_2$   ...(i)
From the conservation of energy,
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} m v_1^2+\frac{1}{2} m v_2^2+\Delta E$    ...(ii)
from Eq. (i),
$v^2=v_1^2+v_2^2+2 v_1 v_2$
From Eq. (ii),
$\begin{array}{rlrl} & & v^2 & =v_1^2+v_2^2+\frac{2 \Delta E}{m} \\ & \therefore & 2 v_1 v_2 & =\frac{2 \Delta E}{m} \\ & \therefore & \left(v_1-v_2\right)^2 & =\left(v_1+v_2\right)^2-4 v_1 v_2 \\ & & =v^2-\frac{4 \Delta E}{m}\end{array}$
As, $v_1-v_2$ must be real
$\Rightarrow \quad v^2-\frac{4 \Delta E}{m} \geq 0$
or
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \geq 2 \Delta E$
The maximum energy that can be absorbed by hydrogen atom in ground state to go into excited state is 10.2 eV . Therefore, the minimum kinetic energy required is
$\frac{1}{2} m v_{\min }^2=2 \times 10.2 \mathrm{eV}=20.4 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball is projected from the point $O$ with velocity $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with horizontal as shown in the figure. At highest point of its trajectory, it strikes a smooth plane of inclination $30^{\circ}$ at point $A$. The collision is perfectly inelastic. The maximum height from the ground attained by the ball is
questions.solution:
Speed of ball before collision is $v=u \cos 60^{\circ}=20 \times \frac{1}{2}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ Since, collision is perfectly inelastic ( $e=0$ ), so the ball will not bounce. It will move along the plane with velocity
$v^{\prime}=v \cos 30^{\circ}=\frac{10 \sqrt{3}}{2}=5 \sqrt{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\therefore$ Maximum height attained by the ball $\begin{aligned} H & =\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 60^{\circ}}{2 g}+\frac{v^{\prime 2}}{2 g} \\ H & =\frac{(20)^2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2}{20}+\frac{(5 \sqrt{3})^2}{20} \\ & =\frac{300}{20}+\frac{75}{20}=18.75 \mathrm{~m} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bullet of mass 10 g is fired from a gun of mass 1 kg with recoil velocity of gun is $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The muzzle velocity will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Nucleus of mass number $A$, originally at rest, emits the $\alpha$-particle with speed $v$. The daughter nucleus recoils with a speed of
questions.solution: According to conservation of momentum $4 v=(A-4) v^{\prime} \Rightarrow v^{\prime}=\frac{4 v}{A-4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: During inelastic collision of two particles
options: $(\mathrm{KE})_{\text {final }}$ must be greater or less than $(\mathrm{KE})_{\text {initial }}$
questions.solution: During inelastic collision of two particles kinetic energy after collision is not equal to kinetic energy before collision. Here, KE appears in other forms. In some cases, (KE) final $\left.^{ \lt (K E}\right)_{\text {initial }}$ such as when initial KE is converted into internal energy of the product (as heat, elastic or excitation energy) while in other cases $(\mathrm{KE})_{\text {final }}\gt(\mathrm{KE})_{\text {initial }}$ such as when internal energy stored in the colliding particles is released.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A mass 3 m , initially at rest at the origin explodes into three fragments of equal mass. Two of the fragments have speed $v$ each and move perpendicular to each other. The third fragment will move with a speed

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two bodies of different masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ have equal momenta. Their kinetic energies $E_1$ and $E_2$ are in the ratio
questions.solution:
Given - Momentum of two bodies are same i.e. $p_1=p_2$ and mass of two bodies $m_1$ and $m_2$
The relationship between the kinetic energy (KE) and Linear momentum ( p ) is -
Momentum of first body whose mass is $\mathrm{m}_1$.
$p=\sqrt{2 m_1 K E_1}$ $\qquad ...\mathrm{(1)}$
Momentum of second body whose mass is $\mathrm{m}_2$
$p=\sqrt{2 m_2 K E_2}$ $\qquad ...\mathrm{(2)}$
Divide equation (1) and (2).
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{p}{p}=\frac{\sqrt{2 m_1 K E_1}}{\sqrt{2 m_2 K E_2}} \\
& \sqrt{2 m_2 K E_2}=\sqrt{2 m_1 K E_1}
\end{aligned}$
By squaring both side, we get
$\begin{aligned}
& 2 m_2 K E_2=2 m_1 K E_1 \\
& \frac{K E_1}{K E_2}=\frac{m_2}{m_1}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A force of 10 N acts on a body of mass 20 kg for 10 s . Change in its momentum is
questions.solution:
$d p=F \times d t$
$=10 \times 10=100 \mathrm{~kg}\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A bomb of mass 9 kg explodes into two parts. One part of mass 3 kg moves with velocity $16 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, then the KE of the other part is
questions.solution:
From the law of conservation of momentum
$3 \times 16+6 \times v=9 \times 0$
or $v=-8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\Rightarrow v=8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ (numerically)
Therefore, its kinetic energy
$K=\frac{1}{2} \times 6 \times(8)^2=192 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Two identical masses $m$ moving with velocities $u_1$ and $u_2$ collide perfectly inelastically. Find the loss in energy.
questions.solution:
Loss in kinetic energy
$=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m_1 m_2\left(u_1-u_2\right)^2}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} \frac{m \cdot m\left(u_1-u_2\right)^2}{(m+m)} \\ & =\frac{m}{4}\left(u_1-u_2\right)^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The separation between C and O -atoms in CO is $1.2 Å$. The distance of carbon atom from the centre of mass is
questions.solution: The centre of mass will be slightly farther from $C$-atom (because it is lighter), so correct choice will be (2).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: Four identical balls, $A, B, C$ and $D$ are placed on a smooth horizontal plane such that $B, C$ and $D$ are in one line and are in contact and $A$ is kept along the same line but a distance away. Now, $A$ is set in motion with speed $v$ along the line and it strikes the ball $B$. After this collision, which is elastic.
options: Balls $A, B, C$ will be at rest and $D$ will move with speed $v$
$A$ will be at rest, where $B, C$ and $D$ will move together with speed $v / 3$
A will rebound with speed $v / 2$ while $B, C$ and $D$ will move together with speed $v / 2$
$A, B, C$ and $D$ will move together with speed $v / 4$
questions.solution: It is a case of elastic collision, whole of energy of $A$ is transferred to $D$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A ball falls vertically on to a floor, with momentum $p$, and then bounces repeatedly, the coefficient of restitution is $e$. The total momentum imparted by the ball to the floor is
questions.solution:
When a particle undergoes normal collision with a floor or a wall, with coefficient of restitution $e$, the speed after collision is e times the speed before collision. Therefore, change in momentum after Ist impact is $e p-(-p)=p(1+e)$
After the second impact, change in momentum would be $e(e p)-(-e p)=e p(1+e)$ and soon. Therefore, total change in momentum of ball $\begin{aligned} & =p(1+e)\left[1+e+e^2+\ldots\right] \\ & =p\left(\frac{1+e}{1-e}\right)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: The momentum of a body is increased by $25 \%$. The kinetic energy is increased by about
questions.solution:
The relation between $K$ and $p$ is
$p=\sqrt{2 K} m$
when increased by $25 \%$, we have
$p^{\prime}=p+\frac{25}{100} p=\frac{5 p}{4}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \frac{4 p}{5 p}=\sqrt{\frac{K_1}{K_2}} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{K_1}{K_2}=\frac{16}{25}
\end{array}$
Hence, percentage increase is
$=\frac{25-16}{16} \times 100=\frac{9}{16} \times 100=56.25 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: For a body moving with relativistic speed if the velocity is doubled, then
options: its linear momentum will be less than double
its linear momentum will be more than double
questions.solution:
From Einstein's theorem
Relativistic momentum $=\frac{m_0 v}{\sqrt{1-v^2 / c^2}}$
Putting $v=2 v$
we get $p=\frac{m_0(2 v)}{\sqrt{1-(2 v)^2 / c^2}}$
$=2\left(\frac{m_0 v}{\sqrt{1-(2 v)^2 / c^2}}\right)$
So the momentum becomes more than the double.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: In an explosion a body breaks up into pieces of unequal masses. In this case
options: both parts will have numerically equal momentum
lighter part will have more momentum
heavier part will have more momentum
both parts will have equal kinetic energy
questions.solution:
From law of conservation of linear momentum i.e., final momentum = initial momentum
$m_1 v_1+m_2 v_2=0$...i(i)
where $m_1$ and $m_2$ are masses of pieces.
(initial momentum is zero because body is stationary)
From Eq. (i), $m_1 v_1=-m_2 v_2$
So, numerical value of momentum of both pieces will be equal.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A mass $3 m$, initially at rest at the origin explodes into three fragments of equal mass. Two of the fragments have speed $v$ each and move perpendicular to each other. The third fragment will move with a speed
questions.solution:
From law of conservation of momentum
$\begin{aligned} & m v-m v^{\prime} \cos \theta=0...(i) \\ & m v-m v^{\prime} \sin \theta=0...(ii) \end{aligned}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\begin{aligned} & \theta=45^{\circ} \\ & v^{\prime}=\frac{v}{\cos \theta}=v \sqrt{2} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.chapter: Center of Mass Momentum and Collision
questions.q: A sphere of mass $m$ moving with a constant velocity $u$ hits another stationary sphere of the same mass. If $e$ is the coefficient of restitution, then the ratio of velocities of the two spheres after collision $\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)$ will be
questions.solution:
Conservation of momentum (gives)
$m u+0=m v_1+m v_2$
$\Rightarrow \quad v_1+v_2=u\ldots$ (i)
Newton's experimental formula,
$v_1-v_2=-e\left[u_1-u_2\right] \text { gives }$
$v_1-v_2=-e u\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$v_1=\frac{(1-e) u}{2}$
$v_2=\frac{(1+e) u}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{1-e}{1+e}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric resistance of a wire is $R$. If the length of the wire is increased to doubled by stretching it, then the new resistance of the wire is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  As, } R=\rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}=\rho \cdot \frac{L^2}{A \cdot L}=\rho \cdot \frac{L^2}{V} ;(V=\text { Volume }) \\ & \therefore \quad R \propto L^2 \Rightarrow \frac{R_2}{R_1}=\left(\frac{L_2}{L_1}\right)^2=\left(\frac{2 L_1}{L_1}\right)^2=4 \\ & \therefore \quad R_2=4 R_1=4 R\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current of 2 A flows in the system of conductors as shown in the figure. The potential difference $V_P-V_R$ will be :
questions.solution:
Resistance between $Q$ and $S$, $R^{\prime}=\frac{5 \times 10}{5+10}=\frac{50}{15}=\frac{10}{3} \Omega$
Potential difference across $Q$ and $S$, $V_Q-V_S=2 \times \frac{10}{3}=\frac{20}{3} \mathrm{~V}$
Current through arm QPS, $\begin{aligned} I_1 & =\frac{20}{3 \times 5}=\frac{4}{3} \mathrm{~A} \\ V_Q-V_P & =\frac{4}{3} \times 2=\frac{8}{3} \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
Current through arm QRS, $\begin{aligned} I_2 & =\frac{20}{3 \times 10}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~A} \\ V_Q-V_R & =\frac{2}{3} \times 3=2 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$ Hence, $\begin{aligned} V_P-V_R & =\left(V_Q-V_R\right)-\left(V_Q-V_P\right) \\ & =2-\frac{8}{3}=-\frac{2}{3} \approx-1 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge of $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{C}$ moves at $30 \mathrm{rev} / \mathrm{s}$ in a circle of diameter 80 cm . The current linked with the circuit is :
questions.solution:
$I=\frac{q}{T}=q v$
$=2 \times 10^{-2} \times 30=0.60 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of resistance $5 \Omega$ is drawn out so that its length is increased by twice its original length, its new resistance is
questions.solution:
Resistance of a wire is given by
$R=\frac{\rho L}{A}$
where, $\rho=$ resistivity, $L=$ length of the wire
$A=$ area of cross- section of the wire
Equating initial and finial volumes of the wire, we can write
$A L=A^{\prime} L^{\prime}=A^{\prime} \times(2 L) \Rightarrow A^{\prime}=\frac{A}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{R^{\prime}}{R}=\frac{\rho L^{\prime} / A^{\prime}}{\rho L / A}=\left(\frac{L^{\prime}}{L}\right)\left(\frac{A}{A^{\prime}}\right)$
$=\left(\frac{2 L}{L}\right)\left(\frac{A}{A / 2}\right)=4$
$\Rightarrow \quad R^{\prime}=4 R=4(5)=20 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric current of 2 A passes through a wire of resistance $25 \Omega$. How much heat will be generated in 1 min ?
questions.solution:
Given, $I=2.0 \mathrm{~A}$
$R=25 \Omega$
$t=1 \mathrm{~min}=60 \mathrm{~s}$
$\therefore$ Heat produced, $H=I^2 R t$
$\begin{aligned} & =(2)^2 \times 25 \times 60 \\ & =4 \times 25 \times 60 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =6 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Five equal capacitors each with capacitance $C$ are connected as shown in figure. Then, the equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
Consider the given circuit,
The circuit can be redrawn as shown below.
The circuit is a balanced bridge. We can remove the capacitor between $C$ and $D$.
Thus, equivalent capacitance between $A$ and $B$ is $C_{A B}=\frac{C}{2}+\frac{C}{2}=C$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: On a heater coil it is written that, 250 V , 500 W . What is the resistance of this coil?
questions.solution:
Given $V=250 \mathrm{~V}$ and $P=500 \mathrm{~W}$
As we know that,
$P=\frac{V^2}{R}$
$\Rightarrow \quad R=V^2 / P=\frac{250 \times 250}{500}$
$=\frac{625}{5}=125 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric kettle has two coils. When one coil is switched on, it takes 10 min to boil water and when the second coil is switched on it takes 20 min to boil same amount of water. The time taken by electric kettle to boil water when-both coil are used in parallel, is found to be $n$ second. Find the value of $n$.
questions.solution:
Heat produced in a resistance $R$ in time $t$ is given by
$H=\frac{V^2}{R} t[\text { where }, V=\text { source voltage }]$
For first coil,
$H_1=\frac{V^2}{R_1} t_1=\frac{V^2}{R_1} \times 10 \times 60 \left[\because t_1=10 \times 60 \mathrm{~s}\right]$
For second coil, $H_2=\frac{V^2}{R_2} t_2 \left[\because t_2=20 \times 60 \mathrm{~s}\right]$
$=\frac{V^2}{R_2} \times 20 \times 60$
Given that, $H_1=H_2$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\Rightarrow & \frac{V^2}{R_1} \times 10 \times 60=\frac{V^2}{R_2} \times 20 \times 60 \\
\Rightarrow & R_2=2 R_1..(i)
\end{array}$
Let $t$ be the time taken by electric kettle to boil water when both coil are used in parallel, then
$\begin{aligned}
H_p= & \left(\frac{V^2}{R_1}+\frac{V^2}{R_2}\right) \times t=V^2 t\left(\frac{1}{R_1}+\frac{1}{2 R_1}\right)[\text { from Eq. (i) }] \\
& H_p=\frac{3 V^{2}t}{2 R_1}
\end{aligned}$
But, $H_P=H_1$
$\therefore \frac{3 V^2 t}{2 R_1}=\frac{V^2}{R_1} \times 10 \times 60$
$\Rightarrow t=400 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The resistance of a wire at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $20 \Omega$ and at $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $60 \Omega$. At which temperature its resistance will be $25 \Omega$ ?
questions.solution:
Resistance, $R_t=R_0(1+\alpha t)$
$20=R_0(1+20 \alpha) \text { and } 60=R_0(1+500 \alpha)$
From this equation, we can find
$\begin{aligned} R_0 & =18.33 \Omega \\ \alpha & =4.54 \times 10^{-3} \\ R_t & =25 \Omega\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned}& \text { Again, } \quad R_t=R_0(1+\alpha t) \\
& 25=18.33\left(1+4.54 \times 10^{-3} t\right)\end{aligned}$
$\text { We find } t=80^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Consider four circuits shown in the figure below. In which circuit power dissipated is greatèst?
(Neglect the internal resistance of the power supply).
questions.solution:
Let each resistance is $R$
In choice (a) $P=\frac{E^2}{R_{\mathrm{eq}}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad P_1=\frac{E^2}{\frac{R}{2}}=\frac{2 E^2}{R}$
In choice (b) $\begin{aligned} R_{\mathrm{eq}} & =R+R=2 R \\ P_2 & =\frac{E^2}{2 R}=0.5 \frac{E^2}{R} \end{aligned}$
In choice (c) $R_{\mathrm{eq}}=\frac{R}{2}+R=\frac{3 R}{2}$ $P_3=\frac{2 E^2}{3 R}=0.6 \frac{E^2}{R}$
In choice (d) $\begin{aligned} R & =\frac{2 R \times R}{2 R+R}=\frac{2 R}{3} \\ P_4 & =\frac{3 E^2}{2 R}=1.5 \frac{E^2}{R} \end{aligned}$
So, $P_1$ is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The starter motor of a car draw a current $I=300 \mathrm{~A}$ from the battery of voltage 12 V . If the car starts only after 2 minutes, what is the energy drawn from the battery?
questions.solution:
Energy drawn from the battery
$\begin{aligned}
E & =V I t \\
& =12 \times 300 \times 2 \times 60 \\
& =432 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J} \\
& =432 \mathrm{~kJ}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A uniform wire of resistance $9 \Omega$ is cut into 3 equal parts. They are connected in the form of equilateral triangle $A B C$. A cell of emf 2 V and negligible inernal resistance is connected across B and C. Potential difference across $A B$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{r}R^{\prime}=\frac{9}{3}=3 \Omega \\ R_{\text {net }}=2 \Omega\end{array}$ PD across AB $V^{\prime}=i \times R^{\prime}=\frac{V}{R_{\text {net }}} \times R=\frac{2}{2} \times 3=3 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A battery of emf 12 V and internal resistance $2 \Omega$ is connected in series with a tangent galvanometer of resistance $4 \Omega$. The deflection is $60^{\circ}$ when the plane of the coil is along the magnetic meridian. To get a deflection of $30^{\circ}$, the resistance to be connected in series with the tangent galvanometer is
questions.solution:
$I=k \tan \theta, k$ being reduction factor and $I \propto \frac{1}{R}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{R+r}{(R+r)+R^{\prime}} =\frac{\tan \theta^{\prime}}{\tan \theta} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{4+2}{6+R^{\prime}} =\frac{\tan 30^{\circ}}{\tan 60^{\circ}} \\ & \Rightarrow R^{\prime} =12 \Omega \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperature coefficient of resistance of a wire is $0.00125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$. At 300 K , its resistance is $1 \Omega$. At what temperature the resistance of the wire will be $2 \Omega$ ?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a potentiometer experiment two cells of emf $E_1$ and $E_2$ are used in series and in conjunction and the balancing length is found to be 58 cm of the wire. If the polarity of $E_2$ is reversed, then the balancing length becomes 29 cm . The ratio $\frac{E_1}{E_2}$ of the emfs of the two cells is
questions.solution:
$E_1+E_2=k \times 58 \ldots(i)$
$E_1-E_2=k \times 29 \ldots(i i)$
$\therefore \frac{E_1+E_2}{E_1-E_2}=2$
$\Rightarrow \frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{3}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire of length 100 cm is connected to a cell of emf 2 V and negligible internal resistance. The resistance of the wire is $3 \Omega$, the additional resistance required to produce a potential difference of $1 \mathrm{mV} / \mathrm{cm}$ is
questions.solution:
Resistance per unit length of wire $x=\frac{3}{100}=3 \times 10^{-2} \Omega \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}$
Equivalent length of wire $l^{\prime}=\frac{R+3}{3 \times 10^{-2}}$ $R$ being additional resistance $\begin{array}{rlrl} & & \frac{E}{k} & =\frac{R+3}{3 \times 10^{-2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{2}{1 \times 10^{-3}} & =\frac{R+3}{3 \times 10^{-2}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad & R & =57 \Omega \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric motor operates on a 50 V supply and a current of 12 A . If the efficiency of the motor is $30 \%$, what is the resistance of the winding of the motor ?
questions.solution:
$I=\frac{E-e}{R}$...(i)
and $\eta \times 100=\left(\frac{e}{E}\right) \times 100$...(ii)
$\begin{array}{lll}\therefore & 30 & =\frac{e}{50} \times 100 \\ \Rightarrow & e & =15 \mathrm{~V} \\ \text { Thus, } & R & =\frac{50-15}{12}=2.9 \Omega\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure a carbon resistor has bands of different colours on its body as mentioned in the figure. The value of the resistance is
questions.solution:
\begin{array}{cccc} White & Brown & Red & Silver \\9, & 1 & 10^2 & 10 \%\end{array}
$i e, \quad R=9.1 \mathrm{k} \Omega$ with tolerance $\pm 10 \%$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The magnitude of current $i$ in ampere unit is
questions.solution:
Applying Kirchhoff's law in the given figure. At junction $A$ $i+i_1+i_2=1$...(i) For loop (1) $\Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned} &-60 i+(15+5) i_1=0 \\ & i i_1=3 i\end{aligned}$....(ii) For loop (ii) $\begin{aligned} & \\ \Rightarrow & -(15+5) i_1+10 i_2 \end{aligned}=0$
On solving Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we get $i=0.1 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three $2 \Omega$ resistors are connected to form a triangle. The resistance between any two corners is
questions.solution:
In triangle formation, two resistors are in series and their sum is connected in parallel. Therefore,
$\begin{aligned} & R^{\prime}=2 \Omega+2 \Omega=4 \Omega \\ & \frac{1}{R^{\prime \prime}}=\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{2}=\frac{1+2}{4}=\frac{3}{4} \Omega \\ & R^{\prime \prime}=\frac{4}{3} \Omega\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cell of emf $E$ is connected across a resistance $R$. The potential difference between the terminals of the cell is found to be $V$. The internal resistance of the cell must be
questions.solution:
Internal resistance $(r)$ is given by
Also,
$\begin{aligned}
& r=\frac{E-V}{i} \\
& i=\frac{V}{R}, \text { we have } \\
& r=\frac{E-V}{V / R} \Rightarrow r=R\left(\frac{E}{V}-1\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron at rest is accelerated through a potential difference of 200 V . If the electron acquires a velocity of $8.4 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$, its $\frac{e}{m}$ ratio is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{1}{2} m v^2=e V \\ & \Rightarrow  \frac{e}{m}=\frac{v^2}{2 V}\end{aligned}$
Given, $V=200$ volt, $v=8.4 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}$
$=8.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore \frac{e}{m}=\frac{\left(8.4 \times 10^6\right)^2}{2 \times 200}=1.76 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A voltmeter of resistance $998 \Omega$ is connected across a cell of emf 2 V and internal resistance $2 \Omega$. The potential difference across the voltmeter is
questions.solution:
From Ohm's law $i=\frac{E}{R+r}=\frac{2}{998+2}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$

Potential difference across the voltmeter is $V=i R=\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 998=1.996 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $10 \%$ of the main current is to be passed through the moving coil galvanometer of resistance $99 \Omega$, then the required shunt resistance will be
questions.solution:
Here, $I_g=\frac{I}{10}, G=99 \Omega$
$\because$ The shunt, $\quad S=\frac{I_g}{I-I_g} \cdot G$
$\therefore \quad S=\frac{I / 10}{I-\frac{I}{10}} \times 99$
$\Rightarrow \quad S=11 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the adjoining circuit, the resistances are given in ohm, $X$ and $Y$ are unknown resistances. The current through the $10 \Omega$ resistance is 3A while that through the resistance $X$ is 1 A . No current passes through the galvanometer. The values of the unknown resistances $X$ and $Y$ are respectively
questions.solution:
When no current passes through the galvanometer, the bridge is balanced hence,
$\begin{aligned}
& \frac{P}{Q}=\frac{R}{S} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{24+X}{10}=\frac{84+Y}{30}
\end{aligned}$
Also, applying Kirchhoff's law
$\begin{aligned}
\sum i R & =\Sigma i^{\prime} R^{\prime} (parallel)\\
(X+24)+(84)+Y & =3(10+30) \\
\Rightarrow  X+Y & =12 .....(i)\\
\text { and }  3 X-Y & =12 ....(ii)
\end{aligned}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$X=6, Y=6 \text { (neglecting negative value) }$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
When 1 g hydrogen
( $E C E=1.044 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ ) forms water, 34 kcal heat is liberated. The minimum voltage required to decompose water is :
questions.solution:
$q=\frac{m}{z}=\frac{1 \times 10^{-3}}{1.044 \times 10^{-8}}=\frac{10^5}{1.044} C$
Given, $\mathrm{H}=34 \mathrm{k}-\mathrm{cal}=34 \times 10^3 \times 4.2 \mathrm{~J}$ Heat produced, $H=V q$ $\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad 34 \times 10^3 \times 4.2=\frac{V \times 10^5}{1.044} \\ & \therefore \quad V=\frac{34 \times 10^3 \times 4.2 \times 1.044}{10^5}=1.5 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a potentiometer experiment for measuring the emf of a cell, the null point is at 240 cm when we have a $500 \Omega$ resistor in series with the cell and galvanometer. If the series resistance reduced to half, then the null point will be at
questions.solution:
The resistance in series with galvanometer does not affect the null point.
So, it will obtained at 240 cm .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: . A coil of wire of resistance $50 \Omega$ is embedded in a black of ice and a potential difference of 210 V is applied across it. The amount of ice which melts in 1 s is :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Heat produced }=\frac{V^2 t}{4.2 R}=m L \\ & \text { or } m=\frac{V^2 t}{4.2 R L}=\frac{\left.(210)^2 \times 1\right)}{4.2 \times 50 \times 80}=2.62 \mathrm{~g}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: We have a galvanometer of resistance $25 \Omega$. It is shunted by $2.5 \Omega$ wire. The part of the total current that flows through the galvanometer is given as
questions.solution:
When a galvanometer is connected to the shunt resistance, then we have potential drop across the galvanometer = potential drop across the shunt
i.e.
$i G=\left(i-i_0\right) S$   ...(i)
Here, $i=$ current through the galvanometer
$G=$ resistance of the galvanometer
$i_0=$ current through the shunt resistance
$S=$ shunt resistance
From Eq. (i), we have the part of the total current that flows through the galvanometer is
$\frac{i}{i_0}=\frac{S}{G+S}$
Substituting, $S=2.5 \Omega, G=25 \Omega$, we get
$\frac{i}{i_0}=\frac{1}{11}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil having $n$ turns and resistance $R \Omega$ is connected with a galvanometer of resistance $4 R \Omega$. This combination is moved in time $t$ second from a magnetic field $w_1$ weber to $w_2$ weber. The induced current in the circuit is
questions.solution:
Current is given by $I=\frac{E}{R^{\prime}}$
Substituting, $E=-\frac{n d \phi}{d t}$, we get $I=-\frac{n}{R^{\prime}} \cdot \frac{d \phi}{d t}$ ...(i) Given that $w_1$ and $w_2$ are the values of the flux associated with one turn of the coil.
Therefore, Eq. (i) becomes, $I=-\frac{n}{R^{\prime}}\left[\frac{w_2-w_1}{t_2-t_1}\right]$ ...(ii) Total resistance of the combination is $R^{\prime}=R+4 R=5 R$, substituting the values of $R^{\prime}$ and $\left(t_2-t_1\right)=t$ in Eq. (ii), we get $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad & I=-\frac{n}{5 R}\left(\frac{w_2-w_1}{t}\right) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & I=\frac{-n\left(w_2-w_1\right)}{5 R t}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the equivalent resistance across $A B$
questions.solution:
$R_1$ and $R_2$ are in parallel order, so their equivalent resistance $R^{\prime}=\frac{2 \times 2}{2+2}=1 \Omega$
Similarly, equivalent resistance of $R_3$ and $R_4$ $R^{\prime \prime}=1 \Omega$
$R^{\prime}$ and $R^{\prime \prime}$ are in series order, so their equivalent resistance $\begin{aligned} R^{\prime \prime \prime} & =R^{\prime}+R^{\prime \prime} \\ & =1+1=2 \Omega \end{aligned}$ $R^{\prime \prime \prime}$ and $R_5$ are in parallel. So, $\quad R_{A B}=\frac{2 \times 2}{2+2}=1 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the following circuit reading of voltmeter $V$ is
questions.solution:
PD between $X$ and $Y$ is $V_{X Y}=V_X-V_Y=1 \times 4$ and PD between $X$ and $Z$ is $V_{x Z}=V_x-V_Z=1 \times 16=16 \mathrm{~V}$
Now, we get potential difference between $Y$ and $Z$ i.e., reading of voltmeter is $V_Y-V_Z=12 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of a potentiometer wire is 1 . A cell of emf $E$ is balanced at length $1 / 3$ from the positive end of the wire. If the length of the wire is increased by $1 / 2$. Then, at what distance will the same cell give a balance point?
questions.solution:
Let $x$ be the desired length.
Potential gradient in the first case $=\frac{E_0}{l}$ $E=\left(\frac{1}{3}\right) \cdot\left(\frac{E_0}{1}\right)=\frac{E_0}{3}$ ...(i) Potential gradient in the second case. $=\frac{E_0}{31 / 2}=\frac{2 E_0}{31}$ $\therefore \quad E=(x) \frac{2 E_0}{31}$ ...(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\frac{E_0}{3}=\left(\frac{2 E_0}{31}\right) x$ $\Rightarrow \quad x=\frac{1}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Consider the cylindrical element as shown in the figure. Current flowing the through element is $I$ and resistivity of material of the cylinder is $\rho$. Choose the correct option out the following.
options: Power loss in first half is four times the power loss in second half
Voltage drop in first half is twice of voltage drop in second half
Current density in both halves are equal
Electric field in both halves is equal
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \frac{R_1}{R_2}=\frac{A_1}{A_2}= & \frac{4}{1} \\ & \frac{P_1}{P_2}=\frac{I^2 R_1}{I^2 R_2}=\frac{4}{1} \Rightarrow \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{I R_1}{I R_2}=\frac{4}{1} \\ & \frac{J_1}{J_2}=\frac{1}{4}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A voltmeter of resistance $998 \Omega$ is connected across a cell of emf 2 V and internal resistance $2 \Omega$. The potential difference, across the voltmeter is
questions.solution:
