From Ohm's law $i=\frac{E}{R+r}=\frac{2}{998+2}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$
Potential difference across the voltmeter is $\begin{aligned} V & =i R=\left(2 \times 10^{-3}\right) \times 998 \\ & =1.996 \mathrm{~V} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: As the temperature of hot junction increases, the thermo emf
questions.solution: if the temperature of the hot junction is increased, the thermo e.m.f. may either increase or decrease depending upon the temperature.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If 1 A of current is passed through $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$ solution for 10 s , the number of copper atoms deposite at the cathode will be about
questions.solution:
Number of Cu ions liberated is
$\begin{array}{rl}
n & =\frac{I \cdot t}{e(\text { valence electron) }} \\
n & =\frac{1 \times 10}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2} \\
n & n=3.125 \times 10^{19} \\
\therefore \quad n & \approx 3.1 \times 10^{19}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $10 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ capacitor is charged to a potential difference of 50 V and is connected to another uncharged capacitor in parallel. Now the common potential difference becomes 20 V . The capacitance of second capacitor is
questions.solution:
$C_1=10 \mu \mathrm{~F}, V=50 \mathrm{~V}$
Charge on first capacitor $\begin{aligned} q & =C_1 V \\ & =10 \times 50=500 \mu \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$ Common potential $V=\frac{\text { total charge }}{\text { total capacitance }}$ $\therefore \quad 20=\frac{500}{C_1+C_2}$ or $20=\frac{500}{10+C_2}$ or $C_2=15 \mu \mathrm{~F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A galvanometer of resistance $20 \Omega$ is to be converted into an ammeter of range 1 A . If a current of 1 mA produces full scale deflection, the shunt required for the purpose is
questions.solution: $S=\frac{I_g G}{I-I_g}=\frac{10^{-3} \times 20}{1-1 \times 10^{-3}}=0.02 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $10 \%$ of the current passes through a moving coil galvanometer of resistance $99 \Omega$. Then the shunt resistance will be
questions.solution:
Resistance of galvanometer $G=99 \Omega$ Resistance of shunt $S=\frac{i_g G}{i-i_g}$
$=\frac{10 \times 99}{100-10}=11 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At what temperature will the resistance of a copper wire become three times its value at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? (temperature coefficient of resistance for copper $=4 \times 10^{-3} /{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ )
questions.solution:
From definition of temperature resistance coefficient
$\begin{aligned} R_t & =R_0(1+\alpha t) \\ 3 R_0 & =R_0\left(1+4 \times 10^{-3} t\right) \\ 3 & --1+4 \times 10^{-3} t \\ 4 & \times 10^{-3} t=2 \\ t & =\frac{2}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A current of 1.6 A is passed through a solution of $\mathrm{CuSO}_4$. How many $\mathrm{Cu}^{++}$ions are liberated in one minute?
(electronic charge $=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$ )
questions.solution:
$i=1.6 \mathrm{~A}, t=1$ minute $=60 \mathrm{~s}$
Charge on one $\mathrm{Cu}^{++}=2 e$ Let $n \mathrm{Cu}^{++}$are liberated from $\begin{aligned} Q & =n e \\ i t & =n(2 e) \\ 1.6 \times 60 & =n\left(2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right) \\ n & =3 \times 10^{20} \text { ions }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the electrical network shown in the figure, the potential difference across $3 \Omega$ resistance will be
questions.solution:
As $3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$ resistances are in parallel their equivalent resistance will be $2 \Omega$. Here $2 \Omega$ and $4 \Omega$ are in series, their equivalent resistance will be $6 \Omega$.
From current distribution law $\begin{aligned} & i_1=\frac{12 \times 18}{18}=12 \mathrm{~A} \\ & i_2=\frac{6 \times 18}{18}=6 \mathrm{~A} \end{aligned}$
Now 12 A current is entering in parallel combination of $3 \Omega$ and $6 \Omega$ again from current distribution law $i_1^{\prime}=\frac{6 \times 12}{9}=8 \mathrm{~A}$ $i_2^{\prime}=\frac{3 \times 12}{9}=4 \mathrm{~A}$ $\therefore$ Potential difference across $3 \Omega$ resistance $=8 \times 3=24 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical cells whether connected in parallel or in series gives the same current when connected to an external resistance $1.5 \Omega$. Find the value of internal resistance of each cell
questions.solution:
Let $n$ cells be in series and $m$ in parallel, then
$\frac{n E}{R+n r}=\frac{E}{R+\frac{r}{m}}$
$\Rightarrow n\left[R+\frac{r}{m}\right]=R+n r$
$\Rightarrow n R m+n r=R m+m n r$
$\Rightarrow 6+2 r=3+4 r$
$\Rightarrow 2 r=3$
$\Rightarrow R=1.5 \Omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A voltmeter has resistance of $G$ ohms and range $V$ volts. The value of resistance used in series to convert it into a voltmeter of range $n V$ volts is
questions.solution:
Suppose resistance $R$ is connected in series with voltmeter as shown. By Ohm's law
$\Rightarrow R =(n-1) G \\ \text { where } i_g=\frac{V}{G}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A battery of emf 10 V is connected to resistance as shown in figure. The potential difference $V_A-V_B$ between the points $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
$R_{\mathrm{eq}}=5 \Omega$, current $i=\frac{10}{5}=2 \mathrm{~A}$ and current in each branch $=1 \mathrm{~A}$. Potential difference between $C$ and $A$,
$V_C-V_A=1 \times 1=1 \mathrm{~V}...(i)$ Potential difference between $C$ and $B$ $V_C-V_B=1 \times 3=3 V...(ii)$ On solving Eqs. (i) and (ii) we get $V_A-V_B=2 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bulbs 40 W and 60 W and rated voltage 240 V are connected in series across a potential difference of 420 V . Which bulb will work at above its rated voltage?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} \text { Resistance of } 40 \mathrm{Wbulb} & =\frac{240 \times 240}{40} \\ & =1440 \Omega\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Its safe current }=\frac{240}{1440} \\ & =0.167 \mathrm{~A} \\ & \begin{aligned} \text { Resistance of } 60 \mathrm{~W} \text { bulb } & =\frac{240 \times 240}{60} \\ & =960 \Omega\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
Its safe current $=\frac{240}{960}=0.25 \mathrm{~A}$
When connected in series to 420 V supply, then the current
$\begin{aligned} i=\frac{420}{1440+960} & =\frac{420}{2400} \\ & =0.175 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$
Thus, current is greater for 40 W bulb, so it will fuse.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Under what conditions current passing through the resistance $R$ can be increased by short circuiting the battery of emf $E_2$ ? The internal resistances of the two batteries are $r_1$ and $r_2$ respectively.
questions.solution:
The current through the circuit before the battery of emf $E_2$ is short circuited, is
$i_1=\frac{E_1+E_2}{r_1+r_2+R}$   ...(i)
After short circuiting the battery of emf $E_2$, current through the resistance $R$ would be
$i_2=\frac{E_1}{R+I_1}$   ...(ii)
Since, from Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$i_2 \gt i_1$
$\therefore \quad \frac{E_1}{R+r_1} \gt \frac{E_1+E_2}{R+r_1+r_2}$
This gives $\quad E_1 r_2 \gt E_2\left(R+r_1\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum current that flow in the fuse wire before it blows out, varies with the radius $r$ as
questions.solution:
$A s, I^2 R=h A$
$P^2\left(\frac{\rho L}{\pi r^2}\right)=h \times 2 \pi r l, \quad l \propto r^{3 / 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Effective capacitance between $A$ and $B$ in the figure shown is (all capacitances are in $\mu \mathrm{F}$ )
questions.solution:
The points $C$ and $D$ will be at same potenitals since, $\frac{3}{6}=\frac{4}{8}$. Therefore, capacitance of $2 \mu \mathrm{~F}$ will be unaffected. So, the equivalent circuit can be shown as the effective capacitance in upper arm in series, is given by
$C_1=\frac{3 \times 6}{3+6}=\frac{18}{9}=2 \mu \mathrm{~F}$
The effective capacitance in lower arm in series, is given by $C_2=\frac{4 \times 8}{4+8}=\frac{32}{12}=\frac{8}{3} \mu \mathrm{~F}$
Hence, the resultant capacitance in parallel is given by $C=C_1+C_2=2+\frac{8}{3}=\frac{14}{3} \mu \mathrm{~F}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two conductors of the same material have their diameters in the ratio 1:2 and their lengths in the ratio $2: 1$. If the temperature difference between their ends is the same, then the ratio of amounts of heat conducted per second through them will be
questions.solution:
The resistance of conductor or $\quad R \propto \frac{l}{r^2}$ $R=\frac{\rho l}{A}=\frac{\rho l}{\pi r^2}$ $\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \frac{R_1}{R_2} & =\frac{l_1}{l_2} \times \frac{r_2^2}{r_1^2} \\ & =\frac{2}{1} \times\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^2=\frac{8}{1} \end{aligned}$
Thermal potentials between the ends of the rods are same. So, heat conducted per second $\begin{aligned} & H=\frac{Q}{t} \propto \frac{1}{R} \\ \therefore & \frac{H_1}{H_2}=\frac{R_2}{R_1}=\frac{1}{8}=1: 8 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following statements is wrong?
options: Ammeter is placed in parallel across the conductor in a circuit
Voltmeter is placed in parallel across the conductor in a circuit
questions.solution: Ammeter is always connected in series with circuit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron, helium ion $\left(\mathrm{He}^{++}\right)$and proton having the same kinetic energy. The relation between their respective de-Broglie wavelengths $\lambda_\theta$, $\lambda_{\mathrm{He}^{++}}$and $\lambda_p$ is
questions.solution:
de-Broglie wavelength is given by
$\begin{array}{l} 
\lambda =\frac{h}{p}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m k}} \\
\lambda \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{m}} \quad\left(\because k_e=k_p=k_{\mathrm{He}^{++}}\right) \\
\text {As, } m_{\mathrm{He}^{++}} \gt m_p \gt m_\theta \\
\therefore \lambda_{\mathrm{He}^{++}}  \lt \lambda_p \lt \lambda_\theta \\
\lambda_\theta \gt \lambda_p \gt \lambda_{\mathrm{He}^{++}}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The stopping potential for photoelectric emission from a metal surface is plotted along $Y$-axis and frequency $v$ of incident light along $X$-axis. A straight line is obtained as shown. Planck's constant is given by
options: product of slope of the line and charge on the electron
intercept along $Y$-axis divided by charge on the electron
product of intercept along $X$-axis and mass of the electron
questions.solution:
Einstein's equation is $V=\frac{h \nu}{e}-\frac{h \nu_0}{e}$
Here, slope of line $=\frac{h}{e}$ $\Rightarrow h=$ Planck's constant $=$ slope $\times \mathcal{e}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photon and an electron have equal energy $E \cdot \lambda_{\text {photon }} / \lambda_{\text {electron }}$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda_{\text {photon }}=\frac{h c}{E} \\ & \lambda_{\text {photon }}=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 m E}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{\lambda_{\text {photon }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}}=c \sqrt{\frac{2 m}{E}} \\ & \frac{\lambda_{\text {photon }}}{\lambda_{\text {electron }}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{E}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\lambda$ is the incident wavelength and $\lambda_0$ is the threshold wavelength for a metal surface, photoelectric effect takes place only, if
questions.solution: For photoelectric effect to take place,' the incident wavelength $(\lambda)$ should be equal to or smaller than the threshold wavelength $\left(\lambda_0\right)$ i.e., \(\lambda \leq \lambda_0\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the frequency of incident photon on metal surface is doubled, then stopping potential will become
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$h f=W_0+e V_0...(i)$
If frequency is doubled, then
$\begin{aligned}
h(2 f) & =W_0+e V_0^{\prime} \\
2 h f & =W_0+e V_0^{\prime}...(ii)
\end{aligned}$
Substituting hf from Eq. (i) in Eq. (ii), we have, 
or $V_0^{\prime}=2 V_0+\frac{W_0}{e}$
or $V_0^{\prime}\gt2 V_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons coming out of a metal surface is 10 eV . The minimum voltage required to stop, the emission of electrons from this metal surface is
questions.solution:
So, the graph between acceleration and time is a line parallel to time axis as acceleration is constant.
Here, $\quad K_{\max }=10 \mathrm{eV}$
So, the stopping potential, $e V_0=K_{\text {max }}$
$\Rightarrow \quad e V_0=10 \mathrm{eV}$
or $\quad V_0=-10 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Photons of 5.5 eV energy fall on the surface of the metal emitting photoelectrons of maximum kinetic energy 4.0 eV . The stopping voltage required for these electrons is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What will be the kinetic energy of an electron having de-Broglie wavelength $2 Å$ ?
questions.solution:
The de-Broglie wavelength associated with electron
$\lambda_\theta=\frac{1.227}{\sqrt{V}} \mathrm{~nm}$
$\begin{aligned} V & =\frac{(12.27)^2}{\lambda_e^2} \times 10^{-2} \\ & =\frac{150}{\lambda_e^2} \times 10^{-2}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow \quad V=\frac{150}{(0.2)^2}$
$=\frac{150 \times 10^{-2}}{4 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\therefore \quad E=\mathrm{V} \mathrm{eV}=37.5 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The wavelength of incident light falling on a photosensitive surface is changed from $2000 Å$ to $2100 Å$. The corresponding change in stopping potential is
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} \lambda_1=2000 Å & =2000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
$\lambda_2=2100 Å$
$=2.1 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{h c}{\lambda_1}=W+e V_0\ldots$ (i)
and$\frac{h c}{\lambda_2}=W+e V_0^{\prime}\ldots$ (ii)
Subtracting Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
$h c\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right)=e\left(V_0-V_0^{\prime}\right)$
Change in stopping potential
$\Delta V=V_0-V_0^{\prime}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{h c}{e}\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \\ & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{16 \times 10^{-19}}\left(\frac{1}{2 \times 10^{-7}}-\frac{1}{2.1 \times 10^{-7}}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{6.6 \times 3}{16}\left(\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{2.1}\right) \\ & =\frac{6.6 \times 3 \times 0.1}{16 \times 2 \times 2.1}=0.3 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In photoelectric effect, the photo current
options: increases with increase of frequency of incident photon
decreases with increase of frequency of incident photon
does not depend on the frequency of photon but depends only on intensity of is cident light
depends both on intensit, and frequency of incident beam
questions.solution: When intensity of incident photon increases number of electrons emitted from the surface increases. Thus current increases. It is independent of frequency of photon.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoemissive cell with exciting wave length $\lambda$, the fastest electron has a speed $v$. If the exciting wavelength is changed to $3 \lambda / 4$, then the speed of the fastest emitted electron will be
questions.solution:
From Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W\ldots$ (i)
and $\quad \frac{1}{2} m v^{\prime 2}=\frac{h c}{3 \lambda / 4}-W\ldots$ (ii)
On dividing Eq. (ii) by Eq. (i), we get
$\left(\frac{v^{\prime}}{v}\right)^2=\frac{\frac{4 h c}{3 \lambda}-W}{\frac{h c}{\lambda}-W}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\because & W \gt 0 \\ \therefore & v^{\prime} \gt v\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Out of a photon and an electron, the equation $E=P C$, is valid for
questions.solution:
Energy is given by
$E=\frac{m_0 c^2}{\sqrt{1-\frac{v^2}{c^2}}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad E^2=\frac{m_0^2 c^6}{c^2-v^2}$
Momentum $P$ is given by
$P=\frac{m_0 v}{\sqrt{1-\left(\frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)}}$
$P^2 c^2=\frac{m_0^2 c^4 v^2}{c^2-v^2}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore & E^2-P^2 c^2 & =m_0^2 c^4 \\ \Rightarrow & & E^2 & =P^2 c^2+m_0^2 c^4\end{aligned}$
For photon, rest mass
$m_0=0, \quad$ so $E=P c$
For electron, $\quad m_0 \neq 0$, so $E \neq P c$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoemissive cell with exciting wavelength $\lambda$, the fastest electron has speed $v$. If the exciting wavelength is changed by $3 \lambda / 4$, the speed of the fastest emitted electron will be
questions.solution:
From $E=W_0+\frac{1}{2} m v^2 \max \Rightarrow v_{\max }=\sqrt{\frac{2 E}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m}}$ (where $E=\frac{h \mathrm{c}}{\lambda}$ ) If wavelength of incident light charges from $\lambda$ to $\frac{3 \lambda}{4}$ (decreases). Let energy of incident light charges from $E$ to $E^{\prime}$ and speed of fastest electron changes from $v$ to $v$ then $\begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{\frac{2 E}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m}} ...(i)\\ \text { and } \quad v & =\sqrt{\frac{2 E^{\prime}}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m}}...(ii) \end{aligned}$
and As $\quad E \propto \frac{1}{\lambda} \Rightarrow E^{\prime}=\frac{4}{3} E$ Hence, $\quad \quad \quad=\sqrt{\frac{2\left(\frac{4}{3} E\right)}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad v^{\prime}=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{1 / 2} \sqrt{\frac{2 E}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{1 / 2}}}$ $\Rightarrow \quad V=\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{1 / 2} X=\sqrt{\frac{2 E}{m}-\frac{2 W_0}{m\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{1 / 2}}} \gt v$ So $\quad v^{\prime}\gt\left(\frac{4}{3}\right)^{1 / 2} v$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Threshold wavelength of a metal is $4000 \mathrm{~A}^{\circ}$. It light of wavelength $3000 Å$ irradiates the surface, the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron is
questions.solution:
If the maximum kinetic energy of photoelectron emitted from the surface of a metal is $E_K$ and $W$ is the work function of the metal, the from Einstein's photoelectric equation we have
$E_K=h v-W$
where, $h v$ is the energy of the photon absorbed by the electron in the metal.
Also $\quad v=\frac{c}{\lambda}$
$=\frac{\text { velocity }}{\text { wavelength }}$
$=E_K=\frac{h c}{\lambda}-\frac{h c}{\lambda_0}=h c\left(\frac{1}{\lambda}-\frac{1}{\lambda_0}\right)$
Given, $\begin{aligned} \lambda & =3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m} \\ \lambda_0 & =4 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m} .\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad E_K=6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times & 3 \times 10^8 \\ & \times\left(\frac{1}{3 \times 10^{-7}}-\frac{1}{4 \times 10^{-7}}\right) \mathrm{J}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{19.8 \times 10^{-19}}{12 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \mathrm{eV}$
$=1.03 \approx 1 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electron gun, the control grid is given a negative potential, relative to cathode in order to
questions.solution: In an electron gun, the control grid is given a negative potential relative to cathode in order to repel the electrons so that a converging beam of electrons emerges from gun.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the work function of a potential is 6.875 eV , its threshold wavelength will be (Take $=c=3 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ )
questions.solution:
$W=\frac{h c}{\lambda}$
where $h$ is planck's constant, c is speed of light, $\lambda$ is wavelength
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda=\frac{h c}{W}=\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{6.825 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ & \lambda=1800 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: de-Broglie wavelength of a body of mass 1 kg moving with velocity of $2000 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \lambda=\frac{h}{m v} & =\frac{6.6 \times 10^{-34}}{1 \times 2000} \\ & =3.3 \times 10^{-37} \mathrm{~m}=3.3 \times 10^{-27} Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An atom of mass $M$ which is in the state of rest emits a photon of wavelength $\lambda$. As a result, the atom will deflect with the kinetic energy equal to ( $h$ is Planck's constant)
questions.solution:
Momentum of photon, $p=\frac{h}{\lambda}$ Kinetic energy of photon of mass $M, K=\frac{p^2}{2 M}$ 
or $\quad K=\frac{h^2}{2 M \lambda^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rest energy of an electron is 0.511 MeV . The electron is accelerated from rest to a velocity 0.5 c . The change in its energy will be
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's equation relation between rest energy and moving energy is
$E=E_0\left(1-\frac{v^2}{c^2}\right)^{-1 / 2}$
Given, $\quad v=0.5 c$
$\therefore \quad E=E_0\left(1-\frac{(0.5 c)^2}{c^2}\right)^{-1 / 2}$
$\begin{aligned} E & =E_0(1-0.25)^{-1 / 2} \\ & =\frac{E_0}{\sqrt{0.75}}\end{aligned}$
Change in energy $\Delta E=E-E_0$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{E_0}{\sqrt{0.75}}-E_0 \\ & =E_0\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.75}}-1\right) \\ & =0.511\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{0.75}}-1\right) \\ & =0.079 \mathrm{MeV}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A p-type semiconductor has acceptor levels 57 meV above the valence band. The maximum wavelength of light required to create a hole
questions.solution:
$E=57 \mathrm{meV}=57 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{eV}$
$\therefore \quad \lambda=\frac{12375}{E}=\frac{12375}{57 \times 10^{-3}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =217.105 \times 10^3 Å \\ \lambda & =217105 Å\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a neon discharge tube $2.9 \times 10^{18} \mathrm{Ne}^{+}$ions move to the right each second while $1.2 \times 10^{18}$ electrons move to the left per second, electrons charge is $1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$. The current in the discharge tube is
questions.solution:
Direction of current is opposite to the direction of flow of electron and in the same direction of positive ion.
Total current $I=I_{\mathrm{Ne}}+I_{\text {electron }}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(2.9 \times 10^{18}+1.2 \times 10^{18}\right) e \\ & =4.1 \times 10^{18} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \\ & =0.66 \mathrm{~A}\end{aligned}$
Direction of current will be the direction of flow of $\mathrm{Ne}^{+}$ion i. e. towards right.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron is accelerated through a potential difference of 200 V . If $e / m$ for the electron be $1.6 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$, the velocity acquired by the electron will be
questions.solution:
$V=200 \mathrm{~V}, \frac{e}{m}=1.6 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$
Kinetic energy of electron $=\mathrm{eV}$ i.e, $\quad \frac{1}{2} m v^2=e V$
or $v^2=\frac{2 e}{m} V$
$\quad \begin{aligned} \text{or} \quad v & =\sqrt{2 \frac{e}{m} V} \\ & =\sqrt{2 \times 1.6 \times 10^{11} \times 200} \\ & =8 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Light of wavelength $2475 Å$ is incident on barium. Photoelectrons emitted describe a circle of radius 100 cm by a magnetic field of flux density $\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}} \times 10^{-5}$ tesla. The value of work function of the barium is ........ eV. . given, $\left.\frac{e}{m}=1.7 \times 10^{11}\right)$
questions.solution:
Radius of circular path described by a charged particle in a magnetic field B is given by
$r=\sqrt{\frac{2 m K}{q B}} \Rightarrow K=\frac{q^2 B^2 r^2}{2 m}=\left(\frac{e}{m}\right) \frac{e B^2 r^2}{2}$
where, $K=$ kinetic energy of a particle
$q=$ charge on a particle
$r=$ radius of circular path
$=\frac{1}{2} \times 1.7 \times 10^{11} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{17}} \times 10^{-5}\right)^2 \times(1)^2$
$=8 \times 10^{-20} \mathrm{~J}=0.5 \mathrm{eV}$
Using the relation,
$E=\phi+K_{\max } \Rightarrow \phi=E-K_{\max }$
where, $E=h v=\frac{h c}{\lambda}=\frac{12375}{\lambda(\text { in } Å)}$
$=\left(\frac{12375}{2475}\right) \mathrm{eV}-0.5 \mathrm{eV}=4.5 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton accelerated through a potential difference of 100 V , has de-Broglie wavelength $\lambda_0$. The de-Broglie wavelength of an $\alpha$-particle, accelerated through 800 V is
questions.solution:
As, $\lambda=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2 q V m}} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{q V m}}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & \frac{\lambda_\alpha}{\lambda_p}=\sqrt{\frac{q_p V_p m_p}{q_\alpha V_\alpha m_\alpha}}=\sqrt{\frac{1 \times 100 \times 1}{2 \times 800 \times 4}}=\frac{1}{8} \\ \therefore & \lambda_\alpha=\frac{\lambda_p}{8}=\frac{\lambda_0}{8}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The photoelectric cut-off voltage in an experiment was found to be 1.5 V . The work function for the material used in the experiment was 4.2 eV . The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons that emitted was
questions.solution:
If stopping potential is $V_0$, then maximum kinetic energy of photoelectrons is given by
$E_k=e V$
Given, $V=1.5$ volt
$E_k=1.5 \mathrm{eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The electric field of light wave is given is $E=10^3 \cos \left(\frac{2 \pi x}{5 \times 10^{-7}}-2 \pi \times 6 \times 10^{14} t\right) \hat{j} N / C .$
This light falls on a metal plate of work function 1.5 eV . The stopping potential of the photoelectron is $\qquad$ V . (Energy of photon $=\frac{1240}{\lambda(\text { in } \mathrm{nm})} \mathrm{eV}$ ).
questions.solution:
$E=E_0 \cos (k x-\omega t)$ $\therefore \quad \omega=2 \pi \times 6 \times 10^{14}$ $\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & f=\frac{\omega}{2 \pi}=\frac{2 \pi \times 6 \times 10^{14}}{2 \pi}=6 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & \phi=\text { Energy of photon }=\frac{1240}{\lambda(\text { in } \mathrm{nm})} \mathrm{eV} \\ \therefore & \lambda=\frac{c}{f}=\frac{3 \times 10^8}{6 \times 10^{14}}=0.5 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}=500 \mathrm{~nm} \\ \therefore & \phi=\frac{1240}{500} \mathrm{eV}=2.48 \mathrm{eV} \end{array}$
Now, by Einstein photoelectric equation, $\begin{array}{rlrl} & & \phi & =\phi_0+\mathrm{KE}=\phi_0+e V_s \quad\left(\because \mathrm{KE}=e V_s\right) \\ \Rightarrow & 2.48 & =1.5+e V_s \\ & & e V_s & =2.48-1.5=0.98 \mathrm{eV} \\ & V_s & =0.98 \mathrm{~V} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron of an atom transits from $n_1$ to $n_2$. In which of the following, maximum frequency of photon will be emitted?
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{ll}\text {As } & E_1 \gt E_2 \\ \therefore & v_1 \gt v_2\end{array}$
ie, photons of higher frequency will be emitted if transition takes place from $n=2$ to $n=1$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In wave mechanics, the angular momentum of an electron is given by
questions.solution:
In wave mechanics, the magnitude of angular momentum of an electron is
$L=\sqrt{l(l+1) \frac{h}{2 \pi}}$
where, $l$ can have the values $0,1,2 \ldots \ldots(n+1)$ $n$ is principal quantum number.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The minimum intensity of light to be detected by human eye is $10^{-10} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$. The number of photons of wavelength $5.6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$ entering the eye, with pupil area $10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^2$, per second for vision will be nearly
questions.solution:
On using $I=\frac{P}{A}$; where $P=$ radiation power $\Rightarrow \quad P=I \times A \Rightarrow \frac{n h c}{t \lambda}=I A \Rightarrow \frac{n}{t}=\frac{I A \lambda}{h c}$
Hence, number of photons entering per sec the eye $\left(\frac{n}{t}\right)=\frac{10^{-10} \times 10^{-6} \times 5.6 \times 10^{-7}}{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}=300$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The de-Broglie wavelength of a neutron at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $\lambda$. What will be its wavelength at $927^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \lambda_{\text {neutron }} \propto \frac{1}{\sqrt{T}} \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_2}{T_1}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2}=\sqrt{\frac{(273+927)}{(273+27)}}=\sqrt{\frac{1200}{300}}=2 \Rightarrow \lambda_2=\frac{\lambda}{2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the frequency of light which ejects electron from a metal surface fully stopped by a retarding potential of 3 V . The photoelectric effect begins in this metal at a frequency of $6 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}$.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,
$E=h v-w$
If $V_s$ is retarding or stopping potential and $v_0$ is the threshold frequency, then the above equation becomes
$\begin{aligned} e V_s & =h v-h v_0 \\ h v & =e V_s+h v_0 \\ v & =\frac{e V_s}{h}+v_0\end{aligned}$
Hence, $e=1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{C}$
$V_s=3 \mathrm{~V}, v_0=6 \times 10^{-14} \mathrm{~Hz}$
Therefore, required frequency,
$\begin{aligned} v & =\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 3}{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}+6 \times 10^{14} \\ & =\frac{4.8}{6.63} \times 10^{15}+6 \times 10^{14} \\ & =0.723 \times 10^{15}+6 \times 10^{14} \\ & =7.23 \times 10^{14}+6 \times 10^{14} \\ & =13.23 \times 10^{14} \mathrm{~Hz} \\ & =1.323 \times 10^{15} \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil of 100 turns carries a current of 5 mA and creates a magnetic flux of $10^{-5} \mathrm{wb}$. The inductance is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & L=\frac{\phi}{n I}=\frac{10^{-5}}{100 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}=\frac{10^{-2}}{100 \times 5} \\ = & \frac{10^{-4}}{5} \mathrm{H}=\frac{10^{-1}}{5} \mathrm{mH} \\ = & 0.02 \mathrm{mH}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bar magnet is released into a copper ring directly below it. The acceleration of the magnet will be
options: equal to the acceleration due to gravity at that place
less than the acceleration due to gravity at that place
greater than the acceleration due to gravity at that place
twice the acceleration due to gravity at that place
questions.solution: According to Lenz's law acceleration of the magnet will be less than the acceleration due to gravity due to the fact that emf induced in the ring opposes the motion of magnet.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Faraday constant
options: depends on the amount of the electrolyte
depends on the current in the electrolyte
depends on the amount of charge passed through the electrolyte
questions.solution: Faraday constant is a universal constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils $X$ and $Y$ are placed in a circuit such that a current changes by 3 A in coil $X$ and magnetic flux changes of 1.2 Wb occurs in $Y$. The value of mutual inductance of the coils is
questions.solution:
Given, $d \phi=1.2W b, M=?$
$d I=3 \mathrm{~A}$
As, $\quad \phi=M I$
$\therefore \quad d \phi=M d I$
$\therefore \quad M=\frac{d \phi}{d I}$
$=\frac{1.2}{3}=0.4 \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils each of self-inductance $L$ are connected in parallel. If they are separated by a large distance, then what will be the self-inductance of combination?
questions.solution:
In parallel combination, the resultant inductance $L_P$,
$\frac{1}{L_P}=\frac{1}{L_1}+\frac{1}{L_2}$
$=\frac{1}{L}+\frac{1}{L}=\frac{2}{L}$
$\Rightarrow \quad L_P=\frac{L}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short solenoid of length 4 cm , radius 2 cm and 100 turns is placed inside and on the axis of a long solenoid of length 80 cm and 1500 turns. A current of 3 A flows through the short solenoid. The mutual inductance of two solenoids is
questions.solution:
$M_{21}=\frac{\mu_0 N_1 N_2 A_2}{l_1}$
$\therefore M_{21}=\frac{\left(4 \times 3.14 \times 10^{-7}\right) \times 1500 \times 100\times\left\{3.14\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2\right\}}{80 \times 10^{-4}}$
$M_{21}=2.96 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{H}$
$\Rightarrow M_{12}=M_{21}=2.96 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{H}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A vertical disc of diameter 10 cm makes 20 revolutions per second about a horizontal axis passing through its centre. A uniform magnetic field $10^{-1} \mathrm{~T}$ acts perpendicular to the plane of the disc. If the potential difference between its centre and rim is found to be $\frac{\pi}{x} \times 10^{-p}$ Volt, then find the ratio of $x / p$.
questions.solution:
As we know, disc is equivalent to a rod of length $r$. Divide disc into rods, each rod can be replaced by a battery and these batteries are would be paralial in arrangement.
According to the question, radius of disc, $r=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~cm}=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$ Angular velocity, $\omega=20 \times 2 \pi=40 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$ Magnetic field, $B=10^{-1} \mathrm{~T}$ The emf of a disc is given as $e=\frac{1}{2} B \omega r^2=\frac{1}{2}\left(10^{-1}\right)(40 \pi)(0.05)^2$ $\Rightarrow \quad V_0-V_A=1.57 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~V}$ $=\frac{\pi}{2} \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~V}=\frac{\pi}{x} \times 10^{-p} \mathrm{~V}$ (given) $\therefore \quad \frac{x}{p}=\frac{2}{2}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An inductor is connected to a battary through a switch induced emf is $a$ when the switch is pressed and $e_2$ when the switch is opened. Then
questions.solution: When the switch is closed, the current grows in the circuit but due to self-inductance of the switch inductor the growth is slow. On disclosing the switch the current suddenly decrease to zero on account of the infinite resistance of the circuit. Thus, rate cf growth of the current is lower as compared to the rate of decays

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in a coil changes from 0 to 2 A in 0.05 s . If the induced emf is 80 V , the self-inductance of the coil is,
questions.solution:
From the formula, $\varepsilon=-L \frac{d I}{d t}$
Take magnitude on both sides.
$\begin{array}{ll} 
& |\varepsilon|=\left|-L \frac{d I}{d t}\right| \text { or } \varepsilon=L \frac{d I}{d t} \\
\therefore & 80=L\left(\frac{2}{0.05}\right) \Rightarrow 80=L \times 40 \\
\Rightarrow & L=\frac{80}{40} \Rightarrow L=2 \mathrm{H}
\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circuit area $0.01 \mathrm{~m}^2$ is kept inside a magnetic field which is normal to its plane. The magnetic field changes from 2 T to 1 T in 1 ms . If the resistance of the circuit is $2 \Omega$. The amount of heat evolved is
questions.solution:
Induced emf produced in coil
$e=\frac{-d \phi}{d t}=\frac{-d}{d t}(B A)$
$\therefore |e|=A \frac{d B}{d t}=0.01 \times \frac{1}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$
$|e|=10 \mathrm{~V}$
Current produced in coil,
$i=\frac{|e|}{R}=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~A}$
Heat evolved-
$\begin{aligned} i^2 R t & =(5)^2 \times(2) \times 1 \times 10^{-3} \\ & =0.05 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 10 m long copper wire while remaining in the east-west horizontal direction is falling down with a speed of $5.0 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field $=0.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ the emf developed between the ends of the wires is
questions.solution:
Given, $l=10 \mathrm{~m}, v=5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

and $\quad H=0.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ Induced emf $e=H v l$ $\begin{aligned} & =0.3 \times 10^{-4} \times 5 \times 10 \\ & =1.5 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~V} \\ & =1.5 \mathrm{mV}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: ECE of copper is $3.3 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{C}$. If 100 kWh energy is consumed at 33 V in a copper voltameter, then mass of copper liberated is
questions.solution:
Mass of copper liberated
$\begin{aligned}
m & =z i t=z\left(\frac{P}{V}\right) t \\
& =\left(3.3 \times 10^{-7}\right)\left(\frac{100 \times 36 \times 10^5}{33}\right) \\
= 3.6 kg
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil having an area of $2 \mathrm{~m}^2$ is placed in a magnetic field which changes from $1 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ to $4 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ in 2 s . The emf induced in the coil will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Induced emf } e=n A \frac{d B}{d t} \\ & A=\text { area of coil }=2 m^2 \\ & \text { and } \\ & \qquad e=\frac{n=1}{2}+\frac{1 \times(4-1)}{2}=3 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A disc of radius 0.1 m is rotating with a frequency $10 \mathrm{rev} / \mathrm{s}$ in a normal magnetic field of strength 0.1 T . Net induced emf is
questions.solution:
Net induced emf due to disc is
$\begin{aligned} \varepsilon & =\frac{1}{2} B \omega l^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 0.1 \times 2 \pi \times 10 \times 0.1 \times 0.1 \\ & =\pi \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Current in a coil changes from 5 A to 10 A in 0.2 s . If the coefficient of self-induction is 10 H , then the induced emf is
questions.solution:
Induced emf in the coil is given by
$e=\frac{L d i}{d t}$
or, $e=10 \times \frac{(10-5)}{0.2}$
$\Rightarrow e=250 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A physicist works in a laboratory where the magnetic field is 2 T . She wears a necklace enclosing area $0.01 \mathrm{~m}^2$ in such a way that the plane of the necklace is normal to the field and is having a resistance, $R=0.01 \Omega$. Because of power failure, the field decays to 1 T in time $10^{-3} \mathrm{~s}$. Then what is the total heat produced in her necklace?

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils have mutual inductance of 1.5 H . If current in primary coil is suddenly raised to 5 A in one milli second, the induced emf in the secondary coil is
questions.solution:
Magnetic flux linked with the secondary due to current in the primary.
$\phi_2=M I_1$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d \phi_2}{d t}=M \frac{d l_1}{d t}$
$\Rightarrow$ Induced emf in the secondary
$\varepsilon_2=\frac{d \phi_2}{d t}=M \frac{d l_1}{d t}$
where, $M=$ mutual inductance $=15 \mathrm{H}$
$\frac{d l_1}{d t}=\frac{5}{1 \times 10^{-3}}=5000 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{s}$
$\therefore \quad \varepsilon_2=-1.5 \times 5000 \mathrm{~V}$
$=7.5 \times 1000 V=7500 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time constant of $L-R$ circuit is
questions.solution:
The instantaneous current in $L-R$ circuit during growth is given by $I=I_0\left(1-e^{-\frac{R_t}{L}}\right)$
Since, all the exponential terms are dimensionless, therefore, it follows that factor $\frac{R}{L} t$ should be dimensionless i.e. $\frac{R}{L} t=\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]$ $\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{R}{L}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]}{[\mathrm{T}]}=\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right] \\ \Rightarrow & {\left[\frac{\mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{R}}\right]=[\mathrm{T}]}\end{array}$ Hence, the time constant of $L-R$ circuit is $\left[\frac{L}{R}\right]$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Find the inductance of a unit length of two parallel wires, each of radius $a$, whose centres are at distance $d$ apart and carry equal currents in opposite directions. Neglect the flux within the wire.
questions.solution:
Since, the wires are infinite, so the system of these two wires can be considered as closed rectangle of infinite length and breadth equal to $d$. Flux through strip $\phi=\int_a^{d-a} \frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi r}(l d r)=\frac{\mu_0 I}{2 \pi} \ln \left(\frac{d-a}{a}\right)$
The other wire produces the same result, so the total flux through the rectangle $A B C D$ is $\phi_{\text {total }}=\frac{\mu_0 I I}{\pi} \ln \left(\frac{d-a}{a}\right)$ The total inductance of length 1 $L=\frac{I_{\text {total }}}{I}=\frac{\mu_0 I}{\pi} \ln \left(\frac{d-a}{a}\right)$ Inductance per unit length $=\frac{L}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{\pi} \ln \left(\frac{d-a}{a}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coils have mutual inductance 0.005 H . The current changes in the first coil according to equation $I=I_0 \sin \omega t$, where $I_0=10 \mathrm{~A}$ and $\omega=100 \pi \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The maximum value of emf in the second coil is
questions.solution:
The instantaneous current in the AC circuit is given by $I=I_0 \sin \omega t$ $\begin{aligned} & \frac{d I}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(I_0 \sin \omega t\right) \\ & \frac{d I}{d t}=\left(I_0 \cos \omega t\right) \omega \end{aligned}$ Substituting $\quad I_0=10 \mathrm{~A}, \omega=100 \pi$ $\begin{aligned} \cos (100 \pi) & =1, \text { we get } \\ \frac{d I}{d t} & =1000 \pi \end{aligned}$
Hence, induced emf is given by $E=M \frac{d I}{d t}$
On putting $M=0.005 \mathrm{H}, \frac{d I}{d t}=1000 \pi$, we get $E=(5 \pi) \mathrm{V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid has an inductance of 60 H and a resistance of $30 \Omega$. If it is connected to a 100 V battery, how long will it take for the current to reach $\frac{e-1}{e}=63.2 \%$ of its final value?
questions.solution:
Given, $\begin{aligned} & L=60 \mathrm{H} \\ & R=30 \Omega \\ & V=100 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$
In $L-R$ circuit current to reach $63.2 \%$ of its final value in time constant. Equation of current in $L-R$ circuit $I=I_0\left(1-e^{-R t / L}\right)$ Required time $=$ time constant $=\frac{L}{R}=\frac{60}{30}=2 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: X-rays and $\gamma$-rays of the same energies may be distinguished by
questions.solution: The production of X-rays is an atomic property whereas the production of $\gamma$-rays is a nuclear property.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In an electromagnetic wave, the electric and magnetising fields are $100 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}$ and $0.265 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. The maximum energy flow is
questions.solution: Maximum rate of energy flow, $S=E_0 \times H_0$ Given, $E_0=100 \mathrm{~V} / \mathrm{m}, H_0=0.265 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$ $\therefore \quad S=100 \times 0.265=26.5 \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Thermal radiation exist in which part of electromagnetic spectrum?
questions.solution: Thermal radiations exists in infrared part of the electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A laser device produces amplification in the
questions.solution: A laser device produces amplification in the ultraviolet or visible region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The atomic number (Z) of an element whose $k_a$ wavelength is $\lambda$ is 11. The atomic number of an element whose $k_a$, wavelength is $4 \lambda$ is equal to
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & & (Z-1)^2 \lambda & =\text { constant } \\ & \therefore & \left(10^2\right) \lambda & =4 \lambda(Z-1)^2 \\ \Rightarrow & & Z & =6\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a colour of light the wavelength for air is $6000 Å$ and in water the wavelength is $4500 Å$. Then the speed of light in water will be
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { (b) We know that } v \propto \lambda \\ & \begin{array}{l}\Rightarrow \quad \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\frac{\lambda_1}{\lambda_2} \\ \therefore V_2=\frac{v_1}{\lambda_1} \times \lambda_2 \\ =3 \times 10^8 \times \frac{4500}{6000}=2.25 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{array}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In which one of the following regions of the electromagnetic spectrum will the vibrational motion of molecules give rise to absorption?
questions.solution: Molecular spectra due to vibrational motion lies in the microwave region of electromagnetic spectrum. Due to Kirchhoffs law in spectroscopy the same will be absorbed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: X-ray of wavelength $\lambda=2 Å$ is emitted from the metal target. The potential difference applied across the cathode and the metal target is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Electromagnetic waves travel only through
options: oscillating electric and magnetic fields whose directions are perpendicular to each other
questions.solution: Electromagnetic waves travel through oscillating electric and magnetic fields whose directions are perpendicular to each other.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The distance between charges $5 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{C}$ and $-2.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{C}$ is 0.2 m . The distance at which is third charge should be placed from $4 e$ in order that it will not experience any force along the line joining the two charges is
questions.solution:
From the question $F_1=F_2$ $5 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{C} \quad-2.7 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{C}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{5 \times 10^{-11} \times q}{(0.2+x)^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{2.7 \times 10^{-11} \times q}{x^2} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad x=0.556 \mathrm{~m} \text { from IInd charge. }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle of mass 0.003 g is held stationary in space by placing it in a downward direction of electric field of $6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C}$. Then the magnitude of the charge is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Given that $q_1+q_2=q$. For what ratio $q_1 / q_2$ will the force between $q_1$ and $q_2$ be maximum ?
questions.solution:
$F=\frac{q_1 q_2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}=\frac{q_1\left(q-q_1\right)}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2}$
For maximum force between $q_1$ and $\begin{array}{rlrl} & \frac{d F}{d q_1} & =0 \\ \therefore & q-2 q_1 & =0 \\ \text { or } & q_1 & =\frac{q}{2} & \text { or } \frac{q_1}{q}=0.5\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Due to the dipole shown in figure, the electric intensity will be parallel to the dipole axis at the point :
questions.solution: $\vec{E}$ is parallel to dipole axis, at both the points $P$ and $Q$, i.e. on axial line as well as equatorial line.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric charge in uniform motion produces
questions.solution: An electric charge in uniform motion produces both electric field and magnetic field.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting sphere of radius $R$ is charged to a potential of $V$ volt. Then the electric field at a distance $r( \gt R)$ from the centre of sphere would be :
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \quad A s V=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 R} \\ & \therefore \quad \frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=V R \text { and } E=\frac{q}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^2} \\ & \therefore \quad E=\frac{V R}{r^2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If an electron has an initial velocity in a direction different from that of an electric field, the path of the electron is :
questions.solution: The path is a parabola, because initial velocity can be resolved into two rectangular components, one along $\vec{E}$ and other perpendicular to $\vec{E}$. The former decreases at a constant rate and later is unaffected. The resultant path is, therefore a parabola.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric dipole shown in the figure. Work done to move a charge particle of $1 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $Q$ to $P$ is $x \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~J}$, then the value of $x$ is ..........

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the direction of the electric field at the centre $O$ of the square in the figure shown below? Given that, $q=10 \mathrm{nC}$ and the side of the square is 5 cm .
questions.solution:
$A D=B C=\sqrt{(5)^2+(5)^2}=\sqrt{25+25}=\sqrt{2} \times 5 \mathrm{~cm}$
$\Rightarrow A O=B O=C O=O D=\frac{5 \sqrt{2}}{2} \mathrm{~cm}$ The electric field, $E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{q}{r^2}$ So, $\quad E_A=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 4}{25 \times 2}$ $\begin{aligned} & =7.2 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \text { along } O D \\ E_B & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 4}{25 \times 2} \\ & =14.4 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \text { along } O B \\ E_C & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 4}{25 \times 2} \\ & =7.2 \text { along } O C \\ E_D & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 2 \times 10 \times 10^{-9} \times 4}{25 \times 2} \\ & =14.4 \text { along } O A\end{aligned}$ Resultant of $\begin{aligned} E_A \text { and } E_D, E_1 & =(14.4-7.2) \\ & =7.2 \mathrm{NC}^{-1} \text { along } O A .\end{aligned}$ Resultant of $E_B$ and $E_C, E_2=(14.4-7.2)$ $=7.2 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$ along $O B$. Since, $E_1$ and $E_2$ are perpendicular to each other. $\therefore E=\sqrt{E_1^2+E_2^2}$ is along $45^{\circ}$ to $O A$ upward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Small rain drops of the same size are charged to potential $V$ volt each. If $n$ such drops coalesce to form a single drop, then the potential of the bigger drop is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { (b) } \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3 \times n=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad R=n^{1 / 3} r \\ & \therefore \quad V_{\text {big }}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{Q}{R}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \times \frac{n q}{n^{1 / 3} r}=n^{2 / 3} \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small sphere is charged to a potential of 50 V and a big hollow sphere is charged to potential of 100 V . Charge will flow from the smaller sphere to the bigger one when
options: the smaller one is placed inside the bigger one and connected by a wire
the bigger one is placed inside the smaller one and connected by means of a wire
bigger one placed by the side of the smaller one and connected by a wire
smaller one placed by the side of the bigger one
questions.solution: The charge will flow from the outer surface of smaller one to bigger one only in case (1).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric potential is +100 V at a distance of 10 cm from a point charge $q$. Then, $q$ is equal to
questions.solution:
$V=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}$
Given, $\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}=9 \times 10^9, V=+100$ volt,
$r=10 \mathrm{~cm}=0.10 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore 100=\left(9 \times 10^9\right) \times \frac{9}{0.10}$
$\Rightarrow q=\frac{100 \times 0.10}{9 \times 10^9}=1.1 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electric dipole is placed at an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with an electric field of intensity $2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{NC}^{-1}$. It experiences a torque equal to 4 Nm . Calculate the charge on the dipole if the dipole length is 2 cm .

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle of mass $m$ and charge $q$ is released from rest in a electric field of magnitude $E$. The kinetic energy of the particle after $t$ second is
questions.solution:
Force on a particle with charge $q$ in electric field $E$ is
$\begin{aligned} F & =q E=m a \\ 
\therefore a & =\frac{q E}{m}\end{aligned}$
here $a=\frac{v}{t}$
$\therefore \frac{v}{t}=\frac{q E}{m} \Rightarrow v=\frac{q E t}{m}$
Also, kinetic energy is due to velocity $(v)$ is
$K=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{q E t}{m}\right)^2=\frac{q^2 E^2 t^2}{2 m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron beam accelerated from rest through a potential difference of 5000 V in vaccum is allowed to impinge on a surface normally. The incident current is $50 \mu \mathrm{~A}$ and if the electron comes to rest on striking the surface, the force on it is
questions.solution:
Energy $=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=5000 \mathrm{eV}$
$\begin{aligned} & =5000 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ m v_2 & =\sqrt{2 \times 5000 \times\left(1.6 \times 10^{-19}\right)} \mathrm{m} \\ & =4 \times 10^{-8} \sqrt{m}\end{aligned}$
Number of electrons striking per second is
$\begin{aligned} & n=\frac{q}{e}=\frac{I t}{e}=\frac{50 \times 10^{-6} \times 1}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \\ \Rightarrow \quad n & =31.25 \times 10^{13}\end{aligned}$
Force = Change of momentum per second
$\begin{aligned} & =n(m v)=31.25 \times 10^{13} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} \sqrt{m} \\ & =125 \times 10^5 \sqrt{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}=1.1924 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two equal point charges, $Q=+\sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{C}$ are placed at each of the two opposite corners of a square and equal point charges $q$ at each of the other two corners. The value of $q$, so that the resultant force on $Q$ is zero is
questions.solution:
From Coulomb's law $F=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{Q^2}{2 a^2}$ $F_1$ and $F_2$ will be directed as shown, for this both $q$ should be negative $\begin{aligned} & F_1=F_2=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{Q q}{a^2} \\ & F_{12}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{2} Q q}{a^2} \end{aligned}$ For equilibrium of $Q$ we have
$\begin{aligned} F & =-F_{12} \\ \frac{Q^2}{2 a^2} & =-\frac{\sqrt{2} Q q}{a^2}\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow q=\frac{Q}{2 \sqrt{2}}$ Given $Q=+\sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{C}$ $\therefore \quad q=-\frac{\sqrt{2} \mu \mathrm{C}}{2 \sqrt{2}}=-0.5 \mu \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two point charges $2 q$ and $8 q$ are placed at a distance $r$ apart. Where should a third charge $-q$ be placed between them, so that the electrical potential energy of the system is minimum?
options: At a distance of $r / 3$ from $2 q$
At a distance of $2 r / 3$ from $2 q$
At a distance of $r / 16$ from $2 q$
questions.solution:
Let the third charge $-q$ is placed at a distance of $x$ from the charge $2 q$. Then, the potential energy of the system is
$U=k\left[\frac{2 q(8 q)}{r}-\frac{(2 q)(q)}{x}-\frac{(8 q)(q)}{r-x}\right]$
Here, $\quad k=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0}$
$U$ is minimum, where $\frac{2}{x}+\frac{8}{r-x}$ is maximum
Let
$\frac{2}{x}+\frac{8}{r-x}=y$
For $y$ to be maximum, $\frac{d y}{d x}=0$
$-\frac{2}{x^2}+\frac{8}{(r-x)^2}=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{x}{r-x}=\sqrt{\frac{2}{8}}=\frac{1}{2} \Rightarrow x=\frac{r}{3}$
But at $x=\frac{r}{3}, \frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}=$ positive i.e. at $x=\frac{r}{3}, y$ is minimum or $U$ is maximum. So, there cannot be any point between them where the potential energy is minimum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three infinitely charged sheets are kept parallel to $x-y$ plane having charge densities as shown. Then, the value of electric field at ' $P$ ' is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \mathbf{E}_p & =\frac{\sigma}{2 \varepsilon_0}(-\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\frac{(-2 \sigma)}{2 \varepsilon_0}(\hat{\mathbf{k}})+\frac{(-\sigma)}{2 \varepsilon_0}(\hat{\mathbf{k}}) \\ & =\frac{-2 \sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \hat{\mathbf{k}}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron is moving round the nucleus of a hydrogen atom in a circular orbit of radius $r$. The coulomb force $\mathbf{F}$ between the two is
questions.solution:
$\mathbf{F}=-\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}=\mathbf{r}$
$\begin{aligned} & \mathbf{F}=-k \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}=\mathbf{r} \\ & \mathbf{F}=-k \frac{e \cdot e}{r^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}}=-k \frac{e^2}{r^2} \hat{\mathbf{r}} \\ & \hat{\mathbf{r}}=\frac{\mathbf{r}}{|\mathbf{r}|}=\frac{\mathbf{r}}{r} \\ & F=-k \frac{e^2}{r^2} \cdot \frac{\mathbf{r}}{r}=-k \frac{e^2}{r^3} \mathbf{r}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How does, the electric field $(E)$ between the plates of a charged cylindrical capacitor vary with the distance $r$ from the axis of the cylinder?
questions.solution: Electric field due to outer plate is zero and so the electric field due to inner cylindrical charged conductor varies inversely as the distance from the axis.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Millikan's oil drop experiment, an oil drop carrying a charge $Q$ is held stationary by a potential difference 2400 V between the plates. To keep a drop of half the radius stationary, the potential difference had to be made 600 V . What is the charge on the second drop?
questions.solution:
Force on charge, $F=Q E$
or $F=\frac{Q V}{d} \quad(\because V=E d)$
For drop to be stationary, 
weight of drop = force due to charge i.e., $\quad m g=\frac{Q V}{d}$
For two drops, $\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{m_1}{m_2}$
$\frac{Q_1}{Q_2} \cdot \frac{V_1}{V_2}=\frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi r_1^3  \rho}{\frac{4}{3} \pi r_2^3 \rho}$
$\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}=\frac{r_2^3}{r_1^3} \times \frac{V_1}{V_2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{Q_2}{Q}=\frac{(r / 2)^3}{r^3} \times \frac{2400}{600}$
$Q_2=\frac{Q}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force of interaction between two charges $q_1=6 \mu \mathrm{C}$ and $q_2=2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is 12 N . If charge $q=-2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ is added to each of the charges, then the new force of interaction is
questions.solution:
New force
$\begin{aligned}
F & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(6-2)(2-2)}{r^2} \times 10^{-12} \\
& =0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge of $10^{-9} \mathrm{C}$ is placed on each of the 64 identical drops of radius 2 cm . They are then combined to form a bigger drop. Its potential will be
questions.solution:
Volume of bigger drop = volume of 64 small drops i.e., $\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3=64 \times \frac{4}{3} \pi r^3$
or $\quad R=4 r$
Potential on small drop
$V_1=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{q}{r}$
Potential on bigger drop
$\begin{aligned} V_2 & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{64 q}{R} \\ V_2 & =\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{64 q}{4 r} \\ & =\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 64 \times 10^{-9}}{4 \times 2 \times 10^{-2}} \\ & =7.2 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~V}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The potential at a point at a distance $r$ from the centre of an electric dipole is proportional to

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At great distances from an electric dipole, the electric field strength due to the dipole varies with the distance as
questions.solution:
For a dipole of length $2 l$

$E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 K} \cdot \frac{2 p r}{\left(r^2-l^2\right)^2}$
When $(l\lt \lt r)$, then $l^2$ may be neglected in comparison to $r^2$. $\begin{array}{cc} \therefore & E=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 K} \frac{2 p r}{r^4} \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 K} \frac{2 p}{r^3} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{C} \\ \Rightarrow & E \propto \frac{1}{r^3} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A comb is run through wet hair on a rainy day, then
options: it will attract large number of small bits of paper
it will not go through the hair
it will not attract small bits of paper
questions.solution: A dry comb, when passed through hair, gets charged due to friction and because of this it induces an opposite charge on the paper. The electrostatic force of attraction between the comb and the paper is much greater than the weight of paper, therefore, it is lifted. On a wet day, the hair is wet, the friction is quite less and hence, comb does not get charged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two particles of charges $+e$ and $+2 e$ are at 16 cm away from each other. Where should another charge $q$ be placed between them, so that the system remains in equilibrium?
questions.solution: For the system to be in equilibrium, we have force on charge $q$ due to $+e=$ force on charge $q$ due to $+2 e$ i.e. $\quad \frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{e . q}{x^2}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \cdot \frac{2 e \cdot q}{(16-x)^2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad 2 x^2=(16-x)^2$ $\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad & 2 x^2=256+x^2-32 x \\ & {\left[\because(a-b)^2=a^2+b^2-2 a b\right] }\end{aligned}$ $\Rightarrow x^2+32 x-256=0$\ldots(i) On solving Eq. (i), we get $x=6.63 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charge $q$ is located at the centre of a cube. The electric flux through any face is
questions.solution:
Gauss's law
$\phi=\frac{q}{\varepsilon_0}$
$\phi^{\prime}=\frac{\phi}{6}=\frac{q}{6 \varepsilon_0}=\frac{4 \pi q}{6\left(4 \pi \varepsilon_0\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Millikan's oil drop experiment, a charged drop of mass $1.8 \times 10^{-13} \mathrm{~kg}$ is stationary between its plates. The distance between its plates is 0.9 cm and potential difference is 2000 V . The number of electrons in the drop is
questions.solution:
Drop is stationary, i.e., weight of drop = electric force
i.e., $\quad m g=q E$
or $\quad m g=q \frac{V}{d}$
or $\quad m g=n e \frac{V}{d}$
$\therefore \quad n=\frac{m g d}{e V}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1.8 \times 10^{-13} \times 10 \times 0.9 \times 10^{-2}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 2000} \\ n & =50\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The circular divisions of shown screw gauge are 50 . It moves 0.5 mm on main scale in one rotation. The diameter of the ball is
questions.solution:
$\text { Zero error }=5 \times \frac{0.5}{50}=0.05 \mathrm{~mm}$
Actual measurement
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 0.5 \mathrm{~mm}+25 \times \frac{0.5}{50}-0.05 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =1 \mathrm{~mm}+0.25 \mathrm{~mm}-0.05 \mathrm{~mm}=1.20 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The length of the second's pendulum is decreased by 0.3 cm when it is shifted to Chennai from London. If the acceleration due to gravity at London is $981 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}^2$, the acceleration due to gravity at Chennai is
questions.solution:
$L_1=\frac{g_1 T^2}{4 \pi^2}=\frac{g_1}{\pi^2} ; L_2=\frac{g_2 T^2}{4 \pi^2}=\frac{g_2}{\pi^2}$
Since, length is decreased, $g_2$ is less than $g_1$. $\therefore \quad L_1-L_2=\frac{g_1-g_2}{\pi^2}$ or $\quad\left(L_1-L_2\right) \pi^2=g_1-g_2$ or $\quad 0.3 \times 10=g_1-g_2$ $\therefore \quad g_2=981-3=978 \mathrm{~cm} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of KE required to be given to the satellite to escape earth's gravitational field to the KE required to be given, so that the satellite moves in a circular orbit just above earth's atmosphere is :
questions.solution:
$v_e=\sqrt{2 g R}$ and $v_o=\sqrt{g R}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{KE}_1}{\mathrm{KE}_2}=\frac{\frac{1}{2} m v_e^2}{\frac{1}{2} m v_o^2}=\frac{v_\theta^2}{v_o^2}=2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite revolves very near to the earth surface. Its speed should be around
questions.solution: Orbital speed of satellite near the earth surface is approximately $8 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A geo-stationary satellite is orbiting the earth at height of $6 R$ above the surface of the earth, $R$ being the radius of earth. The time period of another satellite at a height of $2.5 R$ from the surface of the earth is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the density of earth is doubled keeping its radius constant, then acceleration due to gravity $g$ is
questions.solution:
$g=\frac{G M_e}{R_e^2}=G \frac{\frac{4}{3} \pi R_e^3 d}{R_e^2}=\frac{4}{3} \pi G R_e d$ $g \propto d$
So, if density is doubled, then acceleration due to gravity will also be doubled.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The acceleration due to gravity becomes $g / 2$ ( $g=$ acceleration due to gravity on the surface of the earth) at a height equals to
questions.solution:
The acceleration due to gravity,
$g=\frac{G M}{R^2}\ldots$ (i)
At a height $h$ above the surface of the earth, the acceleration due to gravity is
$g^{\prime}=\frac{G M}{(R+h)^2}\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{g}{g^{\prime}}=\left(\frac{R+h}{R}\right)^2=\left(1+\frac{h}{R}\right)^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{g^{\prime}}{g^{\prime}}=\left(1+\frac{h}{R}\right)^{-2}=\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right)^2$
Here,
$g^{\prime}=\frac{g}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{g / 2}{g}=\left(1-\frac{2 h}{R}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{2 h}{R}=\frac{1}{2}$ or $h=\frac{R}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Imagine a light planet revolving around a very massive star in a circular orbit of radius $R$ with a period of revolution $T$. If the gravitational force of attraction between the planet and the star is proportional to $R^{-5 / R}$, then $T^2$ is proportional to :
questions.solution:
Gravitational force $\left(=\frac{G M m}{R^{5 / 2}}\right)$ provides the necessary centripetal force $\left(i e, m R \omega^2\right)$. So,
$\frac{G M m}{R^{5 / 2}}=m R \omega^2=m R\left(\frac{2 \pi}{T}\right)^2=\frac{4 \pi^2 m R}{T^2}$
or
$T^2=\frac{4 \pi^2 R^{7 / 2}}{G M}$
i.e,
$T^2 \propto R^{7 / 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass $m$ rises to a height $h=\frac{R}{5}$ from the earth's surface, where $R$ is the earth's radius. If $g$ is acceleration due to gravity at the earth's surface, the increase in potential energy will be
questions.solution:
Increase in potential energy
$=$ Final potential energy - Initial potential energy
$=-\frac{\text { GMm }}{\left(R+\frac{R}{5}\right)}-\left(-\frac{G M m}{R}\right)$
$=-\frac{5}{6} \frac{G M m}{R}+\frac{G M m}{R}$
$=\frac{1}{6} \frac{G M m}{R}=\frac{1}{6} \frac{G M m R}{R^2}$
$=\frac{1}{6} m g R \quad\left[\because g=\frac{G M}{R^2}\right]$
$=\frac{5}{6} m g h \quad\left[\because h=\frac{R}{5}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An artificial satellite moving in a circular orbit around the earth has a total (kinetic + potential) energy $E_0$, its potential energy is
questions.solution:
Total energy, $E_0=-\frac{G M m}{2 r}\ldots$ (i)
and potential energy, $V=-\frac{G M m}{r}\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$=2 E_0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a given density of a planet, the orbital speed of satellite near the surface of the planet of radius $R$ is proportional to
questions.solution:
Applying Newton's second law to a circular orbit, we have
$\frac{m v^2}{r}=\frac{4 \pi^2 r m}{T^2}=\frac{G M m}{r^2}$
where, $m$ is the mass of satellite, and $v$ is the orbital speed
$T$ is the time period
$\therefore \quad T=\frac{2 \pi r^{3 / 2}}{\sqrt{G M}}$
For $r \approx R$
and $M=\frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \rho(\rho=$ density of planet $)$
$T=\sqrt{\frac{3 \pi}{\rho G}}$
i.e. $T$ is independent of $R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the energy required to raise a satellite upto a height $h$ above the earth to the kinetic energy of the satellite into the orbit is
questions.solution:
Energy required to raise a satellite upto a height $h$,
$E_1=\Delta U=\frac{m g h}{1+\frac{h}{R}}$   ...(i)
$E_2=$ energy required to put in orbit
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{G M}{r}\right) \text { as } v_0=\text { orbital speed } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{G M}{R+h}\right)=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{G M}{R^2}\right) \frac{R}{1+\frac{h}{R}}\end{aligned}$   ...(ii)
$\Rightarrow \quad E_2=\frac{m g R}{2\left(1+\frac{h}{R}\right)}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{E_1}{E_2}=\frac{2 h}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A system of binary stars of masses $m_A$ and $m_B$ are moving in circular orbits of radii $r_A$ and $r_B$ respectively. If $T_A$ and $T_B$ are the time periods of masses $m_A$ and $m_B$ respectively, then
questions.solution:
$\frac{G m_A m_B}{\left(r_A+r_B\right)^2}=\frac{m_A r_A 4 \pi^2}{T_A^2}=\frac{m_B r_B 4 \pi^2}{T_B^2}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & m_A r_A & =m_B r_B \\ \therefore & T_A & =T_B\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Radius of orbit of satellite of earth is $R$. Its kinetic energy is proportional to
questions.solution:
Gravitational force provides the required centripetal force. The gravitational force provides the required centripetal force in orbit of earth.
$\therefore \quad \frac{G M_e M}{R^2}=\frac{m v_0^2}{R}$ $v_0=\sqrt{\frac{G M_e}{R}}$ kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} m v_0^2$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m\left(\frac{\mathrm{G} M_{\mathrm{e}}}{R}\right)^{2 / 2}$ $=\frac{1}{2} \frac{m G M_e}{R}$ $K E \propto \frac{1}{R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An earth satelites $S$ has orbit radius which is 4 times that of communication satellite $C$. The period of revolution of $S$ will be
questions.solution:
Larger the distance of planet from the sun, larger will be its period of revolution around the sun.
Kepler's third low planetary motion the square of the period of revolution of any planet around the sun is directly proportional to the cube of the semi-major axis of its elliptical orbit
$\therefore \quad T^2 \propto R^3$
$\frac{T_s}{T_c}=\left(\frac{R_s}{R_c}\right)^{3 / 2}$
given $\quad R_s=4 R_c$
$\begin{aligned} & \qquad \frac{T_s}{T_c}=\left(\frac{4 R_c}{R_c}\right)^{3 / 2}=8 \\ & \text { for } T_c=1 \text { day } \\ & T_s=8 \text { days. }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The speed with which the earth have to rotate on its axis so that a person on the equator would weight $(3 / 5)^{\text {th }}$ as much as present. [Radius of earth $=6400 \mathrm{~km}$ ]
questions.solution:
The apparent weight of person on the equator (latitude $\lambda=0$ ) is given by
$\omega^{\prime}=w-m R \omega^2$
Here, $\therefore \quad \omega^{\prime}=(3 / 5) \mathrm{w}=(3 / 5) \mathrm{mg} \quad[\because \omega=m g]$
or $\quad m R \omega^2=m g-(3 / 5) m g=\left(\frac{2}{5}\right) m g$
or $\quad \omega^2=\frac{2 g}{5 R}$
$\omega=\sqrt{\frac{2 g}{5 R}}$
Here, $g=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$
and $\quad R=6400 \mathrm{~km}=6400 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~m}$
$\therefore \quad \omega=\sqrt{\left(\frac{2}{5} \times \frac{9.8}{6400 \times 10^3}\right)} \mathrm{rads}^{-1}$
$=7.82 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{rads}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Escape velocity at surface of earth is 11.2 $\mathrm{Km} / \mathrm{s}$. Escape velocity from a planet whose mass is the same as that of earth and radius $1 / 4$ that of earth, is
questions.solution:
At a crtain velocity of projection the body will go out of the gravitational field of the earth and will never return to the earth, this velocity is known as escape velocity.
$v_\theta=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M_e}{R_e}}$
Given, $\quad M_e=M_p, R_p=\frac{R_e}{4}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_p}{v_e}=\sqrt{\frac{M_e}{M_e} \times \frac{R_e}{R_e / 4}}=\sqrt{4}=2$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad v_\rho & =2 v_e=2 \times 11.2 \\ & =22.4 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Escape velocity from earth's surface is $11 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$. If radius of a planet is double than that of earth and density is same as that of earth, then the escape velocity from this planet will be
questions.solution:
Escape velocity $\begin{aligned} v_e & =\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}} \\ & =R \sqrt{\frac{8}{3} G \rho}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad v \propto R$
We can say, if radius of a planet is double than that of earth then escape velocity from this planet will be $22 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At what height $h$ above earth, the value of $g$ becomes $g / 2$ ? $(R=$ Radius of earth)
questions.solution:
The value of acceleration due to gravity at height $h$ (when $h$ is not negligible as compared to $R$ )
$g^{\prime}=g \frac{R^2}{(R+h)^2}$
Here,
$g^{\prime}=\frac{g}{2}$
$\therefore  \frac{g}{2}=g \frac{R^2}{(R+h)^2}$
or
$\frac{1}{2}=\frac{R^2}{(R+h)^2}$
or
$\sqrt{\frac{1}{2}}=\frac{R}{R+h}$
or
$R+h=\sqrt{2} R$
$\therefore h=(\sqrt{2}-1) R$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How much energy will necessary for making a body of 500 kg escape from the earth? $\left(g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right.$, radius of the earth $=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$ )
questions.solution:
Given $m=500 \mathrm{~kg}, g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
AND $R_e=6.4 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~m}$
Energy required to scape
$\begin{aligned} E & =m g R_e \\ & =500 \times 9.8 \times 6.4 \times 10^6 \\ & =3.1 \times 10^{10} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time period of a geostationary satellite at a height 36000 km is 24 h . A spy satellite orbits earth at a height 6400 km . What will be the time period of spy satellite? [Radius of the earth $=6400 \mathrm{~km}$ ]
questions.solution:
From Kepler's third law of planetary motion
$T^2 \propto R^3$
$\therefore  \frac{T_2^2}{T_1^2}=\frac{R_2^3}{R_1^3}$
or
$\frac{T_2^2}{(24)^2}=\left(\frac{6400+6400}{36000+6400}\right)^3$
or $T_2^2=(24)^2 \times\left(\frac{16}{53}\right)^3$
$\Rightarrow T_2=4 \mathrm{~h}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A satellite goes along an elliptical path arounds earth. The rate of change of area swept by the same line joining earth and the satellite is proportional to :
questions.solution:
Areal velocity $=\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{L}{2 m}$
$=\frac{m v r}{2 m}=\frac{v r}{2}=\frac{r}{2} \sqrt{\frac{G M}{r}}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{G M r}$
So, $\frac{d A}{d t} \propto \sqrt{r}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the radius of a planet is three times the radius of the earth. Both have same mass-densities. $v_P$ and $v_E$ are the escape velocities of the planet and the earth respectively, then
questions.solution:
The escape velocity of a planet is given by,
$v=\sqrt{\frac{2 G M}{R}}=\sqrt{\frac{2 G}{R} \times \frac{4}{3} \pi R^3 \cdot \rho}$
$\therefore \quad V \propto R \sqrt{\rho}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_P}{v_E}=\frac{R_P \sqrt{\rho_P}}{R_E \sqrt{\rho_E}}=\frac{R_P}{R_E} \quad\left(\because \rho_P=\rho_E\right)$
$\therefore \quad \frac{v_P}{v_E}=\frac{3 R_E}{R_E}=3 \quad\left(\because R_P=3 R_E\right)$
$\therefore \quad v_P=3 v_E$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: How much deep inside the earth (radius $R$ ) should a man go, so that his weight becomes one-fourth of that on the earth's surface?
questions.solution:
Suppose depth inside the earth is $h$.
As we know that, inside the earth $g_i \propto\left(\frac{R-h}{R^3}\right)$ ....(i) On the surface of the earth $g_s \propto \frac{1}{R^2}$ ....(ii) From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $\frac{g_i}{g_c}=\frac{(R-h) R^2}{R^3}=\frac{R-h}{R}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{4}=\frac{R-h}{R}$ $\Rightarrow \quad R=4 R-4 h \Rightarrow 3 R=4 h$ $\Rightarrow \quad h=\frac{3}{4} R$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A research satellite of mass 200 kg circle the earth in an orbit of average radius $\frac{3 R}{2}$, where $R$ is the radius of the earth. Assuming the gravitational pull on a mass of 1 kg on the earth's surface to be 10 N , the pull on the satellite will be
questions.solution:
Given,
\(\begin{aligned}
& 10=\frac{G \times 6 \times 10^{24} \times 1}{\left(6.4 \times 10^6\right)^2} \\
& G=6.82 \times 10^{-11} \mathrm{Nm}^2 / \mathrm{kg}^2
\end{aligned}\)
Therefore,
\(F_{\text {satellite }}=\frac{6.82 \times 10^{-11} \times 6 \times 10^{24} \times 200}{\left(\frac{3 \times 6.4 \times 10^6}{2}\right)^2}=889 \mathrm{~N}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a planetary motion, the areal velocity of position vector of a planet depends on angular velocity ( $\omega$ ) and distance $(r)$ of the planet from the sun. The correct relation for areal velocity $\left(\frac{d A}{d t}\right)$ is
questions.solution:
Areal velocity, $\frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{L}{2 m}$
where, $L=$ angular momentum
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{d A}{d t}=\frac{m r^2 \omega}{2 m} \quad\left[\because L=m r^2 \omega\right]$
$\propto r^2 \omega$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body weights 500 N on the surface of the earth. How much would it weigl half way below the surface of the earth?
questions.solution:
Value of acceleration due to gravity decreases on going below the surface of the earth. weight (w) of a body is defined as product of mass ( $m$ ) and acceleration due to gravity ( $g$ ) $w=m g$
The value of $g$ below the surface of earth at a distance $h$ is given by $g^{\prime}=g\left(1-\frac{h}{R}\right)$ $\Rightarrow \quad W^{\prime}=W\left(1-\frac{h}{R}\right)$ Given, $h=\frac{R}{2}, W=500 \mathrm{~N}$ $W^{\prime}=500\left(1-\frac{1}{2}\right)$ $=\frac{500}{2}=250 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical cylinders contain helium at 2.5 atm and argon at 1 atm respectively. If both the gases are filled in one of the cylinders, the pressure would be :
questions.solution:
Total pressure, when the two gases are filled in one cylinder is equal to sum of the pressures of the individual gases.
Thus, total pressure $=2.5+1=3.5 \mathrm{~atm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The specific heat at constant volume for the monoatomic argon is $0.075 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K}$, whereas its gram molecular specific heat is $C_V=2.98 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{K}$. The mass of the carbon atom is
questions.solution:
Molar specific heat
$=$ Molecular weight $\times$ gram specific heat.
$C_V=M \times C_V$
$\begin{aligned} 2.98 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}-\mathrm{K} & =M \times 0.075 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{kg}-\mathrm{K} \\ & =\frac{M \times 0.075 \times 10^3}{10^3} \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}-\mathrm{K}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Molecular weight of argon,
$M=\frac{2.98}{0.075}=39.7 \mathrm{~g}$
i.e. mass of $6.023 \times 10^{23}$ atom $=39.7 \mathrm{~g}$
Therefore, mass of
$\begin{aligned}
\text { single atom } & =\frac{39.7}{6.023 \times 10^{23}} \\
& =6.60 \times 10^{-23} \mathrm{~g}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If one mole of a monoatomic gas $\left(\gamma=\frac{5}{3}\right)$ is mixed with one mole diatomic gas $\left(\gamma=\frac{7}{5}\right)$, the value of $\gamma$ for the mixture is
questions.solution:
For monoatomic gas,
$\gamma_1=\frac{C_{p_1}}{C_{V_1}}=\frac{5}{3}$ and $C_{p_1}-C_{V_1}=R$
this gives $C_{\rho_1}=\frac{5}{2} R$ and $C_{V_1}=\frac{3}{2} R$
For diatomic gas,
$\gamma_2=\frac{C_{\rho_2}}{C_{V_2}}=7 / 5$ and $C_{p_2}-C_{V_2}=R$
$\Rightarrow \quad C_{P_2}=\frac{7}{2} R \quad$ and $\quad C_{V_2}=\frac{5}{2} R$
Now, we can write,
$\gamma_{\text {mix }}=\frac{n_1 C_{p_1}+n_2 C_{\rho_2}}{n_1 C_{V_1}+n_2 C_{V_2}}$
$=\frac{C_{\rho_1}+C_{\rho_2}}{C_{V_1}+C_{V_2}}=\frac{\frac{5}{2} R+\frac{7}{2} R}{\frac{3}{2} R+\frac{5}{2} R}=\frac{12}{8}=\frac{3}{2}=1.5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The molar specific heat of oxygen at constant pressure $C_p=7.03 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $R=8.31 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{mol}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The amount of heat taken by 5 moles of oxygen when heated at constant volume from $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ will be approximately.
questions.solution:
Given, $C_p=7.03 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol}{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, R=8.315 / \mathrm{mol}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$,
$\begin{gathered}n=5 \text { moles, } T_2=20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, T_1=10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \\ \Delta Q=?\end{gathered}$
$\therefore$ Heat, $\Delta Q=n C_V \Delta T$
$=n\left(C_p-R\right) \Delta T \quad\left[\because C_p-C_V=R\right]$
$\begin{aligned} & =5\left[7.03-\frac{8.31}{4.2}\right] \times(20-10) \\ & =253 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 5 mol of hydrogen gas is heated from $30^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ at constant pressure. Heat given to the gas is (given $R=2 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{mol} \mathrm{deg}$ )
questions.solution:
$Q=n C_p \Delta T$
For hydrogen
$C_p=\frac{7}{2} R$
So,
$\begin{aligned} Q & =n \frac{7}{2} R \Delta T \\ & =5 \times \frac{7}{2} \times 2 \times 30 \\ & =1050 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two gases are at absolute temperatures 300 K and 350 K respectively. The ratio of average kinetic energy of their molecules is
questions.solution:
From the relation,
$\begin{aligned}
& \overline{\mathrm{KE}}= \frac{3}{2} k T \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{(\overline{\mathrm{KE}})_1}{(\overline{\mathrm{KE}})_2}=\frac{T_1}{T_2} \quad \mathrm{(become k is constant)}
\end{aligned}$
Given, $\quad T_1=300 \mathrm{~K}, T_2=350 \mathrm{~K}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{(\mathrm{KE})_1}{(\mathrm{KE})_2}=\frac{300}{350}=\frac{6}{7}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following relation correct? ($v_{\text {rms }}$-root mean square velocity, $\bar{v}$-mean velocity and $v_{\mathrm{mp}}$-most probable velocity)
questions.solution:
The correct relation is $v_{\mathrm{rms}} \gt \bar{v} \gt v_{\mathrm{mp}}$
As $\quad \bar{v}=1.6 \sqrt{\frac{R T}{M}}, v_{\mathrm{rms}}=1.73 \sqrt{\frac{R T}{M}}$
and $\quad v_{\mathrm{mp}}=1.41 \sqrt{\frac{R T}{M}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A diatomic gas is heated at certain pressure. What fraction of the heat energy is used to increase the internal energy?
questions.solution:
$f=\frac{\Delta V}{\left(\Delta Q_p\right)}=\frac{\Delta Q_V}{\left(\Delta Q_p\right)_p}=\frac{\mu C V \Delta T}{\mu C p \Delta T}=\frac{1}{T}$
$\begin{aligned} & f=\frac{7}{5} \\ & f=\frac{5}{7}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mixture of two gases is contained in a vessel. The gas 1 is monoatomic and gas 2 is diatomic and the ratio of their molecular masses $M_1 / M_2=1 / 4$. What is the ratio of root mean square speeds of the molecules of two gases?
questions.solution:
From kinetic theory of gases
$p=\frac{1}{3} \frac{M}{V} \cdot e^2$
Since, $p$ and $V$ are constant (same)
$\therefore \quad M_1 e_1^2=M_2 e_2^2$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{e_1^2}{e_2^2}=\frac{M_2}{M_1}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{e_1}{e_2}=\sqrt{\frac{M_2}{M_1}}=\sqrt{4 / 1}=2 \quad\left[\right.$ given, $\left.\frac{M_1}{M_2}=\frac{1}{4}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
