questions.q: Two moles of helium are mixed with $n$ moles of hydrogen. The root mean square (rms) speed of gas molecules in the mixture is $\sqrt{2}$ times the speed of sound in the mixture. Then, the value of $n$ is
questions.solution:
$\because v_{\text {mis }}=\sqrt{\frac{3 R T}{M}}$ and $v_{\text {sound }}=\sqrt{\frac{\gamma R T}{M}}$,
$v_{\mathrm{rms}}=2 v_{\text {sound }}$
i.e. $\quad \gamma=\frac{3}{2}=$ ratio of $\frac{C_p}{C_V}$ for the mixture
$C_V=\frac{n_1 C_{V_1}+n_2 C_{V_2}}{n_1+n_2}$
and
$C_p=\frac{n_1 C_{p_1}+n_2 C_{p_2}}{n_1+n_2}$
$\therefore \quad \gamma=\frac{C_P}{C_V}=\frac{n_1 C_{p_1}+n_2 C_{p_2}}{n_1 C_{V_1}+n_2 C_{V_2}}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{3}{2}=\frac{2\left(\frac{5}{2} R\right)+n\left(\frac{7}{2} R\right)}{2\left(\frac{3}{2} R\right)+n\left(\frac{5}{2} R\right)}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \frac{3}{2}=\frac{10+7 n}{6+5 n} \\ \Rightarrow & n=2\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the rms velocity of a gas is $v$, then
questions.solution:
Rms velocity is given by
$v=\sqrt{\frac{3 k T}{m}} \text { or } v^2=\frac{3 k T}{m}$
For a gas, $k$ and $m$ are constants.
$\therefore \frac{v^2}{T}=\text { constant }$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are attached to a string which passes over a frictionless smooth pulley. When $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}$, $m_2=6 \mathrm{~kg}$, the acceleration of masses is
questions.solution:
Acceleration of the system
$\begin{aligned}
a & =\frac{m_1-m_2}{m_1+m_2} g=\frac{10-6}{10+6} \times g \\
& =\frac{4}{16} \times 10=2.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A simple spring has length L and force constant $k$. It is cut into two springs of lengths $l_1$ and $L_2$ such that $l_1=n L_2$ ($n=$ an integer). The force constant of spring of length $l_1$ is :
questions.solution:
Since $L_1+n L_2$ where $n$ is an integer, so the spring is made of $(n+1)$ equal parts in length each of length $I_2$
$\therefore \quad \| \quad \frac{1}{k}=\frac{(n+1)}{k^{\prime}}$
or $k^{\prime}=(n+1) k$
The spring of length $l_1\left(=n l_2\right)$ will be equivalent to $n$ springs connected in series where spring constant
$k^{\prime \prime}=\frac{k^{\prime}}{n}=\frac{(n+1) k}{n}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time period of a mass suspended from a spring is $T$. If the spring is cut into three equal parts and connected in parallel. The same mass is suspended from these parallel springs, then the new time period of the mass will be
questions.solution:
As, spring constant, $(k) \propto \frac{1}{\text { length of spring }(l)}$ $\because$ The spring cut into three equal parts $\therefore k^{\prime}=3 k$ (For each spring) We know that, time period of a body connected with spring. $T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}$ ...(i) The parallel arrangement of the spring is given as
$\begin{aligned} & T^{\prime}=\begin{array}{l}\text { Time period of springs }\left(k^{\prime}=3 k\right) \text { connected in } \\ \text { parallel }\end{array} \\ & \qquad \\ & \qquad=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{3 k^{\prime}}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{3 \times 3 k}} \\ & \\ & \\ & =\frac{1}{3}\left(2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{m}{k}}\right)=\frac{T}{3} \quad \text { [From Eq. (i)] }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body cools in 7 min from $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. What times (in min) does it take to cool from $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $28^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, if surrounding temperature is $10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ? (Assume Newton's law of cooling)
questions.solution:
From Newton's law of cooling $\frac{\theta_1-\theta_2}{t}=\left(\frac{\theta_1+\theta_2}{2}-\theta_0\right)$ Therefore, $\begin{aligned} & \frac{60^{\circ}-40^{\circ}}{7} \propto\left(\frac{60^{\circ}+40^{\circ}}{2}-10^{\circ}\right)...(i) \\ & \frac{40^{\circ}-28^{\circ}}{t} \propto\left(\frac{40^{\circ}+28^{\circ}}{2}-10^{\circ}\right)....(ii) \end{aligned}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $t=7 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $2 \mathrm{~kg}, 3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 5 kg are connected to each other with light string and are then placed on a frictionless surface as shown in the figure. The system is pulled by a force $F=10 \mathrm{~N}$, then tension $T_1$ is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spring of force constant $k$ is cut into two pieces such that one piece is double the length of other. Then, the long piece will have a force constant of
questions.solution:
Let length of the original spring is $L$.
$\longleftarrow L \longrightarrow \quad \longleftarrow \frac{L}{3} \longrightarrow \longleftarrow \frac{2 L}{3} \longrightarrow$ As we know that, Spring constant, $k \propto \frac{1}{\text { length }}$ $\Rightarrow \quad k \propto \frac{1}{L}$ $k_1 \propto \frac{3}{L}$ $k_2 \propto \frac{3}{21}$ $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{k_2}{k}=\frac{3 / 2 L}{1 / L}=\frac{3}{2}$ $\Rightarrow \quad k_2=\frac{3}{2} k$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fireman weighing 80 kg slides down a pole. If the resisting force of friction is 720 N , his acceleration would be : (take $\left.g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Force of friction $=720 \mathrm{~N}$
If $a$ is the acceleration of fireman, sliding down the pole, then
$\begin{aligned} m g-F & =m a \\ a & =\frac{m g-F}{m} \\ & =\frac{(80 \times 10-720)}{80} \\ & =1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A car turns on a banked road of radius 300 m . If the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road is 0.3 , then maximum speed of the car on the turn is
$\left[\text { Take }, g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right. \text { ] }$
questions.solution:
Given, $r=300 \mathrm{~m}, \mu=0.3$,
$g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
If $v$ is the velocity of the vehicle while turning on the road, then
$\begin{array}{rlrl} 
& & \frac{m v^2}{r} & \leq \mu m g \\
\end{array}$
$\Rightarrow  v \leq \sqrt{\mu r g}$
$\Rightarrow v_{\max }=\sqrt{\mu r g}$
$=\sqrt{0.3 \times 300 \times 10}=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man weighing 100 kg slides down a light rope with an acceleration of $1.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. If $g=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$, the tension of the rope is
questions.solution: The resultant of tension and weight given acceleration to the man, hence $m g-T=m a$ $\Rightarrow T=m g-m a=m(g-a)$ Given, $m=100 \mathrm{~kg}$, $\begin{aligned} & g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, a=1.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2} . \\ & \therefore \quad T=100(9.8-1.8)=800 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A mass $m$ is placed on a wedge (triangular block) of mass $M$. The wedge moves on a smooth horizontal surface what should be the force $F$ applied on the wedge to the right, so that $m$ remains stationary with respect to wedge. (Ignore any friction)
questions.solution:
From figure, $m g \sin \theta=m a \cos \theta$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=g \tan \theta$ $\therefore$ Force, $\quad F=(M+m) a$ $=(M+m) g \tan \theta$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the arrangement, the minimum value of tension in the string to prevent it from sliding down is
questions.solution:
Limiting friction along the plane upward
$=\mu_s m g \cos \theta$
$\begin{aligned} & =0.8 \times 1 \times 10 \times \cos 37^{\circ} \\ & =0.8 \times 10 \times \frac{4}{5} \\ & =6.4 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$
Force responsible for downward motion
$=m g \sin \theta$
$\begin{aligned} & =1 \times 10 \times \sin 37^{\circ} \\ & =1 \times 10 \times \frac{3}{5}\end{aligned}$
$=6 \mathrm{~N}$
As, downward force  < limiting friction.
So, frictional force = downward force.
Hence, tension in string is zero.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the system shown in figure $M_1 \gt M_2$ and pulley and threads are ideal. System is held at rest by thread $B C$. Just after thread $B C$ is burnt.
options: Magnitude of acceleration of both masses will be $\frac{M_1-M_2}{M_1+M_2} g$
Acceleration of $M_1$ and $M_2$ will be equal to zero
Acceleration of $M_1$ will be equal to zero, which that of $M_2$ will be $\frac{M_1-M_2}{M_2} g$ upward
questions.solution:
As $M_1 \gt M_2$, so tension in thread connecting the block $B$ and support $C$ is $\left(M_1-M_2\right) g$. When this thread is burnt, this tension disappears. In this case tension in spring is $M_1 g$ and it is elongated. So, tension in string connecting $A$ and $B$ is $M_1 g$. Hence, resultant force on $A$ remains zero because tension in string is balanced by tension in spring. The block $B$ has a net upward force $\left(M_1-M_2\right) g$, so initial acceleration of block $B$ is
$\frac{\left(M_1-M_2\right) g}{M_2}$
Thus, initial acceleration of $M_1$ is zero and that of $M_2$ is $\frac{\left(M_1-M_2\right) g}{M_2}$ upward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A large slab of mass 5 kg lies on a smooth horizontal surface, with a block of mass 4 kg lying on the top of it, the coefficient of friction between the block and the slab is Q.25. If the block is pulled horizontally by a force of $F=6 \mathrm{~N}$, the work done by the force of friction on the slab between the instants $t=2 \mathrm{~s}$ and $t=3 \mathrm{~s}$ is
questions.solution:
Maximum frictional force between the slab and the block $f_{\max }=\mu N=\mu m g=\frac{1}{4} \times 4 \times 10=10 \mathrm{~N}$
Evidently, $f \propto f_{\max }$ So, the two bodies will move together as a single unit. If a be their combined acceleration, then $a=\frac{F}{m+M}=\frac{6}{4+5}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ Therefore, frictional force acting can be obtained as $f=M a=\frac{2}{3} \times 5=\frac{10}{3} N$ Using $\quad S=\frac{1}{2} a t^2$ $S(2)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3}(2)^2=\frac{4}{3}$ and $\quad S(3)=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{3} \times(3)^2=3$ Therefore, work done by friction $=F[S(3)-S(2)]$ $=\frac{10}{3}\left[3-\frac{4}{3}\right]=\frac{50}{9}=5.55 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass $m$ is lying on the edge having inclination angle $\alpha=\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{5}\right)$. Wedge is moving with a constant acceleration, $a=2 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$. The minimum value of coefficient of friction $\mu$, so that $m$ remains stationary with respect to wedge is
questions.solution:
FBD of $m$ in frame of wedge
$N=m g \cos (\alpha)-m a \sin (\alpha)$ Now, $f=\mu N=m a \cos \alpha+m g \sin \alpha$ $\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \mu=\frac{a \cos \alpha+g \sin \alpha}{g \cos \alpha-a \sin \alpha} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \mu=\frac{a+g \tan \alpha}{g-a \tan \alpha}=\frac{5}{12} \Rightarrow \mu=\frac{5}{12}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two unequal masses are connected on two sides of a light string passing over a light and smooth pulley as shown in the figure. The system is released from the rest. The larger mass is stoped for a moment, is after the system is set into motion. The time elapsed before the string is tight again is
questions.solution:
Net pulling force $=2 g-1 g=10 \mathrm{~N}$
Mass being pulled $=2+1=3 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\therefore$ Acceleration of the system is $a=\frac{10}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\therefore$ Velocity of both the blocks at $t=1 \mathrm{~s}$ will be
$v_0=a t=\left(\frac{10}{3}\right)(1)=\frac{10}{3} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Now at this moment, velocity of 2 kg becomes zero, while that of 1 kg block is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ upwards. Hence, string becomes tight again when displacement of 1 kg block = displacement of 2 kg block.
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & v_0 t-\frac{1}{2} g t^2=\frac{1}{2} g t^2 \\ \Rightarrow & t=\frac{v_0}{g}=\frac{\frac{10}{3}}{10}=\frac{1}{3} \mathrm{~s}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two blocks $A$ and $B$ are placed one over the other on a smooth horizontal surface. The maximum horizontal force that can be applied on the upper block $B$, so that $A$ and $B$ move without separation is 49 N . The coefficient of friction between $A$ and $B$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $F=49 \mathrm{~N}, M_A=7 \mathrm{~kg}, M_B=3 \mathrm{~kg}, \mu=$ ?
Equation of motion of two blocks-system
$F_{\max }=\mu\left(M_A+M_B\right) \cdot g$
$\begin{aligned} \mu & =\frac{F}{\left(M_A+M_B\right) \cdot g} \\ & =\frac{49}{(7+3) \cdot 9 \cdot 8}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{49}{10 \times 9.8} \\ & =0.5\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The kinetic energy $k$ of a particle moving along a circle of radius $R$ depends on the distance covered. It is given as $\mathrm{KE}=a s^2$, where $a$ is a constant. The force acting on the particle is
questions.solution:
In non-uniform circular motion two forces will work on a particle $F_c$ and $F_t$. So, the net force $F_{\text {Net }}=\sqrt{F_c^2+F_t^2}$...(i) Centripetal force $F_c=\frac{m v^2}{R}=\frac{2 a s^2}{R}$...(ii) [given $\frac{1}{2} m v^2=a s^2$ given] Again from $\frac{1}{2} m v^2=a s^2$ $\Rightarrow \quad v^2=\frac{2 a s^2}{m}$
$\Rightarrow \quad v=s \sqrt{\frac{2 a}{m}}$ Tangential acceleration $a_t=\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d v}{d s} \cdot \frac{d s}{d t}$ $\Rightarrow \quad a_t=\frac{d}{d s}\left[s \sqrt{\frac{2 a}{m}}\right] \cdot v$ $a_t=v \sqrt{\frac{2 a}{m}}=s \sqrt{\frac{2 a}{m}} \sqrt{\frac{2 a}{m}}=\frac{2 a s}{m}$ and $\quad F_t=m a_t=2 a s$...(iii) Now, on substituting value of $F_c$ and $F_t$ in Eq. (i), we get $\therefore F_{\text {Net }}=\sqrt{\left(\frac{2 a s^2}{R}\right)^2+(2 a s)^2}=2 a s\left[1+\frac{s^2}{R^2}\right]^{1 / 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $2 \mathrm{~kg}, 3 \mathrm{~kg}$ and 5 kg are connected to each other with light string and are then placed on a frictionless surface as shown in the figure. The system is pulled by a force $F=10 \mathrm{~N}$, then tension $T_1=$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { We have } T_1 & =\frac{\left(m_2+m_3\right) F}{m_1+m_2+m_3} \\ \text { Now } T_1 & =\frac{(3+5) 10}{2+3+5}=8 \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The acceleration of block $B$ in the figure will be
questions.solution:
When the block $m_2$ moves downward with acceleration $a$, the acceleration of mass $m_1$ will be 2a, because it covers double distance in the same time in comparison to $m_2$. Let $T$ is the tension in the string.
From the free body diagram of $A$ and $B$ we get $\begin{aligned} T & =m_1 2 a ...(i)\\ m_2 g-2 T & =m_2 a...(ii) \end{aligned}$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $a=\frac{m_2 g}{\left(4 m_1+m_2\right)}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car is moving along a straight horizontal road with a speed $v_0$. If the coefficient of friction between the tyres and the road is $\mu$, the shortest distance in which the car be stopped is
questions.solution:
Work done against frictional force equals the kinetic energy of the body.
when a body of mass $m$, moves with velocity $v$, it has kinetic energy $k=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$, this energy is utilized in doing work against the frictional force between the tyres of the car and road.
$\therefore$ kinetic energy $=$ work done against friction force
$\frac{1}{2} mv^2=\mu \mathrm{mgs}$
where $s$ is the distance in which the car is stopped and $\mu$ is coefficient of kinetic friction.
Given $V=V_0$
$s=\frac{v_0^2}{2 \mu g}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $n$ small balls, each of mass $m$ impinge elastically each second on a surface with a velocity $u$, then the force experienced by the surface in one second, will be
questions.solution:
Force experienced by the surface is equal to change in momentum in one second.
From Newton's second law of motion, the rate of change of momentum of a body is equal to the external force applied on the body and the change in momentum always takes place in the direction of the force.
Momentum of ball before impact $=m u$
Momentum after impact $=-m u$
Change in momentum $=m u-(-m u)=2 m u$
Change in momentum due to $n$ balls in one second is $=2 \mathrm{mnu}$.
Change of momentum = force experienced per second $=2 \mathrm{mnu}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two weights $w_1$ and $w_2$ are suspended to be the two strings on a frictionless pulley. When the pulled up with an acceleration $g$ then the tension in the string is
questions.solution: The free body diagram of the given situation is shown. Taking the upward and downward motions respectively, we get $\begin{aligned} T_1-m g & =m_1(g+a) \\ T-2 m g & =m_1 a\end{aligned}$ for second weight $\begin{aligned} m_2 g-T & =m_2(g-a) \\ 2 m_2 g-T & =m_2 a \\ T & =\frac{4 m_1 m_2 g}{\left(m_1+m_2\right)} \\ w_1=m_1 g, m_2 & =m_2 g \text { hence switch } \\ T & =\frac{4 \mathrm{w}_1 \mathrm{w}_2}{\left(\mathrm{w}_1+\mathrm{w}_2\right)}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a person with a spring balance and a body hanging from it goes up and up in an aeroplane, then the reading of the weight of the body as indicated by the spring balance will
questions.solution: Initially, due to upward acceleration apparent weight of the body increases but then it decreases due to decrease in gravity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Three blocks of masses $m_1, m_2$ and $m_3$ are connected by massless string as shown kept on a frictionless table.
They are pulled with a force $T_3=40$ N.If $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_3=4 \mathrm{~kg}$, the tension $T_2$ will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves in the $x y$-plane under the action of a force $F$ such that the components of its linear momentum $\mathbf{p}$ at any time $t$ are $p_x=2 \cos t, p_y=\sin t$. The angle between $\mathbf{F}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ at time $t$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & p_x=2 \cos t, p_y=2 \sin t \\ & \begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \mathbf{p}=p_x \mathbf{i}+p_y \mathbf{j} \\ &=2 \cos t \mathbf{i}+2 \sin t \mathbf{j}\end{aligned} \\ & \text { Force }=\text { rate of change of momentum } \\ & \quad=\frac{d \mathbf{p}}{d t}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{F}=-2 \sin t \mathbf{i}+2 \cos t \mathbf{j} \\ & \therefore \cos \theta=\frac{\mathbf{F} \cdot \mathbf{p}}{|\mathbf{F}| \cdot|\mathbf{p}|} \\ & =\frac{(-2 \sin t \mathbf{i}+2 \cos t \mathbf{j})(2 \cos t \mathbf{i}+2 \sin t \mathbf{j})}{\sqrt{4 \sin ^2 t+4 \cos ^2 t} \sqrt{4 \cos ^2 t+4 \sin ^2 t}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \cos \theta=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \theta=90^{\circ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three blocks of masses $m_1, m_2$ are connected by massless strings as shown on a frictionless table. They are pulled with a force $T_3=40 \mathrm{~N}$. If $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=6 \mathrm{~kg}$ and $m_3=4 \mathrm{~kg}$, the tension $T_2$ will be
questions.solution: Given : \(T_3=40 \mathrm{~N}, \mathrm{~m}_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}, \mathrm{~m}_2=6 \mathrm{~kg}\) and \(\mathrm{m}_3=4 \mathrm{~kg}\) Step 1: Acceleration of complete system Total mass of system, \(\mathrm{m}=\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2+\mathrm{m}_3=10+6+4=20 \mathrm{~kg}\) Acceleration of system =a Applying Newton's law, \(\Sigma \mathrm{F}=\mathrm{ma}\) \(\therefore \mathrm{a}=\frac{\mathrm{T}_3}{\mathrm{~m}}=\frac{40 \mathrm{~N}}{20 \mathrm{Kg}}=2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\) Step 2: Calculation of \(\mathrm{T}_2\) From F.B.D OF \(\left(m_1+m_2\right)\) Force \(T_2\) is pulling 1 and 2 blocks. If we consider \(m_1\) and \(m_2\) as a system then \(T_1\) will get cancel as \(T_1\) acts as internal force. (As shown in figure) Therefore from Newton's second law- \(\begin{aligned} \Sigma \mathrm{F} & =\mathrm{ma} \\ \mathrm{~T}_2 & =\left(\mathrm{m}_1+\mathrm{m}_2\right) \mathrm{a}=(10+6) \times 2=32 \mathrm{~N} \end{aligned}\) Hence, Option (D) is correct answer.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A 4 kg block $A$ is placed on the top of a block $B$ of mass 8 kg , which rests on a smooth table. A just slips on $B$ when a force of 12 N is applied on $A$.
Then, the maximum horizontal force required to make both $A$ and $B$ move together is :
questions.solution:
The mass of block $A=4 \mathrm{~kg}$
Force of friction between $A$ and $B=12 \mathrm{~N}$ Mass of block $A$ and $B=4+8=12 \mathrm{~kg}$ $i e$, mass is increased 3 times.
The force of friction also increases 3 times
$=12 \times 3=36 N$
Hence, maximum horizontal force required $=36 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three concurrent forces of the same magnitude are in equilibrium. What is the angle between the forces ? Also, name the triangle formed by the forces as sides
questions.solution:
In $N$ forces of equal magnitude works on a single point and their resultant is zero, then angle between any two forces is $\theta=\frac{360^{\circ}}{N}=\frac{360^{\circ}}{3}=120^{\circ}$
If these 3 vectors are represented by three sides of triangle, then they form equilateral triangle.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force of 5 N gives a mass $M_1$ an acceleration equal to $8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ and $M_2$ an acceleration equal to $24 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. What is the acceleration, if both masses are tied together ?
questions.solution:
Mass $M_1=\frac{5}{8} \mathrm{~kg}$, Mass $M_2=\frac{5}{24} \mathrm{~kg}$
Total maks $=M_1+M_2=\frac{5}{8}+\frac{5}{24}=\frac{20}{24} \mathrm{~kg}$
Therefore, total acceleration in two masses
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{F}{M_1+M_2}=\frac{5}{20 / 24} \\ & =\frac{5 \times 24}{20}=6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure shown, $m_1=10 \mathrm{~kg}, m_2=6 \mathrm{~kg}$, $m_3=4 \mathrm{~kg}$. If $T_3=40 \mathrm{~N}, T_2=?$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The tension in the string in the pulley system shown in the figure is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} 10 g-T & =10 a...(i) \\ T-6 g & =6 a...(ii) \end{aligned}$ From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $T=75 \mathrm{~N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of weight 2 kg is suspended as shown in figure. The tension $T_1$ in the horizontal string (in kg-wt) is
questions.solution:
$T \sin 30^{\circ}=2 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{wt}$
$\Rightarrow T=4 \mathrm{~kg}-\mathrm{wt}$ $\begin{aligned} T_1 & =T \cos 30^{\circ} \\ & =4 \cos 30^{\circ}=2 \sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two blocks $A$ and $B$ of masses $2 m$ and $m$, respectively, are connected by a massless and inextensible string. The whole system is suspended by a massless spring as shown in the figure. The magnitudes of acceleration of $A$ and $B$, immediately after the string is cut, are respectively
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} a_A=\frac{g}{2} \\ \quad a_B=g\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body $A$ of mass 1 kg rests on a smooth surface. Another body $B$ of mass 0.2 kg is placed over $A$ as shown. The coefficient of static friction between $A$ and $B$ is $0.15 . B$ will begin to slide on $A$, if $A$ is pulled with a force greater than
options: It will not slide for any $F$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure shown, a cubical block is held stationary against a rough wall by applying force '$F$', then incorrect statement among the following is
options: F does not apply any torque
questions.solution:
For equilibrium, $\begin{aligned} f & =M g \\ F & =N \end{aligned}$
For rotational equilibrium normal will shift downward.
Hence torque due to friction about centre of mass = Torque due to normal reaction about centre of mass.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two springs of force constants $k_1$ and $k_2$ are connected as shown. The effective spring constant $k$ is
questions.solution:
Effective spring constant of parallel combination
$k_e=k_1+k_2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: With what minimum acceleration can a fireman slide down a rope while breaking strength of the rope is $\frac{2}{3}$ of the weight?
questions.solution:
If man slides down with some acceleration, then its apparent weight decreases. For critical condition rope can bear only $2 / 3$ of his weight. If $a$ is the minimum acceleration, then tension in the rope $=m(g-a)$
= breaking strength
$\Rightarrow \quad m(g-a)=\frac{2}{3} m g$
$\Rightarrow \quad a=g-\frac{2 g}{3}=\frac{g}{3}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of charge $q$ and mass $m$ is moving with a velocity $-2 v \hat{i}(v \neq 0)$ towards a large screen placed in $Y Z$-plane placed at a distance $d$. If there is a magnetic field $B=B_0 \hat{\mathbf{k}}$, the maximum value of $v$ for which the particle will not strike the screen is
questions.solution:
The charged particle will not strike the screen, if $d \gt R$ (Radius of path) $\Rightarrow \quad d\gt\frac{m(2 v)}{B_0 q} \Rightarrow v \lt \frac{q d B_0}{2 m}$
$\therefore \quad v_{\max }=\frac{q d B_0}{2 m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires $P Q$ and $Q R$, carry equal currents I as shown in figure. One end of both the wires extends to infinity $\angle P Q R=\theta$. The magnitude of the magnetic field at $O$ on the bisector of these two wires at a distance $r$ from point $O$ is
questions.solution:
Perpendicular of $O$ from $P Q$ or $Q R$, $a=r \sin \theta / 2$ magnetic field induction at $O$ due to current through $P Q$ and $Q R$ is
$\begin{aligned} B & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{a}\left[\sin \left(90^{\circ}-\theta / 2\right)+\sin 90^{\circ}\right] \times 2 \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{1}{r \sin \theta / 2}(\cos \theta / 2+1) \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \cdot \frac{l}{r} \frac{(1+\cos \theta / 2)}{\sin \theta / 2}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Mixed $\mathrm{He}^{+}$and $\mathrm{O}^{2+}$ ions (mass of $\mathrm{He}^{+}=4 \mathrm{amu}$ and that of $\mathrm{O}^{2+}=16 \mathrm{amu}$ ) beam passes a region of constant perpendicular magnetic field. If kinetic energy of all the ions is same, then
options: $\mathrm{He}^{+}$ions will be deflected more than those of $\mathrm{O}^{2+}$
$\mathrm{He}^{+}$ions will be deflected less than those of $\mathrm{O}^{2+}$
all the ions will be deflected equally
questions.solution:
Radius of path of charged particle $r=\frac{\sqrt{2 m E_k}}{B q}$ $E_k$ remains same, so $\begin{aligned} \frac{r_1}{r_2} & =\sqrt{\frac{m_1}{m_2}} \cdot \frac{q_2}{q_1} \\ & =\sqrt{\frac{4}{16}} \times \frac{2}{1}=\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{2}{1}=\frac{1}{1} \end{aligned}$
So, correct choice is (3).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A bar magnet has a magnetic moment equal to $5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~Wb}-\mathrm{m}$. It is suspended in a magnetising field equal to $8 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~A}-\mathrm{m}^{-1}$.
The magnet vibrates with a period 15 s . The moment of inertia of the magnet is:
questions.solution:
The SI unit of magnetic moment is $\mathrm{A}-\mathrm{m}^2$, but the magnetic moment is given in $\mathrm{Wb}-\mathrm{m}^2$, which is the unit of $\mu_0 M$.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad \mu_0 M & =5 \times 10^{-5} \\ M & =\frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{\mu_0} \mathrm{~Wb}-\mathrm{m} \\ & =\frac{5 \times 10^{-5}}{4 \pi \times 10^{-7}}=\frac{125}{\pi}\end{aligned}$
As
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad I & =\frac{T^2 M B}{4 \pi^2}=\frac{(15)^2\left(\frac{125}{\pi}\right) \times 8 \pi \times 10^{-4}}{4 \pi^2} \\ & =0.57 \mathrm{~kg} \mathrm{~m}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A straight conductor carries a current of 5 A . An electron travelling with a speed of $5 \times 10^6 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ parallel to the wire at a distance of 0.1 m from the conductor, experiences a force of
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {} \quad F=\operatorname{Bev} \text { and } B=\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi r} \\ & \therefore \quad F=\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 \pi r} \times \mathrm{ev} \\ & =\frac{4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 5}{2 \pi \times 0.1} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 5 \times 10^6 \\ & =8 \times 10^{-18} \mathrm{~N}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The time period of a freely suspended magnet is 4 s . If it is broken in length into two equal parts and one part is suspended in the same way, then the time period will be
questions.solution:
Time period of a suspended magnet is given by
$T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I}{M B}}$
where, $I$ = moment of inertia of the magnet about the point of suspension.
$M=$ magnetic moment
$B=$ magnetic field
When the magnet is broken in length
$T^{2^{\prime}}=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I^{\prime}}{M^{\prime} B}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{T}{T^{2^{\prime}}}=\sqrt{\frac{I}{I^{\prime}} \times} \sqrt{\frac{M^{\prime}}{M}}=\sqrt{\frac{m L^2}{m^{\prime} L^{\prime 2}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{L^{\prime}}{L}}$
Here, $m$ and $m$ ' are the masses of the magnet and L and L's are their lengths.
$=\sqrt{\frac{m}{m^{\prime}}} \times \sqrt{\frac{L}{L^{\prime}}}=\sqrt{\frac{m}{m / 2}} \times \sqrt{\frac{L}{L / 2}}=\sqrt{2} \times \sqrt{2}=2$
$\Rightarrow \quad T^{\prime}=\frac{T}{2}=\frac{4}{2}=2 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two long straight wires are set parallel to each other. Each carries a current in the same direction and the separation between them is $2 r$. The intensity of the magnetic field mid-way between them is
questions.solution:
At point P,
$\begin{aligned} B_{\text {net }} & =B_{A B}+B_{C D} \\ & =\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 r}+\frac{\mu_0 i}{2 r}=\frac{\mu_0 i}{r}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long solenoid of length $L$ has a mean diameter $D$. It has $n$ layers of winding of $N$ turns each. If it carries a current $i$, the , magnetic field at its centre will be
questions.solution: Magnetic field due to solenoid is independent of diameter (Because $B=\mu_0 n i$ ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid of 2.5 m length and 2.0 cm diameter possesses 10 turns per cm. A current of 0.5 A is flowing through it. The magnetic induction at axis inside the solenoid is
questions.solution:
The magnetic induction along the axis of solenoid
$B=\mu_0 n I$
where, $n=$ number of turns per unit length
and $\quad I=$ current
$\because \quad \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi}=10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{A}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T} / \mathrm{A} \\ \text { Given, } & n=10 \mathrm{~cm}^{-1}=1000 \mathrm{~m}^{-1}\end{array}$
$I=0.5 \mathrm{~A}$
$\therefore \quad B=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1000 \times 0.5$
$=2 \pi \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A long wire carries a steady current. It is bent into a circle of one turn and the magnetic field at the centre of the coil is $B$. It is then bent into a circular loop of $n$ turns. The magnetic field at the centre of the coil will be
questions.solution:
$\therefore$ Current, $l=2 \pi R=2 \pi n \cdot r$ $\left [\because r=\frac{R}{n}\right]$
The magnetic field at the centre of the coil,
$B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi I}{R}$\ldots(i)
and $B^{\prime}=\left(\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi I}{r}\right) \cdot n$\ldots(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$B^{\prime}=n^2 B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A current carrying coil is bent sharply so as to convert it into a double loop both carrying current in the same direction. If $B$ be the initial magnetic field at the centre, then what will be the final concentric magnetic field?
questions.solution:
Radius of the double loop, $r=\frac{R}{2}$
where, $R$ is the radius of single loop $B$ (centre) $=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 \pi I}{R}$.
So, magnetic field due to each coil is double of that due to single coil.
and total magnetic field
$=2 B+2 B=4 B$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A circular coil of 100 turns has an effective radius of 0.05 m and carries a current of 0.1 A . How much work is required to turn it in an external magnetic field of $1.5 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ through $180^{\circ}$ about its axis perpendicular to the magnetic field? The plane of the coil is initially perpendicular to the magnetic field.
questions.solution:
The potential energy of circular loop in the magnetic field $=-M B \cos \theta$, where $\theta$ is the angle between normal to plane of coil and magnetic field $B$. Initial potential energy, $U_i=-N i A B \cos 0^{\circ}=-N i A B$
When coil is turned through $180^{\circ}$, therefore final potential energy, $U_f=-N i A B \cos 180^{\circ}=N i A B$ $\therefore$ Required work, $W=$ gain in potential energy $=U_f-U_i=N i A B-(-N i A B)=2NiAB$
Here, $N=100, \quad i=0.1 \mathrm{~A}, \quad B=1.5 \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ and radius $r=0.05 \mathrm{~m}$ $\therefore \quad A=\pi r^2=3.14 \times(0.05)^2=7.85 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^2$ $\therefore$ Work, $\quad W=2 \times 100 \times 0.1 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} \times 1.5$ $\Rightarrow \quad W=0.2355 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper wire of diameter 1.6 mm carries a current $I$. The maximum magnetic field due to this wire is $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}$. The value of $l$ is :
questions.solution:
Maximum magnetic field due to wire carrying current is at surface and is given by
$B=\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{2 l}{R}$
or $\quad I=\frac{B R}{2 \times\left(\mu_0 / 4 \pi\right)}$
$\therefore \quad I=\frac{5 \times 10^{-4} \times\left(\frac{1.6 \times 10^{-3}}{2}\right)}{2 \times 10^{-7}}=2 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A short bar magnet is placed with its south pole towards geographical north. The neutral points are situated at a distance of 20 cm from the centre of the magnet. If $B_H=0.3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~Wb} / \mathrm{m}^2$ then the magnetic moment of the magnet is
questions.solution:
At neutral point magnetic field due to magnet = Horizontal component of the earth's magnetic field.
$\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow \frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \cdot \frac{2 M}{r^3}=B_H \Rightarrow \frac{10^{-7} \times 2 \times M \times 1}{(0.2)^3}=0.3 \times 15^4 \\
& \Rightarrow M=1.2 \mathrm{amp} \times \mathrm{m}^2=1200 \mathrm{ab}-\mathrm{amp} \times \mathrm{cm}^2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A coil in the shape of an equilateral triangle of side 1 is suspended between the pole pieces of a permanent magnet such that $B$ is in the plane of the coil. If due to current $i$ in the triangle, a torque $\tau$ acts on it. The side $l$ of the triangle is
questions.solution:
The current flowing clockw:se in an equilateral triangle has a magnetic field in the direction of $\hat{\mathbf{k}}$.
$\begin{aligned} & \tau=\text { BiNA } \sin \theta \\ & \tau=\text { BiNA } \sin 90^{\circ}\end{aligned}$ $\tau=B i \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} I^2 \times 1$ $\left[\because\right.$ Area of equilateral triangle $=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} l^2$ and $N=1$, (assuming] $\Rightarrow \quad l^2=\frac{4 \tau}{\sqrt{3} B i} \Rightarrow l=2\left[\frac{\tau}{B i \sqrt{3}}\right]^{1 / 2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle travels along a straight line with a speed $v$ in a region where both electric field $\mathbf{E}$ and magnetic fields $\mathbf{B}$ are present. It follows that
options: $|\mathbf{E}|=v|\mathbf{B}|$ and the two fields are parallel
$|\mathbf{E}|=v|\mathbf{B}|$ and the two fields are perpendicular
$|\mathbf{B}|=v|\mathbf{E}|$ and the two fields are parallel
$|\mathbf{B}|=v|\mathbf{E}|$ and the two fields are perpendicular
questions.solution:
Force due to magnetic field is $\begin{aligned} F & =q v B \sin \theta \\ \theta & =90^{\circ} \\ F & =q v B....(i) \end{aligned}$ when
Force due to electric field $E$ is $F=q E$....(ii)
Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get $|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{E}}|=v|\overrightarrow{\mathbf{B}}|$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A solenoid of length 50 cm and a radius of cross-section 1 cm has 1000 turns of wire wound over it. If the current carried is 5 A , the magnetic field on its axis, near the centre of the solenoid is approximately (permeability of free space $\left.\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~T}-\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{A}\right)$
questions.solution:
The magnetic field is given by
\(B=\mu_0 n i\)
where \(\mu_0=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} T-m \mathrm{~A}^{-1}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& n=\frac{1000}{50 \times 10^{-2}} i=5 A \\
& \therefore B=4 \pi \times 10^{-7} \times \frac{1000 \times 10^{-2}}{50 \times 10^{-2}} \times 5 \\
& B=1.26 \times 10^{-2} T
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires of same lengths are shaped into a square and a circle. If they carry same current, then the ratio of their magnetic moments is
questions.solution:
Magnetic moment of a loop is given by $p_m=I A$ where, $\quad I=$ current through the loop $A=$ area of loop
Perimeter of square should be equal to the circumference of circle, since wires have same length. Therefore, $4 a=2 \pi r \Rightarrow a=\frac{\pi r}{2}$ ...(i) In Fig. (i), magnetic moment of square loop is given by $\begin{aligned} & p_{m_1}=I\left(A_1\right) \quad \Rightarrow \\ \Rightarrow \quad & p_{m_1}=\frac{I \pi^2 r^2}{4}\end{aligned} \quad$ [from Eq. (i)] ...(ii) In Fig. (ii), magnetic moment of circular loop is given by $\begin{aligned} & p_{m_2}=I\left(A_2\right) \\ & p_{m_2}=I\left(\pi r^2\right) \end{aligned}$ ...(iii) From Eqs. (ii) and (iii), we get $\Rightarrow \quad \frac{p_{m_1}}{p_{m_2}}=\frac{\pi}{4}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The formula for magnetic field of a certain component is given by $B=\frac{\mu_0 N i}{2 \pi r}$
where, $N=$ total number of turns
$i=$ current through it
$r=$ radius.
The component is
questions.solution:
Magnetic field at the centre of a toroid is given by
$B=\frac{\mu_0 N i}{2 \pi r}$
where, $n=\frac{N}{2 \pi r}=$ number of turns per unit length
of the toroid.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bar magnets having same geometry with magnetic moments $m$ and $2 m$, are firstly placed in such a way that their similar poles one are on same side. Then, its time period of oscillation is $T_1$. Now, the polarity of the one of the magnet is reversed. Then, time period of oscillation is $T_2$, then
questions.solution:
When similar poles are on same side time period of oscillation $T_1$ is given by
$T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_1+I_2}{\left(m_1+m_2\right) B_H}}$ [given]
$m_1=2 m, m_2=m$
$T_1=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_1+I_2}{(3 m) B_H}}\ldots$ (i)
When the polarity of magnet is reversed, time period of oscillation $T_2$ is given by
$T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_1+I_2}{\left(m_1-m_2\right) B_H}}$
$T_2=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{I_1+I_2}{m B_H}}\ldots$ (ii)
On comparing Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$T_2 \gt T_1$
or $T_1 \lt T_2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $A, B$ and $C$ are parallel conductors of equal lengths carrying currents $I, I$ and $2 I$ respectively. Distance between $A$ and $B$ is $x$. Distance between $B$ and $C$ is also $x . F_1$ is the force exerted by $B$ on $A . F_2$ is the force exerted by $C$ on $A$. Choose the correct answer.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text {} F_{B A}=\frac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi x} \text { (attractive force) } \\
& \text { and } \quad F_{C A}=\frac{\mu_0(I)(2 I)}{2 \pi(2 x)}=\frac{\mu_0 I^2}{2 \pi x} \text { (repulsive force) } \\
& \text { Hence, } \quad F_{B A}=-F_{C A} \\
& \Rightarrow \quad F_1=-F_2
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force per unit length between two parallel current carrying straight conductors separated by $2 d$ is given by the formula
questions.solution:
Force per unit length between the two parallel current carrying wires
$\frac{F}{L}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi r} i_1 i_2$ $=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi(2 d)} i_1 i_2=\frac{\mu_0 i_1 i_2}{4 \pi d} \quad[\because r=2 d]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire in the form of a square of side a carries a current $i$. Then, the magnetic induction at the centre of the square wire is (magnetic permeability of free space $=\mu_0$ ).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two similar coils are kept mutually perpendicular such that their centres coincide. At the centre, find the ratio of the magnetic field due to one coil and the resultant magnetic field through both coils, if the same current is flown
Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii) we get
$\Delta m=\frac{M C \Delta \theta}{C^2}=\frac{M \times 0.2 \times 100 \times 4.2 \times 10^3}{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2} \mathrm{~J}$
$\frac{\Delta m}{M}=\frac{20 \times 4.2 \times 10^3}{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2}$
\% increase in mass
$=\frac{\Delta m}{M} \times 100=\frac{20 \times 4.2 \times 10^3}{\left(3 \times 10^8\right)^2} \times 100$
$=9.3 \times 10^{-11} \%$
questions.solution:
Where the two coils are kept perpendicular to each other then resultant field in one coil is directed along the axis of coil and the other downwards, hence resultant field is
$B^{\prime}=\sqrt{B^2+B^2}=\sqrt{2} B$ Ratio of magnetic field due to one coil and resultant is $\frac{B}{B \sqrt{2}}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two parallel conductors $A$ and $B$ of equal length carry currents $i$ and $10 i$, respectively, in the same direction. Then
options: $A$ and $B$ will repel each other with same force
$A$ and $B$ will attract each other with same force
$A$ and $B$ will attract each other with different forces
questions.solution:
The magnitude of force acting on length $l$ of each conductor due to other is $\frac{F}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{i_1 i_2}{r}$

$\begin{array}{ll}\text { Given } & i_1=i, i_2=10 i \\ \therefore & \frac{F}{l}=\frac{\mu_0}{2 \pi} \frac{10 i^2}{r}\end{array}$
From Fleming's left hand rule, this is force of attraction.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the figure shown, the magnetic field induction at the point $O$ will be
questions.solution:
Field due to a straight wire of infinite length is $\frac{\mu_0 i}{4 \pi r}$ if the point is on a line perpendicular to its length while at the centre of a semicircular coil is $\frac{\mu_0 \pi i}{4 \pi r}$.
$\begin{aligned} \therefore B & =B_a+B_b+B_c \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{i}{r}+\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{\pi i}{r}+\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{i}{r} \\ & =\frac{\mu_0}{4 \pi} \frac{i}{r}(\pi+2) \text { out of the page }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of magnetic moments of two short magnets which give null deflection in $\tan B$ position at 12 cm and 18 cm from the centre of a deflection magnetometer is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A proton and an $\alpha$-particle enter a uniform magnetic field perpendicularly with the same speed. If proton takes $25 \mu \mathrm{~s}$ to make 5 revolutions, then the periodic time for the $\alpha$-particle would be
questions.solution:
Time period of proton $T_p=\frac{25}{5}=5 \mu \mathrm{~s}$ 
By using $T=\frac{2 \pi m}{q B}$
$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{T_\alpha}{T_p}=\frac{m_\alpha}{m_p} \times \frac{q_p}{q_\alpha}=\frac{4 m_p}{m_p} \times \frac{q_p}{2 q_p}=2 \\ & \Rightarrow  T_\alpha=2 T_p=10 \mu \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A charged particle enters a magnetic field $H$ with its initial velocity making an angle of $45^{\circ}$ with $H$. The path of the particle will be
questions.solution: If charged particle enters in magnetic field making an angle less than $90^{\circ}$, then its path will be a helix.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A conducting wire is moving towards right in a magnetic field $B$. The direction of induced current in the wire is shown in the figure. The direction of magnetic field will be
options: in the plane of paper pointing towards right
in the plane of paper pointing towards left
perpendicular to the plane of paper and downwards
perpendicular to the plane of paper and upwards
questions.solution: According to Fleming's right hand rule, magnetic field will be perpendicular to the plane of paper and act downward.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An electron moving with velocity $2 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, describes a circle in a magnetic field of strength $2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~T}$. If $\frac{e}{m}$ of electron is $1.76 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{kg}$. Then the diameter of the circle will be
questions.solution:
The force exerted on electron moving with velocity $v$ is
$F=e v B \sin \theta$
where $\theta=90^{\circ}$
$\therefore \quad F_B=e v B$...(i)
Centripetal force is given by
$F_C=\frac{m v^2}{r}$...(ii)
Equating Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$r=\frac{m v^2}{e B}$
Diameter $d=2 r=\frac{2 v}{(e / m) B}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad d^{\prime} & =\frac{2 \times 2 \times 10^7}{1.76 \times 10^{11} \times 2 \times 10^{-2}} \\ & =1.1 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A magnet makes 40 oscillations per minute at a place having magnetic field intensity $B_H=0.1 \times 10^{-5}$. At another place, it takes 2.5 s to complete one vibration. The value of the earth's horizontal field at the place
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { By using } T=2 \pi \sqrt{\frac{1}{M B_H}} \Rightarrow \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(B_H\right)_2}{\left(B_H\right)_1}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{60 / 40}{2.5}=\sqrt{\frac{\left(B_H\right)_2}{0.1 \times 10^{-5}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad\left(B_H\right)_2=0.36 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~T}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a coil of resistance $150 \Omega$, a current is induced by changing the magnetic flux through it as shown by figure. The magnitude of flux through the coil is $\qquad$ Wb.
questions.solution:
By Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction,
$\begin{array}{ll}
\varepsilon=\frac{d \phi}{d t}, \text { Also, } \varepsilon=i R \\
\therefore & \frac{d \phi}{d t}=i R \Rightarrow d \phi=i R d t \\
\Rightarrow & \int d \phi=R \int i d t
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad \phi=R \times$ Area of current versus time
$=150 \times \frac{1}{2} \times 0.4 \times 10=300 \mathrm{~Wb}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cylindrical conducting rod is kept with its axis along positive $z$-axis, where a uniform magnetic field exists parallel to $z$-axis. The current induced in the cylinder is
options: opposite to the direction of magnetic field
questions.solution:
Since, $B$ is constant,
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore & & \frac{d \phi}{d t} & =0 \\
\therefore & & i & =0
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two points $A$ and $B$ are situated at distance $x$ and $2 x$ respectively from the nearer pole of a magnet 2 cm long. The ratio of magnetic field at $A$ and $B$ is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin bar magnet of length $2 L$ is bent at the mid-point so that the angle between them is $60^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The new length of the magnet is
questions.solution:
On bending the magnet, the length of the magnet
$\begin{aligned} A C & =A B+B C \\ & =L \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)+L \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right) \\ & =2 L \sin \left(\frac{\theta}{2}\right)=2 L \sin 30^{\circ} \\ & =2 L \times \frac{1}{2}=L\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: - The coereivity of a small bar magnet is $4 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~A} / \mathrm{m}$. It is inserted inside a solenoid of 500 turns and length 1 m to demagnetise it. The amount of current to be passed through the solenoid will be
questions.solution: $H=n i \Rightarrow i=\frac{H}{n}=\frac{4 \times 10^3}{500}=8 \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The horizontal component of earth's magnetic field at a place is $0.4 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~T}$. If the angle of dip is $45^{\circ}$ the value of total intensity of earth's magnetic field is
questions.solution:
Vertical component of earth's magnetic field is
$B_V=B_H \tan \delta$
where, $B_H=$ horizontal component of earth's magnetic field
$\delta=$ angle of dip.
Total intensity of earth's maanetic field
$\begin{aligned} B & =\sqrt{B_H^2+B_V^2} \\ & =\sqrt{B_H^2+B_H^2}\left[\because \tan \delta=\tan 45^{\circ}=1\right] \\ & =\sqrt{2} B_H \\ & =1.44 \times 0.4 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T} \\ & =5.656 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~T} \\ & =0.5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~T}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If the momentum of a body is increased by $100 \%$, then the percentage increase in the kinetic energy is
questions.solution:
$\mathrm{KE}=\frac{p^2}{2 m} \Rightarrow \mathrm{KE}_2=\mathrm{KE}_1\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^2=\mathrm{KE}_1\left(\frac{2 p}{p}\right)^2$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow  \mathrm{KE}_2 & =4 \mathrm{KE}_1 \\ & =\mathrm{KE}_1+3 \mathrm{KE}_1=\mathrm{KE}_1+300 \% \text { of } \mathrm{KE}_1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A student performs an experiment for determination of $g\left(=\frac{4 \pi^2 l}{T^2}\right), l \approx 1 \mathrm{~m}$, and he commits an error of $\Delta l$. For $T$ he takes the time of $n$ oscillations with the stop watch of least count $\Delta T$ and he commits a human error of 0.1 s . For which of the following data, the measurement of $g$ will be most accurate?
questions.solution:
we have to calculate the error in gravity using the formulae
\(\frac{\Delta \mathrm{g}}{\mathrm{~g}}=\frac{\Delta \mathrm{l}}{\mathrm{l}}+\frac{2 \Delta \mathrm{t}}{\mathrm{nt}}\)
where \(g=\) acceleration due to gravity
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{l}=\text { length } \\
& \mathrm{n}=\text { number of oscillations }
\end{aligned}\)
So, after hit and trial method we can say that with option \(D\) we will get the least error.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: To what height should a rectangular cylinder having square base of length 10 cm be filled, so that the total force on the bottom is equal to that on the sides ?
questions.solution:
If $h$ is the height of liquid column in cylinder and $I$ is the length of each side of base. Then
$\begin{aligned} & F_B=(h \rho g)(I \times l)=h \rho g l^2 \\ & F_S=\left(\frac{h \rho g}{2}\right)(4 I \times h)=2 h^2 \rho g l\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad h \rho g l^2=2 h^2 \rho g l$
or $h=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{10}{2}=5 \mathrm{~cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The lower end of a capillary tube is dipped into water and it is seen that water rises through 7.5 cm in the capillary. Given, surface tension of water is $7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1}$ and angle of contact between water and glass capillary tube is zero.
What will be the diameter (in mm ) of the capillary tube? (given, $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ )
questions.solution:
Given, $h=7.5 \mathrm{~cm}=7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned}& S=7.5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{Nm}^{-1} \\
& \theta=\sigma^{\circ}, r=?\end{aligned}$
As, $h=\frac{2 S \cos \theta}{r \rho g} \Rightarrow 2 r=\frac{4 S \cos \theta}{h \rho g}$
$=\frac{4 \times 7.5 \times 10^{-2} \times \cos 0^{\circ}}{7.5 \times 10^{-2} \times 10^3 \times 10}=4 \times 10^{-4}=0.4 \mathrm{~mm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Surface of the lake is at $2^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and depth of the lake is 20 m . Find the temperature of the bottom of the lake
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text {  } \begin{aligned} & \text { From } \frac{P_1}{T_1}= \frac{P_2}{T_2} \\ & \frac{1 \times 10^5}{2+273}=\frac{h d g+1 \times 10^5}{T_2} \\ & {\left[\because P_1=\text { atmospheric pressure }\right] } \\ & h=20 \mathrm{~m}, d=\text { density of water } \\ &= 1 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{m}^3\end{aligned} \\ & \begin{aligned} \frac{1 \times 10^5}{275} & =\frac{20 \times 10^3 \times 10+1 \times 10^5}{T_2} \\ \begin{aligned} \frac{1 \times 10^5}{275}\end{aligned} & =\frac{3 \times 10^5}{T_2} \\ T_2 & =275 \times 3=825 \mathrm{~K} \\ & =825-273 \\ & =552^{\circ} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following materials is non-crystalline?
questions.solution: Wood is non-crystalline.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The breaking force for a wire of diameter $D$ of a material is $F$. The breaking force for a wire of the same material of radius $D$ is
questions.solution:
Breaking stress $=\frac{\text { Breaking force }}{\text { Area }}=$ constant
$\begin{aligned} \frac{F}{\left(\frac{\pi D^2}{4}\right)} & =\frac{F^{\prime}}{\pi D^2} \\ F^{\prime} & =4 F\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The strain stress curves of three wires of different materials are shown in the figure. $P$, $Q$ and $R$ are the elastic limits of the wires. The figure shows that
options: None of the above is true
questions.solution:
On the graph stress is represented on $x$-axis and strain $y$-axis So from the graph $y=\cot \theta=\frac{1}{\tan \theta} \propto \frac{1}{\theta}$ [where, $\theta$ is the angle from stress axis] $\therefore \quad Y_P \lt Y_Q \lt Y_R \quad\left[\text { As } \theta_P \lt \theta_Q \lt \theta_R\right]$
We can say that elasticity of wire $P$ is minimum and $R$ is maximum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The Bulk Modulus for an incompressible liquid is
questions.solution:
Since liquid is incompressible there is no change in volume.
When strain is small the ratio of the normal stress to the volume strain is called the bulk modulus of material of the body.
$\begin{aligned} B & =\frac{\text { normal stress }}{\text { volume strain }} \\ & =\text { normal stress }\end{aligned}$
change in volume/original volume
since, the liquid is incompressible change in volume is zero.
normal stress
$B=\frac{\text { normal stress }}{0}=\infty$
Hence, bulk modulus is infinite.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A copper wire and a steel wire of the same diameter and length are joined end to end and a force is applied which stretches their combined length by 1 cm . Then, the two wires will have
options: the same stress but different strains
the same strain but different stresses
questions.solution: As same force is applied to each wire having equal area of cross-section, so stress is same but extensions will be different for wires of different materials. So, same stress but different strains are there.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The stress versus strain graphs for wires of two materials $A$ and $B$ are as shown in the figure. If $Y_A$ and $Y_B$ are the Young's modulii of the materials, then
questions.solution:
We know that the slope of the line in a stress-strain curve represents the young's modulus for a wire. We also know that the slope of a line can be calculated as the tangent of the angle the line makes with the positive x -axis of the graph.
So, for wire A, the stress-strain line of the material is at an angle of \(60^{\circ}\) from the positive \(x\)-axis. So the slope of the line \(\left(m_A\right)\) will be
\(\begin{aligned}
& m_A=\tan 60^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow m_A=\sqrt{3}
\end{aligned}\)
Hence the young's modulus of wire A will also be \(\sqrt{3}\).
Similarly, for wire B , the stress-strain line of the material is at an angle of \(30^{\circ}\) from the positive \(x\) axis. So, the slope of the line \(\left(m_B\right)\) will be
\(\begin{aligned}
& m_B=\tan 30^{\circ} \\
& \Rightarrow m_B=\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}
\end{aligned}\)
Hence the young's modulus of wire B will also be \(1 / \sqrt{3}\).
Then taking the ratio of the young's modulus for wire A and B , we get
\(\begin{aligned}
& \frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{1 / \sqrt{3}} \\
& \therefore \frac{Y_A}{Y_B}=3
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two steel wires having same length are suspended from a ceiling under the same load. If the ratio of their energy stored per unit volume is $1: 4$, then the ratio of their diameters is found to be $\sqrt{k}: 1$. Find the value of $k$.
questions.solution:
Elastic potential energy stored in a loaded'wire,
$U=\frac{1}{2} \times($ Stress $\times$ Strain $\times$ Volume $)$
$\therefore$ Energy stored per unit volume,
$U=\frac{U}{\text { Volume }}=\frac{1}{2} \times$ Stress $\times$ Strain $=\frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{F}{A}\right)^2 \times \frac{1}{Y}$
$\frac{U_A}{U_B}=\frac{\frac{1}{2 Y} \cdot \frac{F^2}{A_A^2}}{\frac{1}{2 Y} \cdot \frac{F^2}{A_B^2}}=\frac{A_B^2}{A_A^2}, \frac{U_A}{U_B}=\frac{d_B^4}{d_A^4}$
Here,$\frac{U_A}{U_B}=\frac{1}{4}, \frac{d_B^4}{d_A^4}=\frac{1}{4}$ or $\frac{d_B}{d_A}=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$
$\frac{d_A}{d_B}=\sqrt{2}: 1=\sqrt{k}: 1$ (given)
$\therefore \quad k=2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A steel ring of radius $r$ and cross-sectional area $A$ is fitted on to a wooden disc of radius $R(R \gt r)$. If Young's modulus be $Y$, then the force with which the steel ring is expanded, is
questions.solution:
Strain $=\frac{\Delta l}{l}=\frac{2 \pi R-2 \pi r}{2 \pi r}=\left(\frac{R-r}{r}\right)$
Stress $=Y \times$ strain $=\frac{F}{A}$
$\therefore \quad F=Y \times A \times$ strain $=A Y \times\left(\frac{R-r}{r}\right)$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The stress that has to be applied to the ends of a steel wire of length 20 cm to keep its length constant, when its temperature is raised by $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $2.2 \times 10^x \mathrm{~Pa}$. The value of $x$ is $\qquad$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { (Given } Y=2 \times 10^{11} \mathrm{Nm}^{-2}, \\
& \left.\alpha=1.1 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{C}^{-1}\right)
\end{aligned}$
questions.solution:
From Hook's law, $Y=\frac{\text { Stress }}{\text { Strain }}$
$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \quad \text { Stress }=Y \times \text { Strain } \\ & \text { Now, } \quad \text { Strain }=\frac{\Delta L}{L}=\frac{L \alpha \Delta T}{L}=\alpha \Delta T \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Stress }=Y \times \alpha \Delta T \\ & =2 \times 10^{11} \times 1.1 \times 10^{-5} \times 100 \\ & =2.2 \times 10^8 \mathrm{~Pa} \end{aligned}$
According to question, $\begin{aligned} 2.2 \times 10^x & =2.2 \times 10^8 \\ x & =8 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Two wires $A$ and $B$ are of same materials. Their lengths are in the ratio $1: 2$ and diameters are in the ratio $2: 1$. When stretched by force $F_A$ and $F_B$, respectively, they get equal increase in their lengths.
Then the ratio $\frac{F_A}{F_B}$ should be
questions.solution:
Young's modulus, $Y=\frac{F \cdot L}{\pi r^2 \Delta L}$
Both wires are of same material. So, $Y_1=Y_2$ Here, $\begin{aligned} \frac{F_A \cdot L_A}{r_A^2 \Delta L_A} & =\frac{F_B L_B}{r_B^2 \cdot \Delta L_B} \\ \therefore \quad \Delta L_A & =\Delta L_B \\ \frac{F_A}{F_B} & =\frac{L_B}{L_A} \times\left(\frac{r_A}{r_B}\right)^2 \\ & =\frac{2}{1} \times\left(\frac{2}{1}\right)^2=\frac{8}{1} \\ F_A: F_B & =8: 1\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two wires of lengths $l$ and $2 l$, radii $r$ and $2 r$ respectively having same Young's modulus are hung with a weight $m g$. Net elongation is
questions.solution:
Young's modulus, $Y=\frac{m g l}{a_1 l_1}$
$\therefore l_1=\frac{m g l}{Y \pi r^2}...(i)$
and $Y=\frac{m g(2 l)}{a_2 l_2}=\frac{m g(2 l)}{\pi(2 r)^2 l_2}$
or $l_2=\frac{m g l}{2 Y \pi r^2}...(ii)$
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have
$\therefore l_1+l_2=\frac{m g l}{Y \pi r^2}+\frac{m g l}{2 Y \pi r^2}=\frac{3}{2} \frac{m g l}{Y \pi r^2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Temperature of a gas is $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and pressure is changed from $1.01 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$ to $1.165 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~Pa}$. If volume is decreased isothermally by $10 \%$. Bulk modulus of gas is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} B=-\frac{\Delta p}{\left(\frac{\Delta V}{V}\right)} & =-\frac{(1.165-1.01) \times 10^5}{0.1} \\ & =1.55 \times 10^5\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spring balance is attached to the ceiling of a lift. A man hangs his bag on the spring and the spring reads 49 N , when the lift is stationary. If the lift moves downward with an acceleration of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, the reading of the spring balance will be
questions.solution:
When the lift is stationary \(\mathrm{w}=\mathrm{mg}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \Rightarrow 49=m \times 9.8 \\
& m=5 \mathrm{~kg}
\end{aligned}\)
When the lift is moving downward with an acceleration
\(R=m(9.8-a)=5(9.8-5)=24 N\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body starts from the origin and moves along the $x$-axis such that velocity at any instant is given by $\left(4 t^3-2 t\right)$, where $t$ is in second and velocity is in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$. What is the acceleration of the particle, when it is $2 m$ from the origin?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text {Given that } v=4 t^3-2 t \\ & \qquad x=\int v d t, x=t^4-t^2+C \text {, at } t=0, x=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad C=0 \end{aligned}$
When particle is $2 m$ away from the origin, then \(\begin{aligned} & x=t^{4}-t^{2} \\ & \text {as } x=2 \\ & \Rightarrow t^{4}-t^{2}-2=0\end{aligned}\) $\begin{aligned}\Rightarrow & \left(t^2-2\right)\left(t^2+1\right) & =0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad t & =\sqrt{2} \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$ $\begin{aligned} & a=\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(4 t^3-2 t\right) \\ & a=12 t^2-2 \\ & t=\sqrt{2} \mathrm{sec} \\ & a=12 \times(\sqrt{2})^2-2=22 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two points move in the same straight line starting at the same moment from the same point in it. The first moves with constant velocity $u$ and the second with constant acceleration $f$. During the time elapses before the second catches, the first greatest distance between the particle is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball is dropped from top of a tower of 100 m height. Simultaneously another ball was thrown upward from bottom of the tower with a speed of $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. They will cross each other after $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Let the two balls cross each other after time $t$. Height covered by first ball $h_1=\frac{1}{2} g t^2$
Height covered by second ball $h_2=u t-\frac{1}{2} g t^2$ but $h_1+h_2=100$ $\begin{aligned} \frac{1}{2} g t^2+u t-\frac{1}{2} g t^2 & =100 \\ u t & =100 \\ 50 \times t & =100 \Rightarrow t=2 \mathrm{~s} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A car travelling on a straight track moves with uniform velocity of $v_1$ for some time and with unifrom velocity $v_2$ for the next equal time the average velocity on the car is
questions.solution:
Average velocity is equal to ratio of total displacement to total time. Velocity is a vector quantity and average velocity is defined as the total displacement undergone by the body per unit time.
Displacement in first instance $=v_1 t$
Displacement in second instance $=v_2 t$
$\therefore \quad$ Average velocity $=\frac{v_1 t+v_2 t}{t+t}=\frac{v_1+v_2}{2}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass 8 kg is moved by a force $F=3 x \mathrm{~N}$, where $x$ is the distance covered. Initial position is $x=2 \mathrm{~m}$ and the final position is $x=10 \mathrm{~m}$. The initial speed is zero. The final speed is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The displacement-time graph of a particle moving along a straight line is shown in the figure.
The acceleration-time graph of this particle is
questions.solution:
Given, $x=9 t^2$
$\therefore \quad v=\frac{d x}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}\left(9 t^2\right)=\frac{9 d}{d t}\left(t^2\right)=18 t$
also, $\quad a=\frac{d^2 x}{d t^2}=\frac{d v}{d t}=\frac{d}{d t}(18 t)=18$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The acceleration-time graph of a body is shown below
The most probable velocity-time graph of the body is
questions.solution:
From given a - $t$ graph acceleration is increasing at constant rate
$\begin{aligned}
& \therefore \frac{d a}{d t}=k \text { (constant) } \Rightarrow a=k t \text { (by integration) } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d v}{d t}=k t \Rightarrow d v=k t d t \\
& \Rightarrow \quad \int d v=k \int t d t \Rightarrow v=\frac{k t^2}{2}
\end{aligned}$
i.e., $v$ is dependent on time parabolically and parabola is symmetric about $v$-axis and suddenly acceleration becomes zero. i.e., velocity becomes constant. Hence the most stable graph is (c).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A point initially at rest moves along $x$-axis. Its acceleration varies with time as $a=(6 t+5)$ in $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}^2$. If it starts from origin, the distance covered in 2 s is :
questions.solution:
Given, $a=\frac{d v}{d t}=6 t+5$ Integrating it, $\int_0^v d v=\int_0^t(6 t+5) d t$
$v=\frac{6 t^2}{2}+5 t$
As $\quad v=\frac{d s}{d t}$, so $d s=\left(\frac{6 t^2}{2}+5 t\right) d t$ Integrating it, $\int_0^s d s=\int_0^t\left(\frac{6 t^2}{2}+5 t\right) d t$
$s=\frac{3 t^3}{3}+\frac{5 t^2}{2}$
When      $\begin{aligned} t & =2 \mathrm{~s}, s=3 \frac{(2)^3}{3}+\frac{5(2)^2}{2} \\ & =8+10=18 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A police jeep is chasing with velocity of $45 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ a thief in another jeep moving with velocity $153 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$. Police fires a bullet with muzzle velocity of $180 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The velocity it will strike the car of the thief is
questions.solution:
Effective speed of bullet is = Speed of bullet+Speed of police jeep
\(\begin{aligned}
& =18 \mathrm{om} / \mathrm{s}+45 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr} \\
& =18 \mathrm{o}+45 \times \frac{5}{18} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\
& =192.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}\)
Speed of theif's jeep \(=153 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{hr}=42.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
Velocity of bullet with respect to theif's jeep \(=192.5-42.5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)
\(=150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wet open umbrella is held vertical and it whirld about the handle at a uniform rate of 21 revolutions in 44 s . If the rim of the umbrella is circle of 1 m in diameter and the height of the rim above the flour is 4.9 m , the locus of the drop is a circle of radius
questions.solution:
From equation of motion
$h=u t+\frac{1}{2} g t^2$
where $u$ is initial velocity and $t$ is time. Since, $u=0$
$\therefore \quad t=\sqrt{\frac{2 h}{g}}=\frac{\sqrt{2 \times 4.9}}{9.8}=1 \mathrm{~s}$
The horizontal range of the drop $=x$, then
$x=\left(\frac{v_t}{o}\right) t$
Also, $\omega=\frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta t}=\frac{21 \times 2 \pi}{44}=3 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$
Tangential speed $0.5 \times 3 = 1.5$
$x=1.5 \times 1=1.5 \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned} \text {Locus of drop }=\sqrt{x^2+r^2} & =\sqrt{(1.5)^2+(0.5)^2} \\ & =\sqrt{2.5} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A train of 150 m length is going towards north direction at a speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. A parrot flies at a speed of $5 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ towards south direction parallel to the railway track, the time taken by the parrot to cross the train is equal to
questions.solution:
Relative velocity of the parrot w.r.t. the train
$[10-(-5)]=(10-(-5)) \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}=15 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Time taken by the parrot to cross the train
$=\frac{150}{15}=10 \mathrm{~s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A balloon is rising vertically up with a velocity of $29 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. A stone is dropped from it and it reaches the ground in 10 s . The height of the balloon when the stone was dropped from it is $\left(\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\right.$ )
questions.solution:
For a stone which is thrown downwards from a balloon rising upwards, the equation of motion is
$\begin{aligned}
h & =-u t+\frac{1}{2} g t^2 \\
& =-29 \times 10+\frac{1}{2} \times 9.8 \times(10)^2 \\
& =-290+490 \\
& =200 \mathrm{~m}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body is coming with a velocity of $72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}$ on a rough horizontal surface of coefficient of friction 0.5 . If the acceleration due to gravity is $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$, find the minimum distance it can be stopped.
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } u=72 \mathrm{~km} / \mathrm{h}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ & a=\mu g=0.5 \times 10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2 \\ & \text { From, }  v^2=u^2-2 a s\end{aligned}$
$\therefore (0)^2=(20)^2-2 \times 0.5 \times 10 \times s$
$\therefore  s=\frac{20 \times 20}{2 \times 0.5 \times 10}$
or $s=40 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body of mass 10 kg is moving with a constant velocity of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. When a constant force acts for 4 s on it, it moves with a velocity $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ in the opposite direction. The acceleration produced in it is
questions.solution:
According to equation of motion
$v=u+a t$
$\Rightarrow \quad-2=10+a \times 4$
$\therefore \quad a=-3 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A bullet comes out of the barrel of gun of length 2 m with a speed $80 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The average acceleration of the bullet is
questions.solution:
Given, $s=2 \mathrm{~m}, u=80 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v=0$
From $v^2=u^2-2 a s$
$\therefore (0)^2=(80)^2-2 \times a \times 2$
or $a=\frac{80 \times 80}{4}=1600 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A cyclist moving on a circular track of radius 40 m completes half a revolution in 40 s . Its average velocity is :
questions.solution:
Average velocity $=\frac{\text { displacement }}{\text { time }}=\frac{2 r}{t}=\frac{2 \times 40}{40}$
= 2 m/s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A juggler keeps on moving four balls in air throwing the balls after regular intervals. When one ball leaves his hand (speed $=20 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$ ), the position of other balls (height in metre) will be (take $g=10 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ )

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A elevator car whose floor to distance is 2.7 m starts ascending with a constant acceleration of $1.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2, 2 \mathrm{~s}$ after a bolt is begin to fall from the ceiling of the car. The free fall time of the bolt is $\left(g=9.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^e\right)$
questions.solution:
Net acceleration on the elevator car is more than acceleration due to gravity.
Since elevator car is ascending upwards, from Newton's second law, the net force is acting upwards hence resultant acceleration is
$a_t=(g+a)$
$=(9.8+1.2)=11 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Relative velocity of observer to elevator is
$u_r=0$
from the equation
$\begin{aligned} s & =u_r t+\frac{1}{2} a_1 t^2 \\ 2.7 & =0+\frac{1}{2} \times 11 \times t^2 \\ t^2 & =\frac{5.4}{11} \\ t & =\sqrt{\frac{5.4}{11}} \mathrm{~s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle $p$ is moving in a circle of radius $r$ with a uniform speed $v, C$ is the centre of the circle and $A B$ is the diameter. The angular velocity of $p$ about $A$ and $C$ is in the ratio :
questions.solution:
Angular velocity about $A$, $\omega_1=\frac{v}{2 r}$
Angular velocity about $C$, $\begin{aligned} & \omega_2=\frac{v}{r} \\ & \therefore \frac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} \\ &=\frac{v / 2 r}{v / r}=1: 2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Four projectiles are thrown with the same initial speed making angles $27^{\circ}, 36^{\circ}, 43^{\circ}, 51^{\circ}$, with the horizontal. The range of projectiles will be largest for the projectile fired at angle
questions.solution:
Range of a projectile is maximum for angle of projection of $45^{\circ}$.
The nearest value is $43^{\circ}$. Thus, among given values of angles of projection, range will be maximum for $43^{\circ}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A projectile is thrown in upward direction making an angle of $60^{\circ}$ with the horizontal direction with a velocity of $150 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. Then, the time after which its inclination with horizontal is $45^{\circ}$, is
questions.solution:
At the two points of the trajectory during projectile motion, the horizontal component of velocity is same. Then,
$150 \times \frac{1}{2}=v \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \Rightarrow v=\frac{150}{\sqrt{2}} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Initially, $\quad u_y=u \sin 60=\frac{150 \sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
Finally, $\quad v_y=v \sin 45^{\circ}=\frac{150}{\sqrt{2}} \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}=\frac{150}{2} \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$
But
$\begin{aligned}
v_y & =u_y+a_y t \\
\frac{150}{2} & =\frac{150 \sqrt{3}}{2}-10 t
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & 10 t=\frac{150(\sqrt{3}-1)}{2} \\ \Rightarrow & t=7.5(\sqrt{3}-1) \mathrm{s}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone is projected from the ground with velocity $50 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ at an angle $30^{\circ}$ with horizontal. It crosses a wall after 3 s . How far beyond the wall the stone will strike the ground? ( $g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$ )
questions.solution:
Time, $\begin{aligned} T & =\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g} \\ T & =\frac{2 \times 50 \times \sin 30^{\circ}}{10}\end{aligned}$
$=\frac{2 \times 50 \times \frac{1}{2}}{10}=5 \mathrm{~s}$
Time spent after crossing the wall, $t_2=5-3=2 \mathrm{~s}$
$\begin{aligned}\therefore \text { Distance, } x_2 & =u \cos \theta \times 1_2 \\& =50 \times \cos 30^{\circ} \times 2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =50 \times \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times 2 \\ & =50 \sqrt{3}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =50 \times 1.73 \\ & =86.5 \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle of mass $m$ is moving in a horizontal circle of radius $R$ under the centripetal force $=-k / R^2$ where $k$ is a constant. What is the total energy of the particle?
questions.solution:
Centripetal force $=\frac{m v^2}{R}=\frac{k}{R^2}($ in magnitude $)$
$\therefore \quad \mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} m v^2=\frac{1}{2} \frac{k}{R}$
$P E=-\int F d R=-\int \frac{-k}{R^2} d R=-\frac{k}{R}$
$\therefore$ Total energy $=\mathrm{KE}+\mathrm{PE}$
$=\frac{k}{2 R}-\frac{k}{R}=-\frac{k}{2 R}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves with constant speed $v$ along a circular path of radius $r$ and completes the circle in time $T$. The acceleration of the particle is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two stones having different masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ are projected at angles $\theta$ and $\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)$ with same velocity from the same point. The ratio of their maximum heights is
questions.solution:
Maximum height, $H_1=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}\ldots$ (i)
and $H_2=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}{2 g}\ldots$ (ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get
$\frac{H_1}{H_2}=\frac{\sin ^2 \theta}{\sin ^2\left(90^{\circ}-\theta\right)}, \frac{H_1}{H_2}=\frac{\tan ^2 \theta}{1}$
$\therefore \quad H_1: H_2=\tan ^2 \theta: 1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The driver of a car moving towards a rocket launching pad with a speed of 6 $\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}$ observes that the rocket is moving with speed of $10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The upward speed of the rocket as seen by the stationary observer is
questions.solution: Observed speed, $\begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{(10)^2+(6)^2} \\ =\sqrt{100+36} & =\sqrt{136} \simeq 11 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two seconds after projection, a projectile is travelling in a direction inclined at $30^{\circ}$ to the horizontal. After one more sec, it is travelling horizontally, the magnitude and direction of its velocity are
questions.solution:
Let in 2 s body reaches upto point $A$ and after one more sec upto point $B$. Total time of ascent for a body is given 3 s
$\begin{array}{lrl} \text { i.e., } & & t \\ & =\frac{u \sin \theta}{g}=3 \\ \therefore & u \sin \theta & =10 \times 3 \\ & u \sin \theta & =30...(i) \end{array}$
Horizontal component of velocity remains always constant $u \cos \theta=v \cos 30^{\circ}$...(ii)
For vertical upward motion between point $O$ and $A$ $v \sin 30^{\circ}=u \sin \theta-g \times 2 \quad[\text { Using } v=u-g t]$ $\begin{aligned} & v \sin 30^{\circ}=30-20 \quad[A s u \sin \theta=30] \\ & \therefore \quad v=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned}$
Substituting this value $v$, we get in Eq. (ii) $u \cos \theta=20 \cos 30^{\circ}=10 \sqrt{3}$...(iii)
From Eqs. (i) and (iii) $u=20 \sqrt{3} \text { and } \theta=60^{\circ}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a force $F_1$ acts on a particle, frequency is 6 Hz and when a force $F_2$ acts, frequency is 8 Hz . what is the frequency when both the forces act simultaneously in same direction?
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & F=m r \omega^2=m r \times(2 \pi m r n)^2=4 \pi^2 m r^2 \\ & F \propto 2 n^2\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} F & =F_1+F_2 \\ n^2 & =n_1^2+n_2^2 \\ n & =\sqrt{n_1^2+n_2^2} \\ n & =\sqrt{(6)^2+(8)^2} \\ n=\sqrt{100} & =10 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man crosses a 320 m wide river perpendicular to the current in 4 min . If in still water he can swim with a speed $5 / 3$ times that of the current, then the speed of the current, in $\mathrm{mm}^{-1}$ in is
questions.solution:
$v_r^2=v_m^2-v^2$
$v=\frac{320}{4} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}=80 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{min}$
$\begin{aligned} & v_m=\frac{5}{3} v_r \\ & v_r^2=t \frac{5}{3}\left(v_r\right)^2-(80)^2\end{aligned}$
$\frac{16}{9} v_r^2=(80)^2$
$u^2, u_0$ form canours linear

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: From an inclined plane two particles are projected with same speed at same angle $\theta$, one up and other down the plane as shown in figure, which of the following statements is/are correct?
options: The time of flight of each particle is the same
The particles will collide the plane with same speed
Both the particles strike the plane perpendicularly
The particles will collide in mid air if projected simultaneously and time of flight of each particle is less than the time of collision
questions.solution:
Here, $\alpha=2 \theta, \beta=\theta$
Time of flight of $A$ is, $\begin{aligned} T_1 & =\frac{2 u \sin (\alpha-\beta)}{g \cos \beta} \\ & =\frac{2 u \sin (2 \theta-\theta)}{g \cos \theta} \\ & =\frac{2 u}{g} \tan \theta \end{aligned}$ Time of flight of $B$ is, $T_2=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g \cos \theta}$ $=\frac{2 u}{g} \tan \theta$ So, $T_1=T_2$. The acceleration of both the particles is $g$ downwards. Therefore, relative acceleration between the two is zero or relative motion between the two is uniform. The relative velocity of $A$ w.r.t. $B$ is towards $A B$, therefore collision will take place between the two in mid air.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A spot of light $S$ rotates in a horizontal plane with a constant angular velocity of $0.1 \mathrm{rad} / \mathrm{s}$. The spot of light $P$ moves along the wall at a distance of 3 m from $S$. The velocity of spot $P$, where $\theta=45^{\circ}$, is
questions.solution:
The situation is shown in figure.
From figure, $x=r \tan \theta$ $\therefore$ Velocity of $P$ is $v=\frac{d x}{d t}=r \sec ^2 \theta\left(\frac{d \theta}{d t}\right)$ where, $\frac{d \theta}{d t}=$ angular velocity of rotation of spot $\begin{aligned} & \quad=\omega \\ & \therefore \quad v=\omega r \sec ^2 \theta \\ & \text { At } \phi=45^{\circ}, \\ & \text { so } \theta=45^{\circ} \\ & \text { Hence, } \quad \begin{aligned} & v \\ & =0.1 \times 3 \times \sec ^2 45^{\circ} \\ & = \\ & 0.1 \times 3 \times 2=0.6 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is moving along a circular path of radius 5 m with a uniform speed $5 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$. What will be the average acceleration when the particle completes half revolution?
questions.solution:
When the particle completes half revolution, change in velocity $\Delta v=[5-(-5)] \mathrm{m} / \mathrm{s}=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Time taken to complete the half revolution is $t=\frac{\pi r}{v}=\frac{\pi \times 5}{5}=\pi \mathrm{s}$
Thus, average acceleration $=\frac{\Delta v}{t}=\frac{10}{\pi} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ball rolls off the top of stairway with a horizontal velocity of magnitude $1.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. The steps are 0.20 m high and 0.20 m wide. Which step will the ball hit first?
questions.solution:
Given, $x=0.20 \mathrm{~m}, y=0.20 \mathrm{~m}, u=1.8 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
Let the ball strikes the $n$th step of stairs, Vertical distance travelled
$=n y=n \times 0.20=\frac{1}{2} g t^2$
Horizontal distance travelled, $n x=u t$ or $t=\frac{n x}{u}$
$\therefore n y=\frac{1}{2} g \times \frac{n^2 x^2}{u^2}$
or $n=\frac{2 u^2}{g} \frac{y}{x^2}=\frac{2 \times(1.8)^2 \times 0.20}{9.8 \times(0.20)^2}=3.3 \approx 4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stone is thrown at an angle $\theta$ to be horizontal reaches a maximum height $H$. Then the time of flight of stone will be
questions.solution:
$H=\frac{u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{2 g}$ and $T=\frac{2 u \sin \theta}{g}$
$\Rightarrow T^2=\frac{4 u^2 \sin ^2 \theta}{g^2}$
