questions.q: A $p-n$ junction is designed to withstand current up to a maximum in 10 mA . A resistor $R=200 \Omega$ is connected in series with it. When forward biased the diode has a potential drop of 0.5 V . The maximum voltage of the battery required to forward bias the diode is
questions.solution:
When emf of cell is $E$, the potential drop is
$V=E-i r$
where $i$ is current and $r$ the internal resistance.
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Given, } i=10 \mathrm{~mA}=10 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}, R=200 \Omega, \\
& \Rightarrow \quad V=0.5 \text { volt. } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad E=V+\text { ir } \\
& \Rightarrow \quad E=0.5+10 \times 10^{-3} \times 200 \\
& E=1.5 \mathrm{~V}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: What is the Boolean equation for the figure?
questions.solution: In the following figure, output of AND gate is made as input of NOT gate, we get NAND gate

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the inputs of a two input logic gate are 0 and 0 , the output is 1 . When the inputs are 1 and 0 the output is 0 . The logic gate is of the type

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The combination of gates shown in the figure below produces
questions.solution: Here, the output of these gate $Y=\overline{\bar{A}} \cdot \bar{B}$ But according to Boolean algebra, $\bar{A} \cdot \bar{B}=\overline{A+B}$ $\therefore \quad Y=\overline{\overline{\overline{A+B}}}=A+B$ or $Y=A+B$ It means the whole circuit of gates behaves as OR gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two junction diodes one of germanium (Ge) and other of silicon (Si) are connected as shown in figure to a battery of emf 12 V and a load resistance $10 \mathrm{k} \Omega$. The germanium diode conducts at 0.3 V and silicon diode at 0.7 V . When a current flows in the circuit, then the potential of terminal $Y$ will be
questions.solution:
Ge conducts at 0.3 V and Si Conducts at 0.7 V . Both Ge and Si are connected in parallel. When current begins to flow, then the potential difference remains at 0.3 V , so no current flows through Si-diode.
$\therefore$ Potential difference across resistive load $=12-0.3$
$=11.7 \mathrm{~V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the circuit diagram, the current through the Zener diode is
questions.solution:
The voltage drop across $R_2$ is $V_{R_2}=V_z=10 \mathrm{~V}$
The current through $R_2$ is $\begin{aligned} I_{R_2} & =\frac{V_{R_2}}{R_2}=\frac{10}{1500}=0.667 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~A} \\ & =6.67 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}=6.67 \mathrm{~mA} \end{aligned}$
The voltage drop across $R_1$ is $V_{R_1}=15 \mathrm{~V}-V_{R_2}=15 \mathrm{~V}-10 \mathrm{~V}=5 \mathrm{~V}$
The current through $R_1$ is $I_{R_1}=\frac{V_{R_1}}{R_1}=\frac{5 \mathrm{~V}}{500 \Omega}=10^{-2} \mathrm{~A}=10 \times 10^{-3}=10 \mathrm{~mA}$
The current through Zener diode is $I_z=I_{R_1}-I_{R_2}=(10-6.67) \mathrm{mA}=3.33 \mathrm{~mA}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The following configuration of gate is equivalent to
questions.solution: $Y=(A+B) \cdot \overline{A B}$ The given output equation can also be written as $Y=(A+B) \cdot(\bar{A}+\bar{B})$ (Demorgan's theorem) $\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & Y=A \bar{A}+A \bar{B}+B \bar{A}+B \bar{B} \\ \Rightarrow & Y=0+A \bar{B}+\bar{A} B+0 \\ \Rightarrow & Y=\bar{A} B+A \bar{B} \end{array}$ This is the expression for XOR gate.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The correct relation between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ in a transistor is
questions.solution:
We know that
$\alpha=\left(\frac{\Delta i_C}{\Delta i_E}\right)$    .....(i)
and $\quad \beta=\left(\frac{\Delta i_C}{\Delta i_B}\right)=\frac{\Delta i_C}{\Delta i_E} \times \frac{\Delta i_E}{\Delta i_B}$
$=\alpha \frac{\Delta i_E}{\Delta i_B}$    ....(ii)
But $\Delta i_B=\Delta i_E-\Delta i_C$
from (ii)
$\begin{aligned} & \beta=\alpha \frac{\Delta i_E}{\Delta i_E-\Delta i_C}=\frac{\alpha}{1-\frac{\Delta i_C}{\Delta i_E}} \\ & \therefore \beta =\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a transistor biased in the common-emitter mode, the emitter current is
options: nearly equal to the base current
much smaller than the collector current
questions.solution: In common-emitter amplifier, the emitter current is much larger than the base current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The energy gap between the valence and conduction bands of an insulator is about
questions.solution: An insulator has its valence band filled with electrons and its conduction band is empty. The energy gap is large approximately 5.0 eV , so it is not possible for ordinary process to provide enough energy for valence electrons to break out of their valence band and jump into the conduction band where they might carry electric current.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For germanium crystal, the forbidden energy gap in joule is
questions.solution:
Forbidden energy gap for germanium crystal
$\begin{aligned} & =0.76 \mathrm{eV} \\ & =0.76 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J} \\ & =1.216 \times 10^{-19} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The energy gap between the valence band and the conduction band for a material is 6 eV . The material is
questions.solution: Insulators have large energy gap between valence and conduction band (about 6 eV ), while semiconductors have a smaller one and conductors, the smallest energy gap.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transistor is working in common emitter mode. Its amplification factor $(\beta)$ is 80 . If the base current is $250 \mu \mathrm{~A}$, the collector current will be
questions.solution:
Current amplification factor, $\beta=\frac{i_C}{i_B}$
$\therefore i_C=i_B \times \beta$
$=250 \times 80 \mu \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The current in the circuit shown in the figure, considering ideal diode is
questions.solution:
Potential difference across diode
$=3.2-3=0.2 \mathrm{~V}$
$\therefore$ Current through diode
$i=\frac{0.2}{100}=2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The effect of reverse bias in a junction diode on its potential barrier is
questions.solution: Due to reverse biasing, in a junction diode the potential barrier increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are the current gain in the $C B$ and $C E$ configurations respectively of the transistor circuit, then $\frac{\beta-\alpha}{\alpha \beta}=$
questions.solution:
The relation between $\alpha$ and $\beta$ is
$\beta=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}$
Putting this value in the given equation, we have
$\frac{\beta-\alpha}{\alpha \beta}=\frac{\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}-\alpha}{\alpha \cdot \frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha}}=\frac{\alpha^2}{\alpha^2}=1$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A transistor is used in common-emitter configuration. Given its $\alpha=0.9$, calculate the change in collector current when the base current changes by $2 \mu \mathrm{~A}$.
questions.solution: $\begin{array}{rlrl} & \text {} \quad \beta=\frac{\alpha}{1-\alpha} & =\frac{0.9}{1-0.9}=9 \\ \Rightarrow & & \frac{\Delta I_c}{\Delta I_b} & =9 \\ \Rightarrow & & I_c & =9 \times 2 \\ \Rightarrow & & \Delta I_c & =18 \mu \mathrm{~A}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The inputs and outputs for different time intervals are given below the NAND gate
\(\begin{array}{|l|l|l|l|}
\hline \text { Time } & \text { Inputs A } & \text { Inputs B } & \text { Outputs } \mathbf{Y} \\
\hline t_1 \text { to } t_2 & 0 & 1 & \mathrm{P} \\
\hline t_2 \text { to } t_3 & 0 & 0 & \mathrm{Q} \\
\hline t_3 \text { to } t_4 & 1 & 0 & \mathrm{R} \\
\hline t_4 \text { to } t_5 & 1 & 1 & \mathrm{~S} \\
\hline
\end{array}\)
The values taken by \(P, Q, R, S\) are respectively
questions.solution:
NAND gate is obtained when the output of AND gate is made as the input of NOT gate Boolean expression for NAND gate is

\begin{array}{ccc}\hline \boldsymbol{A} & \boldsymbol{B} & \boldsymbol{Y} \\\hline 0 & 0 & 1 \\\hline 1 & 0 & 1 \\\hline 0 & 1 & 1 \\\hline 1 & 1 & 0 \\\hline\end{array}

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following is unipoler transistor?
questions.solution: A field effert transistor is a unipolar device since the conduction of current is due to majority charge carriers only.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the Boolean Algebra A $\cdot$ B is same
questions.solution: $\bar{A} \cdot \bar{B}=\overline{A+B}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the inputs of a two input logic gate are 0 and 0 , the output is 1 . When the inputs are 1 and 0 the output is 0 . The logic gate is of the type
questions.solution: NOR gate is obtained when the output of OR gate is made as the input of NOT gate. Boolean expression for NOR gate is $Y=\overline{A+B}$ Symbol of NOR gate

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If no external voltage is applied across $p-n$ junction, there would be
options: no electric field across the junction
an electric field pointing from $n$-type to p-type side across the junction
an electric field pointing from $p$-type to $n$-type side across the junction
a temporary electric field during formation of p-n junction that would subsequently disappear
questions.solution: Near the junction positive charge is built on $n$-side and negative charge on $P$-side. This sets up potential difference across the junction and internal electric field $E_i$ directed from $n$-side to $P$-side. The equilibrium is established when the field $E_i$ becomes strong enough to stop further diffusion of the majority charge carriers.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The rays of sun are focussed on a piece of ice through a lens of diameter 5 cm , as a result of which 10 g ice melts in 10 min . The amount of heat received from sun, per unit area per minute is :
questions.solution:
Mass of ice melted per minute $=\frac{10}{10}=1 \mathrm{~g}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Quantity of heat used } \\ & \qquad \begin{aligned} \text {Area of lens } & =\pi r^2 \\ & =19.625 \mathrm{~cm}^2\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Amount of heat received from sun
$=\frac{80}{19.625}=4 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{cm}^2 \mathrm{~min}$
Latent heat of fusion $=80$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The ratio of the resistance of conductor at temperature $15^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to its resistance at temperature $37.5^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is $4: 5$. The temperature coefficient of resistance the conductor is
questions.solution:
The resistance at temperature $t$ is
$\begin{aligned} & R_t=R_0(1+\alpha t) \\ & R_t^{\prime}=R_0\left(1+\alpha t^{\prime}\right)\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow  4=1+15 \alpha.....(i)$
$5=1+37.5 \alpha.....(ii)$
$\therefore \frac{4}{5}=\frac{1+15 \alpha}{1+37.5 \alpha}$
$\Rightarrow 4(1+37.5 \alpha)=5(1+15 \alpha)$
$\Rightarrow 75 \alpha=1 \Rightarrow \alpha=\frac{1}{75}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The temperatures of two bodies $A$ and $B$ are respectively $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $327^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The ratio $H_A: H_B$ of the rates of heat radiated by them is
questions.solution:
As we know $Q \propto T^4$
$\Rightarrow \frac{H_A}{H_B}=\left[\frac{273+727}{273+327}\right]^4=\frac{625}{81}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: At which temperature, magnitude of ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and ${ }^{\circ} \mathrm{F}$ are equal?
questions.solution:
As, $\frac{C}{100}=\frac{F-32}{180}$
Now, $\quad C=F$ (given) $\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & \frac{C}{100}=\frac{C-32}{180} \\ \Rightarrow & 180 C=100 C-3200 \\ \Rightarrow & 180 C-100 C=-3200 \\ \Rightarrow & 80 C=-3200 \\ \therefore & C=-\frac{3200}{80}=-40 \\ \therefore & C=F=-40 \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A 2 kg copper block is heated to $500^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and then it is placed on a large block of ice at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the specific heat capacity of copper is water is $3.5 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}^{-\mathrm{kg}^{-1}}$, the amount of ice that can melt is
questions.solution:
Heat emitted by copper = Heat gained by ice
$m c \Delta \theta=m^{\prime} L$
$\Rightarrow  m^{\prime}=\frac{m c \Delta \theta}{L}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { Given, } m=2 \mathrm{~kg}, c=400 \mathrm{~J}^{-\mathrm{kg}^{-1} \mathrm{C}^{-1}} \\
& \Delta \theta=500, L=3.5 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}-\mathrm{kg}^{-1} \\
& \therefore \quad m^{\prime}= \frac{2 \times 400 \times 500}{3.5 \times 10^5}=\frac{8}{7} \mathrm{~kg}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An iron cube floats in a vessel containing mercury at $20^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the temperature is increased by $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the cube will float
options: lower or higher depending on mass of cube
questions.solution:
Lower (conceptual question).
When temperature is increased, then the density will decrease, so cube will float lower.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which of the following process does not occur through convection
options: heating of glass bulb through filament
questions.solution: Heating of glass bulb is by radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In which of the following process, convection does not take place primarilly?
options: Warming of glass bulb due to filament
questions.solution: Warming of glass bulb due to filament is an example of heat transfer due to radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ice starts freezing in a lake with water at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ when the atmospheric temperature is $-10^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the time taken for 1 cm of ice to be formed is 12 min , the time taken for the thickness of the ice to change from 1 to 2 cm will be
options: more than 12 min but less than 24 min
questions.solution:
Time taken by ice to grow a thickness of
$t=\frac{e L}{2 K \theta} y^2$
Hence time interval to change thickness from 0 to $y$, from $y$ to $2 y$ and so on will be in the ratio
$\Delta t_1: \Delta t_2: \Delta t_3$
$\therefore \quad=\left(1-0^2\right):\left(2^2-1^2\right):\left(3^2-2^2\right)$
$\Delta t_1: \Delta t_2: \Delta t_3=1: 3: 5$
According to question $=\Delta t_1 \lt $ terims
$\begin{aligned} \Delta t_2=3 \Delta H & \\ & =3 \times 12=36 \mathrm{~min}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The heat flows through a rod of length 50 cm and area of cross-section $5 \mathrm{~cm}^2$. Its ends are respectively at $25^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $125^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The coefficient of thermal conductivity of the material rod is $0.092 \mathrm{kcal} / \mathrm{m}^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The temperature gradient of the rod is
questions.solution:
Temperature gradient of the rod
$\frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}=\frac{\theta_2-\theta_1}{l}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta \theta}{\Delta x}=\frac{125-25}{50}$
$=\frac{100}{50}=2^{\circ} \mathrm{C} / \mathrm{cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
The molar heat capacity of rock salt at low temperature varies with temperature according to Debye's $T^3$ law
Thus $C=k \frac{T^3}{\theta^3}$ where $k=1940 \mathrm{Jmol}^{-1} \mathrm{~K}^{-1}$
$\theta=281 \mathrm{~K}$
Calculate how much heat is required to raise the temperature of 2 moles of rock salt from 10 K to 50 K
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & d Q=n C d T \\ & \quad d Q=n k \frac{T^3}{\theta^3} d T \\ & Q=\frac{n k}{\theta^3} \int_{T_1}^{T_2} T^3 d T \\ & =\frac{n K}{\theta^3}\left(\frac{T_2^4-T_1^4}{4}\right)  \\ & =\frac{2 \times 1940 \times\left(50^4-10^4\right)}{(281)^3 \times 4} \\ & =272.79 \mathrm{~J}=273 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two bodies $A$ and $B$ having temperatlires $327^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $427^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ are radiating heat to the surrounding. The surrounding temperature is $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The ratio of rates of heat radiation of $A$ to that of $B$ is
questions.solution:
If temperature of surrounding is considered, then net loss of energy of a body by radiation
$Q=\operatorname{Ae\sigma }\left(T^4-T_0^4\right) \Rightarrow Q \propto\left(T^4-T_0^4\right)$
$\therefore  \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}=\frac{T_1^4-T_0^4}{T_2^4-T_0^4}$
$=\frac{(273+327)^4-(273+27)^4}{(273+427)^4-(273+27)^4}$
$=\frac{(600)^4-(300)^4}{(700)^4-(300)^4}=0.52$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a copper sphere is heated, percentage change is
options: equal in radius, volume and density
questions.solution:
Let $R$ be the radius of sphere, $V$ its volume and $\rho$ its density. Then,
$\Delta R=R \alpha \Delta \theta$
and percentage change in radius
$\frac{\Delta R}{R} \times 100=100 \alpha \Delta \theta$   ...(i)
Now, $\quad \Delta V=\gamma v \Delta \theta=3 \alpha v \Delta \theta \quad\{\because \gamma=3 \alpha\}$
$\therefore$ Percentage increase in volume
$=\frac{\Delta V}{V} \times 100=300 \alpha \Delta \theta$   ...(ii)
Again, $\quad \rho^{\prime}=\frac{\rho}{1+\gamma \Delta \theta}=\frac{\rho}{1+3 \alpha \Delta \theta}$
$\therefore \quad \Delta \rho=\rho-\rho^{\prime}$
$=\rho\left[1-\frac{1}{1+3 \alpha \Delta \theta}\right]=\frac{(3 \alpha \Delta \theta) \rho}{1+3 \alpha \Delta \theta}$
Percentage change in density
$=\frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho} \times 100=\frac{300 \alpha \Delta \theta}{1+3 \alpha \Delta \theta}$   ...(iii)
From Eqs. (i), (ii) and (iii), we see that the percentage change is maximum in volume.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Three identical thermal conductors are connected as shown in figure. Considering no heat loss due to radiation, temperature at the junction will be
questions.solution:
Let the temperature of junction be $\theta$. In equilibrium, rate of flow of heat through rod 1 is equal to sum of rate of flow of heat through rods 2 and 3 , ie,
$\left(\frac{d Q}{d t}\right)_1=\left(\frac{d Q}{d t}\right)_2+\left(\frac{d Q}{d t}\right)_3$
$\therefore \frac{K A(\theta-20)}{l}=\frac{K A(60-\theta)}{l}+\frac{K A(70-\theta)}{l}$
$\Rightarrow  \theta-20=130-2 \theta$
$\begin{aligned}
&\text { or }\\
&3 \theta=150
\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \theta=50^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The surface area of a black body is $5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$ and its temperature is $727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The energy radiated by it per minute is $\left(\sigma=5.67 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{m}^2-\mathrm{s}-\mathrm{K}^4\right)$
questions.solution:
From Stefan's law
$E=\sigma T^4 A$
Given,
$T=727^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=727+273=1000 \mathrm{~K}$
$A=5 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m}^2$
$\therefore$ Energy $=5.67 \times 10^{-8} \quad(1000)^4\left(5 \times 10^{-4}\right) 60$
$\therefore E=1.7 \times 10^3 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin copper wire of length $L$ increases in length by $1 \%$, when heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$. What is the percentage change in area when a thin copper plate having dimensions $(10 L \times 2 L)$ is heated from $T_1$ to $T_2$ ?
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} A & =10 L \times 2 L=20 L^2 \\ \frac{\Delta A}{A} \times 100 & =2 \frac{\Delta L}{L} \times 100=2 \times 1=2 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Liquid is filled in a flask upto a certain point. When, the flask is heated, the level of the liquid
questions.solution: Since both the liquid and the flask undergoes volume expansion and the flask expands first therefore the level of the liquid initially falls and then rises.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two rods (one semi-circular and other straight) of same material and of same cross-sectional area are joined as shown in the figure. The points $A$ and $B$ are maintained at different temperature. The ratio of the heat transferred through a cross-section of a semi-circular rod to the heat transferred through a cross-section of the straight rod in a given time is
questions.solution: $\frac{d Q}{d t}=\frac{K A \Delta \theta}{I}$, for both rods $K, A$ and $\Delta \theta$ are same $\therefore \quad \frac{d Q}{d t} \propto \frac{1}{l}$ so $\frac{(d Q / d t)_{\text {semi-circular }}}{(d Q / d t)_{\text {straight }}}=\frac{l_{\text {straight }}}{l_{\text {semi-circular }}}=\frac{2 r}{\pi r}=\frac{2}{\pi}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During an experiment, an ideal gas is found to obey an additional law $V p^2=$ constant. The gas is initially at temperature $T$ and volume $V$. The temperature of the gas will be following, when it expands to a volume 2 V ?
questions.solution:
Given, $V p^2=$ constant
or $V\left[\frac{R T}{V}\right]^2=$ constant or $\frac{T^2}{V}=$ constant
or $\quad \frac{T^{\prime 2}}{T^2}=\frac{V^{\prime}}{V} \Rightarrow \frac{T^2}{T^2}=\frac{2 V}{V} \Rightarrow T^{\prime}=\sqrt{2} T$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An engine has an efficiency of $1 / 3$. The amount of work this engine can perform per kcal of heat input is :
questions.solution:
$\eta=\frac{W}{Q_1}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad w & =\eta Q_1=\frac{1}{3} \times 1000 \mathrm{cal} \\ & =\frac{1000}{3} \times 4.2 \mathrm{~J}=1400 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During an adiabatic process, the pressure $P$ of a fixed mass of an ideal gas changes by $\Delta P$ and its volume $V$ changes by $\Delta V$. If $\gamma=C_P / C_V$, then $\Delta V / V$ is given by:
questions.solution:
$K_{\text {adia }}=\gamma P=-\frac{\Delta P}{\Delta V / V}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta V}{V}=-\frac{\Delta P}{\gamma P}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In the indicator diagram, $A B$ is an isotherm and $B C$ is an adiabat, because :
options: slope of $A B$ is less than slope of $B C$
questions.solution: Slope of isothermal curve $(A B)$ is smaller than slope of adiabatic curve $(B C)$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which equation is valid for adiabatic process?
questions.solution: For an adiabatic process, $T V^{{ }^{-1}}=$ constant.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thermodynamic system is taken through the cycle PQRSP process. The net work done by the system is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { } W_{P Q}=P \Delta V \\ & =100 \times 10^3(300-100) \times 10^{-6} \\ & =100 \times 10^{-3} \times 200=20 \mathrm{~J} \\ & W_{Q R}=0(\because V \text { is constant }) \\ & W_{R S}=P \Delta V \\ & =200 \times 10^3(100-300) \times 10^{-6} \\ & =-200 \times 10^{-3} \times 200=-40 \mathrm{~J} \\ & W_{S P}=0 \end{aligned}$
Net work done $=20+(-40)=-20 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Torentical containers each of volume $V_0$ are joined by a small pipe. The containers contain identical gases at temperature $T_0$ and pressure $P_0$. One container is heated to temperature $2 T_0$ while maintaining the other at the same temperature. The common pressure of the gas is $P$ and $n$ is the number of moles of gas in container at temperature $2 T_0$
questions.solution:
Initially for container $A, p_0 V_0=n_0 R T_0$ For container $B, p_0 V_0=n_0 R T_0$ $\therefore \quad n_0=\frac{p_0 V_0}{R T_0}$ Total number of moles $=n_0+n_0=2 n_0$
Since, even on heating the total number of moles is conserved Hence, $\quad n_1+n_2=2 n_0$...(i) If $p$ be the common pressure, then For container $A, p V_0=n_1 R 2 T_0 \quad \therefore n_1=\frac{p V_0}{2 R T_0}$ For container $A, p V_0=n_2 R T_0 \quad \therefore n_2=\frac{p V_0}{R T_0}$ Substituting the value of $n_0, n_1$ and $n_2$ in Eq. (i) we get $\frac{p V_0}{2 R T_0}+\frac{p V_0}{R T_0}=\frac{2 \cdot p_0 V_0}{R T_0} \Rightarrow p=\frac{4}{3} p_0$
Number of moles in container $A$ (at temperature $2 T_0$ ) $\begin{aligned} & =n_1=\frac{P V_0}{2 R T_0}=\left(\frac{4}{3} p_0\right) \frac{V_0}{2 R T_0} \\ & =\frac{2}{3} \frac{p_0 V_0}{R T_0} \quad\left[\text { As } p=\frac{4}{3} p_0\right] \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An experiment is carried on a fixed amount of gas at different temperature and at high pressure such that it deviates from the ideal gas behaviour. The variation of $\frac{p V}{R T}$ with $p$ is shown in the diagram. The correct variation will correspond to
questions.solution: At lower pressure, we can assume that given gas behaves as ideal gas so $\frac{p V}{R T}=$ constant but when pressure increase, the decrease in volume will not take place in same proportion so $\frac{p V}{R T}$ will increases.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 1 g of water at atmospheric pressure has volume of 1 cc and when boiled it becomes 1681 cc of steam. The heat of vaporisation of water is $540 \mathrm{cal} / \mathrm{g}$. Then the change in its internal energy in this process is
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \text {Initial volume of water }=V_1=1 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \\ & \text {Volume of steam }=V_2=1681 \mathrm{~cm}^3 \\ & \text {Change in volume } \mathrm{dV}=\mathrm{V}_2-\mathrm{V}_1=1681 \\ & =168 \mathrm{ocm}^3 \\ & =168 \mathrm{o} \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\ & \text {Pressure }p=10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2 \\ & \mathrm{dQ}=\mathrm{mL}=1 \times 540 \times 4.2 \\ & =22.68 \mathrm{~J} \\ & \mathrm{dW}=\mathrm{pdV} \\ & =10^5 \times 168 \mathrm{o} \times 10^{-6}=168 \\ & \text {Internal energy dU }=2268-168=2100 \mathrm{~J} \\ & =500 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Ideal gas is contained in a thermally insulated and rigid container and it is heated through a resistance $100 \Omega$ by passing a current of 1 A for five minutes, then change in internal energy of the gas is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \Delta W=0 \quad \therefore \Delta Q=\Delta U \\ & \Delta Q=\Delta U=I^2 R \Delta t=1^2(100)(5 \times 60)=30 \mathrm{~kJ}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two litre of water at initial temperature of $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ is heated by a heater of power 1 kW . If the lid of kettle is opened, then heat is lost at the constant rate of $160 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s}$. Find, the time required to raise the temperature of water to $77^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ with the lid open (specific heat of water $4.2 \mathrm{~kJ} / \mathrm{kg}$ )
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Rate of heat gain } \\ & =1000-160=840 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{s} \\ & \therefore \quad \text { Required time }\end{aligned}$
Ans is 8 min 20 s

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: 8 g of $\mathrm{O}_2, 14 \mathrm{~g}$ of $\mathrm{N}_2$, and 2 g of $\mathrm{CO}_2$ is mixed in a container of 10 L capacity at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. The pressure exerted by the mixture in terms of atmospheric pressure is $\left(R=0.082 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~atm} \mathrm{k}^{-1} \mathrm{~mol}^{-1}\right):$
questions.solution:
$P=\frac{n_1 R T+n_2 R T+n_3 R T}{V}$
$=\left(n_1+n_2+n_3\right) \frac{R T}{V}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left(\frac{8}{16}+\frac{14}{28}+\frac{22}{44}\right) \times \frac{0.082 \times 300}{10} \\ & =\left(\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}+\frac{1}{2}\right) \times 2.46 \\ & =\frac{3}{2} \times 2.46=3.69 \mathrm{~atm} \approx 3.7 \mathrm{~atm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
Internal energy of a gas remains unchanged I. an isothermal process II. an adiabatic process III. a reversible process IV. a cyclic process
Which of these are true?
questions.solution:
For isothermal process, $d U=0$,
i.e., internal energy remains unchanged.
For a cyclic process, $d U=0$,
i.e., internal energy remains unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical containers $A$ and $B$ with frictionless pistons contain the same ideal gas at the same temperature and the same volume $V$. The mass of the gas in $A$ is $m_A$ and that in $B$ is $m_B$. The gas in each container is now allowed to expand isothermally to the same final volume $2 V$. The changes in the pressure in $A$ and $B$ are to be found $\Delta p$ and $1.5 \Delta p$ respectively, then relation for masses will be
questions.solution:
For isothermal expansion of an ideal gas
$p V=$ constant
$\Rightarrow \quad p \Delta V+V \Delta p=0$
For container $A$,
$p_{i A}(V)+V(\Delta p)=0$
$\Rightarrow \quad p_{i A}=-\frac{V \Delta P}{V}=-\Delta p$    .....(i)
where, $p_i$ is initial pressure.
For container $B$,
$p_{\text {iB }}(V)+V(1.5 \Delta p)=V$
$\Rightarrow \quad p_{i B}=-1.5 \Delta p$    ....(ii)
From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we have
$p_{i A}=\frac{p_{i B}}{1.5}=\frac{2}{3} p_{i B}$
$\Rightarrow \quad m_A=\frac{2}{3} m B$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3 m_A=2 m B \quad[\because p \propto m]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A ballon contains $1500 \mathrm{~m}^3$ of a helium at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 4 itmospheric pressure. The volume of helium at $-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ temperature and 2 atmo snheric pressure will be
questions.solution:
The general gas equation is $\frac{P_1 V_1}{T_1}=\frac{P_2 V_2}{T_2}=$ gas
constant
where $p_1, V_1, p_2, V_2$ are pressure and volume at temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$ respectively.
Given $V_1=1500 \mathrm{~m}^3, T_1=27+273=300 \mathrm{~K}$,
$\begin{aligned} p_1 & =4 \mathrm{~atm} \\ T_2 & =-3{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}=273-3=270 \mathrm{~K}, p_2=2 \mathrm{~atm} \\ \therefore \quad V_2 & =\frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} \times \frac{T_2}{p_2} \\ V_2 & =\frac{4 \times 1500}{300} \times \frac{270}{2} \\ V_2 & =2700 \mathrm{~m}^3\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A balloon contains $500 \mathrm{~m}^3$ of He at $27^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 1 atmospheric pressure. The volume of He at $-3^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and 0.5 atmospheric pressure will be
questions.solution:
Using standard gas equation,
$\begin{aligned}
\frac{p_1 V_1}{T_1} & =\frac{p_2 V_2}{T_2} \text { or } V_2=\frac{p_1 V_1 T_2}{p_2 T_1} \\
\therefore \quad & V_2=\frac{1 \times 500 \times(273-3)}{0.5 \times(273+27)} \\
& V_2=\frac{1 \times 500 \times 270}{0.5 \times 300} \\
& V_2=900 \mathrm{~m}^3
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas heat engine operates in a Carnot's cycle between $227^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $127^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. It absorbs $6 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$ at high temperature. The amount of heat converted into work is
questions.solution:
Using the relation
$\frac{W}{Q_1} =\frac{Q_1-Q_2}{Q_1}$
or $\frac{W}{Q_1} =1-\frac{Q_2}{Q_1}$
or $\frac{W}{Q_1} =1-\frac{T_2}{T_1} \quad\left(\therefore \frac{Q_1}{Q_2}=\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right)$
or $W -Q_1\left(1-\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)$
$\therefore W =6 \times 10^4\left[1-\frac{127+273}{227+273}\right]$
or $W= \times 10^4\left(1-\frac{400}{500}\right)$
$= 6 \times 10^4 \times \frac{100}{500}$
$= 1.2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Work done by air when it expands from 50 L to 150 L at a constant pressure of 2 atmosphere is
questions.solution:
Work done
$\begin{gathered}
W=p\left(V_2-V_1\right) \\
\text { Given, } p=2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2, V_1=50 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
V_2=150 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}^3 \\
\therefore \quad W=2 \times 10^5(150-50) 10^{-3} \\
W=2 \times 10^5 \times 100 \times 10^{-3} \\
W=2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{~J}
\end{gathered}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Equal volumes of monoatomic and diatomic gases at the same temperature are given equal quantities of heat. Then,
options: the temperature of diatomic gas will be more
the temperature of monoatomic gas will be more
the temperature of both will be zero
questions.solution:
Energy required to increase the temperature of one mole of monoatomic gas by $\Delta T=3 / 2 \times$ $R \times \Delta T$
Energy required to increase the temperature of one mole of a diatomic gas by $\Delta T=5 / 2 \times$ $R \times \Delta T$
So, for equal volume i.e. equal moles equal amount of heat will increase the temperature of a monoatomic gas more than the diatomic gas

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas at the temperature 250 K is contained in a closed vessel. If the gas is heated through 1 K , then percentage increase in its pressure will be

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The pressure $p$, volume $V$ and temperature $T$ of a gas in the jar $A$ and the other gas in the jar $B$ at pressure $2 p$, volume $V / 4$ and temperature $2 T$, then the ratio of the number of molecules in the jar $A$ and $B$ will be
questions.solution:
We have, ideal gas equation $p V=\mu R T=\left(\frac{N}{N_A}\right) R T$
where, $N=$ number of molecule, $N_A$ =Avogadro number
$\therefore \frac{N_1}{N_2}=\left(\frac{p_1}{p_2}\right)\left(\frac{V_1}{V_2}\right)\left(\frac{T_2}{T_1}\right)=\left(\frac{p}{2 p}\right)\left(\frac{V}{V / 4}\right)\left(\frac{2 T}{T}\right)=\frac{4}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two different isotherms representing the relationship between pressure $p$ and volume $V$ at a given temperature of the same ideal gas are shown for masses $m_1$ and $m_2$ of the gas respectively in the figure given, then
questions.solution:
$p V=n R T=\frac{m}{M} R T$
For lst graph, $p=\frac{m_1}{M} \frac{R T}{V_1}$ For 2nd graph, $p=\frac{m_2}{M} \frac{R T}{V_2}$ Equating the two, we get $\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{m_1}{m_2}=\frac{V_1}{V_2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad m & \propto \frac{1}{V} \end{array}$ $\text { As } V_2 \gt V_1 \Rightarrow m_1 \lt m_2$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An ideal gas expands along the path $A B$ as shown in the $p-V$ diagram. The work done is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Work done } & =\text { area of trapezium } \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times\left(8 \times 10^5+4 \times 10^5\right) \times 0.2 \\ & =1.2 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A gas is suddenly expanded such that its final volume becomes 3 times its initial volume. If the specific heat at constant volume of the gas is $2 R$, then the ratio of initial to final pressures is nearly equal to
questions.solution:
As gas is suddenly expanded so it is an adiabatic process,
ie, $p V^\gamma=$ constant
Given $V_2=3 V_1, C_V=2 R$
$\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & C_p=2 R+R=3 R \\ \Rightarrow & \gamma=\frac{C_p}{C_V}=\frac{3 R}{2 R}=1.5\end{array}$
$\therefore \frac{p_1}{p_2}=\left(\frac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^\gamma=(3)^{1.5}=5.1 \approx 5$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: $1 \mathrm{~cm}^3$ of water at its boiling point absorbs 540 cal of heat to becomes steam with a volume of $1671 \mathrm{~cm}^3$. If the atmospheric pressure $=1.013 \times 10^5 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}^2$ and the mechanical equivalent of heat $=4.19 \mathrm{~J} / \mathrm{cal}$, the energy spent in this process in overcoming intermolecular forces is
questions.solution:
According to first law of thermodynamics
$\begin{aligned}
& & \Delta Q & =\Delta U+\Delta W \\
& \therefore & \Delta U & =\Delta Q-\Delta W
\end{aligned}$
$=540-\frac{p\left(V_2-V_1\right)}{J}$
$\begin{aligned} & =540-\frac{1.013 \times 10^5 \times\left[(1671-1) \times 10^{-6}\right]}{4.2} \\ & =540-40=500 \mathrm{cal}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Saturated vapour is compressed to half its volume without any change in temperature, then the pressure will be
questions.solution: The saturation vapour pressure is the static pressure of a vapour when the vapour pressure of some material is in equilibrium with the liquid phase of that material. The saturation vapour pressure of any material is solely dependent on the temperature of that material. Since, temperature change does not occur in the given case, hence pressure will be the same.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A soap bubble in vacuum has a radius of 3 cm and another soap bubble in vacuum has a radius of 4 cm . If the two bubbles coalesce under isothermal condition, then the radius of the new bubble is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If pressure of a gas contained in a closed vessel is increased by $0.4 \%$ when heated by $1^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, its initial temperature must be
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} & \frac{\mathrm{pV}}{\mathrm{~T}}=\text { constant; } \mathrm{V}=\text { constant } \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{~T}}=\text { constant } \\ & \therefore \frac{\mathrm{p}}{\mathrm{~T}}=\frac{100.4 \mathrm{p}}{1000} \cdot \frac{1}{(\mathrm{~T}+1)} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}=\frac{100.4}{100} \cdot \frac{1}{(\mathrm{~T}+1)} \\ & \frac{1}{\mathrm{~T}}=\frac{100.4}{100 \mathrm{~T}+100} \\ & \mathrm{~T}=250 \mathrm{~K} \end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The unit of $L / R$ is ( where $L=$ inductance and $R=$ resistance $)$
questions.solution: $\because L / R$ is a time constant so the unit of $L / R$ is second.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Number of particles is given by $n=-D \frac{n_2-n_1}{x_2-x_1}$ crossing a unit area perpendicular to $X$-axis is unit time, where $n_1$ and $n_2$ are number of particles per unit volume for the value of $x$ meant to $x_2$ and $x_1$. Find dimensions of $D$ called as diffusion constant.
questions.solution:
Given, number of particle passing from unit area in unit time $=n$
$=\frac{\text { Number of particle }}{A \times t}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^0\right]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^2\right][\mathrm{T}]}=\left[\mathrm{L}^{-2} \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$
$\left[n_1\right]=\left[n_2\right]=$ Number of particle in unit volume
$=\left[L^{-3}\right]$
Now, from the given formula $[D]=\frac{[n]\left[x_2-x_1\right]}{\left[n_2-n_1\right]}$
$=\frac{\left.\left[L^{-2} T^{-1}\right][L\right]}{\left[L^{-3}\right]}$
$=\left[L^2 T^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If $E=$ energy, $G=$ gravitational constant, $I=$ impulse and $M=$ mass, then dimensions of $\frac{G I M^2}{E^2}$ are same as that of
questions.solution:
The SI unit in which $G$ is measured $=\mathrm{N}-\mathrm{m}^2 / \mathrm{kg}^2$
$\therefore$ Its dimensions will be $\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{L}^2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^2\right]}=\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
$\because$ Impulse $=$ Force $\times$ Time $=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right][\mathrm{T}]=\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right]$
$\because$ Dimensions for energy $=\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]$
Now put the dimensions of $G, I, M$ and $E$ in $\frac{G I M^2}{E^2}$
$\begin{aligned}
& =\frac{\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-1}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{ML}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]^2} \\
& =\frac{\left[\mathrm{M}^{-1} \mathrm{~L}^3 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]\left[M L T^{-1}\right]\left[\mathrm{M}^2\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^2 \mathrm{~L}^4 \mathrm{~T}^{-4}\right]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{M}^2 \mathrm{~L}^4 \mathrm{~T}^{-3}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{M}^2 \mathrm{~L}^4 \mathrm{~T}^{-4}\right]} \\
& =\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right]=[T]
\end{aligned}$
The equation $\frac{G I M^2}{E^2}$ has dimension of time.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If force is proportional to square of velocity, then the dimensions of proportionality constant is
questions.solution:
As $F \propto v^2$
or
$F=k v^2$
$\therefore k=\frac{F}{v^2}$
$[k]=\frac{[F]}{\left[v^2\right]}=\frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}$
$=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^0\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: - If $L, C$ and $R$ denote the inductance, capacitance and resistance respectively, the dimensional formula for $C^2 L R$ is
questions.solution:
Given, $\left[C^2 L R\right]=\left[C^2 L^2 \frac{R}{L}\right]=\left[(L C)^2\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)\right]$ and we know that frequency of $L C$ circuits $f=\frac{1}{2 \pi} \frac{1}{\sqrt{L C}}$. Here the dimension of $L C$ is equal to $\left[\mathrm{T}^2\right]$.
$\left[\frac{L}{R}\right]$ gives the time constant of $L-R$ circuit, so that the dimension of $\frac{L}{R}$ is equal to $\left.T T\right]$.
Hence the required dimensions
$\left[(L C)^2\left(\frac{R}{L}\right)\right]=\left[T^2\right]^2\left[T^{-1}\right]=\left[T^3\right] .$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement of an oscillating particle is given by $y=A \sin [B x+C t+D t]$. The dimensional formula for $[A B C D]$ is :
questions.solution:
$y=A \sin [B x+C t+D]$
As each term inside the bracket is dimension less, $\therefore \quad[A]=[y]=[L], \quad[B]=\frac{1}{[x]}=\left[L^{-1}\right]$
$[C]=\frac{1}{[t]}=\left[1^{-1}\right]$ and $D$ is dimensionless. $\therefore[A B C D]=[\mathrm{L}]\left[\mathrm{L}^{-1}\right]\left[\mathrm{T}^{-1}\right][1]=\left[\mathrm{N}^0 \mathrm{~L}^0 \mathrm{~T}^{-1}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The equation $\left(p+\frac{a}{V^2}\right)(V-b)=$ constant. The unit of $a$ is
questions.solution:
According to the principle of dimensional homogenity $[p]=\left[\frac{a}{v^2}\right]$ $\left.\Rightarrow \quad[a]=[p] V^2\right]=\left[M L^{-1} T^{-2}\right]\left[L^6\right]=\left[M L^5 T^{-2}\right]$
Hence, the units of $a=\mathrm{gm} \times \mathrm{cm}^5 \times \mathrm{s}^{-2}$ $=\text { Dyne } \times \mathrm{cm}^4$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Dimensions of Stefan's constant is
questions.solution:
Stefan's constant, $\sigma=\frac{E}{T^4}$ (where, $E$ is energy/s area)
$=\frac{\left[M L^2 T^{-2}\right]}{[T]\left[L^2\right]\left[\theta^4\right]}=\left[M T^{-3} \theta^{-4}\right]$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If force is proportional to square of velocity, then the dimensions of proportionality constant is
questions.solution:
According to the question, $F \propto v^2$
or
$\begin{aligned}
F & =k v^2 \\
k & =\frac{F}{v^2} \Rightarrow \frac{\left[\mathrm{MLT}^{-2}\right]}{\left[\mathrm{L}^2 \mathrm{~T}^{-2}\right]}=\left[\mathrm{ML}^{-1} \mathrm{~T}^0\right]
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Which is different from others in units?
questions.solution: Out of the given choices, Poisson's ratio has not unit.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If value of surface tension of a niquia is 70 dyne/cm, then its value in $\mathrm{N} / \mathrm{m}$ will be
questions.solution:
The unit dyne/cm is CGS system of unit.
The CGS unit of force is dyne and SI unit is Newton. Also there are $10^5$ dynes in one newton and 100 cm in 1 cm .
$\therefore \quad \frac{70 \text { dynes }}{\mathrm{cm}}=\frac{70 \times 10^{-5}}{10^{-2}}=7 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Position of a body with acceleration $a$ is given by $x=k a^m t^n$, here $t$ is time, find the dimension of $m$ and $n$
questions.solution:
Position $x=K a^m t^n$
Writing the dimension on the : th sides.
$\begin{aligned} & {\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L} \mathrm{~T}^0\right]=\left[\mathrm{L}^{-2}\right]^m[T]^n} \\ & {\left[\mathrm{M}^0 \mathrm{~L}^m \mathrm{~T}^{-2 m+n}\right]}\end{aligned}$
On comparing both sides.
$\begin{aligned} m & =1 \\ -2 m+n & =0 \\ n & =2 m=2 \times 1=2\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The coherence of two light sources means that the light waves emitted have
questions.solution: Coherent sources of light are those sources of light which emit light waves of same wavelength, same frequency and are in same phase or having constant phase difference.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel beam of monochromatic light is incident normally on a slit. The diffraction pattern is observed on a screen placed at the focal plane of a convex lens. If the slit width is increased, the central maximum of the diffraction pattern will
questions.solution:
Width of central maximum $\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}, d \text { being slit width. }$
If the slit width is increased the central maximum becomes narrower and more light reaches the region.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's experiment, using red light $(\lambda=6600 Å), 60$ fringes are seen in the field of view. How many fringes will be seen by using violet light $(\lambda=4400 Å)$ ?
questions.solution:
Using red light $(\lambda=6600 Å) 60$ fringes are seen.
Hence, range of field of view is
$60 \times w=60 \times \frac{D \lambda}{d}$
Using light of wavelength $\lambda^{\prime}, n$ fringes are seen, then
$\begin{aligned}
\Rightarrow  60 \times \frac{D \lambda}{d} & =n \times \frac{D \lambda^{\prime}}{d} \\
60 \times \lambda & =n \times \lambda^{\prime} \\
\Rightarrow  n=60 \times \frac{\lambda}{\lambda^{\prime}} & =60 \times \frac{6600}{4400}=90
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment the wavelength of light was changed from $7000 Å$ to $3500 Å$. While doubling the separation between the slits which of the following is not true for this experiment?
options: The width of the fringes changes
The colour of bright fringes changes
The separation between successive bright fringes changes
The separation between successive dark fringes remains unchanged

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, if the source of light changes from orange to blue, then
options: the central bright fringe will become a dark fringe
the distance between consecutive fringes will decrease
the distance between consecutive fringes will increase.
the intensity of the minima will increase.
questions.solution:
We know that in Young's double slit experiment, fringe width $\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}, \beta \propto \lambda$
Since, $\lambda_{\text {blue }} \lt \lambda_{\text {orange }}$
Thus, $\beta_{\text {blue }} \lt \beta_{\text {orange }}$
$\therefore$ In Young's double slit experiment, if the source of light changes from orange to blue, then distance between the consecutive fringes will decrease.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The width of a single slit if the first minimum is observed at an angle $2^{\circ}$ with a light of wavelength $6980 Å$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In Young's double slit experiment, the intensity of light coming from one of the slits is double the intensity from the other slit.
The ratio of the maximum intensity to the minimum intensity in the interference fringe pattern observed is
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} & \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{(\sqrt{2 I}+\sqrt{I})^2}{(\sqrt{2 I}-\sqrt{I})^2}=\left(\frac{\sqrt{2}+1}{\sqrt{2}-1}\right)^2 \\ & \quad=\left[\frac{(\sqrt{2}+1)(\sqrt{2}+1)}{(\sqrt{2}-1)(\sqrt{2}+1)}\right]^2 \\ & \quad=\left(\frac{2+1+2 \sqrt{2}}{2-1}\right)^2 \\ & \quad=\frac{9+8+12 \sqrt{2}}{1} \approx 34\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two sources give interference pattern which is observed on a screen, placed at a distance $D$ from the sources. The fringe width is given as $2 W$. If the distance of sources from the screen is doubled, then the fringe width will
questions.solution:
Given, $W_1=2 W$,
Fringe width, $\quad W=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 2 W=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$
When, $\quad D^{\prime}=2 D$
$W_2=\frac{\lambda D^{\prime}}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \quad W_2=\frac{\lambda 2 D}{d}$
$=2 \times 2 W=4 W$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a single slit diffraction pattern, the distance between the first minimum on the left and the first minimum on the right is 5 mm . The screen on which the diffraction pattern is displayed is at a distance of 80 cm from the slit. The wavelength is $6000 Å$.The slit width in ( mm ) is about
questions.solution:
Slit width, \(w=\frac{D \lambda}{d}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \text {Given, } D=80 \mathrm{~cm}=80 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \lambda=6000 \AA=6000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\
& d=\frac{5}{2} \mathrm{~mm}=\frac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} \mathrm{~m} \\
& \therefore w=\frac{80 \times 10^{-2} \times 6000 \times 10^{-10} \times 2}{5 \times 10^{-3}} \\
& w=0.192 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=0.192 \mathrm{~mm}
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A thin film of soap solution $\left(\mu_s=1.4\right)$ lies on the top of a glass plate ( $\mu_g=1.5$ ). When incident light is almost normal to the plate, two adjacent reflection maxima are observed at two wavelengths 420 nm and 630 nm . The minimum thickness of the soap solution is
questions.solution:
For the reflection at the air-soap solution interface, the phase difference is $\pi$
For reflection at the interface of soap solution to the glass also there will be a phase difference of $\pi$. $\therefore$ The condition for maximum intensity $=2 \mu t=n \lambda$ ...(i) $\begin{array}{rlrl} & \text { For } n , & n \lambda_1 & =(n-1) \lambda_2 \\ & & n(420) & =(n-1) 630 \\ & & n(630-420) & =630 \\ \therefore & n(210) & =630 \\ \Rightarrow & & n & =3\end{array}$ This is the maximum order where they coincide, From Eq. (i), This is the maximum order where they coincide, From Eq. (i), $\begin{array}{ll} \therefore & 2 \times 1.4 \times t=3 \times 420 \\ \Rightarrow & t=\frac{2 \times 420}{2 \times 1.40}=450 \mathrm{~nm} \end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two identical coherent sources placed on a diameter of a circle of radius $R$ at separation $x(\ll R)$ symmetrically about the centre of the circle. The sources emit identical wavelength $\lambda$ each. The number of points on the circle with maximum intensity is $(x=5 \lambda)$
questions.solution:
Path difference at $P$ is $\Delta x=2\left(\frac{x}{2} \cos \theta\right)=x \cos \theta$
For intensity to be maximum $\Delta x=n \lambda \quad(n=0,1, \ldots \ldots)$ $\Rightarrow \quad \begin{aligned} x \cos \theta & =n \lambda \\ \cos \theta & =\frac{n \lambda}{x} \text { or } \cos \theta \ngtr 1\end{aligned}$ $\begin{array}{ll}\therefore & \frac{n \lambda}{x} \ngtr 1 \\ \therefore & n \ngtr \frac{x}{\lambda}\end{array}$ $\begin{aligned} & \text { Substituting } x=5 \lambda \\ & \qquad n \ngtr 5 \text { or } n=1,2,3,4,5, \ldots \ldots .\end{aligned}$ Therefore, in all four quadrants, there can be 20 maximas. There are more maximas at $\theta=0^{\circ}$ and $\theta=180^{\circ}$. But $n=5$ corresponds to $\theta=90^{\circ}$ and $\theta=270^{\circ}$, which are coming only twice. While we have multiplied it four times. Therefore, total number of maximas still 20.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In interference pattern, the energy is
questions.solution: In interference of light energy is neither created nor destroyed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam of light of wavelength 600 nm from a source falls on a single slit 1 mm wide and the resulting diffraction pattern is observed on a screen 2 m away. The distance between the first dark fringes on either side of the central bright fring is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a Young's double slit experiment, the separation between the two slits is 0.9 mm and the fringes are observed one metre away. If it produces the second dark fringe at a distance of 1 mm from the central fringe, the wavelength of the monochromatic source of light used is
questions.solution:
The distance between two consecutive dark or bright fringes is recognised as $\beta$ (fringe width) and that between central fringe and first dark fringe on either side is $\frac{\beta}{2}$.
Given, spacing between second dark fringe and central fringe $=\beta+\frac{\beta}{2}$ or
$\frac{3 \beta}{2}=1 \mathrm{~mm}$ (Given)
or $\quad \beta=\frac{2}{3} \times 1 \mathrm{~mm}$ or $\frac{\lambda D}{d}=\frac{2}{3} \mathrm{~mm}$
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & \lambda=\frac{2}{3} \times 10^{-3} \times \frac{0.9 \times 10^{-3}}{1} \\
\Rightarrow & \lambda=0.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m}
\end{array}$
$\therefore \quad$ Wavelength of the monochromatic source of light
$\lambda=600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m}=600 \mathrm{~nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two slits 4 mm apart, are illuminated by light of wavelength $6000 Å$. What will be the fringe width on a screen placed 2 m from the slits?
questions.solution:
Separation of slits $d=4 \mathrm{~mm}=4 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$ Wavelength $\lambda=6000 Å=6 \times 10^{-7} \mathrm{~m}$
Distance of screen $D=2 \mathrm{~m}$ Fringe width $\beta=\frac{\lambda D}{d}$ $\begin{aligned} & =\frac{6 \times 10^{-7} \times 2}{4 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =3 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{~m} \\ & =0.3 \mathrm{~mm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, fringes of width $\beta$ are produced on the screen kept at a distance of 1 m from the slit. When the screen is moved away by $5 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$, fringe width changes by $3 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~m}$. The separation between the slits is $1 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}$. The wavelength of light used is......nm.
questions.solution:
Since, we know that in Young's double slit experiment.
$\beta=\frac{D \lambda}{d}$   ...(i)
$\beta^{\prime}=\frac{D^{\prime} \lambda}{d}$   ...(ii)
Subtract Eq. (ii) from Eq. (i), we get
$\beta-\beta^{\prime}=\frac{\lambda\left(D-D^{\prime}\right)}{d}$
Substituting the given values; we get
$3 \times 10^{-5}=\frac{\lambda \times 5 \times 10^{-2}}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$
$\begin{array}{ll}\Rightarrow & \lambda=\frac{3 \times 10^{-8}}{5 \times 10^{-2}}=0.6 \times 10^{-6} \mathrm{~m} \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda=600 \times 10^{-9} \mathrm{~m} \\ \Rightarrow & \lambda=600 \mathrm{~nm}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Two coherent sources of intensity ratio $9: 4$ produce interference. What is the ratio between maxima and minima in the interference pattern produced?
questions.solution:
Intensity $\left(I=k a^2\right)$, where $a$ is amplitude and $k$ is a constant.
$\begin{array}{ll}
\therefore & I_1=k a_1^2 \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{I_1}{I_2}=\frac{a_1^2}{a_2^2}=\frac{9}{4} \\
\Rightarrow & \frac{a_1}{a_2}=\frac{3}{2}
\end{array}$
Maximum intensity
$\begin{aligned}
I_{\max } & =\left(a_1+a_2\right) \\
& =(3+2)=5
\end{aligned}$
Minimum intensity
$I_{\min }=\left(a_1-a_2\right)=(3-2)=1$
$\therefore \frac{I_{\max }}{I_{\min }}=\frac{5}{1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, when wavelength used is $600 Å$ and the screen is 40 cm from the slits, then the fringes are 0.012 cm apart. What is the distance between the slits?
questions.solution:
Given, $D=40 \mathrm{~cm}=40 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}$
$\begin{aligned} \lambda & =600 Å=6000 \times 10^{-10} \mathrm{~m} \\ W & =0.012 \mathrm{~cm}=0.012 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~m}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore$ Fringe width,
$W=\frac{D \lambda}{d}$
$\Rightarrow \quad d=\frac{D \lambda}{W}$
where, $d=$ distance between the slits
$d=\frac{40 \times 10^{-2} \times 6000 \times 10^{-10}}{0.012 \times 10^{-2}}$
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 10^{-3} \mathrm{~m}=2 \mathrm{~mm} \\ & =0.2 \mathrm{~cm}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: First diffraction minima due to a single slit of width $10^{-4} \mathrm{~cm}$ is at $\theta=30^{\circ}$. Then wavelength of the light used is
questions.solution:
$a \sin \theta=1 \times \lambda$
$\Rightarrow \lambda=a \sin \theta=10^{-4} \times \frac{1}{2}=\frac{10 \times 10^{-5}}{2}$
$=5 \times 10^{-5} \mathrm{~cm}=5000 Å$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In Young's double slit experiment, slit separation is 0.6 mm and the separation between slit and screen is 1.2 m . The angular width is (the wavelength of light used is $4800 Å$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} \text { Angular width } \theta & =\frac{\beta}{D} \\ & =\frac{\lambda}{d}=\frac{4800 \times 10^{-10}}{0.6 \times 10^{-3}} \\ & =8 \times 10^{-4} \mathrm{rad}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Fundamental frequency of a sonometer wire is $n$, if the tension is made 3 times and length and diameter are also increased 3 times, what is the new frequency?
questions.solution:
Fundamental frequency,
$\begin{aligned}
& n=\frac{v}{2l}=\frac{\sqrt{T / \mu}}{2l}=\frac{\sqrt{T / \rho S}}{2l}=\frac{\sqrt{T / \rho \pi r^2}}{2l} \\
&\therefore n \propto \frac{\sqrt{T}}{2l}
\end{aligned}$
Given that tension, diameter and length all are made 3 times.
$\therefore n$ will become $\frac{1}{3 \sqrt{3}}$ times.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If an observer moves towards stationary source, then the apparent frequency is given by
questions.solution:
Generalised formula for apparent frequency is
$f_a=\left(\frac{v \pm v_o}{v \mp v_s}\right) f_0$
According to the question source is stationary,
$v_s=0$
$f_a=f^{\prime}=\left(\frac{v \pm v_0}{v}\right) f_0$
Observer is moving towards the source
$\Rightarrow f_a=f^{\prime}=\left(\frac{v+v_0}{v}\right) f$
where, $v=$ speed of the wave
$v_o=$ speed of the observer
$f=$ original frequency of the source

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a gas, two waves of wavelengths 1 m and 1.01 m are superposed and produce 10 beats in 3 s . The velocity of sound in the medium is
questions.solution:
The velocity $(v)$ is given by
$v=f \lambda$
$\Rightarrow f=\frac{v}{\lambda}$
Number of beats $=$ difference in frequencies
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore  f_1-f_2 & =v\left(\frac{1}{\lambda_1}-\frac{1}{\lambda_2}\right) \\
\frac{10}{3} & =v\left(\frac{1}{1}-\frac{1}{1.01}\right)
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{10}{3} = v\left(\frac{0.01}{1.01}\right) \\ & \Rightarrow v =\frac{10 \times 1.01}{3 \times 0.01}=336.7 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heavy uniform rope hangs vertically from the ceiling, with its lower end free. A disturbance on the rope travelling upwards from the lower end has a velocity $v$ at a distance $x$ from the lower end such that :
questions.solution:
Let $m=$ mass per unit length of rope
$T=$ tension in the rope at a distance $x$ from the lower end
$\therefore T=(\mathrm{mg}) \mathrm{x}=$ weight of $x$ metre of rope
As      $v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$
$\therefore \quad v=\sqrt{\frac{m g x}{m}}=\sqrt{g x}$
i.e., $\quad v \propto \sqrt{x}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stationary police car sounds a siren with a frequency of 990 Hz . If the speed of sound is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, an observer, driving towards the car with a speed of $33 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, will hear a frequency of
questions.solution:
From Doppler's effect the perceived frequency $f^{\prime}$ is given by
$f^{\prime}=f\left(\frac{v+v_0}{v}\right)$
Given, $f=990 \mathrm{~Hz}, v=300 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v_0=33 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$
$\begin{aligned}
\therefore f^{\prime} & =990\left(\frac{330+33}{330}\right) \\
& =990 \times \frac{363}{330}=1089 \mathrm{~Hz}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The frequency of sonometer wire is $f$, but when the weights producing the tensions are completely immersed in water, the frequency becomes $f / 2$ and on immersing the weights in a certain liquid, the frequency becomes $f / 3$. The specific gravity of the liquid is
questions.solution:
$\because f \propto \sqrt{T}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{\mathrm{f}_{\text {air }}}{\mathrm{f}_{\text {water }}} & =\sqrt{\frac{W_{\text {air }}}{W_{\text {water }}}}=\sqrt{\frac{V \rho g}{V \rho g-V \rho_w g}} \\ & =\frac{f}{\frac{f}{2}}=\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\rho-\rho_w}}\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & 2 & =\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\rho-\rho_w}} \\ \Rightarrow & & 4 \rho-4 \rho_w & =\rho \\ \Rightarrow & & \rho & =\frac{4}{3} \rho_w\end{aligned}$
Similarly in second case,
$\frac{f}{\frac{f}{3}}=\sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\rho-\rho_L}}$
$\Rightarrow \quad 3=\sqrt{\frac{\frac{4}{3} \rho_w}{4-\frac{3 \rho_L}{\rho_w}}}=\sqrt{\frac{4}{4-\frac{3 \rho_L}{\rho_w}}}$
Here, $\quad \frac{\rho_L}{\rho_w}=$ specific gravity (say $S$ )
$\begin{array}{cc}\Rightarrow & 9=\frac{4}{4-3 S} \\ \Rightarrow & 36-27 S=4 \Rightarrow S=\frac{32}{27}\end{array}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: If a wave travelling in positive $x$-direction with $A=0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, velocity $=360 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ and $\lambda=60 \mathrm{~m}$, then correct expression for the wave is
questions.solution:
General equation for a plane progressive wave travelling along positive $x$-direction is given by
$y=A \sin (\omega t-k x)$
$=A \sin \left[2 \pi\left(v t-\frac{x}{\lambda}\right)\right]\ldots$ (i)
Here, it is given
$v=360 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \lambda=60 \mathrm{~m}$
We know that, frequency $v=\frac{v}{\lambda}=\frac{360}{60}=6 \mathrm{~Hz}$ Substituting $A=0.2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, \mathrm{v}=6 \mathrm{~Hz}$
$\begin{aligned} & \lambda=60 \mathrm{~m} \text { in Eq. (i), we get } \\ & y=0.2 \sin \left[2 \pi\left(6 t-\frac{x}{60}\right)\right]\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Equations of a stationary and a travelling waves are as follows, $y_1=a \sin k x \cos \omega t$ and $y_2=a \sin (\omega t-k x)$. The phase difference between two points $x_1=\frac{\pi}{3 k}$ and $x_2=\frac{3 \pi}{2 k}$ are $\phi_1$ and $\phi_2$ respectively for two waves. The ratio $\frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}$ is
questions.solution:
At $x_1=\frac{\pi}{3 k}$ and $x_2=\frac{3 \pi}{2 k}$
$\sin k x_1$ or $\sin k x_2$ is not zero.
Therefore, neither of $x_1$ nor $x_2$ is a node.
$\Delta x=x_2-x_1=\left(\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{3}\right) \frac{\pi}{k}=\Delta x=\frac{7 \pi}{6 k}$
Since, $\quad \frac{2 \pi}{k}\gt\Delta x\gt\frac{\pi}{k}$
$\lambda\gt\Delta x\gt\frac{\lambda}{2}$  $\left\{k=\frac{2 \pi}{\lambda}\right\}$
Therefore,
$\phi_1=\pi$
and
$\begin{aligned}
& \phi_2=k \\
& \Delta x=\frac{7 \pi}{6k}
\end{aligned}$
Therefore, $\quad \frac{\phi_1}{\phi_2}=\frac{6}{7}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An open organ pipe resonated with frequency $f_1$ and 2nd harmonic. Now, one end is closed and the frequency is slowly increased, then it resonates with frequency $f_2$ and $n$th harmonic then,
questions.solution: \(\begin{aligned} f_1 & =\frac{1}{l} \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}} \\ f_2 & =\frac{n}{4 l} \sqrt{\frac{B}{\rho}} \Rightarrow \frac{f_1}{f_2}=\frac{4}{n} \Rightarrow f_2=\frac{n_{f_1}}{4}\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A massless rod is suspended by two identical strings $A B$ and $C D$ of equal length. $A$ block of mass $m$ is suspended from point $O$ such that $B O$ is equal to ' $x$ '. Further, it is observed that the frequency of 1 st harmonic (fundamental frequency) in $A B$ is equal to 2 nd harmonic frequency in $C D$. Then, length of $B O$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{array}{r}\frac{1}{2 / \sqrt{\frac{T_1}{\mu}}=\frac{1}{l} \sqrt{\frac{T_2}{\mu}}} \\ T_2=\frac{T_1}{4}\end{array}$
For rotational equilibrium, $\begin{aligned} & \\ T_1 x & =T_2(L-x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad & x=5 \end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The speed of a wave on a string is $150 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ when the tension is 120 N . The percentage increase in the tension in order to raise the wave speed by $20 \%$ is
questions.solution:
We know that $v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$
$v \propto \sqrt{T}$
$\begin{aligned} & \frac{v_1}{v_2}=\sqrt{\frac{T_1}{T_2}} \\ & \frac{T_1}{T_2}=\left(\frac{v_1}{v_2}\right)^2 \\ & \frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1}=\frac{v_1^2-v_1^2}{v_1^2} \\ & v_2=v_1+\frac{20}{100} v_1=\frac{120}{100} v_1=\frac{6}{5} v_1 \\ &= \frac{6}{5} \times 150\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \frac{T_2-T_1}{T_1} & =\frac{(180)^2-(150)^2}{(150)^2} \\ & =\frac{30 \times 330}{150 \times 180}=0.47 \\ & =\frac{T_2-T_1}{T} \times 100=0.44 \times 100=44 \%\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Oxygen is 16 times heavier than hydrogen. Equal volumes of hydrogen and oxygen are mixed. The ratio of speed of sound in the mixture to that in hydrogen is

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A man, standing between two cliffs, claps his hands and starts hearing a series of echoes at intervals of one second. If the speed of sound in air is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, the distance between the cliffs is
questions.solution:
Let the distance between the two cliffs be $d$. Since, the man is standing midway between the two cliffs, then the distance of man from either end is $d / 2$.
The distance travelled by sound (in producing an echo)
$2 \times \frac{d}{2}=v \times t \Rightarrow d=340 \times 1=340 \mathrm{~m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sound has an intensity of $2 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2$. Its intensity level in decibels is $\left(\log _{10} 2=0.3\right)$
questions.solution: $\begin{aligned} L & =10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \\ \therefore \quad L & =10 \log _{10}\left(\frac{2 \times 10^{-8}}{10^{-2}}\right) \\ & \left(\because I_0=10^{-12} \mathrm{~W} / \mathrm{m}^2\right) \\ L & =10 \log _{10}\left(2 \times 10^4\right) \\ L & =10\left[\log _{10} 2+\log _{10}(10)^4\right] \\ L & =10(4.3) \\ L & =43 \text { decibel }\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An open pipe is suddenly closed at one end with the result that the frequency of third harmonic of the closed pipe is termed to be higher by 100 Hz than the fundamental frequency of the open pipe. The fundamental frequency of the open pipe is`
questions.solution:
Fundamental frequency of open pipe $n_1=\frac{v}{2 l}$
Frequency of third harmonic of closed pipe $\begin{aligned} n_1 & =\frac{3 v}{4 l} \\ \therefore \quad \frac{3 v}{4 l}-\frac{v}{2 l} & =100 \\ \frac{v}{4 l} & =100 \\ \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{v}{2 l}\right) & =100 \\ \therefore \quad n_1 & =200 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Solar radiation is
questions.solution: Solar radiations are transverse electromagnetic waves. The central core of the sun emits a continuous electromagnetic spectrum.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Fundamental frequency of an open pipe is $f_o$, Fundamental frequency when it is half filled with water is
questions.solution:
If an open pipe is half submerged in water, it will become a closed organ pipe of length half that of open pipe as shown in figure. So, its frequency will become, $f_c=\frac{v}{4\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)}=\frac{v}{2 L}=f_o$
$i e$, equal to that of open pipe ie, frequency will remain unchanged.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sounding source of frequency 500 Hz moves towards a stationary observer with a velocity $30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. If the velocity of sound in air is $330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, find the frequency heared by the observer.
questions.solution: Here, $n=500 \mathrm{~Hz}, v_o=0$ $v_s=30 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}, v=330 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$ $\therefore$ From, $n^{\prime}=n\left(\frac{v-v_o}{v-v_s}\right)$ $\begin{aligned} & =500\left(\frac{330}{330-30}\right) \\ & =550 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The apparent frequency of the whistle of an engine changes in the ratio $9: 8$ as the engine passes a stationary observer. If the velocity of the sound is $340 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$, then the velocity of the engine is
questions.solution:
From Doppler's effect, perceived frequency
$\begin{aligned} f^{\prime} & =f\left(\frac{v-v_o}{v-v_s}\right) \\ \frac{9}{8} & =\frac{340}{340-v_s}\end{aligned}$
$\Rightarrow  9\left(340-v_s\right)=8 \times 340$
$\therefore v_s=37.7 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1} \approx 40 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A string of density $7.5 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$ and area of cross-section $0.2 \mathrm{~mm}^2$ is stretched under a tension of 20 N . When it is plucked at the mid-point, the speed of the transverse wave on the wire is
questions.solution:
The speed of transverse wave
$v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}$
$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } T=20 \mathrm{~N}, \frac{M}{l}=\frac{d \times A l}{l}=d \times A \\ & \therefore v=\sqrt{\frac{20 \times 10^{-3}}{7.5 \times 0.2 \times\left(10^{-3}\right)^2}} \\ & \therefore v \approx 116 \mathrm{~ms}^{-1}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Standing waves are formed on a string when interference occurs between two waves having
options: the same amplitude travelling in the same direction with no phase difference between them
the same amplitude, travelling in the opposite direction with no phase difference between them
different amplitudes travelling in the same direction
different amplitudes travelling in the opposite direction
questions.solution: When two identical transverse or longitudinal progressive waves travel in a bounded medium with the same speed, but in opposite directions, then by their superposition a new type of wave is produced which appears stationary in the medium. Thus, is called stationary wave.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When the wavelength of sound changes from 1 m to 1.01 m , the number of beats heard are 4 . The velocity of sound is
questions.solution:
Let $n_1$ and $n_2$ be the frequencies of the two notes of wavelengths $\lambda_1$ and $\lambda_2$.
Here, $\lambda_1=1 \mathrm{~m}, \lambda_2=1.01 \mathrm{~m}$
If $v$ is velocity of sound, then
$n_1=\frac{v}{1} \text { and } n_2=\frac{v}{1.01}$
If the number of beats produced per second is $b$, then
$\begin{aligned}
& b=n_1-n_2 \\
& 4=\frac{v}{1}-\frac{v}{1.01} \\
&\therefore v=\frac{4.04}{0.01}=404 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A stretched sonometer wire is in unison with a tuning fork. When length of wire is increase by $1 \%$, the number of beats heard per second is 5. Then the frequency of the fork is :
questions.solution:
Let frequency of tuning fork be $n_1$
$\frac{n_2}{n_1}=\frac{100}{101}$
As $n_1-n_2=5$
So, $n_1=505 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A string fixed at both ends oscillates in 5 segments, length 10 m and velocity of wave is $20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. What is the frequency?
questions.solution:
Frequency of vibration is given by
$n=\frac{p}{2 l} \sqrt{\frac{T}{m}} \left(\text { where } v=\sqrt{\frac{T}{m}}\right)$
$\therefore n=\frac{p v}{2 l}=\frac{5 \times 20}{2 \times 10}=5 \mathrm{~Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Fifty six tuning forks are arranged in a series such that each fork gives 6 beats per second with the preceding one. Assuming the frequency of the last fork to be double of the first fork, the frequency of the last fork should be
questions.solution:
From question,
$n_1+55 \times 6=n_2$
$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow & & n+55 \times 6 & =2 n \\ \Rightarrow & & n & =330 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$
$\therefore \quad n_2=2 n$
$\begin{aligned} & =2 \times 330 \\ & =660 \mathrm{~Hz}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The work done by all the forces (external and internal) on a system is equal to change in
questions.solution:
According to work-energy theorem,
$W_{\text {external }}+W_{\text {internal }}=K_f-K_i=\Delta K$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The displacement $x$ and time $t$ for a particle are related to each other as $t=\sqrt{x}+3$. What is work done in first 6 s of its motion ?
questions.solution:
$t=\sqrt{x}+3$
or $\quad x=(t-3)^2$
Now, $\quad v=\frac{d x}{d t}=2(t-3)$
At $t=0, v_1=2(-3)=-6$
At $\quad t=6, v_2=2(6-3)=6$
Work done = change in KE
$=\frac{1}{2} m v_2^2-\frac{1}{2} m v_1^2=0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: An object $A$ moving horizontally with kinetic energy of 800 J experiences a constant opposing force of 100 N , while moving from a place $x$ to a place $y$, where $x y$ is 2 m . What is the energy of $A$ at $y$ ?
questions.solution:
Energy at $y=$ total KE - work done from $x$ to $y$
$=800-100 \times 2=600 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle is moving in a vertical circle. The tensions in the string when passing through two positions at angles $30^{\circ}$ and $60^{\circ}$ from vertical (lowest positions) are $T_1$ and $T_2$, respectively. Then
options: tension in the string always remains the same
questions.solution:
Tension, \(\mathrm{T}=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}+\mathrm{mg} \cos \theta\)
For \(\theta=30^{\circ}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}_1=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}+\mathrm{mg} \cos 30^{\circ} \\
& =\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \mathrm{mg}
\end{aligned}\)
For \(\theta=60^{\circ}\)
\(\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{T}_2=\frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}+\mathrm{mgcos}^{60^{\circ}} \\
& \therefore \frac{\mathrm{mv}^2}{\mathrm{r}}+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{mg} \\
& \mathrm{~T}_1 > \mathrm{T}_2
\end{aligned}\)

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
A particle is projected from the surface of the earth with an initial speed of $4.0 \mathrm{kms}^{-1}$. The maximum height (in km) attained by the particle is _____
[Radius of earth $=6400 \mathrm{~km}$ and $\mathrm{g}=9.8 \mathrm{~ms}^{-2}$ ]

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A body moves a distance of 10 m under the action of force $F=10 \mathrm{~N}$. If the work done is 25 J , the angle which the force makes with the direction of motion is
questions.solution:
Work done, $W=F d \cos \theta$
$\begin{aligned}
25 & =10 \times 10 \cos \theta \\
\cos \theta & =\frac{1}{4} \Rightarrow \theta=\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{4}\right)
\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A block of mass 1 kg slides down a curved track that is one quadrant of a circle of radius 1 m . Speed of the block at the bottom is $2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$. Work done by the frictional force on the block when it reaches at the bottom is
questions.solution:
Given, $m=1 \mathrm{~kg}$,
$\begin{aligned} v & =2 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s} \\ R & =1 \mathrm{~m} \\ W & =?\end{aligned}$
From energy conservation,
$m g h+W=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$
$\begin{aligned} W & =\frac{1}{2} m v^2-m g h \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 1(2)^2-1 \times 10=-8 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle which is constrained to move along the $x$-axis, is subjected to a force in the same direction which varies with the distance $x$ of the particle from the origin is $F(x)=-k x+a x^3$. Here, $k$ and $a$ are positive constants. For $x \geq 0$, the functional from of the potential energy $U_{(x)}$ of the particle is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned} & \text { We know } F=-\frac{d U}{d x} \\ & \Rightarrow d U=-F \cdot d x \\ & \Rightarrow U=-\int_0^x\left(-k x+a x^3\right) d x \Rightarrow U=\frac{k x^2}{2}-\frac{a x^4}{4} \\ & \therefore \text { We get } U=0 \text { at } x=0 \text { and } x=\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{a}} \\ & \text { Also we get } U=\text { negative for } x\gt\sqrt{\frac{2 k}{a}} \end{aligned}$
From the given function we can see that $F=0$ at $x=0$ i.e., slope of $U-x$ graph is zero at $x=0$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Simple pendulum of length $l$ has a maximum angular displacement $\theta$. The maximum kinetic energy of the bob is
questions.solution: In the equilibrium position energy is in the form of kinetic energy. Height of bob at maximum angular displacement is $\begin{aligned} h & =l-l \cos \theta \\ & =l(1-\cos \theta) \\ \text { Also } \mathrm{PE} & =\mathrm{KE} \\ m g h & =m g l(1-\cos \theta)\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A running man has half the kinetic energy of that of a boy of half of his mass. The man speed up $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, so as the have same kinetic energy as that of a boy. The original speed of the man is
questions.solution:
The kinetic energy of moving body is equal to half the product of the mass $(m)$ of the body and the square of its speed $\left(v^2\right)$.
kinetic energy $=\frac{1}{2} \times$ mass $\times(\text { speed })^2$
i.e., $\quad=\frac{1}{2} m v^2$
Let mass of man is $M \mathrm{~kg}$ and speed is $v$ and speed of body is $v_1$, then
$\frac{1}{2} M v^2=\frac{1}{2}\left\{\frac{1}{2} \frac{M}{2} v_1^2\right\}$   .....(i)
when man speed up by $1 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$, than
$v=v+1$
$\therefore \quad \frac{1}{2} M(v+1)^2=\frac{1}{2}\left\{\frac{M}{2}\right\} \cdot v_1^2$    ....(ii)
Dividing Eqs. (i) by (ii), we get
$\frac{v^2}{(v+1)^2}=\frac{1}{2}$
$\sqrt{2} v=v+1$
$v=\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}-1} \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Due to a force of $(6 \mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}) \mathrm{N}$ the displacement of h
questions.solution:
Work done $W=F \times \mathbf{d}$
$\begin{aligned} & =(6 \mathbf{i}+2 \mathbf{j}) \times(3 \mathbf{i}-\mathbf{j}) \\ & =6 \times 3+2 \times-1 \\ & =18-2=16 \mathbf{J} \\ W & =16 \mathbf{J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A fighter plane is moving in a vertical circle of radius $r$. Its minimum velocity at the highest point $A$ of the circle will be
questions.solution:
When a body tied to the end of a string is revolved in a vertical circle, the speed of the body is different at different points of the circular paths.

The resultant of tension $(T)$ and weight ( mg ) provides the necessary centripetal force i.e., $\quad T+m g=\frac{m v_A^2}{r}$
Tension ( $T$ ) becomes zero at the points of minimum velocity, therefore $\begin{aligned} & \therefore & 0+m g & =\frac{m v_A^2}{r} \\ & \Rightarrow & v_A & =\sqrt{g r}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In which case does the potential energy decrease?
options: On moving a body against gravitational force
On the rising of an air bubble in water
questions.solution: When an air bubble rises in water, it moves upward due to the buoyant force. As it rises, its gravitational potential energy might increase slightly, but the total potential energy (gravitational + potential energy from pressure) decreases because the bubble moves from a high-pressure region (deeper in water) to a lower-pressure region (near the surface).

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Water is falling on the blades of a turbine at a rate of $100 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}$. If the height of the fall is 100 m , the power transferred to the turbine will be approximately
questions.solution:
Power $=\frac{\text { work }}{\text { time }}$
$=\frac{m g h}{t}=\left(\frac{m}{t}\right) g h$
Given, $\frac{m}{t}=100 \mathrm{~kg} / \mathrm{s}, h=100 \mathrm{~m}, g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
$\therefore$ Power $=100 \times 10 \times 100$
$=100 \mathrm{~kW}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metre scale is standing vertically on the earth's surface on one of its end. It now falls on earth without slipping. Find the velocity with which the free end of the scale strikes the earth. $\left(g=10 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2\right)$
questions.solution:
Velocity of free end
$\begin{aligned} v & =\sqrt{3 g h} \\ & =\sqrt{3 \times 10 \times 1} \\ & =5.47 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A wire in the form of semi-circle of radius $r$ rotates about its diameter with angular velocity $\omega$ in a magnetic field $B$. The axis of rotation is perpendicular to the field. The total resistance of the circuit is $R$. If the mean power generated per period of rotation is $\frac{\left(B \pi r^2(\omega)^2\right.}{x R}$, then the value of $x$ is
questions.solution:
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { (8) } \phi=\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{A}=B A \cos \theta=B A \cos \omega t,(\because \theta=\omega t) \\
& \text { Now, emf }(e)=-\frac{d \phi}{d t}=-\frac{d}{d t}(B A \cos \omega t) \\
& =\omega B A \sin \omega t \\
& i=\frac{e}{R}=\frac{\omega B A \sin \omega^t}{R} \\
& \therefore \quad P_{\text {ins }}=i^2 R=\left(\frac{\omega^2 B^2 A^2}{R^2} \sin ^2 \omega t\right) \cdot R \\
& =\frac{\omega^2 B^2 A^2}{R} \sin ^2 \omega t
\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad P_{\mathrm{avg}} & =\frac{\int_0^T P_{\mathrm{ins}} \times d t}{\int_0^T d t} \\ & =\frac{\omega^2 B^2 A^2}{R} \cdot \frac{\int_0^T \sin ^2 \omega t d t}{\int_0^T d t} \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \cdot \frac{\omega^2 B^2 A^2}{R} \quad\left(\because \lt \sin ^2 \omega t\gt_{t=T}=\frac{1}{2}\right) \\ & =\frac{\left(B \pi r^2 \omega\right)^2}{8 R} \quad\left(\because A=\frac{\pi r^2}{2}\right) \\ \therefore \quad x & =8\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F$ acting on a body depends on its displacement $S$ as $F \propto S^{-1 / 3}$. The power delivered by $F$ will depend on displacement as
questions.solution:
$\because F \propto S^{-1 / 3}$ or $a \propto S^{-1 / 3}$
or $V d \nu \propto S^{-1 / 3} d s\left(a=V \cdot \frac{d V}{d s}\right)$
Integrating we have, $V^2 \propto S^{2 / 3}$
or
$V \propto S^{1 / 3}$
Now, power $P \propto F \cdot V \propto S^{-1 / 3} \cdot S^{1 / 3}$ or
$P \propto S^0$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force $F=(2+x)$ acts on a particle in $x$-direction, where F is in newton and $x$ in metre. The work done by this force during'a displacement from $x=1.0 \mathrm{~m}$ to $x=2.0 \mathrm{~m}$ is
questions.solution:
The work done in small displacement from $x$ to $x+d x$ is
$d W=F d x=(2+x) d x$
Hence,
$W=\int_1^2 d W=\int_1^2(2+x) d x=\int_1^2 2 d x+\int_1^2 x d x$
$=\left[2 x+\frac{x^2}{2}\right]_1^2=3.5 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A force of 20 N acts on a body of mass 2 kg initially at rest. Find the work done in 2 s .
questions.solution:
$F=\frac{d p}{d t} \Rightarrow p=F d t$
where $p=$ momentum $=m v$
$\therefore m v=F d t$
$\Rightarrow v=\frac{F d t}{m}$
Given, $F=20 \mathrm{~N}, d t=2 \mathrm{~s}, m=2 \mathrm{~kg}$
$\therefore v=\frac{20 \times 2}{2}=20 \mathrm{~m} / \mathrm{s}^2$
Work done $=\mathrm{KE}=\frac{1}{2} \times 2 \times(20)^2=400 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: When a spring is stretched by 2 cm , it stores 100 J of energy. If it is stretched further by 2 cm , the stored energy will be increased by
questions.solution:
Ist case, $\begin{aligned} U & =\frac{1}{2} k x_1^2 \\ 100 & =\frac{1}{2} k\left(2 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \\ k & =\frac{200}{4 \times 10^{-4}} \\ & =50 \times 10^4 \end{aligned}$
IInd case $\begin{aligned} U & =\frac{1}{2} k x_2^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 10^4\left(4 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \\ & =25 \times 10^4 \times 16 \times 10^{-4} \\ & =400 \mathrm{~J} \end{aligned}$
So, stored energy increases by $400-100=300 \mathrm{~J}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A heavy mass is attached to a thin wire and is whirled in a vertical circle. The wire is most likely to break
options: when the mass is at the highest point of the circle
when the mass is at the lowest point of the circle
at an angle of $\cos ^{-1}\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)$ from the upward vertical
questions.solution: The tension at the highest point is zero, but tension at the lowest point is 6 times the weight of the body. Hence, wire is most likely to break at the lowest point.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The force constant of weightless spring is $16 \mathrm{~N} / \mathrm{m}$. A body of mass 1.0 kg suspended from it is pulled down through 5 cm and then released. The maximum kinetic energy of the system (spring + body) will be
questions.solution:
Maximum KE of the system
= Maximum PE of the system
$\begin{aligned} & =\frac{1}{2} k x^2 \\ & =\frac{1}{2} \times 16 \times\left(5 \times 10^{-2}\right)^2 \\ & =2 \times 10^{-2} \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A particle moves along $X$-axis from $x=0$ to $x=5 \mathrm{~cm}$ under the influence of force given by $F=\left(7-2 x+3 x^2\right) \mathrm{N}$. The work done in the process is
questions.solution:
The given force varies with distance. Therefore, work done,
$W=\int_0^{x=5} F \cdot d x$
$\begin{aligned} & =\int_0^5\left(7-2 x+3 x^2\right) d x \\ & =\left[7 x-\frac{2 x^2}{2}+\frac{3 x^3}{3}\right]_0^5\end{aligned}$
$\begin{aligned} & =\left[7 \times 5-(5)^2+(5)^3-0\right] \\ & =135 \mathrm{~J}\end{aligned}$
